###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note! (long)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-16T13:14:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d340d92_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net> <25fb80a6.0207121311.5fb73e06@posting.google.com> <3d32ca4a_4@Usenet.com> <217e491a.0207151453.2b703eb1@posting.google.com> <3D335B2B.5C197DC6@shaw.ca>`

```

Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3D335B2B.5C197DC6@shaw.ca...

> Given the topic, don't know where the other languages will be going - but
check out the
> following survey on COBOL usage :-
>
>         http://www.cobolportal.com/developer/future.asp
>
Thanks for this link, Jimmy. It is very interesting. I read and digested it
carefully. (In passing, I have to note that the standard of English in the
document was appalling, with frequent grammatical and spelling
errors...coming from 3 University Professors it makes me wonder about the
standard of education in the US...To be fair, English may not be the first
language of one of the Authors, but let's not get sidetracked...<G>)

Here is what I think about it...

>>>
"This paper will describe the results from a survey taken of professional
business and industry employers who are using COBOL in their information
systems. A discussion of the IS Manager's view of the future of COBOL is
presented. Almost 90 percent of IS Mangers surveyed indicated that COBOL
should continue to be offered in college curriculums, and also nearly 90
percent indicate that both object-oriented and web-based features of the
COBOL language should be integrated into COBOL instruction in college
curriculums. It is hoped that these results will help academia to design
their curriculums to meet the expanding need for IS people over the next
five years."
<<<
Two things to note here.

1. They surveyed sites already committed to COBOL. That's fine, but it doesn
't indicate the "take up" of COBOL by uncommitted sites. If the committed
sites slowly erode (and I believe that is what is happening) then the basis
for this paper is doubtful.
2. They recognise there is no future for COBOL without OO and are moving to
get it taught as part of the new COBOL curriculum. If this succeeds, I would
have to review completely my prediction as to the demise of COBOL. I believe
this may be the one thing that could save it and bring it into the 21st
Century. I got quite excited by this, but calmed down when I read what they
said later <G>. see below.

>>>
"Responses were received from 142 business and industry organizations. The
response rate was 2.8 percent from this business and industry group."
<<<
Hardly an enthusiastic response. Nevertheless, the survey itself is
valuable.

>>>
More than 45 percent of IS manager responses stated that COBOL would
continue to be used over the next 10 years at the existing level while less
than 15 percent indicated that COBOL would be eliminated or replaced in the
organization.
<<<
Most IS managers have NO IDEA what is going to happen in the next 10 MONTHS,
never mind the next 10 YEARS.  Effective planning covers 3 to 6 months
accurately, after that all bets are off.  Their responses are based on what
they see NOW. It ain't necessarily so..

>>>
Slightly more than 30 percent of the respondents indicated that COBOL would
decline in importance of use in application development. However, about 5
percent said that use of COBOL would actually increase over the next 10
years.
<<<
We need to know the experience background of these "Managers". How accurate
are they likely to be?  Anybody can predict anything. It is the basis for
the prediction, and the insight of the predictor, that determine the
accuracy of the prediction.

>>>
Nearly 20 percent of IS managers indicated that object-oriented COBOL would
be used to wrap existing COBOL applications in the development of new
applications. More than 30 percent of respondents indicated that they are
implementing COBOL web-based facilities to enable migration of legacy COBOL
applications to the Internet.
<<<
So around 30% of the respondents to the survey are planning to migrate
existing code and are looking at sensible ways to do this. This is
encouraging, but it still leaves 70% who are happy with the status
quo.("Ostriches".<G>). This is where the decline of COBOL by natural
attrition is likely to occur.

>>>
 Also, more than 30 percent of IS managers stated that they will be using
another computer language development environment to eventually replace
COBOL.
<<<
Ah, so my 70% of ostriches is really only 40% because 30% have already
decided to replace COBOL. (I can't decide whether that is good or bad
news.<G>)

>>>
More than 25 percent of the respondents believe that object-oriented
methodology will become the standard for future application development
while more than 60 percent indicate the belief that object-oriented
methodology will become popular but will not totally replace existing
structured methodology.
<<<
So 15% either don't know or don't care, and 60% cannot let go of what they
do now, although they are being pushed to do so. The good news is that 25%
(more than I expected) can see where it's going. There may be some hope yet.

The problem with this 25% is that they will respond to a survey saying they
believe it will happen, but they are not prepared to bite the bullet and
MAKE it happen. Until we see some real action indicating a change of heart
from Fortress COBOL, the Language remains hanging by a thread. Client/Server
implementations, while very valuable, will not of themselves ensure the
continued life and zestful health of COBOL.

>>>
It seems fairly evident that the results obtained from the present research
positively rejects the null hypothesis. The COBOL language will continue to
be widely used in business application development over the next 10 years.
Neither business nor academia can deny this analysis of the data as
interpreted by the authors.
<<<
Brave words! Unfortunately, not necessarily true words.  Both Business and
Acadaemia can certainly contest, if not refute, the accuracy of "this
analysis of the data as interpreted by the authors".

While certainly better than nothing, the sample is poor. There is no
indication of the background and experience of the "IS Managers", and they
are all from Fortress COBOL installations with a vested interest in
preserving the status quo.

Nevertheless, the fact they are requiring new recruits to have a grounding
in OO techniques (even though they don't understand these techniques
themselves) is some encouragement.

My own assessment is as follows:

This survey gives some glimmers of hope for the future, but it does NOT give
any solid indication of ACTION towards this future. The attitude is
"Acadaemia should provide people who know the latest stuff and then we can
look at maybe doing something about it." There is no commitment to in-house
training NOW.  Ths is understandable because the Managers surveyed do not
themselves understand why it is important. They have an uncomfortable
feeling that the technology is sweeping past COBOL (which indeed it is.) but
they can't put their fingers on what is required or why it is required.

I reckon programmers should take things into their own hands, for the sake
of their own futures. Expand your skill set. Learn Java and OO concepts AS
WELL as COBOL. It can only improve your value. The days when you could get a
lifetime career out of writing COBOL are fast disappearing.

Whether I am right or the Professors are right about COBOL in the next 10
years, can you afford to take the chance?

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note! (long)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T19:24:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uj6lvvop81uk24@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net> <25fb80a6.0207121311.5fb73e06@posting.google.com> <3d32ca4a_4@Usenet.com>`

```
Antony Flew, in "The Dictionary of Philosopy," 1979, p.263,
wrote, of Sir Earl Popper;

"In the field of social and political philosopy, Popper is best
known for his critique of historicism, particularly as exemplified
by Plato, Hegel, and Marx. This is the doctrine that there are
laws or principles of historical development, mastery of which
would enable us to predict the course of human history, much
as the astronomer predicts eclipses. In Popper's view
predictions are only possible for systems that are
'well-isolated, stationary, and recurrent' ('Conjectures and
Refutations,' 1974 edition, p.339) and this does not, and
could not, hold of human society, where among the major
factors determining development are our own decisions
about how to respond to our situation. Thus, for example,
the technology that has become such a powerful influence
on contemporary society could not even in principle been
predicted a century ago. For Popper the choice and
responsibility have to remain with individuals; we can never
have sufficient grounds for saying, 'Society must develop
thus, whether its members want it so or not.'"

Within the 'society' of computer programmers we make
"our own decisions about how to respond to our situation."
While Popper suggests that one should not make
predictions of the future in such societies, it is nonetheless
true that had one predicted the demise of numerous
"COBOL-killer" languages; such predictions would have
had more success than predictions of the demise of
COBOL.

COBOL is somewhat unique in its orientation toward the
expression of most business problems. Wulf (as quoted
in other threads) said "the nature of language shapes
the ways in which we think about problems." COBOL is
well-suited to thinking about solutions to business problems.

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3d32ca4a_4@Usenet.com...
[snip]
>
> My own thoughts on the subject follow, and other comments are interspersed
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL at all in the developing IT landscape.

As long as business exists there will be a market for COBOL.
The question is how often people in "our situation" will decide
to use COBOL. That determines the size of the market.

> I believe the useful life of COBOL will not be more than 10 years. (Note I
> said "useful"...Personally, I shall be writing COBOL for the next 20 years
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> alternatives will appear in the next 10 years)) We are already seeing the
OO
> languages like Java eroding what has traditionally been COBOL's base.

This depends a great deal on one's definition of "what has
traditionally been COBOL's base." I define COBOL's base
as batch processing. That is because standard COBOL has
no interactive capability. While it is possible to run batch Java
programs, that is not its strength.

> The failure of the COBOL user base to embrace OO COBOL (and this has been
> compounded by the dismal failure to get the standard for it released) has
> probably killed any future that the Language might have had.

Once again people in "our situation" determine whether OO
adds value to COBOL. I see little value in OO for most
COBOL applications. I do see value in being able to say
"me too!"

[snip]
> Whatever good reasons there may be for charging a Runtime Fee (and I
accept
> many of John's arguments), the Market place simply cannot and will not
> accept that imposition. (at least it won't long term...)

I can agree with that!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T15:24:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0207121424.3ff7920@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```
Angel <angel@smart.net> wrote in message > 
> And with no resell runtime license fees.  It should 
> be as easy to get the runtime as it is to get the latest
> media player on Windows.  From a jack box that 
> pops up on the screen asking if you want to download it 
> from the web.  Free.

Well actually it is not 'free' (as in freedom) and certainly not
unemcumbered.  To get the latest WMP you need to accept a new EULA.  I
suggest that you read this because clicking it gives up control of
your machine to Microsoft.  It specifically gives them permission to
download new software that they choose onto your machine and to
'update' or 'upgrade' any software that you have and to disable any
software that is on your machine without asking you.

The only thing 'free' is that they didn't ask you to pay money to have
your freedom compromised in this way.

Registering the MS EULA in the past has given them (or their agents
the BSA) the rights to enter your property and inspect your computers
for any transgressions and also given them the right to fine you for
not being able to supply the appropriate paperwork for what is on your
machine.

Now with this new EULA that you probably didn't even notice accepting,
they don't even need to enter your premises to inspect and change the
contents of your computer.

> Make it easy for the market to accept COBOL.

Well you've helped the market accept dictatorship.

> Fujitsu moved in this direction a few years ago.
> They started giving away free their basic COBOL-85 compiler. 
> And with no resell fees for runtime. 

The free compiler is under a student or academic licence.  Have you
actually read this licence ?  Does it actually give you the right to
distribute run-times at all ?

Yes, Fujitsu commercial versions (ie paid for) do give the developer
the right to distribute run-times for free, but you seem to be
arbitralily attributing this to the free student and academic versions
as well.

> It would boost the industry if the vendors sold their COBOL-85 
> compilers at a give away (loss leader) price 
> of $50.00 US.  With no resell runtime fees. 

It may 'boost' the programmers for a very short time, but 'the
industry' is the compiler writers and they would go broke in a week. 
With no compiler writers where would the programmers go ?
 
> Then sell their GUI, web, debuggers, cbl_ subroutines, 
> and compiler extensions, at whatever price 
> the market can bear.

All that would happen is that other companies would write the GUI,
Web, subroutines too and would undersell the compiler writers as they
would have no compiler losses to underwrite.

> As I mentioned, Fujitsu went in this direction.
> I expect Fujitsu to gain more profits than 
> their competitors in the future.

Fujitsu uses their hardware and mainframe business to support their
Cobol development.  Much of what is in Fujitsu Cobol is aimed at
supporting mainframe use, they don't need to make a profit from PC
Cobol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Angel <angel@smart.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T23:11:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.LNX.4.21.0207122247570.28108-100000@smarty.smart.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0207121424.3ff7920@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net> <217e491a.0207121424.3ff7920@posting.google.com>`

```

Thank you for your comments. 

Yes, I did imply that the free compiler from Fujitsu 
allows free runtimes.  Thanks for the correction.

In my mind, the nuisance of having to deal with runtime 
licenses might impair one's ability to sell a product 
to thousands of customers.  Selling is hard enough
as it is, without another reason for the customers
to just leave. 

But from another post, I can see that runtime licenses
are a necessity in some markets.  I stand corrected.

And your comment about Fujitsu is not making a 
profit on their COBOL compiler.  That is sobering,
if it is true.  I would hope all COBOL vendors
could maintain a handsome profit.  If they can not, 
then the customers would really have incentive 
to leave. 

It may be that, within the COBOL industry, runtime
licenses are the nature of the beast.  If that's 
true, I humbly ask compiler vendors to make them 
as gentle as possible.  So as not to scare off 
potential customers.

This concludes my comments.  One need not read
the rather long post following again. 


On 12 Jul 2002, Richard wrote:

> Date: 12 Jul 2002 15:24:42 -0700
> From: Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T22:14:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0207122114.3b843b0a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net>`

```
Angel <angel@smart.net> wrote in message 
> A price on par with various other language vendors. 
> And with no resell runtime license fees.  

You shouldn't think that, say, VB, has no run-time costs.  The
run-time cost is buying Windows, or upgrading to the latest version,
plus Client licences for SQL server or for copies of Access.

The .NET CLR for C#, for example, appears to be free but using it will
result in sending money to MS at some point - for upgarding NT/2000
servers to .NET servers, or upgrading to a new version of SQL Server
that has the CLR embedded, or dumping existing Oracle or DB/2 and
replacing with MS SQL.

Compare this with, say, AcuCobol or RM Cobol where paying for the
run-times allows you to run these on the system that the _user_
chooses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-13T13:43:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8WX8.89391$eF5.2672583@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net> <217e491a.0207122114.3b843b0a@posting.google.com>`

```
That is a red herring Richard. Outside of Linux, you pay for *any* O/S and the associated services.
You could as well deploy under AIX, which is 'free" for unlimited users on any RS/6000(PSeries) machine
you buy from IBM. You still paid IBM for that O/S, they just included the cost in the cost of the machine.

Oh, and the IBM COBOL compiler for AIX is $899 per machine, and has no runtime licensing.

In fact, IBM is a good example there, both OS/400 and AIX used to have nasty restrictive licensing, and
now they don't. IBM makes a LOT more money off both of them now.


"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:217e491a.0207122114.3b843b0a@posting.google.com...
> Angel <angel@smart.net> wrote in message
> > A price on par with various other language vendors.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> chooses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry? Vendors note!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-07-13T13:00:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0207131200.5a5cda99@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207120710460.20531-100000@smarty.smart.net> <217e491a.0207122114.3b843b0a@posting.google.com> <s8WX8.89391$eF5.2672583@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message 
> That is a red herring Richard. Outside of Linux, you pay for *any* O/S and the associated services.

You miss the point.  Yes you pay for the OS and associated services,
but you may pay a single company for all different services, or you
may pay several different companies.  Where there may appear to be
'free' runtimes, it may be that there are other payments to the same
company.

> You could as well deploy under AIX, which is 'free" for unlimited users on any RS/6000(PSeries) machine
> you buy from IBM. You still paid IBM for that O/S, they just included the cost in the cost of the machine.
> 
> Oh, and the IBM COBOL compiler for AIX is $899 per machine, and has no runtime licensing.

Exactly my point.  The RS/6000 includes the cost of AIX and Cobol
run-times.  If AIX was available for non-IBM hardware then it would
not be free.  The IBM Cobol compiler (just like the Fujitsu one) is
aimed at encouraging the use of mainframe services, so IBM will
subsidise the development from its other business.

> In fact, IBM is a good example there, both OS/400 and AIX used to have nasty restrictive licensing, and
> now they don't. IBM makes a LOT more money off both of them now.

I doubt that they make a 'lot' of money from the Cobol products, but
they do make more money from upgrading the hardware to run the new
Cobol systems implemented because the runtime is free.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T01:11:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XGJZ8.48883$88.833945@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afhquj$f40$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <Pine.LNX.4.21.0207102120010.16224-100000@smarty.smart.net> <Fe8X8.90586$q53.2241815@twister.austin.rr.com> <RLIZ8.563835$352.98930@sccrnsc02>`

```
WebLogic, WebSphere and so forth are applications that are written in Java. The JRE necessary to run them comes free with the
distribution, no runtimes at all.

VisualBasic development is essentially a one time cost, the cost of the software.

All software everywhere has a cost associated with it of a platform to host it on, but that is NOT a runtime license fee.

-Paul

## Thread: VB 6 call to Fujitsu COBOL DLL problem — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### VB 6 call to Fujitsu COBOL DLL problem

- **From:** corek@nospam.hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2002-07-02T13:38:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rb73iusdd01jef9mea2o1k2roqcatmjq13@4ax.com>`

```
I have followed the instructions in the Fujitsu handbook, using 
their Programming Staff, for Hicobol,  but when I try and link to it
from the VB
application I get 
Run-Time error '453'

Can't find DLL entry point Hicobol in
and then the Path.

Is it me or VB6?

Cobol
000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020  PROGRAM-ID. HICOBOL.
000030 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000040 DATA DIVISION.
000041 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000050 LINKAGE SECTION.
000070*
000080  01  vbinteger           PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
000100  01  vbstring            PIC X(16).
000120 PROCEDURE DIVISION WITH STDCALL LINKAGE
000160                    USING vbinteger, vbstring.
000170      MOVE 101   TO vbinteger.
000180      MOVE "Hello from COBOL"  to vbstring.
001560
001570      EXIT PROGRAM.
001580 


VB Code
Private Declare Sub Hicobol Lib
"C:\COBOL\fsc\PCOBOL32\samples\Hicobol\HICOBOL.DLL" (vbinteger As
Integer, ByVal vbstring As String)
Private Declare Sub JMPCINT2 Lib "C:\cobol\fsc\pcobol32\F3BIPRCT.DLL"
()
Private Declare Sub JMPCINT3 Lib "C:\cobol\fsc\pcobol32\F3BIPRCT.DLL"
()

Private Sub cmdCbl_Click()
Dim vbinteger As Integer
Dim vbstring As String * 16
Call JMPCINT2
Call Hicobol(vbinteger, vbstring)
txtText.Text = vbstring
Call JMPCINT3
End Sub

Private Sub cmdReset_Click()
txtText.Text = "Text set to Visual Basic"
End Sub
```

#### ↳ Re: VB 6 call to Fujitsu COBOL DLL problem

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@kub.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-07-02T15:57:33+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afsbd9$oda$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`
- **References:** `<rb73iusdd01jef9mea2o1k2roqcatmjq13@4ax.com>`

```
Maybe an error in the call from vb:

Private Declare Sub Hicobol Lib
 "C:\COBOL\fsc\PCOBOL32\samples\Hicobol\HICOBOL.DLL" (vbinteger As
Integer, ByVal vbstring As String)

should be:

Private Declare Sub Hicobol Lib
 "C:\COBOL\fsc\PCOBOL32\samples\Hicobol\HICOBOL.DLL" (ByVal vbinteger As
Integer, ByVal vbstring As String)

you might have forgotten the ByVal of vbinteger in the Hicobol.lib declaration?

Kind regards,

Rik Hendriks

<corek@nospam.hotmail.com> wrote in message news:rb73iusdd01jef9mea2o1k2roqcatmjq13@4ax.com...
> I have followed the instructions in the Fujitsu handbook, using 
> their Programming Staff, for Hicobol,  but when I try and link to it
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> End Sub
```

The

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcfdd616.0207030439.74af3ed8@posting.google.com>`

```
I have a problem with the routine CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST.
my environment:
machine: IBM RS/6000 (AIX)
compiler: microfocus object cobol v4.1.06.
If I use this routine with a file on a directory, which is mounted
with NFS, and the machine with this directory is down, my program
hangs.
I think I have to test, if the machine is down or not, but how?
```

#### ↳ Re: CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST

- **From:** ctimmer@gci.net (Curt Timmerman)
- **Date:** 2002-07-03T16:36:56

- **Message-ID:** `<Xns924057A43318Cctimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32>`

```
g.friedrich@fiuka.de (Friedrich) wrote in 
<bcfdd616.0207030439.74af3ed8@posting.google.com>:

>I have a problem with the routine CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST.
>my environment:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I think I have to test, if the machine is down or not, but how?

Did you give it enough time? When the remote host is down, an NFS request 
will have to time out before an error is returned. It could take a couple 
of minutes.
```

#### ↳ Re: CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST

- **From:** qIroS <qIroS@tlhIngan.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-07-03T18:43:19+01:00

```
ghItlh g.friedrich@fiuka.de (Friedrich) 

>I have a problem with the routine CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST.
>my environment:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I think I have to test, if the machine is down or not, but how?

This will happen with hard NFS mounts.

Make sure you understand the differences, but if you want your program
just to bomb if the NFS server goes away, use soft mount.
```

## Thread: MF Fileshare and CCITCP2 — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [2002-06](2002-06.md#t-ee6aca462d)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF Fileshare and CCITCP2

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-07-03T12:09:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0207031109.70a73022@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0206201729.70a01baf@posting.google.com> <aevcft$5l8$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```
Thanks for your assistance...we've been able to resolve the problem
now.

In fact it appears that CCITCP2 environment variable may not have been
set properly but the real problem was that the "server" was named with
a space in the name. Let's say it was called "abc xyz", while it does
seem that some communication happens it isn't successful and the
client eventually times out. By changing this to "abc-xyz" everyhing
works fine!

I had not really noticed this before (although I did see it, of
course). I thought that even though the IP addresses were dynamic
CCITCP2 should have made it work with whatever IP addresses were set
but I now wonder whether some part of the messages uses the name
itself; in this particular case the space is, I suppose, treated as a
terminator and thus the named is processed incorrectly.

Where we've tested exactly the same set up and installation elsewhere,
where the IP addresses were static, the problem never arose. I suspect
that I will have to check whether these have spaces in the machine
names simply to satisfy my inquisitiveness, now; of course one may
suspect that they all have well-formed names!

graham

function of COBOL II ( such as max, sqrt...)? and I wonder if these

#### ↳ Re: build-in function?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-04T07:20:51+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aem7iu00pbrrjvhg1tgafl73ka8nhhdl1r@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<pmMU8.115658$s82.8506310@news1.calgary.shaw.ca>`

```
On Thu, 04 Jul 2002 00:08:53 GMT, "wenning" <wenning.gao@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>I am new to COBOL world. Does anyone know where I can get a list of build-in
>function of COBOL II ( such as max, sqrt...)? and I wonder if these
>functions are widely used. Thanks. --wenning
>

Very simple:  There are no intrinsic functions in COBOL II.  As simple
as that.

There are builtin function in COBOL For this and that, and you find
them all in the Reference Manual at
 
http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/epubs/pdf/igy3lr00.pdf

following code, i can not get the right return.

......

Environment division.
Input-Output Section.
File-control.
    Select Compare-file Assign to "d:\mywork\test\compare.txt"
           organization is Sequential

#### ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-04T10:37:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RzVU8.4198$Bd2.145@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com>`

```
He Yanchun <heyanchun@163.net> wrote in message
news:96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com...
> I use VA COBOL 3.0.5 to develop. And when i want to read a file using
> following code, i can not get the right return.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's wrong with my code or file? Please help me out! Thank you!

Code '39'  after the open means the open failed. Refer to your manual for
the exact meaning of the code.

Since the OPEN failed, the READ cannot succeed (it's an attempt to read an
unopened file). If you did a DISPLAY FS-CODE after the read, you'd see the
appropriate file status for that, too.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-04T13:15:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7UXU8.548943$%y.36241951@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com>`

```

"He Yanchun" <heyanchun@163.net> wrote in message
news:96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com...
> I use VA COBOL 3.0.5 to develop. And when i want to read a file using
> following code, i can not get the right return.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

1. Usually you can't do both input and output on a sequential file. So, you
should OPEN INPUT.

2. Starting with "Read..." there better be a period somewhere or the "Close"
statement is part of the action undertaken after the first successful read
operation. I recommend:

   READ...
      AT END ...
      NOT AT END ...
      END-READ.

3. Your program only reads one record then quits. Consider:

   READ
   PERFORM UNTIL FS-CODE NOT = '00'
      DISPLAY 'in the read' (or other processing)
      READ
      END-PERFORM.

This is the so-called 'priming read' technique. A slightly longer version
is:

   MOVE 'N' TO EOF-FLAG.
   PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG NOT = 'N'
      READ
         AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
         END-READ
      IF EOF-FLAG = 'N'
         DISPLAY 'in the read' (or other processing)
      ELSE
         CLOSE
      END-IF
   END-PERFORM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** heyanchun@163.net (He Yanchun)

He Yanchun
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<7UXU8.548943$%y.36241951@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> 1. Usually you can't do both input and output on a sequential file. So, you
> should OPEN INPUT.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>    END-PERFORM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T07:52:02+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D254212.77AE4A5E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <7UXU8.548943$%y.36241951@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>       END-PERFORM.

When testing a file status code for successful operation only the first
digit should be checked for zero.  Checking both characters for '00' may
give false failure signals.  The first character of zero indicates
success, the 2nd character may indicate additional information.  Thus
'02' also indicates successful read for example.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-04T23:09:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bB4V8.276180$_j6.13634745@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <7UXU8.548943$%y.36241951@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3D254212.77AE4A5E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D254212.77AE4A5E@Azonic.co.nz...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> '02' also indicates successful read for example.

Not on a sequential file, it doesn't.

My compiler lists the following non-'00' status codes:

02 - Duplicate key (either read or write)
04 - Record length does not conform to fixed attributes
05 - Bad open when OPTIONAL clause present
07 - Open or Close issued with NO REWIND specified
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-06T15:49:27+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D270377.882F0AC9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <7UXU8.548943$%y.36241951@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3D254212.77AE4A5E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
>
> > When testing a file status code for successful operation only the first
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 07 - Open or Close issued with NO REWIND specified

Those are _sucessful_ completions of the operation with additional
information - exactly as I stated.  

For example an '02' indicates that the record has been read or written
successfully and there is a duplicate key where this has been specified
as 'WITH DUPLICATES'.  If the read or write failed because there was a
duplicate key and these were _not_ allowed the the status would be '22'.

The '05' indicates an OPTIONAL file did not exist when it was OPENed for
input.  This is a _successful_ operation because the file is specified
as OPTIONAL.  Even though the file does not exist it has been 'OPENed'
correctly and may be READ, but the AT END or INVALID KEY path will be
taken.  The file should also be CLOSEd before the program finishes.

The '07' indicates that the file has been successfully closed but was
not rewound, as requested.

These are examples of exactly why only the first byte should be tested
for success and the 2nd byte is additional information.  In fact these
are the only code defined in ANS85 in this category, but the next
standard may define more - programs that only test for '00' may give
false failure messages when the next standard system is installed.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T12:16:44+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D25801C.30E4077@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <7UXU8.548943$%y.36241951@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> 1. Usually you can't do both input and output on a sequential file. 

There is no such restriction on disk files.  A disk SEQUENTIAL file can
always be open in I-O mode for READing and REWRITEing.  Certainly it
need to be open OUTPUT or EXTEND for WRITEing new records.

> So, you should OPEN INPUT.

Only if he doesn't want to REWRITE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T08:02:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D254475.BE2ADB60@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com>`

```
He Yanchun wrote:
> 
> The file "d:\mywork\test\compare.txt" is my defined using UltraEdit,as
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>      ----------display after read

You cannot create a 'SEQUENTIAL' file (or a RELATIVE or INDEXED) using a
text editor.  These file types are not just random groups of bytes but
have additional bytes in them that give them a structure.  For example
they may have header information that describes to the Cobol program
what the record length is and there may be record size counts.

A 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' file, however, _is_ just groups of bytes with a
CR/LF record terminators and can be created by a text editor.

When you say in your program that the file is 'SEQUENTIAL' (or RELATIVE
or INDEXED) with a record size as in the FD then the '39' status is
telling you: "No it isn't".

You need to read the data you have created as a LINE SEQUENTIAL file and
if you need it as a SEQUENTIAL or INDEXED you need to write this from
your program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-04T23:16:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LH4V8.122202$Ca2.6598069@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <3D254475.BE2ADB60@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D254475.BE2ADB60@Azonic.co.nz...
> He Yanchun wrote:
> >
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> CR/LF record terminators and can be created by a text editor.

Well he COULD create the required sequential file with a text editor by
defining his file as:

FD Compare-file.
01 compare-field.
    05 first-para             PIC X(10).
    05 second-para            PIC X(10).
    05  filler-crlf            PIC X(2).

to account for the extra carriage-return/line-feed silently inserted by the
text editor. Or since he had only four records in his file, he COULD have
typed:

"1234567890abcdefghij1234567890abcdefghij1234567890abcdefghij1234567890abcde
fghij"

into the text editor and his original FD would have worked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** "Al Shannon" <shannon@allover.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T20:13:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zftV8.1$AY2.1396@news.sjc.globix.net>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <3D254475.BE2ADB60@Azonic.co.nz> <LH4V8.122202$Ca2.6598069@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:LH4V8.122202$Ca2.6598069@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> to account for the extra carriage-return/line-feed silently inserted by
the
> text editor. Or since he had only four records in his file, he COULD have
> typed:
>
>
"1234567890abcdefghij1234567890abcdefghij1234567890abcdefghij1234567890abcde
> fghij"
>
> into the text editor and his original FD would have worked.
>
>

Not true.  Richard Plinston is correct.  Relative, Sequential, and Indexed
files are created and manipulated using the selected record-oriented
filesystem STL or Btrieve (think VSAM on the mainframe).  Text editors
create stream files, defined in VA COBOL as Line-Sequential organization.

Al...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Two questions about VA COBOL,one is for language,the other is for tool.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-06T16:02:44+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D270694.6C8E19@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <3D254475.BE2ADB60@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Jerrymouse wrote:
> > A 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' file, however, _is_ just groups of bytes with a
> > CR/LF record terminators and can be created by a text editor.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> text editor. 

No.  Not necessarily.  It may work on some system _if_ the sequential
files are defined only as fixed length records with no other formatting
_and_ he can guarantee that the editor will put the line ends at the
correct place.  However, I don't know that VA Cobol will work with such
files, I do know that many Cobol systems certainly will not, they write
a file header with information about record length and other details
prior to the first record.

In fact because VA Cobol failed on the OPEN as a SEQUENTIAL file I would
take this as prima-face evidence that it tried to check some header
information and based its '39' status on not finding it.  If the system
only used fixed block records as you postulate then it would have no
basis to report a '39' failure, but would only fail after finding the
last record incomplete.

> Or since he had only four records in his file, he COULD have
> typed:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> into the text editor and his original FD would have worked.

No. Wrong.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Could you tell me the difference between STL or Btrieve

- **From:** "Al Shannon" <shannon@allover.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T21:43:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F%tW8.3$AY2.9894@news.sjc.globix.net>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207032226.2c22e02@posting.google.com> <3D254475.BE2ADB60@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I received some questions via e-mail:

        I want to know more about STL or Btrieve (VSAM on the mainframe),
and the difference among them. Perhaps include QSAM.

        And could you please tell me which filesystem has been used on NT or
AS/400.


For detailed information see the VA COBOL Programming Guide "Chapter 7.
Processing files" available online at

http://www-3.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/va/library/

From the Programming Guide:

    Record-oriented files that are organized as sequential, relative,
indexed, or line
sequential (byte stream) files are accessed through a file system. You can
use
file-system functions to create and manipulate the records in any of these
types of
files.
    VisualAge COBOL supports the following file systems:
    The STL file system, which provides the basic facilities for local
files. It is
provided with VisualAge COBOL, and supports sequential, relative, and
indexed
files.
    The VSAM file system, which allows you to read and write files on remote
systems such as OS/390. It is provided with VisualAge COBOL, and supports
sequential, relative, and indexed files.
    The Btrieve file system, which allows you to access Btrieve files.
Btrieve is a
separate product available from Pervasive Software.
    Tip: By using the Btrieve file system, you can access files created by
VisualAge
CICS Enterprise Application Development.
    Most programs will have the same results on all file systems. However,
files
written using one file system cannot be read using a different file system.
You can select a file system by setting the assignment-name environment
variable
or by using the FILESYS run-time option. All the file systems allow you to
use
COBOL statements to read or write COBOL files.


VSAM and QSAM are mainframe (MVS, zOS) record-oriented filesystem access
methods used by mainframe COBOL.

STL is available on OS/2, Windows, and AIX.  The last I checked, Btrieve is
available on OS/2 and Windows.

Al...
```

<a id="t-6a42c9c1b8"></a>
## Thread: COBOL for OS/390 Side File allocation/DDname — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL for OS/390 Side File allocation/DDname

- **From:** bo774@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Kelly Bert Manning)
- **Date:** 2002-07-04T06:38:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ag0qga$hdd$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
Side Files contain debug information written to SYSDEBUG at compile
time when TEST(NONE,SYM,SEPARATE) is used. The question I asked was how
to allocate them explicitly during run time. Apparently I confused
some readers, since I got comments telling me to use DDname SYSDEBUG, 
which is the Compile time output DDname, not the run time input DDname.

I am interested in using this for getting enhanced CEEDUMP reporting
at run time for IMS MPPs and CICS production programs where SEPARATE 
is an attractive option for reducing object module bloat, etc.

The difficulty is that in an SCM enviroment the side file should be
promoted along with the source, object module, DBRMs, etc. so the
Side File DSname recorded in the object deck no longer exists.

Since there didn't seem to be any advice or reference information I 
ran some experiments and discovered that DDname 
   IGZDBGIN
is used. Attempting to preallocate a copied or renamed version of the
sidefile doesn't work. LE seems to try to dynamically allocate it and
preallocating causes dynamic allocation to fail, at which point LE
gives up and does a regular CEEDUMP and SYSPRINT.

The bypass I have for this is to use a distinctive name for the Side File,
such as coding a date/time stamp as part of the DSname, or a unique version
code. An alias can be created with IDCAMS once the side file has been 
promoted to a different SCM library. I've tried this and it seems to work
quite well, just give the copied version of the file an alias that matches
the DSName that was used for SYSDEBUG at compile time.

The only mention I could find of this DDname was a brief mention of
adding a DCT entry for IGZDBGIN to a CICS region:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea5030/8.2?ACTION=MATCHES&REQUEST=IGZDBGIN&TYPE=FUZZY&SHELF=CEE1BK32&DT=20000711153000&searchTopic=&searchText=TEXT&searchIndex=INDEX&rank=RANK&ScrollTOP=FIRSTHIT#FIRSTHIT
```

You really need to specify the platform with Gentran. For example, I know
Gentran/400 and Gentran/MVS, but the Unix and PC versions are different
animals altogether.

What kind of medical billing? The ANSI ASC X12N X096/X097/X098
HIPAA-mandated stuff? Or the non-HIPAA Medicare ANSI implementations? I know
those well enough. (I assume you are only interested in the ANSI ASC X12
stuff because you wouldn't use Gentran/anything to create/read  NSF 3
professional or NSF 6 facility claims).

As long as you are 'going ANSI:' If you are interested in processing inbound
remittances (ANSI 835) without reinventing the wheel, check out (our
product) The Provider Payment Partner(tm) System at
http://www.providerpaymentpartner.com

(I can't tell if you are a payer or a provider).

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D1BFF1D.C8698057@worldnet.att.net> <uhojletks9b82e@corp.supernews.com> <afhm0k$3d4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I have really enjoyed this thread, and sorry it got off the Logic vs
Mathematics :(  I started out as a student of Philospohy and ended up in the
Computer Sciences.  Logic was a way of proving (aurguing) to a point of
truth.  But then 'truth' is surely in the eyes of the beholder.  With
appologies to Mr. Spock - Logic is not always truth and visa versa.  Logic
is merely a way that leads to clarified thinking.  Mathematics to me has
always been just a tool to prove a hypothesis or observed phenomenom.  So in
that way...Math has proved what builders learned through trial and error,
but by exrapolating from historical events can create theroms that may
predict future events.  If those events prove true in practice then those
events help support the therom as 'true'...but not always.  They may be true
it the fact that the right set of circumstances have not been tried yet to
disprove it.  Much in the way exhaustive testing is not always cost
effective in large projects.  And why so much of production support staff
are always murmurring of why these defects where not caught in development
or system testing.

anyway -

For what is worth Dept:

I have found that 80/20 rule still holds 'true' in construction - 80 percent
of effort is spent on 20 of the problems instead of 60% analysis, 20% coding
and 20% testing.  This is often because Business managers desire tangible
results with the least amount of cost.  If they see developers thinking
instead of doing then the manager's feel that nothing is being done.
Unfortunately we have PM's that are so accommodating that they often
underestimate budgets in order to 'Look good'.  So, best practices show that
the often over-budget projects are more realistic to look at...but then
again - Many PM's are called but few are chosen.

Tom Wymer
QA Manager, INCONMA LLC

SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:0BEB133C20747251.08D61E3FBF340A00.F2BDF4356E29EC1B@lp.airnews.net...
> On Fri, 28 Jun 2002 12:51:49 -0400, "Donald Tees"

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> The biggest drawback in Roman engineering was the lack of decent
numbering
> >> theory, and lack of any arithmetic.  etc.
> >>
> >
> >PS ... not sure why we are arguing ... we could just ask Pete.  He was
there
> >wasn't he?
> >
You just keep a civil tongue in your head young Skippy...

Fortunately for you I have been too busy lately to follow this NG. And when
I do look in, what do I see? You and Mr. Tees showing the utmost disrespect
for my venerable experience...<G>

Well,  for the edification of you both, here are my recollections on Roman
Architecture....

1. The Forum.

Told them it would never work. They couldn't decide how many columns across
and down because matrices hadn't been invented yet. Then there was the
argument about whether the columns should be Ionic, Doric, or Corinthian...
As this affected the cost, Tightfistus (the Business Sponsor) decided Doric
would be best as these were simplest (and cheaper). Immediately, Marblus
(the head Stonemason) called a strike, arguing that his team of Prima Donnas
were being denied their Gods-Given right to indulge their artistic
sensibilities. They picketed the project with signs saying: "NIL BASTARDII
CARBORUNDUS"  ("Nobody grinds us down..."). Unfortunately, Tightfistus (who
was of unquestionable Noble birth) took this personally and got Hittus (the
Head of Security) to execute a few of them until the strike collapsed and
they reluctantly went back to work. However they refused to do any
calculations (and what they did do, they did in Greek...using the very
questionable theories of  Unidimensionyses, who had formulated a one
dimensional geometry where parallel lines not only met, but were actually
the SAME line...) this resulted in some extremely narrow Doric columns which
were incapable of supporting the roof , and had to be replaced before Caesar
inspected the new edifice.)

2. The Collosseum.

Told them it would never work. There was a very ambitious plan to have a
slave at each turnstile with a wax tablet and a stylus. For each revolution
of the turnstile, a spike would be pressed into the the slave's nether
regions, causing him to jump and thus make a mark on the tablet. The idea
was to stop some of the pocketing of revenue  by the Gatekeepers, which was
suspected to be going on. It was felt that if the number of people who
attended could be calculated, this could be multiplied by the unit price at
each turnstile, and the total revenue collected could be matched against
this figure. If the actual amount collected was less than what should have
been collected, certain Gatekeepers would be lion fodder at the next
performance... Tightfistus, (who had been promoted after the Forum debacle
and was now Programme Manager for the new Collosseum Project...) thought
this was a great idea.
The problem was to get the slaves marking their tablets to come up with a
total  of all the marks, before the next week's performance was due to
start. It was decided to have a large bolt of finest Imperial Purple silk
slung on a high pole where everyone could see it. This roll of silk would be
allowed to unroll under its own weight. Any slave who had not totalled the
marks on his tablet by the time the reel was completely unfurled would be
executed by Hittus (who really enjoyed the work).

This period, in which calculations were allowed to be made, thus came to be
known as "reel time".  Tightfistus reported back to the Senate that the new
reel time system would ensure that all the revenues from the Collosseum
could now be accounted for, and the savings would enable the system to pay
for itself, including the costs of stylii, tablets, slaves and silk.

Sadly the system failed because, after a few hundred pokes, the slaves no
longer responded and missed marks. Some slaves colluded with the Gatekeepers
and split the profit. Various schemes were devised to slow down the
unfurling of the silk from off the reel, but these were countered by
efficency experts from the far-off land of Candle, who always detected and
removed the various inhibitors which slowed the spinning of the reel. (The
cost of these had not been considered by Tightfistus, who called in Arturus
Anderseenus, the Legendary Cretan Auditor, who ensured that the missing
marks where written off as faulty wax on the tablet, and thereby instituted
the famous simile: "...to lie like a Cretan..."). It was not long after this
happened that Arturus Anderseenus retired, and handed the business over to
his brother, Nobody Seenus, who got away with everything.

History has shown that a reel time system NEVER pays for itself, so the
whole basis for it was spurious.

I tried to tell them....

Pete.







 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com

Raquel
> > Welch (or whoever is spending this decade filling in... or filling out
for

>
> Well she's 62, so if she still looks good in a thong more power to her.
(I've spent a
> lot of my life expressing my age as "the same as Raquel Welch").
>

I shared an elevator with her once at the Beverly Comstock Hotel where she
used to live in a penthouse apartment. (I was going to visit some friends
who also lived on the same floor.)

This was one of the very few times in my life when I was at a loss for
words...<G>

What do you say? "I'm a fan..." is kind of trite and inadequate. I ended up
telling her that I enjoyed her movies (only marginally better than "I'm a
fan..." <G>) and she expressed surprise that we even got them in New
Zealand... She is some years older than me, but I would be very happy to
indulge the "older woman" fantasy with her.

Not only a truly beautiful woman, but intelligent with it. I'll always
remember her quote that the "brain is the most important erogenous zone".

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T01:10:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d24f027_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <CC4B72B25C3F9867.A3F85A42452C6E76.860FDA2141705A1C@lp.airnews.net> <3D1CEAB2.D7F1E956@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

enoyed your anecdotes. Comments below...

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3D1CEAB2.D7F1E956@shaw.ca...
> And if programming was solely related to mathematics - count me out !. A
little
> tale - way back when I was an apprentice in the RAF, I was short listed as
a
> prospective candidate to go the RAF College at Cranwell, (equivalent to
West
> Point). My commanding officer said to me, "I understand your math is poor.
What
> is 2.5% of �1 ?". If he hadn't given me the answer, (which I have never
forgotten
> <G>), I'd still be sat staring at him across his desk, covered in cobwebs.
You go
> figure what 2.5% is - a �  then had 240 pence.
>
I've got sixpence
Jolly jolly sixpence
I've got sixpence
To last me all my life.
I've got tuppence to spend
And tuppence to lend
And tuppence to take home to my wife!
(Gor Blimey... )
repeat ad infinitum...

(Old Music Hall song).


> Very specifically COBOL is a language just like English or German and a
> communication tool.

Ah, sorry Jimmy. On this point we differ most strongly.

COBOL is NOT a Language like English or German, or Greek or Swahili, or any
other spoken Language.

Spoken languages embody concepts and abstractions, as well as specifics, so
that they can be communicated between Humans. The Communication is two way,
by means of the language.

COBOL is a language for INSTRUCTING a computer to do something. It embodies
no abstractions but only very precise specifics (which must be pre-defined
before they can be referenced.). I haven't seen any computers attempting to
tell me how they feel, or asking me what I thought of that last printout, or
giving me the Lotto numbers for Saturday, in COBOL, with no prompting from
me, and I have been around computers for a very long time...

The VEHICLE which COBOL uses to instruct the computer is a simulation of the
English Language, but in a form so highly stylised and contrived that it is
certainly not fair to call it "language" in the same sense that we apply
this term to English, or German. The best that can be said about COBOL is
that is "English-like" or that it "Looks like English..."; to go beyond this
is to devalue the nature of true Languages.

(Besides, neither English nor German show any signs of dying, or of having
come to the end of their useful lives... But then, neither of these
languages are under the protection of a Standards Authority...<G>)

Pete.







It may well be backed up by mathematical thinking at the
> earliest stages, (although if you dumb it down, ones and noughts are no
more
> complex than the sequencing for Morse code), but to requote Michael., 'It
is the
> artist not the paintbrush".
>
> Being no Einstein, I can still apply logic or reasoning to a problem - but
you
> wont want to recruit me to dream up some new Sort/Merge techniques - give
that
> one to the mathematical boffins !.
>
> PS: Did go for selection, but didn't make it to Cranwell. I guess they
figured if
> you didn't know what 2.5% was, that you wouldn't make a very good 'driver'
for an
> airplane/aeroplane <G>.
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>

<a id="t-856b3a3e13"></a>
## Thread: calling Java from cobol with Microfocus NetExpress : SQL Commit question — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### calling Java from cobol with Microfocus NetExpress : SQL Commit question

- **From:** arnold_lescar@yahoo.fr (Arnold FRA)
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T03:34:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2c51fab.0207050234.1f2516fe@posting.google.com>`

```
hello,
I'm quite new in the java world and perhaps my question will sound
stupid but...
I have a lot of code written in Cobol and I want to use also some java
code inside my existing cobol logic.
I have done some simple tests with NetExpress and Java : it's working
(I just find the documentation very poor on the subject).
Now, I want to do something more complex (and of course more realistic
in my context) :
My cobol program perform a Connect to a database (UDB, Oracle, SQL
Server...).
It may then insert/update data in the database.
The Java code is called : inside this code I want also to have a JDBC
connection to update/insert data (on the same instance).
When I'am back from my java, I do perform some other cobol code and at
the end of my program I can decide if all the updates need to be
committed or rollbacked.
My concern is : will the database updates generated inside the java
code be rollbacked if the rollback is performed from the cobol ?
I don't see how to give the database connection handle to the java
code; the java code will have to get his own JDBC database connection
: so when the java code 'ends', its updates will be committed or
rollbacked.
Does anybody have this kind of issue ?
I've heard of JTA (don't know anything on the suject yet) : is it a
possible solution ?
Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: calling Java from cobol with Microfocus NetExpress : SQL Commit question

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T21:02:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D260A43.CC1E970@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b2c51fab.0207050234.1f2516fe@posting.google.com>`

```


Arnold FRA wrote:

> hello,
> I'm quite new in the java world and perhaps my question will sound
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

Pure guess - but aren't the COBOL and Java CONNECTS distinct from one
another, even though you have your Java 'wrapped' inside COBOL ?

I know it's a question of using the best language for the job - but why
complicate it using Java ? The on-line help book and ESQL Assistant cover
all the bases for doing it in COBOL.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: calling Java from cobol with Microfocus NetExpress : SQL Commit question

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-06T01:23:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d2637fe_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<b2c51fab.0207050234.1f2516fe@posting.google.com>`

```
OK, I don't use NetExpress. (Maybe you are startng to understand why ?<G>)

I believe that once the connection is established with ODBC, that will be
held for all SQL requests from that thread, IRRESPECTIVE of whether they are
issued by JAVA, C++, or COBOL, or anything else....

You are quite right to use whatever is best for the job, (whether it is Java
or COBOL) and you shouldn't have to worry about establising a separate
connection for JDBC. I don't believe you do, but if I'm wrong, then NE is
sadly lacking...

The COMMIT issued by your COBOL should apply to all actions against the
database which were issued from this thread. (ROLLBACK should function the
same way...).

The point is that the actions against the database should not care whether
they were issued by COBOL or anything else...

Try it and see how you get on... JDBC should recognise the established
connection. If it doesn't, then there is a problem with the way NE issues
these function calls.

JTA is not applicable to this problem (in fact, it muddies the water...).

Do NOT issue a JDBC CONNECT. (Assuming your COBOL CONNECT has returned a
successful completion code...)

Treat the Java code as if it were encapsulated within the COBOL code. I
believe this will work.

Let us know how you get on.

Pete.

news:b2c51fab.0207050234.1f2516fe@posting.google.com...

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

"Casper H.S. Dik" <Casper.Dik@Sun.COM> wrote in message

figure

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

Many thanks for that, but it still doesn't quite hit the mark.

After a bit of fiddling, my compile/link now looks like this :

cob -x -Q,2-z -Q,2textoff -P \
    -G  \
    -o trnsn062cob.so  trnsn062.int \
    -lc -lm -lposix4  ...other libraries... \
    -lelf -lnsl -lsocket  -L/opt/ingres/lib -lingres \
    -Q-R/opt/ingres/lib

I had to add the "-Q" to the last entry, otherwise either cob or ld
(no idea which) hacked the first two characters off, giving me

ld: fatal: file R/opt/ingres/lib: cannot open file: No such file or
directory



*Anyway*, the above gets me what ought to be a shared object,
but when I try to use it, I get this :

ld.so.1: testcall.exe: fatal: relocation error: file ./trnsn062cob.so:
symbol _mFg2star: referenced symbol not found


which sounds like the run-time environment can't find the guts of the
COBOL ... errm ... stuff (apologies for the Technical Jargon).  I've
set LD_LIBRARY_PATH in both the compile and run environments
to include $COBDIR/coblib, as per what manual's I've got.
Another posting I found suggested matching "-L" and "-R" entries,
so I tried adding

    -Q-R$COBDIR/coblib

to the end - which made precisely zero difference.

Regards,
    Phill  W.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL: How to convert .int to .so

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T14:57:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.solaris
- **Message-ID:** `<ag4bp1$mro$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<ag1n1o$5r4$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk> <ag1or9$ae8$1@news1.xs4all.nl> <ag45li$qhr$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk>`

```
Hi,

Server Express has the -z option on the cob command to link to shared
object. Server Express is the only Micro Focus COBOL product that directly
supports this format.

Given that you mention $COBDIR/coblib rather the $COBDIR/lib leads me to
believe you are either using OCDS or an earlier product. Shared objects (so)
are not directly supported in these products.

Paul

"Phill W." <p.a.ward@open.ac.uk> wrote in message
news:ag45li$qhr$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk...
> "Casper H.S. Dik" <Casper.Dik@Sun.COM> wrote in message
> news:ag1or9$ae8$1@news1.xs4all.nl...
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "Phill W." <p.a.ward@open.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T15:30:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.solaris
- **Message-ID:** `<ag4afl$sgj$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk>`
- **References:** `<ag1n1o$5r4$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk> <ag1or9$ae8$1@news1.xs4all.nl> <ag45li$qhr$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk> <ag4bp1$mro$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```
Paul,

Yes, I believe I'm inflicted with Server Express' predecessor,
Object COBOL.  Given that O.C. can't provide shared objects in
one step, can you talk me through the two (or more?) steps are that
I /should/ be using?

OK, so I need to compile with "cob" (to produce a regular .o Object
file?) then link this into the shared object with "ld", but I suspect cob
is shoe-horning a load of extra stuff into the linking phase "on my
behalf".  Since it looks like I need to do the latter myself, so it would
be great to know what on Earth it was ...

TIA,
    Phill  W.

"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com> wrote in message
news:ag4bp1$mro$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com...

(so)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL: How to convert .int to .so

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T10:51:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.solaris
- **Message-ID:** `<agbqhe$jnu$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<ag1n1o$5r4$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk> <ag1or9$ae8$1@news1.xs4all.nl> <ag45li$qhr$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk> <ag4bp1$mro$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <ag4afl$sgj$1@yarrow.open.ac.uk>`

```
Beyond my knowledge I'm afraid. I only ever use the cob command to do this.

Are you sure you really need to use shared objects? Shared libraries can
give similar features and can be more efficient in some circumstances.

This quote from the 4.1 manual should tell you if your system supports this:

Note: The -B flag is supported only on UNIX operating systems that support
dynamic linking. If your operating system does support it (that is, the
library libcobol.so exists in $COBDIR/coblib), see your operating system
manual for more details on static and dynamic linking.

Look in your documentation about dynamic linking.

Hope this helps,
Paul

directly

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Correct Length of a string

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T20:14:57+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00obiug49jvo9jmeerhskor8i4t4hpflh6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ac0ej0$vg1$01$1@news.t-online.com> <HMiV8.283352$_j6.13961775@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Jul 2002 15:17:59 GMT, weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
wrote:


>>With the Inspect - Command I can count the Spaces but I need the space
>>between the words.

Good.  Then turn the string around (with FUNCTION REVERSE) and use
inspect tallying.... leading zeroes to count the trails at the end
(which now, of course, are at the beginning.

If you don't have intrinsic function, but reference modification, try
a loop like this


PERFROM VARYING THE-POS FROM LENGTH OF THE-VAR
                             BY -1
                             UNTIL THE-POSITION = 2
                             OR THE-VAR(THE-POS:1) NOT EQUAL SPACE
END-PERFORM

THE-POS is now the number of the last non-blank char

 
      
      
      
      
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,

      He is extremely smart, considering how stupid he is.
      
        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)

What about :
 01 WS-CAMPO-DA-CONTARE.
    05 WS-CARATTERE        PIC X OCCURS 30.
 01 WS-LEN                 PIC 99.



MOVE 30 TO WS-LEN.
MOVE SK-LUOGO TO WS-CAMPO-DA-CONTARE.

PERFORM UNTIL WS-CARATTERE(WS-LEN) NOT = SPACE

  SUBTRACT 1 FROM WS-LEN

## Thread: "No SQL Directives have been set" — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### "No SQL Directives have been set"

- **From:** samhunt90@hotmail.com (Sam)
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T11:24:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e20518e0.0207051024.6d44445d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I'm getting this message on a microfocus compile with embedded SQL.
The problem statement is 'EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC'.

Any idea how to get around this?

Thanks,
Sam90
```

#### ↳ Re: "No SQL Directives have been set"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-05T20:19:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D260033.B815C066@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<e20518e0.0207051024.6d44445d@posting.google.com>`

```


Sam wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any idea how to get around this?

You didn't actually say what message you are getting., but if you are
DECLARING, then you *must* 'DECLARE' something as follows :-

EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
01  SQLSTATE                    pic x(5).
EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.

or

    EXEC SQL DECLARE

    `Clients` TABLE
    ( "CliId"               VARCHAR(3)
     ,"FacId"               VARCHAR(2)
     ,"Shortname"           VARCHAR(30)
     ,"LifeYears"           CURRENCY
     ,"Percent1"            CURRENCY
     ,"Percent2"            CURRENCY
     ,"Range1"              SMALLINT
     ,"Range2"              SMALLINT
     etc......
    ) END-EXEC.

The latter example is taken from the copyfile generated by ESQL
Assistant.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

```

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<e20518e0.0207051024.6d44445d@posting.google.com>`

```
Sounds like you are using NetExpress. Go to the project menu and select
project properties. The SQL directives can be set there. How you fill this
in depends on whether or not you are using ODBC and what your database is.

Regards,
Paul

<a id="t-82fb6fe664"></a>
## Thread: Best Compiler for... — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Best Compiler for...

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-06T02:03:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Okay, I know I am opening up a small can of worms with this, but I really could use some advice. I find myself in the really weird
position of not being able to decide what compiler / platform is best for this job.

The beastly program will either have to be accessible from the Web, easily, reliably, and very efficiently (i.e. - cannot use
WebSphere for this, we are talking about a plain jane text mode application) *or* it is will have to be easily and quickly
distributable, preferably with no runtimes, on PC's and AIX machines.

I'm in quite a funk on this. My initial call is to just write a CICS application and let the users login to it over the internet.
The downside to this is the expense of doing it on the mainframe. For a while, I was looking at Flex-ES to do it, but the commercial
licensing is still running around the $100K mark - 5 times more than the budget for this project will allow.

Then I thought about using CICS under Windows NT, OS/2, or AIX. AIX looked great, but CICS has some issues - I'm not in Rag's class
and  I could not get the damn thing stable, even using DB/2 as the backend. On top of that, it was slow indeed... On a side trip
from that, I though - ah hah! I'll just call C programs and use curses to do the user interface from IBM Cobol directly under AIX.
This sounded great, except the doggon COMPILER would not successfully open files with more than one index on them. (i.e. Alternate
Index) I don't know why, and have NOT figured out why either.

So then I thought, ah hah! I have an old version of MicroFocus COBOL for AIX, I wonder if it would run... and yes it does. But it is
version 3.2, and that is pretty old I think. Also, I am not sure if I can legally move it from the very old and slow AIX machine I
have it on to a faster and more efficient one. It has a few problems with termlib/termcap too, but those are probably fixable.

Moving along, I remembered I have a developer's version of Cobol ReSource (a Wang VS emulated COBOL) on the AIX machine, and it is a
very fast compiler. Licensing for it is not terribly expensive, and I know the guy who supports it down in Australia. This looked
good, but has two small issues - since it want to pretend it is a Wang, it insists on using 32 function keys, which means I would
need to distribute a copy of PuTTY that Tom Brennan modified for me to use file based configuration files and allow really good key
remapping. This may be a wee bit of a hassle for the user, but it was not a major problem. However, the compiler is not up to snuff;
it is supposed to be a 100% compatible Wang VS COBOL-85 compatible product, but it doesn't have a lot of the extensions in it-
Intrinsic Functions, Address data types, etc. The people using it commercially usually don't care, since they are still running and
supporting COBOL 74 programs for the most part.

Having exhausted the my AIX inventory, I briefly glanced at the Tiny COBOL and KOBOL sets, but was not terribly impressed. So I
moved into the PC commercial stuff I have around here.

IBM COBOL is beautiful, as close as I can get to the mainframe. I can run a copy of CICS under OS/2, use IBM COBOL, and allow up to
20 simultaneous users to connect via TN3270, or just about any number of people to connect using a CICS client via TCP/IP.  This is
essentially a zero cost option for me, since I have OS/2 CICS and the IBM compilers. But I don't have a workstation setup with OS/2
to develop and run it on, and I am unwilling to give up one of the high speed workstations/servers to do so unless I absolutely have
to. It does give pretty good performance though, and it is very compatible with the mainframe. I thought about using VA Cobol and
the PC version of the CICS development environment under WIndowsXP, but the CICS development version cannot run under WIndowsXP.
Guess what fool upgraded all the workstations here to WindowsXP? Stopped crashing, but a few pieces of software simply will not run.
(*sigh*)

Netexpress and AcuCOBOL under Windows run nicely, but the redistribution is a bit of pain. Besides, it feels like going to a small
system deployment, and my customers won't like that. They won't really like anything on a PC I think, unless it is running under
Linux or some other form of UNIX. Same kinda applies to Fuji COBOL, which I like a whole lot.

My last point of endeavor was on a AS/400, which is quite doable, but adds a slight extra cost per user to deploy, and it means I
have to use RPG like screen definitions. Still it is doable.

So all this comes down to several options that are doable, but one of those, or something I simply have not thought of at all, is
going to be better than the others. Please feel free to post your opinions, even if your opinion is that I am nut. :) I am usually
not this wishy-washy, but this is a "showplace" application I need to write, which will hopefully lead to significantly more
business.

Thanks in advance guys. Please tear that thinking apart!

-Paul
```

newsgroup on the news.software.ibm.com server.  I recall alternate indexes
should work with COBOL Set for AIX.

Al...

Sorry- I am so close to this that I cannot always see the forest for the trees.
The application will initially need to support 250 simultaneous users, with a growth
projection to about 2600 simultaneous users. It is a transaction environment, and must be
available over the internet, preferably *without* requiring the user to install any software,
but that point is negotiable.

Ideal environment is something like a 3270 application, or a text mode application under UNIX.
Backend database, if I decide to implement it via database, will be DB/2, but I would really prefer
to use VSAM (or equivalent) files. This is a transaction oriented environment, not a heavy query environment.

I cannot give details on the application, but I could probably answer specific questions.
Does the above give you a better idea of why I am a bit perplexed over what platform to choose?
:)

Thanks
-Paul
