###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Compiler for...

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T16:18:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d29ee31_6@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul,
Well, one solution would be to write the application so that you could do
any of the solutions you have proposed.

Have you looked at the 1Spec product from Progeni Corporation?
www.progeni.com.
It's a 4GL that generates pure COBOL.  It's been around for 20 years
(continually updated and improved) but it's not as well known as it should
be.  From a single COBOL like program (which they call a 'spec') you can
generate pure COBOL for any of the compilers and platforms you mentioned in
your message, and many more - including web interfaces, VB interfaces and
'green screen' interaces.  You can even mix and match database technolgoies.

It's an ideal platform for someone developing a product in COBOL if you wish
to be able to sell that product on a wide range of hardware/OS platforms.
The huge banking software company Intreive, Inc. developed all their Savings
and Loan software in 1Spec and supplies it on everything from large Unisys
systems to smaller Unix boxes.  They write their software once, then
generate it for whatever platform, screen type and database the customer
requires.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Compiler for...

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T21:05:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D29FE8F.7DE2D886@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```


Paul Raulerson wrote:
> 
> Ideal environment is something like a 3270 application, or a text mode application under UNIX.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> :)

a web based application has the advantage of keeping all the web code on
the  web server and all the database code on the database server.

I use ColdFusion for the web server and DB2 for the database (os 390) -
other people in our area use NEON; Power Builder and assorted other web
servers to talk to the code and tables.

if you really plan to have thousands of simultaneous users, you will
require some robust server farms and database protections.  Way out of
my area of expertise.  Have you contacted any UNIX support groups?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Compiler for...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T02:21:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aNrW8.62414$p85.1942260@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com> <3D29FE8F.7DE2D886@boeing.com>`

```
Yep, but a host based (i.e. green screen application) has all the same advantages as well, and can be made to operate very quickly.
This essentially has to be a keyboard application, where the users learn the keystrokes quickly and don't have to constantly think
about them.

The UNIX side is the easy part. Deciding on the correct software fit for this is tough, and actually will determine the hardware
more than anything else, I think.
-Paul
```

```
Paul:

You are absolutely correct that a green screen application can operate
at a very high speed.

I have watched this thread and have a question for you......does the
application HAVE to be a web based application or is the requirement
actually that the application MUST be an Internet based application?

There is a huge difference.

<WARNING:  Shameless commercial promotion>

You may wish to consider our Thin Client technology for your
application.  For one, it eliminates the need for a web server and web
browser.  While I realize that this may be considered "blasphemy" in
the world of the big boys such as Microsoft, there are plenty of
advantages to this approach.

I think that the decision for a thin client Internet application can
be made on the basis of who the intended end user group happens to be.

This is NOT a Citrix or Terminal Server based application.  Those
solutions are generic to all Windows applications.  Our Thin Client
technology is specific to COBOL and only sends a very small parameter
block across TCP/IP.  This allows you to continue to operate a very
high speed application across a TCP/IP network.

In addition, we provide utilities for automatic client component
installation and version control updates; data compression; 128 bit
data encryption; file transfer from client to server and vice-versa
and the ability to launch remote executables.

Give me a call at 610-588-9400, ext. 115 or send an e-mail message to
rtwolfe AT flexus DOT com and I'll be happy to explain the software in
more detail.

<END of Shameless Commercial Promotion>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Compiler for...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T16:44:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W5A09.135624$88.2329642@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com> <3D29FE8F.7DE2D886@boeing.com> <aNrW8.62414$p85.1942260@twister.austin.rr.com> <3d2adfed.1546804@news.epix.net>`

```
Well, I want to say THANK YOU to everyone here for all the help that has came my way on makin the difficult descision for this one.
The info below is purely my personal opinion, and should not be taken as review for any of the products mentioned. :)

I finally gave up, loaded OS/2 on a Gigahertz computer with 80 gigs of mirrored drive space, and decided to use a slightly older
version of OS/2 CICS (one that is almost 100% compatible with CICS ESA 4.1,. It is labeled as Transaction Server V4.1 for OS/2.).
This provides a nice development environment very close to the Mainframe world without the excessive Mainframe cost. It also allows
me to easily migrate this to a mainframe at a later time should the (hoped for!) need arise.  This uses a backlevel IBM COBOL
compiler (Version 2.2 Enterprise) and runs fine.

I had some trouble getting OS/2 Warp Server for E-Business to deal with an 80 gig drive space, but it eventually gave in.  The end
result for uses is a simple TN3270 environment and/or a CICS Client environment that works at a nice peppy speed over the internet.
It also is not difficult at all to interface to a Web Server with setup, and I have the advantage of having done so before. :)

The top contenders, in order, were:

Fujitsu COBOL for Linux
IBM COBOL Set for AIX
Liant COBOL for Linux
MicroFocus COBOL under AIX
AS/400 COBOL
COBOL Resource under AIX.

Fujitsu appears to have a beautiful product, no runtimes, etc. The cost per developer is $1500, which is a good bargain. I had a
little difficulty getting to someone who understood what I meant by a 'Green Screen' application, though I eventually did. The
reason I choose not to go this path was just some nagging uncertainty in using so new a compiler, and a little bit of concern that
the compiler may not be able to handle terminal screens as wells as I need. I also have a generic worry that a UNIX TERMIO based
solution would give me 'jerky' screens under heavy processor or network load.

IBM COBOL Set for AIX remains a wonderful choice, but has no screen handling capability. I did check into just writing the screens
with curses or ncurses, and that works, but it means the project would be half in C and half in COBOL. Alternatives presented were
SCREENIO and SPT (which also has a thin client technology). The SCREENIO was okay, and Bob Wolfe says that SPT can be made to easily
run under AIX, but (I am ashamed to admit) I was just not up to dealing with separate products again for display and program logic.
More accurately, I was not ready to deal with unfamiliar new products. And again, there was that concern that UNIX TERMIO solutions
are not going to work all that smoothly, as well as be very dependent upon the users terminal emulation program.

Liant (i.e. R/M)  COBOL sounds really nice, but they are concetrating right now on an XML style solutuion. I personally think that
this is a VERY hot technology for them, and will yeidl lots of benefits. However, I didn't want to be on the bleeding edge right
now! <grin> Also, the run time fees, depspite all the very good reasons to have them, could easily add up to a very signifigant sum
of money, on top of the normal maintenance costs. The Liant compiler itself is $1500, a very reasonable price.

MicroFocus COBOL under AIX really looks good too, but upgrading the license would bring in the need for license runtimes, and that
cost is greater than Liant. To top it off, the compiler/development software is also much more expensive, and so is the maintenance.
(*sigh*) It ia a *good* compiler though!

AS/400 COBOL; this is a tempting solution, especially since IBM dropped the cost of a nicely configured Model 270 this week in half.
The reason this choice was not choosen is really more of a future looking choice; While an AS/400 can handle a mainframe load, it
does not provide nearly the advantages that a real mainframe does for someone with my experience base - as in CICS and VSAM. Also, I
just don't like the way you have to handle screens on a 400- DDS editors or no. :)

Finally, there is COBOL ReSource under AIX, which has a COBOL-74 compilant compiler with some COBOL-85 extensions. The runtime fees
are reasonable, and the compiler is pretty solid. The compiler also will have binary VS emulation capabilty soon, and that would be
a nice plus. (My day job involves a lot of work with a Wang VS system, which is sort of like an older S/370 running VM, but iwth
everyone sharing the same copy of CMS. ;)  This was not choosen because I would have to distribute a specially configured temrinal
package with it, and I felt that it was not a good idea to write a new application in what amounts to COBOL-74.

Thanks again for all the help guys!  Bob/Thane - I would be glad to compile and test the SPII stuff for you under AIX at any time.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL ReSource

- **From:** Thomas Junker <nO_sPaM_tjunker_No_SpAm@tjunker.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T07:47:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2A94C9.905F1A8C@tjunker.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul Raulerson wrote:

> Moving along, I remembered I have a developer's version of Cobol ReSource 
> (a Wang VS emulated COBOL) on the AIX machine...

What are you talking about, Paul?  Emulation is a very loaded word.  You
know perfectly well that COBOL ReSource compiles to native machine code.
If you mean that it's a Wang variant of COBOL, I'll agree.  It's a good
variant, too.  Wang started doing interactive I/O in COBOL 20-25 years
ago 
when AcuCOBOL didn't exist and MicroFocus was a PC-based toy.

> and it is a very fast compiler.

We like to think so, but I don't have much personal comparitive
experience.

> This looked good, but has two small issues - since it want to pretend it is 
> a Wang, it insists on using 32 function keys, which means I would need to 
> distribute a copy of PuTTY that Tom Brennan modified for...really good
> key remapping.  This may be a wee bit of a hassle for the user, but it was 
> not a major problem. 

Only the development tools and utilities use pfkeys above 16. 
Applications 
can easily be written to confine themselves to pf1-12 and Shift pf1-12.

In any case I consider "really good key remapping" a nonnegotiable
prerequisite
for using telnet for most real world applications.  Our large clients
have
generally used WRQ Reflections (top-notch) or Hummingbird (so-so). 
Smaller ones
have used ZOC (shareware) with good results.

> However, the compiler is not up to snuff;
> it is supposed to be a 100% compatible Wang VS COBOL-85 compatible product, 
> but it doesn't have a lot of the extensions in it- 
> Intrinsic Functions, Address data types, etc. 

What you really mean is that *you* haven't gotten your hands on the
fully 
compliant version.  It has passed all the QA tests but there hasn't been
a lot of pressure to field test it and release it.  You have also heard
the 
background to it:  that when Wang canceled the project and laid off the
team, 
the bells and whistles mainframe version of the compiler was right in
the 
middle of being ported to ReSource.  It has been there all along, just
in 
need of the testing, analysis and rehab to make it releasable, which
have
since been done.  Releases are driven by the realities of hundreds of 
production sites that must install upgrades and must be supported, not
by
what you as an individual might like to see released.

> The people using it commercially usually don't care, since they are still 
> running and supporting COBOL 74 programs for the most part.

By and large, yes, they mostly brought their apps from COBOL 74 on the
VS.
Along the way they generally make increasing use of scope structures and 
substrings, which have always been supported, but they have little need
of 
the mostly math FUNCTIONs.  And, having mostly straightforward DP apps,
they 
have little need of the various pointer-related bells and whistles that
are
of interest to thee and me.

Thomas Junker
www.cobolresource.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL ReSource

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T01:10:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZPLW8.60404$eF5.2067317@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com> <3D2A94C9.905F1A8C@tjunker.com>`

```
Hi Tom - I didn't realize you read this newgroup. It's been one of my favorite places for a long time.


I wasn't cutting down ReSource, you already know I like it, and I even like the standard licensing costs,
but darn if I can sell it to these guys without "the bells and whistles." If it had them in it, it would be pretty much of a perfect
choice I think. (And you know I have been looking for a nice fit for ReSource to
do new applications in for a long time.) In this case, no VSBCL required of course.   A really nice point to ReSource is that I can
write C programs where necessary that are pretty well seamless, even with argument passing.:)

Regardless of the semantics, from a user's point of view, it emulates a Wang interface. Things like HELP, CANCEL, and the way INSERT
works are not intuitive to PC or Mainframe users. And if God meant for keyboards to have an ERASE key, he would not have put the END
key where it belongs!! <grin>

I used the little 5300 to write a couple COBOL test programs and demo them, and the customer rather liked it. I could use VS based
COBOL for this easily - but the licensing costs are probably a bit excessive. ReSource did not compile successfully, not being able
to use the Intrinsic functions.

Talk to Casey and see if you can get a full capability version up here, and I'll give it a go.

You already know my feelings about high end emulation - PCOMM works just beautifully, but
PuTTY is providing emulation that is really nice, and it has zero cost to it.

Anyways, at this point, I am not positive what solution I need to use, and a great deal of the code I want to re-use is in CICS
COBOL. In almost any other COBOL, that means a lot of writing OPEN/CLOSE statements, and coding FD's. To avoid that, I need to use
DB/2 or move it to a 400.

I do very much like Thane's and Bob's idea of separating the screen displays; that is again pretty natural to me since to me
interactive = CICS.  I have some studying to do.
-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL ReSource

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Thomas Junker <nO_sPaM_tjunker_No_SpAm@tjunker.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T04:31:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2BB88A.1C7127F6@tjunker.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com> <7vgeiusuhc8436tn5q5uaiig58qoq59d98@4ax.com> <tDKV8.49079$p85.1532840@twister.austin.rr.com> <3D2A94C9.905F1A8C@tjunker.com> <ZPLW8.60404$eF5.2067317@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul Raulerson wrote:

> Hi Tom - I didn't realize you read this newgroup. It's been one of my favorite 
> places for a long time.

I visit once in a great while, and there some folks out there who
occasionally send me heads-up about postings here.

> Regardless of the semantics, from a user's point of view, it emulates a Wang 
> interface. 

As I said, "emulation" is a heavily loaded word.  Many things can be
described as emulations, but in the class of things that provide
environment, facilities, behavior, user interface or other things on one
platform that look like another platform, "emulation" is a very bad
word.  It means, in the historic context, emulation of one machine
instruction set in another machine, like IBM 1400 emulation that used to
run in IBM 350 systems.  It is synonymous with "slow."  In fact, I have
heard that the new sales people at Unicom (a hit-and-run conversion
house) badmouth COBOL ReSource by characterizing it as VS emulation,
which it most certainly is not in this context.  People who believe them
then don't bother to even look at COBOL ReSource, and the one-shot
conversion they buy into separates them forever from Wang COBOL and the
VS/ReSource environment that were the reasons they were using a Wang VS
in the first place.

Thomas Junker
www.cobolresource.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Best Compiler for...

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T23:00:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0207082200.1adcc33a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
I have a couple of different suggestions for you.

Seeing the austin.rr.com in the header trail I know you are in the US.
 You can get Fujitsu COBOL and avoid all runtime fees.  Additionally
there are several deployment options.  Let me highlight just a few
ending with my favorite.

If you have CICS code to leverage - take a look at Fujitsu's WinKicks.
 (http://www.adtools.com).  If you are looking for the least expensive
platform, but want to be scalable in the future - take a look at
PerCOBOL from LegacyJ.  COBOL compiles to Java.

Back to An NT environment - how about taking a gander at making your
transactions "Services" and using Fujitsu's .NET COBOL compiler.

Finally - there's a new Linux compiler also from Fujitsu.  What about
using the tools you have?  Well, there's a way to do that as well -
using the AIX box and the COBOL of your choice.

Available via the internet?  Does it have to be WEB based (that's the
only "Clientless" software -- but even that's a lie, because a browser
must be there).  My personal choice here would be to use any darn
server side you want and then front end it with Flexus SP2. 
(http://www.flexus.com) - works with basically all the compilers. 
Initialy a UI tool, the products have evolved to include a Web
interface, a native desktop GUI and a Thin client (Mature product -
been around for years) that can be hosted in the browser window or sit
on the desktop like any other application - and have a FAST GUI
avialable via the internet -- but not in the feature poor (if you ask
me) UI of a web broswer.

Host on VMS, UNIX, AIX, OS390, NT, Linux - etc etc.. and front end
with any one of the Flexus tools to give you a good user interface.

Depending on your market there might not be a UI at all - make it a
Web service available via .NET (SOAP) and let others sell it for you. 
I have no Idea what the end goal is, but I want to applaud you for
choosing first and foremost to stick with COBOL as your business
programming language.  As you can see from this message - where I
didn't even mention EVERYTHING that's available - COBOL has what it
takes.  There are a ton of options here.  Email me privately leaving
off the H at the end of thaneh in my email address and I'll be happy
to discuss any of these options further with you.
  


"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message news:<AdsV8.45744$p85.1366149@twister.austin.rr.com>...

> position of not being able to decide what compiler / platform is best for this job.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul
```

## Thread: Record lengths in Unix — 7 msgs

### Record lengths in Unix

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@NOSPAMhotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-06T22:15:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uifcj55ubiqjbf@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I am doing a migration from Vax to Tru64 Unix using ksh. We are using Compaq
Cobol. The problem I am having is this:

When using a Write After Advancing I get a CRLF which is making my records 2
bytes longer than the record is defined. I can handle this by defining the
output as pic x(100) and the input as pic x(102).

When I use a Write without an advancing, I can use the same input record
length as my output. The problem with this is that instead of 100, 100 byte
records I have 1, 10000 byte record. This is fine for passing data into
another program but it does my sorts no good. What's the point of sorting
only 1 record.

Has anyone run across this problem? How did you fix it? Is there a way
around this?

Thanks,
Dan
```

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-07-07T06:10:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<uifcj55ubiqjbf@corp.supernews.com>`

```

wrote:

> I am doing a migration from Vax to Tru64 Unix using ksh. We are using Compaq
> Cobol. The problem I am having is this:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Try defining the file to the sort as having "fixed length 100 byte 
records".

What kind of sort are you using? Most sort programs I know of will 
handle fixed record length sequential files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record lengths in Unix

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-07T14:24:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DaYV8.56781$q53.1583876@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<uifcj55ubiqjbf@corp.supernews.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-s2QW1huNvgxL@thishost> <uifrcs1qhgu2f4@corp.supernews.com>`

```
 I'm assuming you are creating a report here or something, in which case, have you tried using a LINE SEQUENTIAL file for the
output? I think that Compaq (DEC) COBOL has that in it, and it essentially writes a variable length record terminated by a EOL
character. (Under UNIX that will be newline.)

Out of curiosity, if you are not writing a report from this, (and why you would be sorting a report I don't
know...) why are you using the "after advancing" clause? Just read the records from one file and write them to another after doing
whatever rearrangement, editing, merging, or whatever you need to do each record. If you need the records to be line delaminated for
sort, instead of truly sequential (or consecutive, whatever it is called on your platform) you should be able to use the LINE
SEQUENTIAL file type.

Sort does like to handle files with a clearly evident EOL character, so I see your issue there. the -z option pretty much just keeps
SORT from terminating if an input line is LONGER than expected, but that isn't exactly what you want.  I admit to being somewhat
curious about this, can you provide a few more details?

Thanks
Paul


"Dan" <deacondan25@NOSPAMhotmail.com> wrote in message news:uifrcs1qhgu2f4@corp.supernews.com...
>
> <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> SORT from terminating if an input line is LONGER than expected, but that
isn't exactly what you want.  I admit to being somewhat
> curious about this, can you provide a few more details?
>
> Thanks
> Paul

Thank you, thank you thank you. LINE SEQUENTIAL  is exactly what I was
looking for. I wish I had know about this option sooner, it would have saved
me hours of grief.
For files from the lan, using /usr/bin/mtools/dos2unix takes care of the
additional byte there.

We have tried all sorts of combos from stripping the extra byte(s)  to
expanding the incoming record size.

To answer your question about "after advancing", I guess it's years of
coding habits(bad?) on MVS and VMS systems. As long as the file is defined
as FB, the system will handle the CRs and LFs without difficulty. So I have
pretty much used "write" and "write after advancing" interchangably. This
move to unix is where we've had all of the problems. Allowing the system to
default the file to sequential is part of the problem. I haven't really used
the "organization is" except when using vsam files.

Here's how to handle the sort when using the "write" statement for the
single record:
          fold -w 142 filein.dat | sort  -o filout.dat -k 1.137,1.142r
"fold" defines the record length and pipes it to sort. Works pretty good.

Another question. Is it possible using unix sort to reformat a record? ie
take a 100 byte record, get the middle 50 bytes and apend 20 additional
characters to the end, sort it, eliminate duplicates and get a 70 byte fixed
record. What I would give for Syncsort.

Many thanks,
Dan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record lengths in Unix

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T03:37:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IN7W8.59673$p85.1780235@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<uifcj55ubiqjbf@corp.supernews.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-s2QW1huNvgxL@thishost> <uifrcs1qhgu2f4@corp.supernews.com> <DaYV8.56781$q53.1583876@twister.austin.rr.com> <uihrqdjgs0bmb7@corp.supernews.com>`

```
You are welcome. I tend to cheat going from MVS to UNIX, I just FTP the blasted files down to the UNIX machine in ASCII mode. That takes plain old sequential files and turns them into line sequential files. At least it works on AIX. ;) 

Believe it or not, there are UNIX tools that do just about everything that SyncSort will do, but you have to put them together. What you essentially want to do is run a filter against the input to strip out the middle 50 bytes, add in the 20, and write out a 70 byte record which you pipe through sort with the -u option. (-u will eliminate duplicate keys, so I am assuming there that keys will not be identical unless the entire record is identical, that may be a bad assumption. If it is, there are other simple ways to do it of course.) 

For the filter, you can use a C program, an Awk or SED script, or even a COBOL program. :) 
In fact, here is a small COBOL program that might work for you on it. (C is even easier, but unless you are familiar with C, the syntax is somewhat arcane.) Several utilities besides AWK and SED also exist for this - you might look at it as a good excuse to learn PERL.  Using the example below, you would run something like 
this: 

cobf < input-line-sequential-file-ending-with-a-line-of-spaces | sort -u 

Hope this helps a bit, if you need more intensive help, e-mail me directly and I'll see what I can do to help you out. I don't have your COBOL compiler available to me here, but I can certainly help you out with the UNIX, C and so forth a bit. 
 
-Paul

      **************
       identification division.
       program-id. 'cobf'.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  ws-input-line                             pic x(100).
       01  ws-output-line                            pic x(70).

       procedure division.
           accept ws-input-line
           perform until ws-input-line = SPACES
             move ws-input-line(25:50) to ws-output-line
             move "12345678901234567890" to ws-output-line(51:20)
             display ws-output-line
             accept ws-input-line
             end-perform
           goback.
 

"Dan" <deacondan25@NOSPAMhotmail.com> wrote in message news:uihrqdjgs0bmb7@corp.supernews.com...
> 
> "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record lengths in Unix

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@NOSPAMhotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-08T20:09:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uikdtfc9g34qf9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<uifcj55ubiqjbf@corp.supernews.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-s2QW1huNvgxL@thishost> <uifrcs1qhgu2f4@corp.supernews.com> <DaYV8.56781$q53.1583876@twister.austin.rr.com> <uihrqdjgs0bmb7@corp.supernews.com> <IN7W8.59673$p85.1780235@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
I haven't had any problems with FTP'd files for testing. However, with the Vax going away...

Yes, in this case, the dups will be on the whole record. Other cases are not so simple, the example is a good start.

There's still alot to learn with Unix. It may take awhile. Like your example below, I still have some difficulty remembering how to use the pipes properly. Your Cobol example is even simpler than what I had envisioned. As for Awk and sed, I still need to learn them. C and Perl may have to wait a while. Since everyone is new to unix, I probably try to stick with Unix utilities, cobol or sas. It will make it easier to turn the system over.
Thanks --Dan
  "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message news:IN7W8.59673$p85.1780235@twister.austin.rr.com...
  You are welcome. I tend to cheat going from MVS to UNIX, I just FTP the blasted files down to the UNIX machine in ASCII mode. That takes plain old sequential files and turns them into line sequential files. At least it works on AIX. ;) 

  Believe it or not, there are UNIX tools that do just about everything that SyncSort will do, but you have to put them together. What you essentially want to do is run a filter against the input to strip out the middle 50 bytes, add in the 20, and write out a 70 byte record which you pipe through sort with the -u option. (-u will eliminate duplicate keys, so I am assuming there that keys will not be identical unless the entire record is identical, that may be a bad assumption. If it is, there are other simple ways to do it of course.) 

  For the filter, you can use a C program, an Awk or SED script, or even a COBOL program. :) 
  In fact, here is a small COBOL program that might work for you on it. (C is even easier, but unless you are familiar with C, the syntax is somewhat arcane.) Several utilities besides AWK and SED also exist for this - you might look at it as a good excuse to learn PERL.  Using the example below, you would run something like 
  this: 

  cobf < input-line-sequential-file-ending-with-a-line-of-spaces | sort -u 

  Hope this helps a bit, if you need more intensive help, e-mail me directly and I'll see what I can do to help you out. I don't have your COBOL compiler available to me here, but I can certainly help you out with the UNIX, C and so forth a bit. 

  -Paul

        **************
         identification division.
         program-id. 'cobf'.
         data division.
         working-storage section.
         01  ws-input-line                             pic x(100).
         01  ws-output-line                            pic x(70).

         procedure division.
             accept ws-input-line
             perform until ws-input-line = SPACES
               move ws-input-line(25:50) to ws-output-line
               move "12345678901234567890" to ws-output-line(51:20)
               display ws-output-line
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible? — 14 msgs

### Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible?

- **From:** sjolund@iobox.com (Xedra Roland)
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T00:42:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d43e3ef.0207082342.21c996c9@posting.google.com>`

```
Our program uses external variables: for example a external table of
fields. The fields are only 30 characters long, and we'd need longer
ones.

I'm not that experienced programmer, so I need your help. Is this
possible somehow:

1) Original table can't be touched (if it's altered, all programs and
sub-programs have to be compiled... not fun)
2) The original fields are referenced as 1-200 in the description
files, and the new ones should be 201-220.
3) The new fields as well as the old ones should be accessed like
this:
some-variable (index) points to original-table (index), when index <
200
some-variable (index) points to second-table (index - 200), when index
> 200

Of course I could just modify every single line with "original-table
(some-index)", but it would mean changing something like 350 lines
(and every line has to be replaced by 5 lines). Not fun either.

Pointers looked wery promising, but... is it possible to access the
contents of a memory-address without first changing the address of an
existing linkage item to the pointers value?

Using AcuCobol.

- Xedra
```

```
as one?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible?

- **From:** sjolund@iobox.com (Xedra Roland)
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T03:27:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d43e3ef.0207100227.73335ee8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0207082342.21c996c9@posting.google.com> <20020709195249.12127.00004689@mb-ft.aol.com>`

```
> As an example, how does the "external table" become available to the pgm?
> Where is the table? Is it an external file that's read into a pgm table
> structure? Is it contained in a file "record"?, etc.

 External variables are in a file (called EXTERNAL.CBL, suprise
suprise), and every program has it in it's working-storage section
(copy "copy/EXTERNAL.CBL").

Well, it's hard to field some useful lines to copy...  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T15:21:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agi57q$edc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0207082342.21c996c9@posting.google.com> <20020709195249.12127.00004689@mb-ft.aol.com> <4d43e3ef.0207100227.73335ee8@posting.google.com>`

```
One thing that it is important to remember is that there is NO GUARANTEE
that two 01-level items with the EXTERNAL attribute will actually be "next
to each other" in physical storage.  In IBM mainframes, this is almost
guaranteed NOT to work.  With some other environments, this will work
SOMETIMES.

If your program logic "requires" that you have a large table with the 1st
part "unchangeable" by any application - and the 2nd part changeable in
specific programs, then you might try having an EXTERNAL table with the
"constant" part and a 2nd table defined large enough to hold both the
constant and changeable parts.  Then as the 1st part of your application
code, copy the constant part to the beginning of the larger table.

As Richard has indicated in another post, you should make certain that each
element of the table is the SAME SIZE.  Otherwise, you have a totally
different programming task.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T08:04:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2D2E18.34C2D6EA@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0207082342.21c996c9@posting.google.com> <20020709195249.12127.00004689@mb-ft.aol.com> <4d43e3ef.0207100227.73335ee8@posting.google.com>`

```
Xedra Roland wrote:
> 
> Well, it's hard to field some useful lines to copy...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>         10  WEXT-FIELD-IN-TABLE-2         pic x(200).

I don't think that you mentioned before that the new entries were of
different size.  This makes it different too.  If the fields were all
the same size then just make the occurs 220 and recompile all programs. 
Different sizes means you can't avoid recoding.

If you made a keying error and field-in-table-2 is actually the same
X(30) then what would be wrong in just recompiling everything with an
OCCURS 220 ?

> 2) And this is what I have to do:
> set W-X to DISP-FIELD-NUMBER (FIELD-NR). * W-X can be over 200
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-if.

This implies that the use of the fields hasn't changed - the MOVE TO is
only going to be 30 characters.

Just change the OCCURS 200 to 220 and recompile all.  The EXTERNAL block
only needs to be the same in every program, not the same as it is now.

>  Oh well, I talked to the "senior programmer", he said that I'll just
> have to change the files manually like in 2)... and that's 125 files.
> Anyway I would like to know if there is a solution :)

Write the code to move from the table in a copy book EXTERNALPD.CBL and
change each program to PERFORM Get-EXTERNAL which does whatever is
necessary.

or: write a callable subroutine which loads and holds the field data and
can be called to return the field.  eg:

CALL "extfield" USING "G" W-X Wext-Field

where "G" is get and "S" is store and "L" is load or similar.

The issue here is not what you need to do _this_ time but what would be
needed _next_ time the size changes.  

Two tables and hard coding 20 and 200 into 125 programs means that this
needs to be redone to all those the next time.  

One table of 220 plus putting the limit value in that copy book, or
doing the access in a copybook too, means that the 125 programs only
will need recompiling.

Putting the whole thing in a separate subprogram means that only that
need change in the future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sjolund@iobox.com (Xedra Roland)
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T22:42:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d43e3ef.0207102142.132a99bd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0207082342.21c996c9@posting.google.com> <20020709195249.12127.00004689@mb-ft.aol.com> <4d43e3ef.0207100227.73335ee8@posting.google.com> <3D2D2E18.34C2D6EA@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> I don't think that you mentioned before that the new entries were of
> different size.  This makes it different too.  If the fields were all
> the same size then just make the occurs 220 and recompile all programs. 
> Different sizes means you can't avoid recoding.

 Oh sorry. They are of different size. And making the original table
larger (from 30 to 200 chars per field) would just waste some memory.
 
> If you made a keying error and field-in-table-2 is actually the same
> X(30) then what would be wrong in just recompiling everything with an
> OCCURS 220 ?

 Well, it's not, so... And recompiling everything means recompiling
2350 files.

> or: write a callable subroutine which loads and holds the field data and
> can be called to return the field.  eg:
> CALL "extfield" USING "G" W-X Wext-Field

 Well, this sounds better. At least no one wouldn't have to change the
programs again if another table is needed. I'll have to think about
it.

 
 - Xedra
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accessing multiple tables like they were one, possible?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T04:14:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020712001435.02903.00000091@mb-ci.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<4d43e3ef.0207102142.132a99bd@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Xedra,

I’m still not sure I "get it". Are you saying you currently have a table
containing 200 30 byte entries and you  want  to extend the table by adding 20
200 byte entries (highly unusual for a table)? But anyway, assuming you have
both segments populated before your pgm is  entered, you can treat them as
separate entities, as I think you were attempting to show in your previous
post.

To access the new extension entries, you can test the index. When it exceeds
200, subtract 200 from it and 
use that value to set the new index. Then reference the extension table using
this index. 

Jack
```

```
> Micro Focus became Merant which went back to Micro Focus.

Intersolv purchased MicroFocus Inc. and renamed the combination as
Merant in Feb 1999.  In November last year Merant sold the MicroFocus
name and products to a new company.  Merant also bought and sold other
software products, such as NetObjects and continues to operate as
consultants and with PVCS and other software.
```

```
Hi Phil,

Go to www.microfocus.com and look at the contact details for your area.

Micro Focus do have a COBOL for Linux but it's not Server Express, it's the
previous generation procuct called Object COBOL Developer Suite. There will
be a Server Express for the OS/390 Linux later in the year/early next year.

Which version of Linux are you using?

One small correction to the history: Micro Focus bought Intersolv. Then
combined entity renamed the company to MERANT. MERANT later spun off Micro
Focus.

Paul
```

<a id="t-f0ac3233b2"></a>
## Thread: Accessing Different DB2 instances thru One COBOL Program on Mainframe — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Accessing Different DB2 instances thru One COBOL Program on Mainframe

- **From:** pandian_cm@yahoo.com (C M Pandian)
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T15:01:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81886f2d.0207091401.6fbc404@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
Is it possible to access two different DB2 instances(Subsystem) thru
One COBOL  Program on the Mainframe. The need is to move the data from
one instance to the other after some filtering and transformations.
Regards,
Pandian.
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing Different DB2 instances thru One COBOL Program on Mainframe

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T21:19:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<116niugat6cgdlh08pd5toki71o4rrfb96@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<81886f2d.0207091401.6fbc404@posting.google.com>`

```
On 9 Jul 2002 15:01:32 -0700 pandian_cm@yahoo.com (C M Pandian) wrote:

:>Is it possible to access two different DB2 instances(Subsystem) thru
:>One COBOL  Program on the Mainframe. The need is to move the data from
:>one instance to the other after some filtering and transformations.

RRSAF & two stage commit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing Different DB2 instances thru One COBOL Program on Mainframe

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T04:58:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y9PW8.687$633.41559142@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<81886f2d.0207091401.6fbc404@posting.google.com>`

```
I don't think so.  A COBOL program that has SQL in it is bound to either a
package or a plan.  That package or plan is associated with just one DB2
subsystem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing Different DB2 instances thru One COBOL Program on Mainframe

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T22:36:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2CB70B.61D69F28@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<81886f2d.0207091401.6fbc404@posting.google.com>`

```


C M Pandian wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pandian.

yes, you can; but you will need to specify what my DBA calls the three
part table name that is in the the other database subsystem.

I am assuming here that they are on the same physical devise and only
logically separated (such as read production tables and insert records
onto test tables).

	Susan A
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing Different DB2 instances thru One COBOL Program on Mainframe

- **From:** "Philip Van Daele" <philip.van.daele@pandora.be>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T07:19:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZjaX8.1989$vw2.390097002@hebe.telenet-ops.be>`
- **References:** `<81886f2d.0207091401.6fbc404@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Pandian,

What you wanna do is convert a table or a database because the needs of the
business changed.
Well I used to do these things when I needed a conversion.

1) Read the table,  reorganise the data and write it to a file.
2) Read the file and write the data to the table/database (this can be any
database on any DB/2 system).

You can also work with the DB/2 unload command and reorganise the file. This
ensures you that you don't bring the performance of DB/2 down just by
testing your stuff.

Greetings,
Philip Van Daele,
Belgium.

                        COMP.LANG.COBOL
```

<a id="t-4da98f30fd"></a>
## Thread: embedded subprograms — 3 msgs

### embedded subprograms

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T22:40:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020709184017.11120.00003054@mb-ci.aol.com>`

```
I am OJTing COBOL85 for mainframes.  
There is a copybook which inserts a complete little program for
handling database errors, with its own little working storage and
its own little procedure division, and so forth, just like a real program.  
It is pretty cool.  But I don't understand the purpose of it. 
It seems to me that it would be better to have an external
program and just make calls to it.
What are the advantgages of an embedded subprogram? 



Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: embedded subprograms

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-09T17:50:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agfpc8$fne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020709184017.11120.00003054@mb-ci.aol.com>`

```
Nested programs (what you are referring to) perform BETTER than even
"statically called" subprograms in an IBM mainframe environment.  That is
the USUAL reason for their existence there.  Another reason, is that some
programmers coming from other programming languages "like" (and/or are used
to) "embedded procedures".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: embedded subprograms

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T02:07:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2D20A4.7050100@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<20020709184017.11120.00003054@mb-ci.aol.com> <agfpc8$fne$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Nested programs (what you are referring to) perform BETTER than even
> "statically called" subprograms in an IBM mainframe environment.  That is
> the USUAL reason for their existence there.  Another reason, is that some
> programmers coming from other programming languages "like" (and/or are used
> to) "embedded procedures".

Another major advantage I found was that they are called like an 
external program, i.e., with parameters, so the nested program can 
address anything the caller can address. This is a difficult issue in 
PERFORMed logic.
```

- **Date:** 2002-07-10T00:19:58+00:00

```
wrote:

>I recently came across the following code:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I found this interesting - Do you like this code or dislike this code?

While I get the gist of it, I don't believe one should ever use Evaluate
statements like that. I think it's better style to use IF statements 
or perform statements embeded witin it, or replace the whole thing
with nested IF-THEN statements.
```

## Thread: Text Editors for Cobol. — 10 msgs

### Text Editors for Cobol.

- **From:** CHARLECHAT@HOTMAIL.COM (Pogo)
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T05:26:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78d90c3e.0207100426.50a80560@posting.google.com>`

```
Good Morning All,

I am using Fujitsu Cobol 4.0 on an NT platform.  I am very
disappointed in the editor delivered with the product and would like
to find another appropriate one.  Back in DOS days I used QEdit
extensively and loved it but don't know anything about their current
product.  Any suggestions?

Regards,
Bob Taylor
```

#### ↳ Re: Text Editors for Cobol.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T13:21:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KxWW8.9161$iB1.542364@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<78d90c3e.0207100426.50a80560@posting.google.com>`

```

"Pogo" <CHARLECHAT@HOTMAIL.COM> wrote in message
news:78d90c3e.0207100426.50a80560@posting.google.com...
> Good Morning All,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> product.  Any suggestions?

If you switch, you'll give up the features of the editor that communicate
with the rest of the suite. There are some things I'd like Fujitsu's editor
to do differently, but I like its ability to understand what the compiler
and linker are doing / have done is quite useful.

The look-and-feel of programming editors is often a matter of strong
personal preference. I tend to use the one supplied by the compiler vendor
because of the integration.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Text Editors for Cobol.

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T16:23:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d2c9a73$1_5@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<78d90c3e.0207100426.50a80560@posting.google.com> <j50piuch4tc96s4g5l30loduftamlpgisn@4ax.com>`

```
Our entire staff uses MultiEdit along with the Fixed Format COBOL Macro
extensions from Progeni.  This is a FREE extension to the MultiEdit editor
that adds a LOT to the editors ability to work with COBOL files, especially
when in Fixed Format.
The free extensions are available in the Download Library of
www.aboutlegacycoding.com under Programmer Utilities.
```

## Thread: [OT] Question about jcl — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [OT] Question about jcl

- **From:** "Capellone" <.capellone@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T17:02:51+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aghigg$cds$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
Hi to everybody, i know i'm out of topic,
but the 2 newsgroups i found where i could leave this kind of messages are
not so much frequented.

But i think most of you all work with jcl, and maybe someone can help me.

I need to simulate a load into a DB2 table. The load is sent thru a jcl
which takes the data  from a file.
I want all the spool as usual, but i don't want really update the table.

I hope you understand what i meant.

thanks
Capellone
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Question about jcl

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-10T22:46:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2CB92A.BB784240@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<aghigg$cds$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```


Capellone wrote:
> 
> Hi to everybody, i know i'm out of topic,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

In order to test the JCL you will have to really load something (I
assume you are planning to use the DB2 Load Utility?).


I have a test where I deliberately attempt to load duplicate records
(which rejected to an error file), this does more then simulate a load,
the table will not load the duplicated records, but the JCL stream will
work fine.

	Susan A
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Question about jcl

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T02:14:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2D222F.3070609@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<aghigg$cds$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <3D2CB92A.BB784240@boeing.com>`

```
Susan A Allen wrote:
> 
> Capellone wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 	Susan A

Maybe the Job card parameter "TYPRUN=SCAN" wound be useful? This would 
validate your JCL but not execute anything user code.

I suspect, though, that you want to execute the load utility but not 
update your database. I don't know DB2, so I can't say one way or the 
other. For straight batch programs, you could assign the output file to 
DUMMY and run the job. Usually the program being invoked would run as 
though it were creating an output file (it's possible to check the 
attributes of the DD cards and, thus, know that the output file was 
dummied out). Could you update a copy of the table, then discard it?
```

```
Capellone
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Question about jcl

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T06:02:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agjl33$13c$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<aghigg$cds$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <3D2CB92A.BB784240@boeing.com> <agjfqq$dp5$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
In article <agjfqq$dp5$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>,
Capellone <.capellone@libero.it> wrote:

[snip]

>I don't have to test my jcl, i need to see if my data follows all the
>integrity, and if it is, i don't want
>it to write anyway, not yet.

It seems like you want to LOAD your table and then do a ROLLBACK on it to 
make sure that your data meet the various table definitions for ranges, 
foreign keys, formats and various constraints; this smells, to me, like 
'let's test this in production'... anyways, I am not sure this is 
possible.

Might you be able to create a test copy of the INDD file and modify a 
field of it to flag it for deletion?  You can run a FileAid against it 
and, for instance, REPL all the street-address fields to '123 PALAZZO 
PAZZO' or change all the user-id fields to 'AIAIAIEE'... or anything 
uniquely associable with your test.  After you run your LOAD you then run 
another batch job with SQL to delete from the table any rows which match 
these criteria.

DD
```

## Thread: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code.... — 25 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

- **From:** satyam_satyam@hotmail.com (Satyam)
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T04:24:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com>`

```
Has anybody used any conversion tool to make the Spaghetti COBOL code
to a Structured COBOL code (on IBM OS/390)? I heard that there is tool
from CA called VISION:Recode. Are there any other similar tools
available? What are the merits & demerits of these tools? What level
of testing is needed after conversion? Does the conversion code need
any manual intervention? Does it increase the performance of the
programs and savings interms of CPU time etc., How much of effort is
required to carry on this task?
Please help. Thanks a lot in advance.

Best Regards, Satyam.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T12:39:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o0fX8.31536$5f3.12998@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com>`

```
If you were interested in an IBM solution, you could have used the COBOL
Structuring Facility.

It would revamp existing code to a structured format - you would set the
guidelines by which it operated.  Manual changes would still probably be
required in some instances.

Advantage - code that is standardized is easier to maintain (provided the
developers understand and adhere to the guidelines).

Disadvantage - if anyone clearly understood the old, unformatted code, they were
usually not likely to be happy with the results.

Any change to any program requires a degree of testing - this is certainly no
exception.

http://www2.ibmlink.ibm.com/cgi-bin/master?xh=J3tobiLEPDrGd42USenGnN9332&request
=salesmanual&parms=H%5f5696%2d737&xhi=usa%2emain%7csalesmanual%5e&xfr=N

It seems that this product is no longer available - no replacement seems to be
evident.

Larry Kahm
```

```
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

IMHO that was a perfectly reasonable question.

My 2c worth... I have found these tools to be not useful. Minor format fixing
programs (beautifiers) are very useful, but restructuring spaghetti code
automatically seems to result in hard to follow code. 

Tim Josling
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T16:02:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agko8o$6en$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com> <agjvg3$mtq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3D2DDB4F.E0CA429A@melbpc.org.au>`

```
In article <3D2DDB4F.E0CA429A@melbpc.org.au>,
Tim Josling  <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>IMHO that was a perfectly reasonable question.

Well... 'reasonable' is, quite obviously, open to interpretation... and my 
Drill Sergeant taught me something about comparing opinions and a certain 
body part... but hey, if I want only agreement then I would speak only 
with my mirror.

It sounded to me like a 'do my homework for me' question involving some 
rather intricate issues; there is no indication whatsoever that the 
inquirer has expended any effort on his own, even to the point of 
contacting the company to verify 'what I heard'.

What restructuring tools are available?  A few, here and there.  What kind 
of system needs restructuring? How large a staff supports it?  What's the 
technical expertise available in-house?  'Savings in terms of CPU time 
etc.'... smells like a fishing-expedition.

>
>My 2c worth... I have found these tools to be not useful. Minor format fixing
>programs (beautifiers) are very useful, but restructuring spaghetti code
>automatically seems to result in hard to follow code. 

What... you mean that paragraph names like 
A751269-C5100-REFORMAT-GL600-REC aren't intuitively obvious?

DD
```

```
Larry,

Sorry for my wordings... after I saw all your comments I realised what
I wrote. I was actually frustrated with the search and calls made to
differenet vendors on this topic. Nobody is aware of these kind of
tools except CA's VISION:Recode. CA is yet to respond to my queries. I
am trying to find out if there any other competitive tools available
in the market. The whole purpose of this is to make the existing code
a structured and easy maintainable. The programs has become so complex
to understand as for the past 15 years many had laid hands on it and
played with it. Now I am finding out ways to make it maintainable. It
is a huge task to take it up manually (instead we can develop a new
code..:-). Hope you understand my situation.

-Satyam.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T22:44:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2F5BC0.DAD3A7BD@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com> <agjvg3$mtq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3D2DDB4F.E0CA429A@melbpc.org.au> <agko8o$6en$1@panix1.panix.com> <xumX8.32767$5f3.4003@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <fd149d96.0207112040.2fa0d96c@posting.google.com>`

```


Satyam wrote:
> in the market. The whole purpose of this is to make the existing code
> a structured and easy maintainable. The programs has become so complex
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> code..:-). Hope you understand my situation.


You will get a much better product with a thoughtful review of the code
you have, develop your rules and standards and hire (1 or more)
experienced programmers to do it properly - a really good analyst will
also be of enormous assistance as will a committed customer.

Old code may have useless processes, patches in the wrong location in
the data stream, work arounds for new rules; out dated rules; code best
used in a table; code better used out of a table and so forth.

In your case, manual will be far better then any conversion routine, as
you must get the logical structure functioning cleanly.  The one time I
saw conversion code it failed to identify duplicate logical routines
and  other obvious code inefficiencies (it was structured); it failed to
address a code 'fall through' and replicated some unwanted structures.


	Susan A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T03:56:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D2E5351.81ADC8D8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com> <agjvg3$mtq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3D2DDB4F.E0CA429A@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
> (snip) 
> My 2c worth... I have found these tools to be not useful. Minor format fixing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tim Josling

I had some experience with IBM's mainframe CSF (COBOL Structuring
Facility).  I would have to say I was a little disappointed.  The
product worked very well at eliminating GO TO and ALTER statements,
which has some value.  For very bad cases of PERFORM...THRU it would
generate working-storage flags to control PERFORM branching in the
generated program.  "Performed by..." comments could be automatically
generated.  CSF could also do some "beautification" and enforce
standard alignment.

I used it convert a couple of real stinkers and installed them into
production.  In general, there would be fewer paragraph names in the
generated program than in the original spaghetti code source file. 
Paragraph names that were eliminated could optionally be propogated as
comments in the target program.  And quite often CSF found large
chucks of dead code in the procedure division that it could not
propogate to the target program.

The biggest difficulty was that uninformative data names and procedure
names were still uninformative in the target program.  The generated
program could be improved even more by an experienced programmer. 

Naturally, I tested every program I converted, and never found a bug
caused by the automated restructuring itself.  My shop eventually
stopped paying for CSF because we weren't using it enough.  But it was
very good at getting rid of GO TO's and ALTER's.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion of Spaghetti cobol code to Structured COBOL code....

- **From:** "Deborah Torrekens" <deborah@phidani.be>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T17:43:38+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d416ecc$0$30453$afc38c87@sisyphus.news.be.easynet.net>`
- **References:** `<fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com>`

```
Satyam,

RainCode http://www.raincode.com has a very efficient COBOL refactoring
technology.
RainCode's approach is to take the COBOL code and renovate it through the
application of automatic improvements.
The re-structuring of the code is done by applying a sequence of passes on
the source, among which:

- Replacing GO TO's by equivalent, but structured constructs.
- Removal of dead components.
- Loop detection and transformation into PERFORM statements.
- Cosmetic enhancements such as: systematic indentation, adding the optional
reserved words (THEN, END-IF, etc.).

Each step can take advantage of the result of the other steps, incrementally
rising the "abstraction level'' of the code.

This method proves to be advantageous in many ways.
- Executability is guaranteed. The risk of error is quite low. If an error
were made, it would be infinitely repeated through the automatic process,
which means that the probability of not catching it is close to null.
- Semantic equivalence between the initial code and the renovated code is
guaranteed.
- The process can be applied at will, even after potential manual changes
were made; improvements can be made incrementally.
- There is no eclipse effect: the program continues to run during the
renovation process, and can be tested at any time.

As of today RainCode offers the renovation as a service, and not as a tool.

Please, don't hesitate to contact me if you need more information.
Best regards,

Deborah Torrekens
PS: check out DVV Insurance's story at: http://www.raincode.com/dvv.htm

"Satyam" <satyam_satyam@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:fd149d96.0207110324.1165aaca@posting.google.com...
> Has anybody used any conversion tool to make the Spaghetti COBOL code
> to a Structured COBOL code (on IBM OS/390)? I heard that there is tool
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Best Regards, Satyam.
```

```
In responce to the post:
 On Fri, 26 Jul 2002 17:43:38 +0200, "Deborah Torrekens"
<deborah@phidani.be> stated...and I replied:

>Satyam,
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Does anyone else see a problem here?  Spaghetti code is a result of
inexperienced programmers writing code that may work but does not
incorporate structured programming techniques.  RainCode would
probably not be needed if programmers used structured programming
standards.

I've had to unravel code written first with a lack of understanding
for database currency and retrieval, and after 26 other programmers
(the source code included fix reports) tried to fix it and (without
fixing the root DB retrival problem) failed.

Now I'm not saying that any specific race is at fault, but this shop
had a high percentage of Indian cobol programmers on the H1B visa
program.  This group was trained and sent to fill positions in IT
departments as low cost, inexperienced, resources.

It was this group that had spent 26 programmers, to fail at fixing
this problem (remember, there was a log of the fixes, with
programmer's name, in the source code).  This State agency got what
they paid for.

As a parting comment...if you write code where the base functionality
is flawed, no effort of "automated recoding" will result in code that
will perform without flaws.

-
One of many Computer Consultants in the USA
http://drshell.home.mindspring.com/
30 years in the computer field, 20 with Unisys.
-
```

## Thread: Cobol test suite from NIST — 5 msgs

- **From:** "Holly Wilper" <holly.l.wilper@intel.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T11:06:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agkalc$aoi@news.or.intel.com>`

```
I need a supply of Cobol tests. I downloaded the Cobol85 conformance suite
from NIST http://www.itl.nist.gov/div897/ctg/cobol_form.htm . Unfortunately
I've never used Cobol before in my life and I do not understand the
instructions for extracting the tests from the form. The site indicates:

"The COBOL Test Suite is a compressed file that must be extracted from a
UNIX system. Please rename the file as "filename" Z. To run the test suite,
you need to extract and set up the COBOL Executive routine program; execute
the COBOL Executive to select and prepare the test programs for compilation,
and then prepare the necessary job scripts to compile and execute the test
programs. "

It extracts into a single file and from there I am clueless. I extract it on
our UNIX system and then back over to Windows to try to compile with Fujitsu
Cobol for .NET, no go. I have tried to contact the contact person at NIST
listed on the page, but she has not returned my email (months ago).

Could anyone tell me how to extract this file to get individual Cobol tests?
Alternatively could you point me at another source for Cobol tests?

Thank you for your time and trouble.

Holly Wilper
```

<a id="t-93c9537b35"></a>
## Thread: Converting ADS to COBOL: Link to dialog with map loses place in calling program — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting ADS to COBOL: Link to dialog with map loses place in calling program

- **From:** garyedotson@hotmail.com (Gary)
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T12:56:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53b6b4e4.0207111156.16b47998@posting.google.com>`

```
I would like to understand how to convert applications 
from IDMS& ADS/O to DB2 & CICS. We are very much 
done with IDMS to DB2 conversion. At least we are able 
to define the rules and guidelines to convert IDMS to DB2. The 
problem is ADS/O to CICS. 
I am interested in how pseudo-conversation can be 
accomplished when converting LINK TO DIALOG statement to LINK 
program-name. In ADS/O it allows to link to a 
DIALOG 
and comes back to the point where it linked. How can 
we
achieve this functionality without rewriting the program. 
There is no change in business functionality and we are trying to 
eliminate rewriting the programs. 


Gary
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting ADS to COBOL: Link to dialog with map loses place in calling program

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-11T23:12:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RhoX8.89352$p85.2397733@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<53b6b4e4.0207111156.16b47998@posting.google.com>`

```
Don't know if it helps any, but if by DIALOG you mean a screen display/read cycle,
then you can wrap it in a subroutine. The typical CICS cycle for that would be:

exec-cics send map(mapname) mapset(mapsetname) from (data-area-name)
  [erase] [data only] [map only] ...
  end-exec
exec cics receive map(mapname) mapset(mapsetname) into (data-area-name)
  end-exec.

It is a little more complex than that, because your program has to check the AID characters and
such to see what the user really did, but not difficult in the least.

So you may need to write some subroutines that take a map/mapset/dataarea and
make the CICS call if you really want to keep it a link thing. Like I said, I don't know if that
helps you at all, I've never dealt with IMS.

-Paul

That looks more complex, but it is pretty flexible, as you can
"Gary" <garyedotson@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:53b6b4e4.0207111156.16b47998@posting.google.com...
```

## Thread: Power-Cobol and CBL_CHECK_File_EXIST — 4 msgs

### Power-Cobol and CBL_CHECK_File_EXIST

- **From:** Hajo Schepker <schepker@web.de>
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T18:59:17+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agn8u5.1us.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Hi everybody,
can anyone tell me, in which document I find the meaning of the
staus-code, returning from above routine within power-cobol?

I received a statuscode value = 1
What means that?
-thanks-
```

#### ↳ Re: Power-Cobol and CBL_CHECK_File_EXIST

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T19:52:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CrGX8.50790$iB1.2824218@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<agn8u5.1us.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de>`

```

"Hajo Schepker" <schepker@web.de> wrote in message
news:agn8u5.1us.2@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de...
> Hi everybody,
> can anyone tell me, in which document I find the meaning of the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> -thanks-

Uh, "CBL Subroutines User's Guide," Chapter 4, page 56

0: Successful
Other than 0: Failure

But you SHOULD NOT be using CBL_FILE_EXIST you should be using
CBL_FILE_EXIST2 because the latter can handle file names with imbedded
spaces while the former cannot.
```

## Thread: Linux COBOL — 11 msgs

### Linux COBOL

- **From:** "Wolf Grossi" <Go2devnull@magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T19:11:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1026501106.518317@newsmaster-03.atnet.at>`

```
Hi folks,

du to _very_agressive_ license policy of XXX-cobol vendor, implemented
just recently, I am considering to migrate several huge COBOL
applications of my customers to open source cobol, ansi-85 compliant
if possible.

What would you recommend for Linux Systems?

I found TinyCobol, Kobol, Fujitsu COBOL (Windows only I guess), but
before starting to test I would like to get your opinion.

Thanks for reading,
Wolf
```

#### ↳ Re: Linux COBOL

- **From:** "R.Freitag" <rfr-mailbox@gmx.de>
- **Date:** 2002-07-13T01:42:34+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<agnpko$975$1@newsreader2.netcologne.de>`

```
Wolf Grossi wrote:

> Hi folks,
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> What would you recommend for Linux Systems?

It depends.

Fujutsu is, afaik, only for Windows. I do not know if it runs with an 
emulator. Tiny Cobol is only Ansi85, there is another Cobol for Ansi74 (!!) 
which shall be updated perhaps. (nobody knows).

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Linux COBOL

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-07-12T19:27:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0207121827.565597d4@posting.google.com>`

```
"Wolf Grossi" <Go2devnull@magro-soft.com> wrote in message n
> 
> What would you recommend for Linux Systems?
> 
> I found TinyCobol, Kobol, Fujitsu COBOL (Windows only I guess), but

I don't believe that TinyCobol or Kobol are 'production quality' yet. 
Fujitsu has announced a Linux version of Cobol, said to go into Beta
soon.  They have had Unix (HP-UX and Sun) compilers for many years so
I would expect a quality product in a short time.  You might even be
able to get on the Beta program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linux COBOL

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-07-14T18:56:54+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<140720021856541954%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<1026501106.518317@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <dOWX8.118533$Im2.5728749@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
In article <dOWX8.118533$Im2.5728749@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>, JJ
<jj@nospam.com> wrote:

> What do you mean by "open source cobol"?  Are you looking for the compiler
> to be open source?
> 
Pretty obviously the meaning is that the source code to the compiler is
OPEN or otherwise free.
For Linux here are the Open Source Compilers currently available.

COBOL compilers and tools for Linux: open source
Tiny COBOL 
http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/ 
The Tiny COBOL project is a COBOL compiler being developed by the free
software community. The goal is to produce a COBOL compiler, which is
ANSI COBOL-74 compliant and later ANSI COBOL-85 compliant. It is being
developed on the Linux OS, using a mix of C and LEX/YACC to produce a
compiler that will generate GNU x86 assembler code. It only runs on an
i386 Linux platform. The Tiny COBOL compiler and runtime library are
licensed under the GNU General Public License
http://www.gnu.org/copyleft/gpl.html/.

GNU COBOL2C 
http://www.gnu.org/software/cobol/ 
GNU Cobol is a Cobol to C translator. GNU Cobol is an open source
project still in alpha release. The GNU Cobol2C project is an effort to
create a COBOL compiler that will have the ability to interact with
existing COBOL libraries and data files. A free compiler download is at
http://metalab.unc.edu/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/.

CobCy 
http://web.tiscalinet.it/albertosantini/cobcy/ 
This is a COBOL to C translator intended to compile ANSI COBOL sources.
The current version is not complete. The parser is YACC based. The rest
of the translator is written in C++.

Tim Josling COBOL 
http://www.geocities.com/timjosling/ 
A GNU Compiler Collection-based COBOL compiler is provided here.

CPB 
http://users.skynet.be/jpa/ 
CPB is a general-purpose COBOL source code analysis tool.
```

## Thread: indexed file — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### indexed file

- **From:** "GG" <ggazzola@inwind.it>
- **Date:** 2002-07-13T13:21:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7PVX8.32903$7N3.749695@twister2.libero.it>`

```
How can I create an indexed file, to execute a batch cobol
program in the PC environment?


            thanks
            Giorgio.
```

#### ↳ Re: indexed file

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-13T13:37:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j2WX8.89293$eF5.2671220@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7PVX8.32903$7N3.749695@twister2.libero.it>`

```
HUH?

To execute a 'batch' file in the PC environment you need either one of two things;

(1) A program which would read a data file and execute each command in it (which if you want
      and Indexed Data File for some reason, could be a COBOL program)   OR

(2) A text file that ends with a .BAT or .CMD extension that contains the list of commands you want
      to execute. This is NOT an indexed file.

Perhaps it is just too early on Saturday morning for me to correctly interpret what you asked. :)

-Paul


"GG" <ggazzola@inwind.it> wrote in message news:7PVX8.32903$7N3.749695@twister2.libero.it...
> How can I create an indexed file, to execute a batch cobol
> program in the PC environment?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
