```
news:3D32FC16.8A6599D9@attglobal.net...
> Did you:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Carlos Lages

Sure you can. Inside the called form you'll have:

MOVE 'TEXT' OF MYFIELD TO DATANAME.

Where

01  DATANAME   PIC X(30) GLOBAL EXTERNAL.

in Working-Storage for both forms.

In fact, the called form doesn't even have to be visible (obviously the
called form must be visible for your application). It gets loaded, does it's
thing (maybe retrieve a record), and goes away.
```

#### ↳ Re: Power Cobol (OCX)

- **From:** pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira)
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T02:06:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5b8d7c7.0207150106.9b9717d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D30DAB3.D2799384@attglobal.net>`

```
Carlos Lages <clages@attglobal.net> wrote in message news:<3D30DAB3.D2799384@attglobal.net>...
> Hi, Its me again
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Carlos lages


Let's assume that I have a single field Form called "Customer" which
has a text field in it. That form by itself will use another OCX
control wich takes care of Database access for the customers table(s).
This Form ("Customer") will have at least 2 propreties: ID, Code, (and
probably Name).
Whenever a user inputs something in the field, the code in the form
will:
- Validate input for format
- Validate input against the Database using the DbAccess OCX
- Fill in the properties if aplicable
- Fire the "Customer_Changed" custom event

In the containing Form, I will write code to handle that event.

My reasons:
The visual object "Customer" will be used whenever a form needs it. 
Using it as an OCX instead of an OLE server let's me trap the events
of the OCX, something I can't do with an OLE server (invoked as such).
Using properties instead of method parameters is easier because every
time a new field is required, I don't have to recompile all the other
modules that use that interface; only the ones that will use the new
field.
If I, later, decide to add funcionality to the "Customer" Form (adding
help for instance, or a "browse" form to pickup customers based on
some strange criteria), that funcionality will be available to all
Forms that already use the "Customer" Form.

regards

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft
```

## Thread: Comparing Alphanumeric fields !! — 3 msgs

### Comparing Alphanumeric fields !!

- **From:** "Jagannath Nori" <jnori@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-14T20:35:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OIaY8.3073$JN4.1004721@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
I am trying to compare two 2 alphanumeric fields and though the values are
same it is skipping the conditional statement. could any one help out  pls
??

The statment is like this

If     var1 equal var2
and  var3  equal var4

    perform xxx

end-if.

here var1 , var2 , var3 and var4 are alphanumeric variables.

many thanx in advance

Jag
```

#### ↳ Re: Comparing Alphanumeric fields !!

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-07-14T07:25:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0207140625.56746734@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<OIaY8.3073$JN4.1004721@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
Hello jag,

I agree it looks puzzling, my guess is that for some reason or other
the variables are not equal.  I suggest you try using the DISPLAY
statement just before the condition statement, to show the actual
values that are present.  Using a debugging tool to inspect the values
just before the statement is executed is another approach that may
help.

good luck, Robert

"Jagannath Nori" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote in message news:<OIaY8.3073$JN4.1004721@news02.tsnz.net>...
> I am trying to compare two 2 alphanumeric fields and though the values are
> same it is skipping the conditional statement. could any one help out  pls
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jag
```

#### ↳ Re: Comparing Alphanumeric fields !!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-14T15:33:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sRgY8.9456$ak5.5151@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<OIaY8.3073$JN4.1004721@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
Well, then, either...

1. Your compiler is buggy. Highly, highly unlikely. Don't bet anything of
value on this.
2. The vars are not really equal - Likely. Check that the vars being
compared are:
   A. The same length (PICTURE IS..).
   B. Use the same character (typically LOW-VALUE or SPACE) to fill unused
character positions.
```

<a id="t-0696e7fed0"></a>
## Thread: Cobol source aligning — 16 msgs

### Cobol source aligning

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-14T21:44:08+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.tsorexx,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8aksga.ef1.ln@news.hinznet.home>`

```
Hello readers,

perhaps I am a bit 'overdozed' but when I sometimes read ancient sources
of Mainframe COBOL looking a bit 'quick and dirty' I guess others must
have had similar problems: 

No nicely arranged VALUES, every TO in a different col, no plain nesting
in WORKING STORAGE at all, no nested IF's, everything lookin a bit
unaligned. It looks like a mess and only due to this it is uncomfortable
to analyze.

Is anybody out there using a tool - just like a comb - to arrange given
source code so that it looks a bit more readable?

I spent some time on a REXX routine but I am still not satisfied with
the results.

Anybody willing to share experience?

I used tools like Cobol Workbench or Visual Cobol but these did not make
my life much easier. I want to have something available in the ISPF Edit
environment.

Okay, ducked and covered......
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol source aligning

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T17:21:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.tsorexx,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pZGY8.1614$QY4.460245@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<8aksga.ef1.ln@news.hinznet.home>`

```

"Harald Hinz" <harald.hinz@ewetel.net> wrote in message
news:8aksga.ef1.ln@news.hinznet.home...
> Hello readers,
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

To tell the truth, I have found the best bet is to clean up a little
manually every time I go into a program.  On the other hand, I have to admit
that the code is so messy because when I wrote it twenty-five years back, I
was not as good a coder as I am now, and when I went through each of those
five or six conversions, I probably did them faster than I should have ... I
tried to use automated programs just as you are suggesting ... give me
another twenty years and these programs will actually be pretty.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol source aligning

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-16T05:41:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.tsorexx,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D33B1FE.AF8E7E0E@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8aksga.ef1.ln@news.hinznet.home>`

```
Harald Hinz wrote:
> 
> Hello readers,
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Okay, ducked and covered......

In 1992 I wrote a COBOL program to convert OS/VS COBOL source code to
VS COBOL II.  I was primarily concerted with fixing up OS/VS COBOL
uses of CURRENT-DATE and WHEN-COMPILED, plus converting BLL cells and
SERVICE RELOAD to COBOL II usage.  If it needed to, it inserted
working-storage items and made changes to procedure division code.  It
also had a couple of PARM overrides to change its conversion
behavior.  I always ran it as a plain batch job under MVS, but it
might be adaptable to run in ISPF.  I used it to convert about 100
programs to COBOL II, a mix of batch and CICS programs.

It also aligns PIC, REDEFINES, and VALUE clauses, and TO objects in
procedure division.  I'd say it works better on data division than
procedure division, depending on what the input COBOL source code
looks like. 

If you're interested, I could zip up a copy of the source code, plus
some notes on how to set up the JCL.  It reads source code as 80-byte
records and writes an 80-byte record file, which you can direct to a
PDS or a QSAM file.  The COBOL program was about 3800 lines, and
should compile clean under COBOL II or higher.

Let me know if you'd like a copy.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<uds8ha.jp.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<8aksga.ef1.ln@news.hinznet.home> <3D33B1FE.AF8E7E0E@worldnet.att.net> <y+D1VHABqqN9EwBY@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> schrieb in 
news:y+D1VHABqqN9EwBY@ld50macca.demon.co.uk:

> In article <3D33B1FE.AF8E7E0E@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
> <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> writes
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

ACK. Thanks to Arnold Trembley I recieved a copy of the program code
yesterday. I had a first look at it and was excited.

The source is perfercly aligned, well structured and extremly readable. 
A good example on how to make program code understandable for the next
generations ;-)) To be honest, it's not exacly my own way of coding
and naming, but due to its look it is easy to understand it and perhaps
add your own ideas....

My experience in cobol program maintenance is as follows:

First time you have to apply some changes to a given program:

- you spend 80% of the time in understanding 'what it does' and
  'how it does that'
- you spend 10 % of the time in applying the change, compile and
  testing it. (including terminal response time ;-)
- you spend the last 10% in admin stuff like promoting the change,
  getting approval and shipping it to the live system (again: including
  terminal responses which might be a bit slower depending on your
  environment)

Since Signore Pareto adviced to have a look at the 80% before checking 
the other 20% it might be very interesting to every programmer to
increase his own performance by using his own tools. This piece of code
is a perfect example in my eyes.

Again. Thanks to Arnold. If you know the word: "Plumber's tubes leak"
remove it from your files :-))

Arnold has his own http site. Thinking about capacity and traffic and
maybe the rules for this group it may be another good solution to make the 
zip-file downloadable there and post the URL to the group.
```

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> schrieb in 
news:3D393F72.76452ACE@worldnet.att.net:

> Harald Hinz wrote:
[..]
>> Again. Thanks to Arnold. If you know the word: "Plumber's tubes leak"
>> remove it from your files :-))
> 
> I'm not sure I understand the reference.

Well then: In Germany we have two common expressions to identify
a certain 'common sense' about craftsmen:

1. Der Schuster hat den schlechtesten Leisten.
transl: Shoemakers wear the poorest boots. 

(a typical anglo-american version of this: Plumber's tubes leak.)

2. Der Schneider hat den besten Zwirn.
the exact opposite: "Taylors wear the finest cloth."

The 'Programming Craftsman' can decide either to play the taylor's or
the shoemaker's (e.g. plumber's) part. 

CB2align to me is a more like a tailor's well designed business suit in order 
to show what he is able to put together. 

The metaphor of a leaking tube in the plumber's home has nothing to do with 
it, that's what i wanted to explain.

>> Arnold has his own http site. Thinking about capacity and traffic and
>> maybe the rules for this group it may be another good solution to make the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's about 22K bytes.

Thanks again. I think the ip-traffic will increase a bit ;-)).

How about the idea of maintaining this code like an 'open source' project?
YMHO.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol source aligning

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T10:20:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>From: Harald Hinz harald.hinz@ewetel.net 
>Date: 2002-07-14 15:44 Eastern Daylight Time

>Is anybody out there using a tool - just like a comb - to arrange given
>source code so that it looks a bit more readable?
>

I used a tool called PATHVU and RETROFIT.  It had several options, 
such as rewriting GO TO code into PERFORM only code.  It was
able to detect dead code, and other things. 

One option did realignment.  You could enter some parameters, 
such as what column to use for destinations, or whether to use
white space, etc.  You also specified whether to realign just
working storage, or just procedure, etc.  

Very cool. 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

<a id="t-3b72a39d07"></a>
## Thread: PowerCOBOL + GEM — 1 msgs

### PowerCOBOL + GEM

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T02:55:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iQqY8.89516$iB1.5158415@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Been meaning to comment on GEM for some time. GEM is the Fujitsu version of
SourceSafe, a source code development and management system.

We keep our source on a server. To edit/compile from there was a time-waster
inasmuch as everything had to be hauled across the network, plus FJ uses
scratch files on the same drive as the source, so God-knows how many
megabytes were moving back and forth.

So, we experimented with GEM with the working library on the local drive.
That is, modules are checked-out of the main library (on the network) and
manipulated locally. Once perfected, the module is checked back in. While
checked out, no one else can have access to the module. Whole thing works
like a charm. Compiles on our larger programs dropped from three minutes to
50 seconds. Couldn't be more pleased.

Well, yes I could. PowerCOBOL projects are treated, by GEM, as binary files.
That means that there is no source-level history and roll-back available.
You'd think FJ could do that, since GEM and PowerCOBOL are both their
products, but, hey, what's art without suffering?
```

## Thread: Cobol and XML — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Cobol and XML

- **From:** pa@schaubroeck.be (Peter Alluyn)
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T15:00:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d32e0e0.12981983@news.eunet.be>`

```
Hello,

We want to generate xml and xsd from our cobol data.  Has anyone got
some exp. with this matter ? Is there some information available on
the internet ?

Thanks,

Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and XML

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@heliotropicsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T15:26:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NQBY8.5092$2v1.3976@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<3d32e0e0.12981983@news.eunet.be>`

```
If you have Compuware Corporation's File-AID/MVS product (Release 8.8.1 and
above) you can generate and XML file from your application data.

If you (or someone in your shop) has access to the May 2002 issue of NaSPA's
"Technical Support" magazine, you can read my article, "Generation X Meets
Tyrannosaurus Rex: Working with XML on the Mainframe."

This article describes a new feature in each of two mainframe products: the
XMLGEN utility in Compuware Corporation's File-AID/MVS Release 8.8.1 and the
XML PARSE statement in IBM's Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and OS/390 Version 3
Release 1.  The former helps generate XML documents; the latter makes it
relatively easy to obtain data from these documents.

I hope that helps!

Larry Kahm
Heliotropic Systems, Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and XML

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>

```
Currently, NetExpress from www.microfocus.com can work with external XML
parsers. There's a white paper on the Micro Focus we site that discusses
XML. Some time in the not too distant future, Net Express will be able to
deal with XML directly.

www.datadirect-technologies.com have an ODBC driver which can read and write
XML directly by mapping XML documents as if they are SQL database tables -
clever stuff.

The XML ODBC driver will work with Net Express.
```

```
        -) in Cobol
        -) in JCL (without cobol

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Number of records

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-15T21:04:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XNGY8.5881$2v1.2966@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<agvafu$ro5$1@newsreader2.netcologne.de>`

```
Answer 2, use DFSort:

//STEP1 EXEC PGM=ICETOOL
//TOOLMSG DD SYSOUT=*
//DFSMSG  DD SYSOUT=*
//IN DD DISP=SHR,DSN=your.dataset.name
//TOOLIN DD *
 COUNT FROM(IN)
/*
```

```
and much, MUCH more
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89355d6f.0207161632.1d7218a@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there some standard IBM supplied proc that can be used to send
email from a JCL?  Basically trying to send a note if a job fails. 
Want to send it to an internet address
```

#### ↳ Re: Email from JCL

- **From:** Harry Carter <hpcarter@attbi.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T01:34:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aqh9juclgoleenbd300rmhqgi3r4gm9fpp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0207161632.1d7218a@posting.google.com>`

```
On 16 Jul 2002 17:32:45 -0700, pakku@soccermail.com (Thomas Charles)
wrote:

>Is there some standard IBM supplied proc that can be used to send
>email from a JCL?  Basically trying to send a note if a job fails. 
>Want to send it to an internet address

Basically you just genner the message to the right sysout class and
destination. Your JES people need to tell you these parms.

As far as the email message itself, You need to check the TCP manual
for the parameters for smtp mail.  I'm going from memory here, You
need three statements before you get to the email message:
Helo:                        <<== yes it's spelled correctly. it's for
		domain name. 
Rcpt To:		the recipient. Use as many times as recipients
?From?		Not sure of the right parm name. It's the from field
Data		Everything past this is ignored by the mainframe in
                                     terms of processing.  But it's
what the recipient's 
		email system will see as the message complete with to,
from, etc. 
Message with To:
From:
etc.

Anyway check the manual for everything as I'm doing it from memory.
```

```
I just found it in: F1AA2030    OS/390 V2R10.0 IBM CS IP User's Guide.
Chapter 6. It explains how to user SMTPNOTE if you want to email from
within TSO as well as the SMTP commands if you send a message to the
right JES output class.
```

#### ↳ Re: Email from JCL

- **From:** mmarkzon@yahoo.com (Mike)
- **Date:** 2002-07-18T08:54:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad066cb2.0207180754.12c5f6e0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0207161632.1d7218a@posting.google.com>`

```
pakku@soccermail.com (Thomas Charles) wrote in message news:<89355d6f.0207161632.1d7218a@posting.google.com>...
> Is there some standard IBM supplied proc that can be used to send
> email from a JCL?  Basically trying to send a note if a job fails. 
> Want to send it to an internet address

Yes, we do this for similar reasons.

//JS040  EXEC PGM=IEBGENER,                                           
//            COND=(1,GT,JS020)                                       
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                                
//SYSIN    DD *                                                       
//SYSUT2   DD SYSOUT=A,DEST=(destination,NETISMTP)  
//SYSUT1   DD DATA                                                    
HELO smtp.yourserver.com
MAIL FROM:<you@yourcompany.COM>
RCPT TO:<you@yourcompany.COM>
DATA                                                                  
TO:<you@yourcompany.COM>
SUBJECT: ERROR PROCESSING SOMEJOB                                    
THERE WAS AN ERROR PROCESSING SOMEJOB.  FIX IT RIGHT AWAY!
.                                                                     
QUIT                                                                  
/*                                                                    
//*
```

```
On my system I have the name of the MVS mainframe entered.  Also, I'm
not sure if NETISMTP is standard or if that's something of ours.

> > file://SYSUT1   DD DATA
> > HELO smtp.yourserver.com
> 
> what if i want to send to mail.something.com?
> do i have just to type mail instead of smtp?

You need to enter the hostname of your outgoing smtp mailserver.  If
that happens to begin with mail, enter mail.something.com.
```

<a id="t-14541f9435"></a>
## Thread: DFHCOMMAREA — 23 this month / 27 total

### DFHCOMMAREA

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-16T18:43:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D34BDA1.9503990C@sprynet.com>`

```
A programmer at my shop came upon a rather strange situation the other
day.  First, let me post a code snippet.

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  DFHCOMMAREA              PIC X(2125).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    IF EIBCALEN = ZEROES                  
        GO TO 9300-XCTL-TO-RE10            
     ELSE                                  
        MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO RE-COMMAREA    
        IF RECOM-TRAN-ID = 'RE10' OR 'RE12'
           PERFORM 1000-SEND-INIT-MAPRE11  
        ELSE    
           PERFORM 2000-RECEIVE-MAP.       

Basically, this program (CICSRE10) can be entered two ways.  If it was
entered via an EXEC CICS RETURN TRANID('RE11') COMMAREA(RE-COMMAREA)
then EIBCALEN would not be zeroes and RECOMM-TRAN-ID would be 'RE11'. 
This should trigger the above to do the perform of 2000-RECEIVE-MAP.

The other way it can be entered is a related program (CICSRE10 or
CICSRE12) doing EXEC CICS XCTL PROGRAM('CICSRE11')
COMMAREA(RE-COMMAREA).  Again, EIBCALEN would not be zeroes.  But in
this case RECOMM-TRAN-ID would be 'RE10' or 'RE12', depending on which
program did the XCTL.  We would in this case perform
1000-SEND-INIT-MAPRE11.

But here's the deal.  Sometimes the "MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO RE-COMMAREA"
seems to have no effect.  In other words, it's as if that line is not
even there (so RE-COMMAREA remains all low-values, or whatever).  We've
used Intertest to trace the program and, even though it seems to 'do'
the move, we can see that nothing is actually moved.

This doesn't happen all of the time, though.  In fact, if the user tries
it and it fails, then logs out (to the VTAM menu) and logs back and
tries it again it may work.

The problem does not seem to occur in production.  Or at least no user
has ever reported it.  One difference between our production CICS and
our test CICS is that production still has the VS COBOL II runtime,
while test has LE/VSE 1.4.1 (VSE/ESA shop...running CICS/VSE 2.3 in both
prod and test).

One thought is this...  Even though DFHCOMMAREA is defined as 2125
bytes, EIBCALEN is never near that size.  In fact, the RETURN and XCTL
both include the LENGTH parameter and it's much less than 2125.  So when
this program does the MOVE it sometimes is accessing storage that is not
available to the program...?  The obvious solution it that case is to
change the MOVE to: MOVE DFHCOMMAREA (1:EIBCALEN) TO RE-COMMAREA. 
RE-COMMAREA is working storage.  It's 2125 bytes, but includes over 1000
bytes of filler.  The original programmer did this anticipating future
expansion...

Anyway, we're just wondering if this sounds like it may be the problem. 
We have a lot of programs that do this kind of thing, though the others
have nowhere near this much filler.  This is the only program we've run
in to this behavior, though, and we've had CICS under LE for three or
four months.  Now we're kind of nervous about going in to production
with LE/VSE, though.

Any thoughts?
```

```
MMM - instead of checking for zeros here, you should probably be checking the length of the returned comm area. Use EIBCALEN to do
this. The zero test might be TRUE when the length of the comm area is zero.

-Paul
```

```
Umm, what?  You are basically saying "instead of checking for zeroes
check for non-zeroes".  No difference (well, except the obvious).  In
any case, it will never be RETURNed to or XCTL'd to with a commarea of
zero.  The only time EIBCALEN is zero is if the transaction is started
by a user on blank CICS screen.  In this case we go to RE10, which is
the menu program.

Paul Raulerson wrote:

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

```

```
I'm sorry - seems to me that you definitely have a length error going on there. Perhaps someone has left the commarea off on return
statement somewhere. Believe it or not, sillier things have happened. Your trouble seems to be pretty much related to that kind of
issue. In other words, what would happen if you *did*
get back a comm area with an unexpected length?

Besides which, you should be using the EIBCALEN to determine how much of that comm area to return anyway.
```

```
Yes, there is a length error.  I was not doubting that.  But it wasn't
that EIBCALEN was zero.  It was that EIBCALEN was less than the
declaired size of DFHCOMMAREA.

Anyway, the solution, as already posted, is to use reference
modification "MOVE DFHCOMMAREA (1:EIBCALEN)".

Frank
```

#### ↳ Re: DFHCOMMAREA

- **From:** David_G_Clark@brown.edu (David_G_Clark)
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T12:47:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ah3p1k$ln8$1@saturn.services.brown.edu>`
- **References:** `<3D34BDA1.9503990C@sprynet.com>`

```

     You've almost got the reason.  This is caused because cobol is generating 
an assembler Move Characters Long instruction because of the lenght you've 
specified.   However with the MVCL,  there cannot be overlap of storage or the 
move never happens and (as far as I know) no error condition is ever raised.   
	The reason it does not fail consistantly is because CICS would need to 
load the programs working storage area next to the passed DFHCOMMAREA which 
would be much shorter than the 2125 you've specified and so result in an 
overlap of fields.   Cobol is going to move 2125 bytes so if the real size is 
125,  then 2000 bytes of whatever follows gets pulled in - if that happend to 
be part of RE-COMMAREA because it happens at that moment in time to follow 
the DFHCOMMARA in memory, then nothing is moved and no error condition (at 
least at the point of move).   
	The only way to avoid is to use matching lengths.  Even when it does 
not fail,  you're getting some sort of data moved to the end of RE-Commarea 
though program might not referance it so it doesn't cause trobles.   Easiest 
way is to use referance modification (which I think can be used with cobol II)
 
MOVE DFHCOMMAREA (1:EIBCALEN) TO RE-COMMAREA

 should fix problem if I've remembered the code correctly.    


In article <3D34BDA1.9503990C@sprynet.com>, infocat@sprynet.com says...
>
>A programmer at my shop came upon a rather strange situation the other
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
>"I'm very seldom naughty" --Willow Rosenberg, 'Buffy the Vampire Slayer'
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DFHCOMMAREA

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T18:39:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D360E24.14A19F0D@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3D34BDA1.9503990C@sprynet.com> <ah3p1k$ln8$1@saturn.services.brown.edu>`

```
Thanks!  This seems the be exactly what it happening.  I would have
never thought of this being the answer (not knowing assembler very well,
in any case), but it makes absolute sense.

Let me make sure I understand exactly.  There are 1576 bytes of
working-storage ahead of the beginning of RE-COMMAREA.  So if the
address of EIBCALEN is, say 200, then DFHCOMMAREA could conceivably
start at 200 bytes ahead of that.  That would mean that if RE-COMMAREA
is greather than 1776, which it is, then DFHCOMMAREA, being defined as
2125 bytes would overlap with RE-COMMAREA.  Does this sound right? 
Interesting.  I assume that the reason that it sometimes works is
because the DFHCOMMAREA being referred to may or may not be immediately
ahead of program working-storage, correct?

Woah!  So it looks like this has nothing to do with COBOL II runtime vs
LE/VSE runtime.  Would perhaps the reason it never seems to happen in
production is because the DFHCOMMAREA is never immediately ahead of the
program's working-storage?  Or maybe it has happened and no one has
reported it to us.  I don't think the transaction is used by too many
people.

Anyway, I think I figured out why no other programs are having this
problem.  Our other major programs that have a commarea this large
handle it a bit differently.  Something like:

IF EIBCALEN = DD-CA-SIZE
    MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO DD-COMMAREA
ELSE IF EIBCALEN = CU-CA-SIZE
    MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO CU-COMMAREA
etc. etc.

Because the destination is the proper size only that number of bytes is
moved.

Thanks a bunch for your help.  We'll fix the offending programs (only 3,
I believe) and go on with our happy programming lives!  :-)

Frank

David_G_Clark wrote:
> 
>      You've almost got the reason.  This is caused because cobol is generating
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…
> >"I'm very seldom naughty" --Willow Rosenberg, 'Buffy the Vampire Slayer'
```

#### ↳ Re: DFHCOMMAREA

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T11:26:58+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<059ijuk6pf7g22bkthi5cpkkbbpug6jria@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3D34BDA1.9503990C@sprynet.com>`

```
On Tue, 16 Jul 2002 18:43:13 -0600, Frank Swarbrick
<infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:

>A programmer at my shop came upon a rather strange situation the other
>day.  First, let me post a code snippet.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

First things first.  MOVE DFHCOMMAREA(1:EIBCALEN) TO RE-COMMAREA is
definitely safe, as opposed to MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO RE-COMMAREA

>We've
>used Intertest to trace the program and, even though it seems to 'do'
>the move, we can see that nothing is actually moved.

This is exactly what is the case.  The size of the areas to be moved
is large enough to call foe MVCL instruction to be used.  MVCL,
though, does not allow a destructive MOVE (like MVS would allow).
Therefore  the MVCL would stop with cc 3 set, which the cobol compiler
does not react on.  This is no bug (neither in CICS nor in Cobol)  It
is just the way the cookie crumbles.

Assume EIBCALEN = 100

Move DFHCOMMAREA to RE-COMMAREA might look like this

 +---------------+          +------------------------------+
 ! passed data   !          !  RE-COMMAREA at data + 200   !
 +---------------+          +------------------------------+
 <-- 100 bytes -->          <--------- 2000 bytes -------->

 <--- EIBCALEN -->
 +-------------------------------------+
 ! DFHCOMMAREA                         ! same address as passed data
 +-------------------------------------+ 
 <--------------- 2000 bytes ---------->


Move DFHCOMMAREA to RE-COMMAREA lead to destructive overlay, as
DFHCOMMAREA and RE-COMMAREA overlap

MOVE DFHCOMMARE(1:EIBCALEN) TO RE-COMMAREA avoids the overlap



Having said that, my question to you?  Why use this unnecessary MOVE
in the first place?  It is just plain "BAD"  (Broken As Designed)

Assume you do the following

PGM A  Links to PGM B passing a commarea
                PGM B MOVES COMMAREA to WS
                PGM B XCTL to PGMC, passsing WS-Commarea

                              PGMC RETURNS (to PGMA)

Data in Commarea of PGMC never show up in A

      
      
      
      
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)        
                                                            
      You will soon meet a stranger who will become your friend.
      
        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)

```

```
> Having said that, my question to you?  Why use this unnecessary MOVE
> in the first place?  It is just plain "BAD"  (Broken As Designed)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Data in Commarea of PGMC never show up in A

I'm not exactly sure what your question is.  Number 1, there are no
LINKs involved.  The only ways to enter the aforementioned program are
A) from a blank CICS screen, in which case EIBCALEN is zero, B) from
itself view a RETURN TRANSID COMMAREA and C) via XCTL PROGRAM COMMAREA
from another program.

Are you referring to the fact that the COMMAREA is moved to
working-storage at all?  If so, all I can say is it's been the shop
standard for 20 years and I can't imagine it being changed now.
```

```
>DFHCOMMAREA to WORKING-STORAGE  is a "Bad Thing" (TM).  But, of
>course, changing a 20 year old shop standard, as bad as it may be, is
>an "Impossible Thing" (TM) and should not necessarily be tried


I think every CICS program in our shop moves DHFCOMMAREA to a place in
WORKING-STORAGE.   However a lot are coded like this:

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  DFHCOMMAREA.
     05  FILLER        PIC X OCCURS 1 TO 3125 DEPENDING ON EIBCALEN.

Which does allow for future expansion.  The WS area on the other hand is
defined as an exact length with all the various fields being used.

Hmmmm - only a 20 year old standard?? Seems to be a tad longer than that if my
poor memory serves.

Eileen
```

```
On 23 Jul 2002 03:10:06 GMT, yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:

>>Yes, that is what I was referring to - Moveing/copying data from
>>DFHCOMMAREA to WORKING-STORAGE  is a "Bad Thing" (TM).  But, of
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, this (similar to reference modification) is safe in the sense
that the destructive overlay problem cannot occur.  But, it still is a
"Bad Thing" (TM) :)

I have seen this type of coding at many shops, yes, and it always
boiled down to

"We have been doing it this way forever"

And, when you go back in time, you will find one COBOL programmer who
set up a skeleton for a CICS program many years ago.  This programmer
never understood the Linkage section in the first place, and moved
everything "up" into the working-storage because that was way more
familiar.  I am not asking you to change the programs - But please,
give a good reason WHY this unnecessary move is done...

And don't even think "This cannot hurt".  It can :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DFHCOMMAREA

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-07-24T02:47:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020723224753.03304.00000420@mb-cp.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7mb%8.97429$88.1595090@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
>From: "Paul Raulerson" praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com 
>Date: 7/23/02 7:46 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>Well, good or bad, sometimes I do move the COMMAREA to WS because I want to
>redefine it, especially when I am using a single program
>that can receive several different COMMAREA definitions.
>I have not had anything particularly bad happen....  (yet! :)
>

Same here.  This is the major reason in our shop for moving the DHFCOMMAREA to
WS.  

As to Volker's remark about the one programmer creating a skeleton... I have
worked at a shop like that, drove me ever loving crazy as the skeleton was
abysmal but I was forced to use it.  Fortunately I didn't discover this shop
standard until I had converted about 150 programs from VOICE to CICS :)
```

- **References:** `<vvriju00g37bq8slba4sk0udlap5sfi4mu@4ax.com> <r64qju0sdbb6mgpe9296h98ascgl6vn3c1@4ax.com> <7mb%8.97429$88.1595090@twister.austin.rr.com> <esosjuce65pk2nk29lq6l25ufn1atvovc7@4ax.com> <ahlt7n$kv0$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> 
> My memory is, admittedly, porous... but aren't there dialects of COBOL
> which did not allow REDEFINES at the 01 level?

There shouldn't be any such restriction in any version that claims to be
Cobol, after all in FDs all 01 level implicitly REDEFINE each other. 
You are probably thinking of OCCURs which should never be applied to an
01 (or 77) level.
```

<a id="t-bc6ee8f79c"></a>
## Thread: A question about REPLACE statement — 2 msgs

### A question about REPLACE statement

- **From:** heyanchun@163.net (He Yanchun)
- **Date:** 2002-07-16T21:07:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96959ca4.0207162007.24f4f4e@posting.google.com>`

```
Can i use REPLACE statement to replace a KEY WORD in the ENVIRONMENT
DIVISION(Input-Output Section).

Such as 
           SELECT 2100-DATA-FILE ASSIGN TO AS2100
                  ORGANIZATION   IS SEQUENTIAL
                  ACCESS         IS SEQUENTIAL
                  FILE STATUS    IS W2100-SEQ-FILE-STATUS .

Can i change "ORGANIZATION   IS SEQUENTIAL" to "ORGANIZATION   IS LINE
SEQUENTIAL".


Best Regards,
He Yanchun
```

#### ↳ Re: A question about REPLACE statement

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T04:40:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D34F5F5.D8257B7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<96959ca4.0207162007.24f4f4e@posting.google.com>`

```


He Yanchun wrote:

> Can i use REPLACE statement to replace a KEY WORD in the ENVIRONMENT
> DIVISION(Input-Output Section).
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> He Yanchun

Yes you can - but YOU WILL GET INTO TROUBLE !!!!! You can't switch
*existing* file formats between ISAM, Relative, Sequential or Line
Sequential - it just wont work. You could play with the copy/replace
syntax to achieve what you want - but I don't believe it is worth the
effort.

You would be much better off with the following type of approach if you
want to be flexible in creating a set of file definitions :-

*>-------------- myISAM.cpy ------------------ ----------------

     select                 (tag)-File
     assign                 (tag)-Filename
     organization         indexed
     access                 dynamic
     record key           (tag)-PrimeKey
     file status              ws-fileStatus.

*>-------------------------------------------------------------

Assume you wanted the above ISAM for a CUSTOMER file :-

copy "myISAM.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==CUS==. (or ==Customer==)

Note that you can have the actual filename in Working Storage together
with the file-status. Under normal circumstances you really shouldn't need
more than one file-status field, as you are checking this for the
*current* file on which you are doing an access.

Do the same type of thing for Relative, Sequential and Line Sequential :-

     select                 (tag)-File
     assign                 (tag)-Filename
     organization           relative
     access                 dynamic
     relative key           ws-RelativeKey

Note : ws-RelativeKey is in Working Storage

     Select               (tag)-File
     assign               (tag)-filename
     organization      sequential
     access              sequential
     file status          ws-filestatus.

     Select               (tag)-File
     assign               (tag)-filename
     organization      line sequential
     access              sequential
     file status          ws-filestatus.

PS: where did you learn to code :-

          SELECT 2100-DATA-FILE ASSIGN TO AS2100

It's horrible and meaningless and an absolute nightmare for somebody who
has to do maintenance on your program at  02:00 hours ! Pick clear and
simple names "CustomerFile", "TransactionFile", "InventoryFile" ,
"CustomerISAM" etc.....

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

### Help Needed On Web Application using Fujitsu COBOL97 CGI-Subroutine

- **From:** calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin)
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T01:14:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<62843e96.0207170014.7c9b8ebf@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi there,

I'm quite new with this group. Wishing all members fine.

I have a set of Fujitsu COBOL development tool with me. I am in the
middle of choosing to use PowerCOBOL, COBOL97 or Web-developmebt using
COBOL97 CGI-subroutines.

Thus, I hope if anyone out there can give me a hand if you have
experience or using the Fujitsu COBOL CGI Subroutines on developing
the web based e-commerce systems.

FYI, I have tried the sample program came with Fujitsu COBOL, but not
able to make it works!

Any other comment is welcome.


Thank you.

Calvin Chin
Ipmuda Berhad
Malaysia
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Needed On Web Application using Fujitsu COBOL97 CGI-Subroutine

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T09:31:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0207170831.75d95bda@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0207170014.7c9b8ebf@posting.google.com>`

```
calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin) wrote in message news:
> FYI, I have tried the sample program came with Fujitsu COBOL, but not
> able to make it works!

Hi Calvin;
  You are not clear to your development goal. Is it a Web-site(CGI) or
a PC-app(PowerCobol-97). If your goal is WWW then you need CGI and you
MUST have a working WebServer (IIS or Apache), which is on your
Windows CD or Http://apache.org for free.
   If above Web-server is working then you probably have a Http-path
error and/or your sample01.htm is being cached to view in I.E. using
file-drive-path verses IIS-webserver-Http. Look in your Web-browser
address bar and make sure it starts with http:// .You need to add a
pre html file to verify your server paths are correct PRIOR to
invoking a more complicated process such as CGI calls.
   Create a "KISS" Simple.html page with only this line 
<p><a href="http://(your_server)/(alias_path)/sample01.htm">         
http://(your_server)/(alias_path_to c:*\cgismp01)/sample01.htm</a></p>

example <a  href="http://mikepc/z/sample01.htm"> 
http://mike/z/sample01.htm</a>
   Add @CBR_ATTACH_TOOL=test to your cobol85.cbr file IF YOU NEED to
start the COBOL-97 debugger.
   Start your IIS Personal Web Manager and in advanced give the
cgismp01.exe *alias_path  execute,read,write,Script Source Access
permissions.
   Start your Simple.html page, click the link in windows explorer OR
set Simple.html in <a href-link from your Personal-Web-Manager e.g.
http://mikepc (no .com etc)  If your IIS is set up properly you will
get Simple.html else your problem is your server setup and/or path.
 IF Simple.html is working from your IIS-link (e.g. http://mikepc)
then you can simplify sample01.htm to <form method="post"
action="cgismp01.exe">   (verses Http: path needed).
However I would suggest if you are not migrating Cobol code and your
goal is WWW-b2b  you set up a PHP-MySql-Apache PC workstation as there
are tons of resources on the WWW or maybe Fujitsu-Linux will include
better MySql-integration? Good Luck Mike
```

<a id="t-20b632e133"></a>
## Thread: IBM mainframes vs. new killer PCs — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM mainframes vs. new killer PCs

- **From:** tmartin9@caramail.com (Timothy Martin)
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T04:02:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf43e12d.0207170302.7142c8d5@posting.google.com>`

```
At least as far back as the early 1990s I have had 
the opinion that, as far as intensive I/O processing 
is concerned, there isn't any Intel type PC that can
compete with mainframe systems.  Yes, for calculations
PCs are ahead of mainframes, but as far as running 
simultaneously 10 programs, each of which is reading
in 2 or 3 100 meg files, and writing out 2 or 3 
100 meg files, mainframe machines are still the king
of the hill. 

But you know, lately I have been looking at the new 
leading edge Intel PCs, and it seems to me that Intel 
is finally starting to fill in the missing parts.
It's no longer just a question of a 2 giga-hertz CPU,
but also these new 1066mhz memory chips, and fast 
buses as well.  And about a year ago also I read that IBM
has made some breakthrough in hard disk platters, and
2 or 3 years from now desktop PCs will have 400 gig
hard disks.  Put 3 or 4 of these new 400 gig drives
into a RAID device, and it will be able to (maybe, I 
wonder) feed a lot of data to these 4 gigs of 1066mhz
memory chips. 

For a total cost much less than $20,000, I'd guess.   

Somewhere along the way, large corporations decided 
they liked their mainframes, because that way they 
could centralize all their data in one system that 
was backed up at least once a day.  When I was working
in New York 10 years ago, everybody knew that a copy
of all the data was shipped out of the city to "Iron
Mountain", or whatever.  The CEO of the company knew
that all the company's most important data would 
always be somewhere.  All of it, backed-up every night.

Only mainframes could do all the processing, and do it
in one room, that was in a secure building.  And then
even have all that data back-up and shipped out. 

I am no expert, but I am looking at these new PCs,
and wondering about them.  Maybe even in terms of 
heavy I/O processing they are starting to match 
mainframes ?  Maybe in a few years we will have Intel
PCs that can do everything a mainframe can do ?

Of course, there would still be the enormous cost of
porting stuff off of MVS, but then corporations 
would finally be able to forever break free of these
expensive leasing contracts with IBM.

I realize this has been discussed before, and even my
non-mainframe friends tell me, a mainframe programmer,
"you have nothing to worry about for the next 20 years",
but I am starting to wonder if maybe one should revise
this to "nothing to worry about for the next 10 years".
With a lot of "porting work" between now and then. 

These new Intel "chipsets" are going to make for killer
PCs I think, even in terms of heavy I/O processing.  I
wonder if us mainframe folks are going to have to revise
our opinions of PCs during the next few years. 
 
Sincerely, I am not looking for trouble with this subject,
but merely wondering if I am wrong, and/or what others
think.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframes vs. new killer PCs

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T16:55:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujbm1n924g9b78@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf43e12d.0207170302.7142c8d5@posting.google.com>`

```
Earlier today I found out something interesting. IBM has,
for years, been working with densely packed decimals (dpd).
This technology allows more efficient decimal floating-point.
The technology is already implemented in Java and other
languages; but IBM is moving it into hardware.

Hmmm! What might that mean?

dpd makes its way into the PowerPC for wide distribution.
The demand for accurate decimal calculations means that
all business applications will be executed using dpd as
the underlying technology. The technology runs faster where
there is hardware support. The hardware support is
provided only with IBM and other PowerPC platforms.
Everyone writing business applications migrates to these
systems to obtain the highest possible speed. Microsoft
loses a substantial part of their installed base to Apple.
Linux loses out to Linux PPC. Unix(tm) vendors move from
Intel to the PowerPC. Intel, losing all their hardware
platforms, files for bankruptcy!

Will 'killer decimal' decimate Intel? <G>

dpd has some bearing on ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD
in the draft COBOL standard. A 16-byte decimal double
supports decimal floating-point for 33 digits with an
exponent of three digits. See
< http://www.alphaworks.ibm.com/tech/decNumber >
for implementation in C. See
< http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal/decifaq.html >
for the Decimal Arithmetic FAQ. See
< http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal/DPDecimal.html >
for information on dpd.

Timothy Martin <tmartin9@caramail.com> wrote in message
news:bf43e12d.0207170302.7142c8d5@posting.google.com...
> At least as far back as the early 1990s I have had
> the opinion that, as far as intensive I/O processing
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> think.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes vs. new killer PCs

- **From:** "Joe Dzurinda" <jdzurinda@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T23:17:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EWmZ8.92770$%%2.3972683@news2.east.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<bf43e12d.0207170302.7142c8d5@posting.google.com> <ujbm1n924g9b78@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:ujbm1n924g9b78@corp.supernews.com...
> >
> > Somewhere along the way, large corporations decided
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

The mainframe is dying!   I've worked on it for 25 years and these are my
observations.     I always worked on new development up until 1996 when I
cashed in on the Millenium craze.     In 6 years I've seen little or no
development of new applications on the mainframe.   I see plenty of C++,
ORACLE, Powerbuilder and numerous others but nothing with COBOL.     About
once a month I hear of a place getting rid of the mainframe in favor of
client/server.    I wonder how much longer I have?   I would like to
continue for another 10 years but I don't think it's going to happen.    In
some ways I'm happy to see the mainframe go away.    One of the biggest
benefits I see is that pc's don't seem to be so much batch processing as a
mainframe.   What does that mean?   No phone calls at 2 am in the morning.
The trend is even more evident to me now living in FL then OH.   There is
only one other mainframe shop here and my money says they're looking at
something else.

Joe Dzurinda
Senior Analyst
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes vs. new killer PCs

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-18T03:49:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020717234922.09794.00000939@mb-fb.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<EWmZ8.92770$%%2.3972683@news2.east.cox.net>`

```
>From: "Joe Dzurinda" jdzurinda@hotmail.com 
>Date: 2002-07-17 19:17 Eastern Daylight Time

>The mainframe is dying!   I've worked on it for 25 years and these are my
>observations.     I always worked on new development up until 1996 when I
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>something else.

In number of customers, mainframes are probably dying.  But the mainframe
customers who remain are very large customers indeed.  
Very large users worry a lot about reliability.  When their systems fail, they
have to deal with very large lawsuits.  Mainframe systems tend to be
more reliable than C/S systems.  That is probably because of the lessons
learned during 30 years of disasters.  The C/S systems seem to me to
have learned nothing at all from the mainframe heritage of mistakes.  
Mainframes have good security.  Client Servers do not have good security. 
If a user wants to have both client-server / internet and good security, he
must be prepared to spend money well in excess of the cost of a high-end
mainframe.  

In terms of appearance, the mainframe is not dying--it is dead.  The rows
of tape drives featured in the movies have disappeared from most sites, and 
a modern mainframe is about the size of a deluxe microwave oven.  

In my opinion, there is not as much demand for Powerbuilder as I thought
there would be.  I think both Powerbuilder and C++ are dying, at about
the same glacial pace that the mainframe is dying.  I would not recommend
either one to a beginner.  I would recommend C or Java or COBOL.  

Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes vs. new killer PCs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T14:05:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2av8ha.jp.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<EWmZ8.92770$%%2.3972683@news2.east.cox.net> <20020717234922.09794.00000939@mb-fb.aol.com>`

```
klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte) wrote in
news:20020717234922.09794.00000939@mb-fb.aol.com: 

> In number of customers, mainframes are probably dying.  But the
> mainframe customers who remain are very large customers indeed.  

Same here in Germany. In case of fusions or overtakings data centers 
even grow. In terms of storage and cpu indeed, not allways in terms of
data center space.

> Very large users worry a lot about reliability.  When their systems
> fail, they have to deal with very large lawsuits.  Mainframe systems
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mistakes.  

One or two days outage probably means that the company is dying. And much
quicker than a mainframe ;-)

> Mainframes have good security.  Client Servers do not have
> good security. If a user wants to have both client-server / internet and
> good security, he must be prepared to spend money well in excess of the
> cost of a high-end mainframe.  

And: If you think of all the mixed environments, you will encounter problems
of both ends.

> In terms of appearance, the mainframe is not dying--it is dead.  The
> rows of tape drives featured in the movies have disappeared from most
> sites, and a modern mainframe is about the size of a deluxe microwave
> oven.  

Good picture. Although processes in mainframe environments tend to be
a bit slower than processes in the 'follower of fashion' environments, but
that alone should not be seen so negative. Mainframes even die longer than
any Wagner opera diva ;-)
 
> In my opinion, there is not as much demand for Powerbuilder as I thought
> there would be.  I think both Powerbuilder and C++ are dying, at about
> the same glacial pace that the mainframe is dying.  I would not
> recommend either one to a beginner.

Let's have a bet on who's dead first ;-) I work in an environment where I can
see both 'worlds' and have a walk between then. Ok, Powerbuilder applications
are cost effective and 'user-friendly' for all those mousenudgers out there 
(no offence indended ;), but there are more reasons for unavailability 
especially in hetergogenious environments. (Think of network mounts, IP-
Address Changes, etc, all that stuff that is handles by a single department 
in mainframe organizations)

> I would recommend C or Java or
> COBOL.  

Yeah. Our trainees still learn how to code COBOL, how to use IMS DB and DB2
before they can have a look at their appreciated and more fashionable C/S
stuff.

And this will last until 1 year before we read the mainframe's death notice
(including funeral date)

<JAKE>
Fix that lighter and light a candle!
</JAKE>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: parsing XML with Cobol and SAX/SAX2

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-18T16:23:20+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ah6pv8$thm$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<ah418k$v63$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <bfdfc3e8.0207172108.67b6b27@posting.google.com> <ah68uk$du7$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```
Hi Ken,

Currently, NetExpress from www.microfocus.com can work with external XML
parsers. There's a white paper on the Micro Focus we site that discusses
XML. Some time in the not too distant future, Net Express will be able to
deal with XML directly.

www.datadirect-technologies.com have an ODBC driver which can read and write
XML directly by mapping XML documents as if they are SQL database tables -
clever stuff.

The XML ODBC driver will work with Net Express and one for development is
supplied with Net Express I believe.

A white paper on the COBOL Portal here discusses, among other issues, XML
http://www.cobolportal.com/resources/articles/20010910_001.asp

Hope this helps,
Paul
```

```
those.  For the COBOL XML Class library we are looking at having a
full DOM as well as a simple parser/writer for just these kinds of
jobs.

About 18 months ago there was a FREE open source XML COBOL parser
mentioned on this group - send me an email to thane at
softwaresimple.com and I'll send you the code I downloaded at that
time ---  if it's not still available on the internet.

I think this is what it's become:

http://xml4cobol.com/
```

<a id="t-f814bba0a1"></a>
## Thread: ABSOLUTELY last chance to comment on next COBOL ANSI/ISO Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### ABSOLUTELY last chance to comment on next COBOL ANSI/ISO Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T13:38:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ah4djb$fqf$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The FDIS (Final Draft International Standard) document is available for
down-load if you check out the link (to a zipped copy) from:

 http://www.incits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

    ***

The following is the information sent to the J4 TAG concerning the
"processing" of this document (within the US - other countries may process
it differently).

NOTE WELL:
  A) the VERY short public review time
  B) the only two "acceptable" US positions "yes" or "no"

    ***

"ACTION REQUESTED
  Date:July 15,  2002
 Project:22-RI
 Reply to:Deborah J. Donovan
 Phone:202-626-5746
 Email:ddonovan@itic.org

    ...

Subject:Advance Notification of ISO/IEC FDIS 1989, Information technology -
Programming Languages - COBOL

The JTC 1 TAG Administrator received the subject FDIS on July 16, 2002. It
is distributed for ISO/IEC JTC 1 final letter ballot. The JTC 1 TAG requires
a recommendation from the U.S. TAG.

The Public Review and Comment period of ISO/IEC FDIS 1989 begins July 26,
2002 and ends August 9, 2002.

This Draft International Standard is being processed simultaneously as a
Draft International Standard and as an American National Standard.

However, as noted in section 5.8.4.3 of the "JTC 1 TAG/TD-1, Organization
and Procedures", updated September 12, 2001,

 "When the JTC 1 TAG Administrator receives an FDIS ballot, the
administrator contacts the appropriate TAG Administrator to get an update on
the TAG position on the final CD ballot. If the TAG Administrator determines
that no substantial differences exist between the final CD and the DIS , the
recommended JTC 1 TAG position on the final DIS ballot shall be the same as
the TAG position on the final CD ballot. The JTC 1 TAG Administrator will
then ballot the DIS."

ACTION #1 - Recommendation for JTC 1 TAG

The FDIS ballot began July 4, 2002 and ends September 4, 2002 (2-months
only). The committee should confirm that its position on the FDIS is the
same as it was for the FCD, or alternately, submit a proposed U.S. National
Body position.
The committee should conduct a 30-day letter ballot or roll call vote if
comments were accommodated at the FCD ballot resolution meeting, in
preparation for the FDIS ballot. The two-third voting approval is required.

The last position forwarded by INCITS/J4 for the FCD vote was to approve
with comments.

The position options are: approval of the technical content as presented or
disapprove for the technical reasons to be stated. Technical and editorial
comments will not be considered, as noted in section 9.7 of the 4th Edition
of the JTC 1 Directives.

IMPORTANT
The notification should be received no later than July 30, 2002 so that, if
necessary, a 10-day reconsideration ballot may be conducted and the U.S.
vote forwarded to ANSI by the JTC 1 closing date of Septmber 4, 2002.

ACTION #2 - Notification to INCITS

The committee should notify the INCITS Secretariat if this standard is
unsuitable for adoption as an American National Standard. A vote of majority
approval accompanied by comments specifying the reasons for non-adoption is
required. The initiation for adoption will begin at the FDAM stage. The
recommendation for non-adoption should be sent simultaneously with the
recommendation for the JTC 1 TAG. INCITS will adopt the ISO/IEC Standard
unless notified, see INCITS Adoption Policy."
```

### Next COBOL Standard

```
FYI,
  As I read the current FDIS zipped file (available from the INCITS web
page), it contains the following:

"The technical content of this document is identical to ISO/IEC FDIS 1989,
Information technology - Programming languages - COBOL

ITI/INCITS claim a copyright interest in the COBOL standard to the extent
that these organizations have verified that the COBOL standard has met the
standard setting requirements of these organizations. However, ITI/INCITS
claims no copyright in the underlying technical specifications of the COBOL
standard and, to the extent that consent is required, INCITS freely consents
to use of the underlying technical specification by any person without
charge. No person reproducing, improving, or using the standard shall claim
that any modifications or improvements have been adopted as part of any
"official" COBOL standards and/or the standard setting processes of
ANSI/ITI/INCITS."

NOTE WELL:
  As I read the "body" of the document, the (traditional) Acknowledgement
*HAS BEEN REMOVED* and every page of the document contains the following
words:

 "� ISO/IEC 2002 - All rights reserved"
```

#### ↳ Re: Next COBOL Standard

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T22:27:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D35F051.324EAFDA@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ah4ob2$k6q$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`


## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 ... install problems on WIN 2000 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 ... install problems on WIN 2000

- **From:** "BPR2" <news1@augursw.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-17T22:29:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WcmZ8.29092$WsS.20707@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Has anybody installed the Fujitsu COBOL V4.0 (included with Mastering COBOL
by Carol Baroudi) on Windows 2000?

I'm sure that it is possible, but will probably take me quite a while to
figure out how.  If anyone has already done this, I could sure use some tips
to shorten the puzzle solving time.

I am using WIN 2000 SP 2, all current fixes installed.  System already has
Borland C++ Builder installed and working properly.

I just installed the Application Compatibility Tool V1.5 (V 2.0 says it
requires WIN 2K SP3 ... not released yet).  There a lot of possible
'patches' that I can choose from in order to 'fool' InstallShield into
thinking that it is getting the correct version info returned from Windows
during the install process.

Unfortunately none of these appear to be a good match for a level check of
SP3 on NT 4 which is what the error message implies that InstallShield is
looking for.

I would like to know more precisely what Data Item(s) that this Install
process is actually inspecting and what it expects to be returned.

Or, if you have already completed this install ... can you reply with a the
'magic' incantations required?

The install 'problem' symptom is a message from InstallShield saying that
Windows NT Service Pack 3 must be installed before attempting to install
Fujitsu COBOL V4.0.

TIA!
```

#### ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T01:46:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1cKZ8.150626$uk2.57271670@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net>`

```

"Steve Ely" <sbely@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net...
> I have a numeric value in a pic 9(5).
> I need to get the hex value of of that number
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an easy way to do this?

Are you trying to display the hex value of 13000 as 32C8? If that's the
case, you need to put it in a PIC X(4), not a PIC X(2). There's probably a
function to do this, but I don't know what it is. You could write a module
to make sure the input is 65535 or below. Then divide by 4096, then divide
the remainder by 256, then divide the remainder by 16. These values, and the
final remainder, will all be values from 0 thru 15. Convert 10 - 15 to A -
F, then assemble using reference modification. (If the output is to be
signed, make sure the input is 32767 or below.)

If, instead, you want 13000 to be converted interally to hex, just move it
to a PIC 9(5) COMP. However, I believe this is compiler dependant. On a
mainframe, this will actually only take up 2 storage bytes, even though it
is defined as (5). BINARY (in stead of COMP) may work for all compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T10:43:38-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D38259A.6080102@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net> <1cKZ8.150626$uk2.57271670@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3d377fc0.4586310@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net> <UgTZ8.277$Fb6.181@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> Sheesh.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> (NOTE: THIS *IS* COMPILER DEPENDENT!!)

Based on the "x(2)" target field, seems to me that this is the correct 
solution, except the target field needs to be defined as S9(5) Binary 
[Sync]. On an IBM mainframe the value must be in the range -32768 thru 
+32767, i.e., x'8000' thru x'7fff'. If the field isn't signed, you can 
go as high at 64k-1.

The advantage of the Sync operand is that the compiler will boundary 
align the field and generate LH/STH instructions.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T13:16:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EiUZ8.173371$Bt1.9364663@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net>`

```

"Steve Ely" <sbely@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net...
> I have a numeric value in a pic 9(5).
> I need to get the hex value of of that number
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an easy way to do this?

Assuming an Intel platform:

01  MYHEX-GROUP.
  02  FILLER            PIC X(2).
  02  MYHEX-NUMBER  PIC X(2).
01  MYRAW-NUMBER REDEFINES MYHEX-GROUP PIC S9(7) COMP-4.

MOVE 13000 TO MYRAW-NUMBER
   (MYHEX-GROUP now contains 00 00 32 68)
MOVE MYHEX-NUMBER TO anywhere-you-want as a 2-byte field.

Note the COMP-4 definition above may be compiler-dependent. You want a
representation that is straight binary.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T06:19:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0207190519.75c80375@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net>`

```
sbely@swbell.net (Steve Ely) wrote in message news:<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net>...
> I have a numeric value in a pic 9(5).
> I need to get the hex value of of that number
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an easy way to do this?

It's easy but it depends on the compiler you're using.

01  mynumeric pic 99999 value 13000.
01  myinteger pic 9(4) comp.
01  myhex redefines myinteger pic xx.


compute myinteger = mynumeric.
or 
add 0 mynumeric giving myinteger
or
move mynumeric to myinteger.

that should do it unless the compiler byte flips the integer, then
you'll have to find how to flip them back. The largest 5 digit number
you can use is 64KB.

some compilers may have to be forced to NOT truncate at 4 digits.
There's probably a NOTRUNC option or something.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T17:08:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D383965.D5EBA638@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net>`

```
Steve Ely wrote:
> 
> I have a numeric value in a pic 9(5).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an easy way to do this?

Divide by 16 taking the remainder, use the remainder+1 as an index into
a table with '0123456789ABCDEF'. Each divide give the next low-order
digit so decrement the output index.

eg:

        PERFORM 
            VARYING Hex-Digit FROM 4 BY -1
              UNTIL Hex-Digit < 1

            DIVIDE Numeric-Value BY 16
                      GIVING Numeric-Value
                   REMAINDER Digit-Index
            ADD 1           TO Digit-Index
            Move Hex-Char(Digit-Index) TO Hex-Value(Hex-Digit)
         END-PERFORM

or similar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

```
the high order digits first.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T13:24:38-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujgie9io40il1c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3D383965.D5EBA638@Azonic.co.nz> <20020719055704.00538.00000196@mb-fh.aol.com>`

```

Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020719055704.00538.00000196@mb-fh.aol.com...
> >From: Richard Plinston riplin@Azonic.co.nz
> >Date: 2002-07-19 12:08 Eastern Daylight Time
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> the high order digits first.

This may be made more flexible with a few changes.

Define the maximum possible unsigned integer as input,
an alphanumeric capable of holding the binary character
representation as output, and a radix value for control.

1 numeric-value comp pic 9(18). *> assumes 64-bits
1 alpha-value pic x(64).
1 radix comp pic 99.

1 digit-index comp pic 99.
1 digit-value comp pic 99.
1 char-value pic x(16) value "0123456789ABCDEF".

convert-number-to-character.
    move all "0" to alpha-value
    perform varying digit-index from 1 by 1
      until digit-index > 64
            or numeric-value = 0
        divide numeric-value by radix
          giving numeric-value
          remainder digit-value
        add 1 to digit value
        move char-value (digit-value:1)
          to alpha-value (digit-index:1)
    end-perform.

The result, in alpha-value, is reversed from normal representation,
so the user reverses the part needed.

    move 13000 to numeric-value
    move 16 to radix
    perform convert-number-to-character
    move function reverse (alpha-value (1:4))
      to my-hex-value

my-hex-value has a value of "32C8".

Radix values of more than 16 may be used by extending
char-value to include additional values. Including the
remaining capital letters allows for radix 36.

Furthermore, adding formating routines that use alpha-value
as input allows more flexibility for display. For example,
a routine to format hexidecimal by inserting spaces after
pairs from alpha-value provides a familiar 'dump' pattern.

1 formatted-hex-display pic x(24).
1 format-index comp pic 99.

format-hex.
    move 1 to format-index
    perform varying digit-index from 1 by 2
      until digit-index > 16
        move alpha-value (digit-index:2)
          to formatted-hex-display (format-index:3)
        add 3 to format-index
    end-perform.

Then, after the perform of convert-number-to-character;

    perform format-hex
    move function reverse (formatted-hex-display(1:11))
        to my-hex-display

shows "00 00 32 C8".

Some formatting problems may be solved more easily by
reversing the values before formatting. Translation of
internal numeric forms to external forms is only one of
the problems where this seems to work well.

Note: The above code snippets were not tested.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T22:55:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c22f77$58f8a8a0$8ac2f943@chottel>`

```
Here is some code I found on the IBM newsgroup:

> > Is there a way to convert hex to dec and dec to hex in Cobol,
> > like X2D() and D2X() in Rexx ? Thanks from Mark Leung
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>            PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > 4

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in article
<ujgie9io40il1c@corp.supernews.com>...
> 
> Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >Divide by 16 taking the remainder, use the remainder+1 as an index
into
> > >a table with '0123456789ABCDEF'. Each divide give the next low-order
> > >digit so decrement the output index.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

"backwards."
> > I'm afraid I would have thoughtlessly started hammering away at
> > the high order digits first.
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How to covert Decimal values to hex

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T13:31:21-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahc6co$bot$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net>`

```
Here's a solution for an IBM Mainframe that will convert/display a numeric
value up to 2147483647 (X'7FFFFFFF') as a 2-8 byte EBCDIC value. The COBOL
Compile option TRUNC(BIN) should be specified to avoid high-order truncation
of binary data.

03  WS-PACKED PIC 9(08)V9 PACKED-DECIMAL.
03  FILLER REDEFINES WS-PACKED.
      05  WS-FWORD PIC 9(09) BINARY.
      05  FILLER PIC X(01).
03  WS-DISPLAY PIC 9(08)V9.
03  WS-DISPLAY-X REDEFINES WS-DISPLAY
                               PIC X(09).
03  WS-NUMERIC PIC 9(05) VALUE 13000.

MOVE ZERO TO WS-PACKED.
MOVE WS-NUMERIC TO WS-FWORD.
* WS-PACKED EQUALS X'000032C80F'
MOVE WS-PACKED TO WS-DISPLAY.
* UNPACKS AS X'F0F0F0F0F3F2FCF8F0'

INSPECT WS-DISPLAY-X CONVERTING
X'FAFBFCFDFEFF' TO 'ABCDEF'.

At this point, WS-DISPLAY-X (5:4) is ===> '32C8'

Hope this works in your environment.

Bill

"Steve Ely" <sbely@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:3d3763c5.86739982@news.dallas.sbcglobal.net...
> I have a numeric value in a pic 9(5).
> I need to get the hex value of of that number
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an easy way to do this?
```
