## Thread: SCREEN-SECTION in Fujitsu Cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### SCREEN-SECTION in Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** prat85635@yahoo.com (prat)
- **Date:** 2002-07-18T18:15:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<246cc779.0207181715.36967466@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,

I need some help on few things

1. I have a field in the screen section of my program using Fujitsu
Cobol 6.0 enterprise edition.

This is field is used to transmit all kinds of messages. In the old
UNISYS code some special character is being used to HIGHLIGHT the
error messages and transmit the other messages without doing anything.

so when some error occurs then the error message is highlighted and
displayed on the screen.

I would like to know how can i simulate the same thing in my program.

2.In the screen-section how can i move from a particular field to
another field without using the row and column position.

suppose there are 10 fields in the screen and the cursor is currently
at position 3 and if there is an error the cursor should be positioned
at position seven. so i would like to know how can i move the cursor
back and forth.

3. How can i use OCCURS clause in my screen-section.

Any help in this regard is appreciated.

Thanks
Prat
```

#### ↳ Re: SCREEN-SECTION in Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T05:03:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D379C08.5899B7A1@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<246cc779.0207181715.36967466@posting.google.com>`

```


prat wrote:

> Hi all,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Prat

First off - check the adtools site to see if there are any Fujitsu
examples for using Screen Section. I use Micro Focus, but I *think* the
F/J and M/F approaches are fairly similar. Firstly some pseudo code to
achieve what I *think* you want to do.

Assume you have a screen :-

01 Scrn-CustomerRecord.
    05 Scrn-CustomerAccountNo line x col x pic ...
    05 Scrn-CustomerName line x col x pic
    05 Scrn-City....
    05 Scrn-AccountBalance.....

I'm vague on the syntax as it's a long time since I used Screen Section.

Pseudo :-

    Set RecordNotFound to true     (Level 88s)
    Set Record NotChanged to true (Level 88s)
    Display empty CustomerScreen
    accept Scrn-CustomerNo

    if  CustomerNo is blank - end of routine (user doesn't want to enter
info)
    if not blank Read CustomerFile
       if RecordFound
        set RecordFound to true
        display fields
          now go to 'Change' routine
       else set RecordChanged to true
            start accepting individual fields, incrementing through your
field numbers -
                accept scrn-name, accept scrn-city, accept scrn-balance.
                having accepted all input fields for new record now go to
'Change'routine

    Accept Scrn-CustomerName - if blank you have an error
    use the bottom Line (24) to display your error message with BLINK,
        HIGHLIGHT, REVERSE (???)
    now go back to Accept Scrn-CustomerName

    if that one is OK now accept scrn-Nextfield2 etc

    when you have finished new record input you go to 'Change' routine

You may or may not have CBL_MOUSE routines - if you do it makes life
easier.
If you haven't MOUSE routines use Line 24 to display

'Enter field Number to change :-   ..  Press <Enter> to finish"

To get Field Numbers part of your initial display has to contain 'labelss'
before each field, such as :-

    .... Account Number -------
    (1) Name ------------------
    (2) City--------------------
    (3) etc----------------------.
    (4) Account Balance -----------

How do you position - Note I have NO FIELD NUMBER for Account Number - we
don't want the user changing this, (they can enter an AccountNumber BUT
they can't change it !), so when you accept a FieldNumber, something like
following :-

    AcceptFieldNumber
    evaluate true
        when FieldNumber = zero - end of routine
        when FieldNumber = 1  perform ACCEPT-NAME (accept scrn-name)
        when FieldNumber = 2  perform ACCEPT-CITY
        when FieldNumber = 3 etc....
    End-evaluate

Each time you change a field set RecordChanged to true. User keys in say
'4' and you then go to routine to Accept Scrn-AccountBalance. When user
enters field number zero - they are at the end. Now display another
message line with

                " (O)K or (C)ancel"

    if OK ( 88 OK value "O', 'o")

        if RecordChanged

            if RecordFound
               perform REWRITE-record

                else perform WRITE-new-record
            End-if
        End-if

   else  display Empty Screen
End-if

Now the occurs bit : This is an extract from a Micro Focus program - but
I'm guessing you will find the Fujitsu syntax fairly close on 'occurs' :-

 screen section.
 01 g-admouse.
   02 background-color 7 foreground-color 1.
    03 blank screen.
    03 line 2 col 15 value "USING THE MOUSE POINTER TO MOVE AROUND
-                          " FIELDS" background-color 3 underline.
    03 line 4 col 8  value "Move the mouse to the field in which y
-                          "ou wish to enter data, then".
    03 line 5 col 8  value "press the left hand  button  on the mo
-                          "use to move the text cursor".
    03 line 6 col 8  value "to the selected field.  Press 'Enter'
-                          "key to terminate.".
    03 line 8 col 4 value "SALES FIGURES" foreground-color 4
                                          underline.
   02 background-color 7 foreground-color 6.
    03 line 10 col 13 value "  JAN   FEB   MAR   APR   MAY   JUN
-" JUL   AUG   SEP   OCT".
    03 line 11 col 13 value "ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½
-"ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½Ä¿".
    03 occurs 6.
     05 line + 1 col 13 value "ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½
-" ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½     ï¿½".
     05 line + 1 col 13  value "ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½
-"ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½Ä´".
    03 line 23 col 13 value "ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½
-"ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½".
    03 line 12 col 4 value "Jam".
    03 line + 2 col 4 value "Honey".
    03 line + 2 col 4 value "Sugar".
    03 line + 2 col 4 value "Bread".
    03 line + 2 col 4 value "Flour".
    03 line + 2 col 4 value "Butter".
    03 line 12 col 15.
   02 background-color 7 foreground-color 0.
     04 occurs 6.
      05 occurs 10.
       06 pic 999 using ws-item.
       06 col + 4.
      05 line + 2 col - 60.

Not too clear on what you meant by cursor positions 3 and 7. Assuming you
accept
scrn-city and it's at start position 3, if you have an error, you aren't
concerned where the cursor finishes but after displaying your error
message go to accept scrn-city again which automatically puts you back at
the start position 3.

HTH

If any Fujitsu users can elaborate where the F/J syntax is different to
above - please do so.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !!

- **From:** Harald Hinz <harald.hinz@ewetel.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T12:48:46+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eqq8ha.jp.ln@news.hinznet.home>`
- **References:** `<%8OZ8.7574$JN4.1251413@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
"Jagannath Nori" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote in
news:%8OZ8.7574$JN4.1251413@news02.tsnz.net: 

> I am writing a  cobol/db2 pgm which has a  curosr in it. I have declared
> the cursor in the working storage and have included the DCLGEN variables
> before the declare. 

AFAIK you should declare the cursor within PROCEDURE DIVISION. Our company's
standards even point out that the declaration of cursor should be the first
part AFTER the PROCEDURE DIVISION statements. But if your DB2 precompiler
has no problems with CURSOR within WS, it should also be okay. Maybe just 
worth a try...

> I am passing values into the variables which are
> used as host variables in the declare cursor pgm before I fetch it. 

No errors or warnings in compile list? Maybe incompatible field types?
If you have XPEDITER available, you could test it interactively.

> I am
> not able to fetch it as always it is returning SQL +100, though I am
> feeding in values for which there should be a definite result set , any
> ideas ??? 

Hm, the more I think about it, the less ideas.

- you are not pointing to the correct database
- database is temporarily not availabe 
  (inactive... hm that should result in 04E...)
- you did not code an OPEN CURSOR before FETCH

Best guess:

- declare host variables in WS
- declare cursor in PD
- check sql codes of DECLARE, OPEN and FETCH (in this sequence)

Questions:

Is the environment pure DB2 or DB2 and IMS?
Any ON SQLERROR statements included in compile list?
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T12:06:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UgTZ8.278$Fb6.116@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<%8OZ8.7574$JN4.1251413@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
The fact the the SQLcode is NO_DATA tells you the query is being executed
'as requested.'  If something else was wrong, you'd get SQL_ERROR (-1).

As someone else said, see if you can run your query through SPUFI or QMF or
something; then check your 'manufactured' SQL statement to see if it
matches.

Hmm, I see this...

>I am passing values into the variables which are used as host variables in
the declare cursor pgm before I fetch it.

You are not trying to use a CURSOR to vary the parameters of the SELECT, are
you? It doesn't work that way. The parameters for the query and associated
result set are fixed when you open the cursor.If you are trying to use
parameters, you need to look at using PREPARE.
```

```
Hi Jag,

You may already know this, but it should be said. Make sure your
variables have been set before the CURSOR is OPENed. Once the CURSOR
is opened the "sequential file" that will be "read" by the FETCHes has
already been "created"
(or not :-)).

HTH, Jack.

…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !!

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-07-21T06:01:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020721020129.16551.00000082@mb-fh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<EFVZ8.6531$H67.34638@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`

```
Hi Jag,

It helps when the problem is fully explained. To rephrase MCM's reply about
performance and multiple opens: @#$*&*YES*.

I was involved w/a pgm that did 80K open/closes for a flat file. The job ran
3hrs+. It was rewritten to eliminate them; it ran in 10 mins.

Your situation is even worse: you create a temp table for every o/c you
perform. If you're dealing w/100 or 1000 recs you may be able to explain it
away without too much embarrassment.  

Jack
```

<a id="t-7e3506eb5c"></a>
## Thread: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing? — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T09:19:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ah93km$rht$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

All righty... it must be something simple but I'm missing it.  Got a 
jobstream that in its first step disables an online transaction to lock 
out the users; I've grovelled for assistance on that one already.  The job 
runs and as a last step enables the transaction so the users can do their 
using.

Obviously the first step executes always... or, well, at least most of the 
time as Nothing in Life is Always (including this statement).  The 
difficulty comes when something blows up; the enabling step must always 
execute... or as much of 'always' as the first step executes.

Right now it coded as:

//PS080     EXEC  PGM=MVSCMD,PARM='OCITOPE,030',COND=EVEN

... and will execute EVEN when a preceding step ABENDS.  The other evening 
I was testing a mod and I typo'd an override statement for a STEPLIB; this 
resulted in an error of STEPLIB - DATA SET NOT 	FOUND in PS070... and 
everything else just flushed right out.

I've tested using COND=ONLY and that flushes out, as well; in slogging 
through the manuals at

<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/DOCNUM/GC28-1757/CCONTENTS>

... and ...

<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IEA1B504/CCONTENTS?DT=19980112101805>

... I haven't been able to find a solution.

Might someone be able to point me in an appropriate direction?

Thanks much.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T13:43:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ah9mjp$dv5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ah93km$rht$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
It might be a "kludge" (sp?)  - but what would happen if the same step that
"stops" the transaction also wrote out JCL (to the internal reader) for a
job with the same name as this one - but that "turns on" the users.  The OS
will NOT let the other job "start" while an identically named job is
running - but it will start some time (hopefully SOON) after the first job
ends - no matter HOW it ends.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T13:19:27-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0207191219.5a3e406e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ah93km$rht$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I have never done it myself, but I believe that you can submit JCL
from a JCL job by writing to the ddname INTRDR or something like that.
 You could do this as the second step of the job and make the
submitted job depend upon the completion of the first job, which I
believe can be done by parameters on the job statement of the second
job.  The second job could then do the task that you were trying to do
with the last step of your original job.

Robert

docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<ah93km$rht$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> All righty... it must be something simple but I'm missing it.  Got a 
> jobstream that in its first step disables an online transaction to lock 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T17:09:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0207191609.7c265daf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ah93km$rht$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Further to my earlier response, you could use a step such as the
following to submit a job from within a job

//STEP2     EXEC PGM=IEBGENER
//SYSOUT    DD SYSOUT=X
//SYSIN     DD DUMMY
//SYSUT1    DD DATA
JCL GOES HERE (EITHER DON'T USE ANY /* IN IT OR SPECIFY THE
TERMINATING DELIMITER WITH A DLM PARAMETER ON THE DD STATEMENT)
/*
//SYSUT2    DD SYSOUT=(,INTRDR)
  
with regard to Bill Klein's response regarding deferral of execution
until the original job has completed, I agree, except that I am not
certain that using the same job name will work on all systems.  My JCL
manual says to contact your system programmers to find out how to do
it.  It also used to be the case that jobs with the same class on the
job statement could only execute one at a time, but that may be
installation dependent.

Robert

> jobstream that in its first step disables an online transaction to lock 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T12:14:45+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c4dijuo4j4d2u7rvcu8np62a2ia0vfbfsb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ah93km$rht$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc

 //MYJOB JOB ......
 //S1 EXEC PGM=IEBGENER
 //SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
 //SYSIN DD DUMMY
 //DD1    DD DISP=(NEW,CATLG),DSN=SOME.CONVENIE.NTFILE,
 //          UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(TRK,(0))
 //SYSUT1 DD DATA,DLM='><'
 //MYJOB JOB ....  notice identical job name, but that isn't enough
 //S1 EXEC PGM=MVSCMD,PARM='OCITOPE,030'
 //DD1 DD DISP=(OLD,DELETE),DSN=SOME.CONVENIE.NTFILE  notice DISP=OLD
 //SYSUT2 DD SYSOUT=(A,INTRDR)
 ><
 //S2  EXEC PGM=disable_transaction (sounds familiar)
 //DD1 DD  DISP=OLD,DSN=*.S1.DD1
 //S3  EXEC whatsoever
 //DD1 DD  DISP=OLD,DSN=*.S1.DD1
 //

S1 would submit a job with the same jobname.  In most, but not all,
systems this second job would now wait

To make the whole thing bullet-proof, S1 allocates a file and gets an
exclusive lock.  All other steps in the job get the same exclusive
lock to the same file.  Even if the job submitted by s1 gets started,
it will be "waiting for datasets" until the original job ends

If MYJOB fails for any reason, no matter, the submitted job will run
and enable the transaction again

Note:  The jobnames don't even have to be identical in this construct,
but they may be identical

HTH

      
      
      
      
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

```

I leave this quoted above for others who will be able to use it... but I, 
alas, will not.  It is along the same lines at the solution recommended by 
Mr Klein and another (whose name escapes me now, on a Saturday morn, 
before my second cup o' mud) and uses the SUB to the INTDRD... this was 
shown to He Who Signs My Timesheets and he responded 'Hell, *I* can barely 
understand this... you think Ops will?  All we gotta do is set up a second 
job, a single-step OPEN, and schedule it through CA7 to run after the main 
job... nothing fancy.'

I am usually *very* wary about any solution that is suggested that begins 
with 'All ya gotta do is...' ... but in this case it seems the only 
drawback is the paperwork necessary in getting another job into Prod.

Thanks much!

DD

```
Hi Doc,

This won't solve your problem (the separate job does that) but may soothe your
confusion.

The COND=ONLY didn't work because "dataset not found" is not an abend, but a
JCL error. For the same reason, the abend DISPs in the failing step do not take
effect at termination.

Regards, Jack.
```

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>

```
Technically, there is no step termination because there is no step
initiation.  I believe that if any non-temporary data sets are allocated
in the JCL for the step that they will not be allocated  because of the
DATA SET NOT FOUND problem.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] JCL - Execute When Flushing?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T02:54:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020722225402.25566.00000204@mb-cd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ahe9u8$kfp$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I'm wandering in kinda late in this discussion but a few things caught my eye.

A second job with the same name probably won't execute while the first is
running, however I don't think CA7 will accept a 2nd job with the same name if
it isn't submitted via the internal reader via the first job.  

I'm no CA7 expert by a long shot (and hubby is sound asleep or I'd ask him) but
you also have to contend with dependencies and other nonsense when putting jobs
into CA7 and I don't see how a job would be dependent on itself (ie same name).

Continuing on with further thought...

In my shop we close the on-line files instead of diabling the transaction. 
Programs have checks for closed file that give users messages that they system
is down for maintenance.  Since the schedules are pretty much set some even
have time checks in them letting the user know when the file should be
available again.

Also in some batch programs if something goes wrong we have IF statements in
the JCL that will cause steps to either execute or not depending on return
codes of other steps.

Anyway - that's my .00002 cents.

Eileen
```

>
>In my shop we close the on-line files instead of diabling the transaction. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>available again.

Interesting approach but... it Won't Work Here.  (How many times has 
*that* been heard before?)  This shop's scheduling is haphazard, to say 
the least; so much can (and will) go wrong at any time that the job I'm 
running uses batch SQL to UPDATE some dummy rows in a Prod table so it can 
be sure to get all the transactions since it has last run.

That being said... the easiest way to keep most of the users out of the 
system (the reason for disabling a transaction... the system's Main Menu, 
actually) is by disabling the main menu.  This isn't foolproof, no, as 
there's always the risk that a user will invoke a sub-menu bu TranID 
directly... but what's Life without a bit of Risk?

Thanks much!

DD
```

>That being said... the easiest way to keep most of the users out of the 
>system (the reason for disabling a transaction... the system's Main Menu, 
>actually) is by disabling the main menu.  This isn't foolproof, no, as 
>there's always the risk that a user will invoke a sub-menu bu TranID 
>directly... but what's Life without a bit of Risk?

Ah yes - the main menu/sub menu debachle.  We have the time/file checks in both
programs, and in the actual processing programs as well as the user can be in
one of those at the time the file is closed.  There are also checks that the
user has come through a menu instead of directly by the sub menu or processing
programs transactions as well. 

While we can't totally monkey proof our systems we give it the ol' college try!

I checked with hubby dearest and he said that it may be possible to put more
than one job into CA7 with the same name but they would have to have differenct
SCHID's for sure but that would be sort of circumventing the system and will
probably cause major migrains.  He believes 'he who signs your timesheet'
should  .... (I can't say in polite company).

Eileen

## Thread: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !! — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-19T23:37:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5p1_8.2765$927.1293@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
(Answered via email and posted back to newsgroup where it belongs).

-----
From: "Jagannath Nori" <jnori@bigfoot.com>
To: <michael.mattias@gte.net>
Sent: Friday, July 19, 2002 2:08 PM
Subject: Re: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !!

Hi,

I am writing a  pgm which has 2 cursors. The first cursor's select retrieves
values which will be used as input values(hostvariables in the select
statement in the where condition)  for the IInd cursor. Is it OK if I  keep
opening and closing the second cursor for each fetch of the first cursor  so
that this way I assure that there are always values in the second cursor
before it is opened ?? By doing like this will  the performance get effected
??

I have aalready queried thru SPUFI and the  result comes ok so the problem
in processing the IInd cursor !!

============
RESPONSE
============

Yeah, performance will likely be adversely affected.

Assuming I understand your challenge correctly....

" Execute a query. The result set of this query is one or more rows which
provide parameters for a second query,



which returns exactly zero or one row"

If so, I would create the cursor for the first query, and PREPARE the
statement for the second query. I would not use a second cursor for the
second query.

But if .. the second query can return more than one row, you should rewrite
your SQL so the second query is a subquery of the first query. Come to think
of it, you could rewrite the SQL anyway to get at the second query's result
set using one OPEN of one cursor regardless of the number of rows returned
by either query. (And having thought about it, I would not bother with
PREPARE and EXECUTE).

If you need help doing this, please POST (do not email to me) the two SQL
statements you are using. (A little text explantion or program code would be
helpful, too). Someone here will surely be able to combine those two SQL
statements into one. (Also, I'm not really good at this myself; I just know
it can be done).

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol/Db2 Cursor !!

- **From:** "Jagannath Nori" <jnori@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-21T06:29:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o_h_8.7872$JN4.1322371@news02.tsnz.net>`
- **References:** `<5p1_8.2765$927.1293@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
Michael,

Sorry to have mailed u !! I clicked the wrong button.

The SQL cursors am working on both the queries have possibilities of
returning more than 1 row so have to code cursors for both.

Ist SQL : select  trn_date, st_nbr, reg_nbr, trn_nbr, amt, stat_ind
               from st_data
               where stat_ind in ('a' , 'b')

For each fetch of this cursor I have to doa  query on the IInd SQL and
update the STAT_IND to proper value at the end of compare . I have to keep
doing it till the Ist cursor hits +100.

IInd SQL

              sel vch_nbr,  id_nbr
              from vch_data
              where trn_date = :st_data.trn-date
              and     st_nbr   = :st_data.st_nbr

So I don't see how I can put this in a  sub query . I have not used PREPARE
and EXECUTE as well.  Does this info give  a  idea to u ??


Thankx
Jag

## Thread: Cobol web language? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Cobol web language?

- **From:** irgendein@gmx.net (Roger Schmid)
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T10:59:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahbfqq$s1p$1@rex.ip-plus.net>`

```
Hy,

Just for fun and to learn more about cobol:

Is there a way to run cobol together with apache on a linux box?
Actually PHP does the work, so it's might possible to replace PHP with
cobol. I have found cobolscript, but IMHO you have to pay for that. 

May I haven't seen any hints in your faq.

   thanks,
    Roger
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol web language?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-21T08:22:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D3A6150.1102FB4F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ahbfqq$s1p$1@rex.ip-plus.net>`

```
Roger Schmid wrote:
> 
> Is there a way to run cobol together with apache on a linux box?
> Actually PHP does the work, so it's might possible to replace PHP with
> cobol. I have found cobolscript, but IMHO you have to pay for that.

All that is required for CGI is that the program be able to read from
stdin, write to stdout and read environment variables.  Many Cobols can
do this.  There are some callable routines that will hide the mechanism
involved but these are not necessary as it should be possible with
ACCEPT and DISPLAY.

I have Cobol code that will work with several web servers on various
OSes, including Linux/Apache.

Go to Google comp.lang.cobol in thread "How I can get the request query
string?".

However this is for MF and there may be some differences required for
other compilers.

CobolScript is not quite Cobol but similar.  It does do CGI easily and
is only $50.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol web language?

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T12:04:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahgscq$5hg$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<ahbfqq$s1p$1@rex.ip-plus.net>`

```
Micro Focus COBOL can do this.

The products are Net Express on Windows, Object COBOL Developer Suite on
Linux and Server Express on the Unix platforms.

See www.microfocus.com

## Thread: Here's a cute one... — 10 msgs

### Here's a cute one...

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T19:13:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Under Windows XP. 

I wrote a quick and dirty little program this morning to convert a SEQUENTIAL file to a LINE SEQUENTIAL file so that it would be trivial to upload to the mainframe (while still doing an ASCII->EDCIDIC conversion via FTP :) 

The program will NOT run under Visual Age COBOL! It consistently returns a File Status error of 39 (incompatible file). 

For the heck of it, I compiled the same source code with Microfocus NetExpress and under AcuCOBOL. 
Both of them worked, but even though the output file compares exactly using SUPERC from SPF, the files are more than 2 megabytes different in size!  Duh...

Is this just some weird Windows thing going on, or am I really loosing my mind? Opinions on the first speculation welcome... <grin> 

The input file is just a stream of data written in 416 byte records, nothing unusual about it that I know of. 
Code and data sample included below for your amusement. ;)  The filenames in the code are hardcoded because this is just a one time program... 

-Paul

--------

      **********************************************
      * CONVERT EXPORTED SEQUENTIAL FILE TO
      * 416 BYTE LINE SEQUENTIAL FILE
      **********************************************
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. CVT001.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT INFILE
             ASSIGN            TO "PA.DAT"
             ORGANIZATION      IS SEQUENTIAL
             ACCESS MODE       IS SEQUENTIAL.

           SELECT OUTFILE
             ASSIGN            TO "PA1.IMP"
             ORGANIZATION      IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD INFILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 416 CHARACTERS
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01 INFILE-RECORD         PIC X(416).

       FD OUTFILE.
       01 OUTFILE-RECORD        PIC X(416).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  EOF                  PIC X(1)  VALUE 'N'.
       01  RECCOUNT             PIC 9(10) VALUE ZEROS.


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-PROGRAM.
           DISPLAY 'CVT001 PROGRAM IN PROGRESS'
           DISPLAY 'OPENING INPUT FILE...'
           OPEN I-O INFILE
           DISPLAY 'OPENING OUTPUT FILE....'
           OPEN OUTPUT OUTFILE
           DISPLAY 'LOADING FILE NOW...'
           MOVE ZEROS TO RECCOUNT
           READ INFILE
             AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF
             END-READ
           PERFORM UNTIL EOF = 'Y'
             ADD 1 TO RECCOUNT
             MOVE INFILE-RECORD TO OUTFILE-RECORD
             WRITE OUTFILE-RECORD
             READ INFILE
               AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF
               END-READ
             END-PERFORM
           DISPLAY 'LOADED ' RECCOUNT ' RECORDS.'
           DISPLAY 'CLOSING FILES'
           CLOSE INFILE
           CLOSE OUTFILE
           GOBACK.

----
Sample of input data (no wraps or returns are in the data):

1 Choice Real Estate-North
                20120   Route                                           19
        Cranberry Township      PA  16066-6208  412-776-9705B12/95   2nd Edition
 1995    6531    6531A                   40.698780   -80.114880              420
19Butler County                               6280Pittsburgh, PA MSA
                1 2 3 Store
                                        Coventry Mall
                        Pottstown               PA  19464       610-323-9729B08/
95   1st Edition 1995    5947                            40.242360   -75.636660
             42029Chester County                              6160Philadelphia,
PA-NJ PMSA                      1 Choice Real Estate
                                                222     Penna Ave
                                        Irwin                   PA  15642
412-863-3950B12/95   1st Edition 1996    6531A   6531                    40.3160
40   -79.719480              42129Westmoreland County                         62
80Pittsburgh, PA MSA                            1 Choice Real Estate-Shope Agcy
                                                                742     S Main S
t                                                       Greensburg
PA  15601-4137  412-832-2800B12/95   1st Edition 1996    6531
         40.292940   -79.544460              42129Westmoreland County
              6280Pittsburgh, PA MSA                            
```

#### ↳ Re: Here's a cute one...

- **From:** "Al Shannon" <shannon@allover.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-21T00:25:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Dlt_8.25$AY2.52192@news.sjc.globix.net>`
- **References:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
The problem is in your assumption of the physical structure of SEQUENTIAL
file.  In VA COBOL, SEQUENTIAL files are support by the STL filesystem,
Btrieve, or workstation VSAM.  Typically, files in these filesystems will
contain header information about the file: organization, record length, etc.
Trying to open a file that wasn't created specifically by the filesystem
you're using will invariably lead to file status 39.  The exception is LINE
SEQUENTIAL, which is supported by the native byte-stream files of records
terminated by CRLF's on Windows.

If the file consists of 416 byte records, you don't need to even convert it,
do you, before you ftp it to the mainframe?  Just add the CFLF at the end of
each line.

Al...



"Paul Raulerson" praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com
wrote in message news:zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com...
Under Windows XP.

I wrote a quick and dirty little program this morning to convert a
SEQUENTIAL file to a LINE SEQUENTIAL file so that it would be trivial to
upload to the mainframe (while still doing an ASCII->EDCIDIC conversion via
FTP :)

The program will NOT run under Visual Age COBOL! It consistently returns a
File Status error of 39 (incompatible file).

For the heck of it, I compiled the same source code with Microfocus
NetExpress and under AcuCOBOL.
Both of them worked, but even though the output file compares exactly using
SUPERC from SPF, the files are more than 2 megabytes different in size!
Duh...

Is this just some weird Windows thing going on, or am I really loosing my
mind? Opinions on the first speculation welcome... <grin>

The input file is just a stream of data written in 416 byte records, nothing
unusual about it that I know of.
Code and data sample included below for your amusement. ;)  The filenames in
the code are hardcoded because this is just a one time program...

-Paul

--------

      **********************************************
      * CONVERT EXPORTED SEQUENTIAL FILE TO
      * 416 BYTE LINE SEQUENTIAL FILE
      **********************************************
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. CVT001.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT INFILE
             ASSIGN            TO "PA.DAT"
             ORGANIZATION      IS SEQUENTIAL
             ACCESS MODE       IS SEQUENTIAL.

           SELECT OUTFILE
             ASSIGN            TO "PA1.IMP"
             ORGANIZATION      IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD INFILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 416 CHARACTERS
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01 INFILE-RECORD         PIC X(416).

       FD OUTFILE.
       01 OUTFILE-RECORD        PIC X(416).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  EOF                  PIC X(1)  VALUE 'N'.
       01  RECCOUNT             PIC 9(10) VALUE ZEROS.


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-PROGRAM.
           DISPLAY 'CVT001 PROGRAM IN PROGRESS'
           DISPLAY 'OPENING INPUT FILE...'
           OPEN I-O INFILE
           DISPLAY 'OPENING OUTPUT FILE....'
           OPEN OUTPUT OUTFILE
           DISPLAY 'LOADING FILE NOW...'
           MOVE ZEROS TO RECCOUNT
           READ INFILE
             AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF
             END-READ
           PERFORM UNTIL EOF = 'Y'
             ADD 1 TO RECCOUNT
             MOVE INFILE-RECORD TO OUTFILE-RECORD
             WRITE OUTFILE-RECORD
             READ INFILE
               AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF
               END-READ
             END-PERFORM
           DISPLAY 'LOADED ' RECCOUNT ' RECORDS.'
           DISPLAY 'CLOSING FILES'
           CLOSE INFILE
           CLOSE OUTFILE
           GOBACK.

----
Sample of input data (no wraps or returns are in the data):

1 Choice Real Estate-North
                20120   Route                                           19
        Cranberry Township      PA  16066-6208  412-776-9705B12/95   2nd
Edition

420

                                        Coventry Mall

95   1st Edition 1995    5947
                         40.242360   -75.636660
             42029Chester County
6160Philadelphia,
PA-NJ PMSA                      1 Choice Real Estate
                                                222     Penna Ave
                                        Irwin                   PA  15642

40   -79.719480              42129Westmoreland County

80Pittsburgh, PA MSA                            1 Choice Real Estate-Shope

#### ↳ Re: Here's a cute one...

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T21:05:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d41b531.11615227@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>I wrote a quick and dirty little program this morning to convert a 
>SEQUENTIAL file to a LINE SEQUENTIAL file 
[...]
>but even though the output file compares exactly 
>using SUPERC from SPF, the files are more than 2 megabytes different in 
>size!  Duh...
>Is this just some weird Windows thing going on, or am I really loosing =
>my mind? Opinions on the first speculation welcome... <grin>

In your SEQUENTIAL file the records are all physikally the same size
while LINE SEQUENTIAL replaces trailings spaces by a single record
delimiter.

With a logical record length of ten, the input

"foo bar   "
"foobar    "

becomes

"foo bar<"
"foobar<"

where "<" represents the record delimiter (x"0D0A" in Win
environments). In the example above your conversion saved 3 bytes (5
on Unix). If SUPERC supports binary diffing, try that...

Kind regards

Sven
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Here's a cute one...

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T22:04:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MHj09.302323$Bt1.15480668@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com> <3d41b531.11615227@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```

"Sven Brueggemann" <SBrueggemann@gmx.net> wrote in message
news:3d41b531.11615227@news.btx.dtag.de...
> "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> on Unix). If SUPERC supports binary diffing, try that...

True. SOME systems truncate trailing blanks on LINE SEQUENTIAL files
(Fujitsu COBOL does not), then append a CR/LF pair. If, however, there are
no trailing blanks, the file GROWS by numrecs x 2 bytes. So, the LINE
SEQUENTIAL file resulting from the copy could be (up to) lots smaller or
somewhat larger, depending on the preponderance of records with trailing
blanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Here's a cute one...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T22:52:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OWn09.10160$DN4.2047276@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com> <3d41b531.11615227@news.btx.dtag.de> <MHj09.302323$Bt1.15480668@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:MHj09.302323$Bt1.15480668@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Sven Brueggemann" <SBrueggemann@gmx.net> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >Is this just some weird Windows thing going on, or am I really loosing
=
> > >my mind? Opinions on the first speculation welcome... <grin>
> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have an over-riding class I call "Editor compatable" for that damned
Fujitsu indiosyncracy.  It is most annoying with text files, as accidentally
increasing the line size can result in an extra record being added when
reading it back.  That can be avoided with a true line sequential file by
just by making the record size on the read extra big, and ignoring trailing
blanks.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Here's a cute one...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T12:59:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iPw09.306677$iX5.15405336@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com> <3d41b531.11615227@news.btx.dtag.de> <MHj09.302323$Bt1.15480668@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <OWn09.10160$DN4.2047276@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

> > True. SOME systems truncate trailing blanks on LINE SEQUENTIAL files
> > (Fujitsu COBOL does not), then append a CR/LF pair. If, however, there
are
> > no trailing blanks, the file GROWS by numrecs x 2 bytes. So, the LINE
> > SEQUENTIAL file resulting from the copy could be (up to) lots smaller or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fujitsu indiosyncracy.  It is most annoying with text files, as
accidentally
> increasing the line size can result in an extra record being added when
> reading it back.  That can be avoided with a true line sequential file by
> just by making the record size on the read extra big, and ignoring
trailing
> blanks.

In Version 6, Fujitsu added a runtime directive to truncate trailing blanks
on LINE SEQUENTIAL files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Here's a cute one...

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T11:22:55+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D42747F.2AEB5123@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<zDi_8.63777$88.1137119@twister.austin.rr.com> <3d41b531.11615227@news.btx.dtag.de> <MHj09.302323$Bt1.15480668@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> > >using SUPERC from SPF, the files are more than 2 megabytes different in
> > >size!  Duh...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> no trailing blanks, the file GROWS by numrecs x 2 bytes. 

Or by numrecs x 1 byte on some systems (LF on Unix, it would be CR on
Mac) and/or if options specify single byte terminators.

So, the LINE
> SEQUENTIAL file resulting from the copy could be (up to) lots smaller or
> somewhat larger, depending on the preponderance of records with trailing
> blanks.

There are also many other issues.  eg if the file contains control
characters (or BINARY or PACKED data) and these characters may be
prefixed with a null character on output (depending on vendor and
options) making the line sequential larger, and/or blocks of spaces may
be replaced by tab characters making this smaller.

OTOH the sequential file may cater for records of different sizes
(depending on vendor/options) and thus have a header block and record
length/pointer headers and thus the line sequential becomes smaller.

And then again some vendors may have options to transparently use
compressed data in files (not in LINE SEQUENTIAL).  I have reduced 6
MByte of data to a 1.4 Mbyte file using this.

Of course some of these may result in file comparisons failing unless
the same file techniques are used.
```

### excel to mainframe through Microfocus

- **From:** kamur@lycos.com (Kumar)
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T15:39:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7517e3e1.0207201439.752467fd@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,

for cobol testing, I create test cases first on Excel sheet..(after
reviewing with the team)..then I create the actual file on
mainframe...Is there a simple way of creating the file on MVS directly
from Excel...(the mvs file has many comp fields which I document in
excel as text values..)...Is there a simple way to do this so as to
avoid duplication of work???  I have Microfocus also at my
shop..someone mentioned it should be easier thru
Microfocus...Appreciate any response..

Thanks
kamur

#### ↳ Re: excel to mainframe through Microfocus

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T12:00:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahgs4k$5eq$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<7517e3e1.0207201439.752467fd@posting.google.com>`

```
If you are using NetExpress, the PC version of Micro Focus COBOL, then you
could extract the data from an excel spreadsheet using the excel ODBC driver
with embedded SQL in the COBOL program. Then write out the data in the form
you want and FTP it to the mainframe.

Hope this helps,
Paul

Hi,

This can be done but not by using ODBC. It's done using the 'INVOKE'
statement to control excel via it's OLE controls.

If you have Net Express, look in in the
.....\NETEXPRESS\BASE\DEMO\OLEDEMOS\EXCEL for a demo program to do exactly
what you suggest. But, as I said, not with ODBC.

The Excel ODBC driver (supplied by Microsoft) gives you SQL access to the
data in the spreadsheet and NetExpress can use ODBC as it's SQL layer.

Regards,
Paul

## Thread: Micro Focus Cobol: How to stop cursor from moving? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Micro Focus Cobol: How to stop cursor from moving?

- **From:** ah_dallas@hotmail.com (D.T.H.)
- **Date:** 2002-07-20T18:11:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dac5a01e.0207201711.269741f4@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello Micro Focus experts, 
I have a Micro focus Cobol screen that looks like the following:
  _   customer name 1
  _   Customer name 2
  _   customer name 3
  _   Customer name ..
  _   Customer name n
This screen has a selection field and a name field.  The selection
field is a enterable field so the user can move the cursor next to the
customer and press
enter to display more information on the selected customer.  Each page
on the
screen allows 14 displayable lines.  

My problem is that, if I am at the last screen and there are only 3
customers left to be displayed, how can I prevent the user from moving
the cursor down to line #4, #5 and so on?

Appreciate any suggestion either to my email ah_dallas@hotmail.com or
posted here.

Thanks, DTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol: How to stop cursor from moving?

- **From:** "ames J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-21T01:51:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D3A14A4.DF37B627@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<dac5a01e.0207201711.269741f4@posting.google.com>`

```


"D.T.H." wrote:

> Hello Micro Focus experts,
> I have a Micro focus Cobol screen that looks like the following:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks, DTH

If only I could find some of my old screen code.... <G>. What you are
doing is akin to emulating a Listbox in a Windows Dialog. Using GUIs - if
the user selects from the list - with GUI-ing you are able to test if
there is an 'entry/index' at Line/Element x of the list - if not, ignore.

So you have a parallel problem. You've set yourself a maximum of 14
entries for display, so I'm assuming you are reading a file into a W/S
Table - with an OCCURS 1 to 14 depending on ws-MaxList and you are also
using the OCCURS clause in your Screen Section ? Each time you build your
table to display, you store the maximum number of entries, (ws-MaxList).
For 'full' screens this will always be 14. In the case of your last
display, your ws-MaxList =  3 - just don't allow user to select beyond 3,
i.e., they may select 4 or 5, so -   (a) Either ignore or (b) Give them an
error message.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-5eb5f873b2"></a>
## Thread: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL — 65 this month / 82 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T04:18:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Much of the data contained in business systems is representational;
that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior. One of the
descriptions of object-orientation is "objects interacting". Because
representational 'objects' have no behavior, they cannot interact
with other objects. Indeed, it is important that such data never
'change state'.

Example: A sale event creates a sale 'object' that represents the
sale. If the item sold is returned, a sale-return event occurs that
creates a sale-return 'object' that represents that event. The
original sale 'object' has no change in its state.

The behavioral abstractions that are discussed as part of
object-orientation do not occur as often in business applications
as in other applications. What occurs frequently in business
applications is process abstractions, such as: validate (edit),
sort, update, report, extract (filter), transform, accumulate, etc.
The combination of these abstractions are the design patterns
of business data processing.

Example: The summarize pattern might be seen to consist of:
    extract -> sort -> accumulate -> update.

Process abstractions may be used in object-orientation by
creating a class with at least one method to define the behavior
of the process. The object data may consist of, as a property,
a collection of objects to hold the representation of events.
Then if a 'summarize' method is invoked with a reference to a
collection of detail objects, the method 'summarize' the detail
and fill its collection with summary objects. Other processes
would use this collection as required by the application.

It would be tempting to call 'summarize' the behavior of an
empty collection, or of an empty file, or a data-item with the
value zero. I think doing so would be a mistake.

Data processing (DP) differs from 'good' OO applications in
at least two ways.

First, DP has a lot of representational data, as discussed
above; OO applications have data in which the state changes.

Second, the nature of 'good' OO applications is that they have
all objects in memory at one time. This may be seen by looking
at OO's roots in simulation and modeling. For these applications,
as I understand them, pairs of timing events are passed to each
of the objects. With the first event, the object examines the
output state of other relavent objects and determines what its
next output state will be. On the second timing event the object
changes its output state. With word processing and spreadsheets,
the events are values or commands entered by a user. Each value
or command changes the state of some object and any object
may be affected, by that change, at any time. An essential
element is that the events are either synchronized in pairs or
asynchronous with propogation.

With DP, only as much data is loaded into memory as is required
and the events that change values often have no immediate effect.
This is because the effect of events that change states are
synchronized to fit a schedule or are asynchronous on demand.

I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL because the characteristics
that make for 'good' OO applications are different than the
characteristics of most DP.

This does not mean that I feel there are no opportunities to
use OO COBOL. It only means that I feel we must not jump into
OO without first ensuring that an application is well-suited
to OO.
```

#### ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T13:32:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<khX_8.1157$5l.154429@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com...
> Much of the data contained in business systems is representational;
> that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior. One of the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes.  In fact, business operates in exactly this manner.  In business
systems, a debit or a credit transaction is perhaps the most common object
created.  However, it is quite incorrect to say that it will never change
state.  In fact, the requirements of accounting may be that the original
properties of date, original comment, time, and amount never change, but
that certain flags in the object (like posted to the invoicing system) do
change.


> The behavioral abstractions that are discussed as part of
> object-orientation do not occur as often in business applications
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>

To tell the truth, I cannot figure out what you mean by the rest of this.
What is the significance of the quotations whenever you use the word "good"
in the above?  My comment is sorta: Then use "bad" OO applications.  I have
not a clue about what is good and bad oop.  I have written Cobol for 35
years, and I have written oop Cobol for five, and I have  degree in computer
science, and have written at least 5 or 6 million lines of Cobol (at least
if I cheat and count lines of copy books each time they were used).
However, I learnt oop by converting a couple of big legacy systems, then
wrote some stuff that I sell, I can assure you it does all sorts of really
neat stuff.

If the neat stuff that it is doing for me and my bussiness systems is really
bad OO Cobol, then I'll just have to live with the consequences, I guess.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T18:17:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujp0kvij7cvt92@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <khX_8.1157$5l.154429@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:khX_8.1157$5l.154429@news20.bellglobal.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> change.

Postability is not a behavior of the record of accounting events.
Well I am not suggesting you make any changes, consider the
following:

1. For each class with a 'post' flag, refactor the class to remove
    the flag to a separate class called 'postable'.
2. Create new classes, using multiple inheritance, as
    'postable-whatever', or create 'postable-whatever'
    through single inheritance of 'postable' and aggregation
    of 'whatever'.

What I hope is clear is that the 'post' flag is not a property of
a transaction; but, rather, an artifact introduced into the object
by the process abstraction 'post' and that the transaction,
itself, has no state changes or other behavior.

It is also possible to provide, an overridable method in
'postable', called 'post'; then 'post' in 'postable-whatever'
provides the concrete behavior required by the process
abstraction 'post'.

The options then are:

1. Invoke 'post' immediately after creation of that which is
    'postable'.
2. Defer invoking 'post' until required, in which case:
  a. A process 'post-all-that-is postable' may iterate through
      a collection of 'postable' objects invoking 'post' on each.
  b. A process 'post-transactions' may iterate through a
      collection of 'postable-transaction' objects invoking
      'post' on each.
3. If 2b applies, don't bother with the above; just do what
    you have been doing.

> > The behavioral abstractions that are discussed as part of
> > object-orientation do not occur as often in business applications
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> What is the significance of the quotations whenever you use the word
"good"
> in the above?

Much of the literature I have read states that OO is not right for
every application, yet suggests that all programming should be
OOP, this is inconsistent. What I see is that some applications
are well-suited or a good-fit for OO; but I am not convinced that
OO is an improvement, or a sufficient improvement, over existing
methods. I also have the notion that the use of OO in some
situations may be really, really bad.

What I did not make clear is that my references to OO refer to
what may be regarded as pure OO and not a hybrid of OO and
imperative non-OO (procedural is too vague for my tatse). In this
sense, pure OO has no records, no groups, and no redefinitions,
except possibly at external interfaces. This is because records
are classes of the record-name, groups are classes of the
group-name, and redefinitions are subclasses of a class-id
which describes the redefined area. Also there are no
copybooks since definitions occur once in classes and reuse
of definitions is accomplished by inheritance.

Hence, my use of 'good' refers to those types of applications
where the use of OO in its purer forms has indisputably
satisfactory results. The antithesis of 'good' is those
applications in which the results may be unsatisfactory.
I do not see the use of pure OO in most business applications
as able to achieve indisputable staisfactory results.

>  My comment is sorta: Then use "bad" OO applications.  I have
> not a clue about what is good and bad oop.  I have written Cobol for 35
> years, and I have written oop Cobol for five, and I have  degree in
computer
> science, and have written at least 5 or 6 million lines of Cobol (at least
> if I cheat and count lines of copy books each time they were used).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If the neat stuff that it is doing for me and my bussiness systems is
really
> bad OO Cobol, then I'll just have to live with the consequences, I guess.

One comment I saw recently discussed sociological effects
(Hawthorne effect) as they may occur in one aspect of
programming. This provoked the question of whether, or to
what extent, the adaptation of languages to provide for OO
may cause programmers to perceive that the use of OO
is better than it really is. It happened to me, briefly, when
I finally began to understand OO. Since then the reality
I see has very little neat stuff.

I do see advantages to using some of the OO features of
COBOL-2002 to accomplish modular programming more
easily than in COBOL-85. Based upon prior discussions, I
think this is close to what you and others are doing -- not
pure OO; but enough of the features to feel comfortable
with your choice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T09:42:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W_c%8.9683$sb5.668239@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <khX_8.1157$5l.154429@news20.bellglobal.com> <ujp0kvij7cvt92@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message

> Much of the literature I have read states that OO is not right for
> every application, yet suggests that all programming should be
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I do not get this.  Cobol OOP, by nature contains Cobol non-oop.  It has in
it ordinary old procedures, and ordinary old data fields and they can do all
the same things that the Cobol we know and love has always been able to so.
In fact, you cannot write a Cobol OOP program without at least a Prcedure
Division.  You can make that a simple "EXIT METHOD" if the only thing you
want to do is make it an OOP object.

You seem to think that somehow the programer gets contanimated, and as soon
as a they get infected by oop they become incapable of programing a simple
alter statement.  Hell, you are quite welcome to alter GOTO's in methods in
an oop procedure if that is what you really want.  It is a language
extension, not a religion.

> What I did not make clear is that my references to OO refer to
> what may be regarded as pure OO and not a hybrid of OO and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well then, it is obvious why you do not like OOP. You have invented your own
little construct that does not work, and does not even exist in the real
world.  After doing that, you wrote a nice little essay criticizing it.
There is no such thing as "pure OOP" with no procedural bits. Not in Cobol,
anyway.  You seem to be talking about an academic abstraction that exists
only in your own head.  Why post about it?

I'd be real curious to see some Cobol code that actually did something with
an "object" without using procedural code.

>
> One comment I saw recently discussed sociological effects
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ah.  Well, there is no doubt that the arrival of new tools can certainly
spark some creative juices.

The old saying is that when all you have is a hammer, everything starts
looking like a nail.  Your approach seems to be "lets evaluate this
screwdriver for putting in nails", go into short a dialogue about the mass
of the handle, then scrap it.

I am glad I have not been comtaminated by academic purity, and can use even
glue and string to make the damand thing work.  I do not have to limit
myself to tools that are "pure", "good", and get good marks in a classroom.

Donald

P.S.  I once heard a(n) (un)civil engineer say to an academic: "It's a
fucking hole in the ground, not a watch".  I believe the student was
pounding in a wooden stick, and attempting to put it within 1/10 of an inch
at the time.  The roadbed they were building was 20 miles long.

Donald.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T15:29:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujrd6f14lv1tfb@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <khX_8.1157$5l.154429@news20.bellglobal.com> <ujp0kvij7cvt92@corp.supernews.com> <W_c%8.9683$sb5.668239@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:W_c%8.9683$sb5.668239@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
>
[snip]
> > What I did not make clear is that my references to OO refer to
> > what may be regarded as pure OO and not a hybrid of OO and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well then, it is obvious why you do not like OOP. You have invented your
own
> little construct that does not work, and does not even exist in the real
> world.  After doing that, you wrote a nice little essay criticizing it.
> There is no such thing as "pure OOP" with no procedural bits. Not in
Cobol,
> anyway.  You seem to be talking about an academic abstraction that exists
> only in your own head.  Why post about it?

In C++, struct is treated as a class with all members public.
In Java, there is no struct. In Eiffel, there are no records.

Converting any program from these languages into COBOL
would result in WORKING-STORAGE of the object looking
as I described above, elementary 01s from top to bottom
-- no records -- no groups -- no redefinitions.

This is no acedemic abstraction -- it is the reality of other
languages. I simply applied that reality to COBOL.
```
