#### ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T01:04:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c231e4$dd9306e0$d7ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
COBOL programs have many data structures i.e. group level items in
WORKING-STORAGE and PROCEDURE DIVISION statements that operate on the data
structures.  In a highly structured program the PROCEDURE DIVISION code
would be in paragraphs that are PERFORMed.  Each of these data structures
and the code that operates on them would be potential object candidates. 
From this point of view their seems to be many opportunities for OO
programming in COBOL.

I guess an OO purist would protest that you really should go back and use
OO design principles and redesign from scratch with objects in mind.

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in article
<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com>...
> Much of the data contained in business systems is representational;
> that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior. One of the
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T22:09:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujpe7i5c6j2c48@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <01c231e4$dd9306e0$d7ccf943@chottel>`

```

Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c231e4$dd9306e0$d7ccf943@chottel...
> COBOL programs have many data structures i.e. group level items in
> WORKING-STORAGE and PROCEDURE DIVISION statements that operate on the data
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming in COBOL.

Yes, many opportunities.

> I guess an OO purist would protest that you really should go back and use
> OO design principles and redesign from scratch with objects in mind.

Current purist seem to suggest wrapping the COBOL with
language du jour; then replace it when necessary. ;-)

There is recognition that OOD cannot find all the classes;
hence the choice is to refactor existing classes when a
suitable class has been found. One of the questions I have
is whether refactoring should ever be so fine-grained as
to effect the representation on external storage or to greatly
affect the speed of conversion from external records to
internal objects.

My current thinking is that  less-than-pure classes create
few, if any, problems in COBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T02:38:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com...
> Much of the data contained in business systems is
representational;
> that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior. One of
the
> descriptions of object-orientation is "objects interacting".
Because
> representational 'objects' have no behavior, they cannot
interact
> with other objects. Indeed, it is important that such data
never
> 'change state'.

Perhaps it's a mismatch between our respective vocabularies,
but this line of deductions does not make sense to me.

I should in advance warn you that I have very little
OO-COBOL-specific knowledge.  You've made references to
object-orientation in general, though, and that would be the
perspective from which my remarks are made.  From what I have
seen of OO-COBOL, though, it appears to have comparable
constructs to other languages I've used.

Stateless objects are a useful pattern in an object-oriented
design, but they're not the only game in town.  You can get
tremendous benefits out of using one of these instead of a
stateful object.  Eventually, though, you'll have to remember
something beyond a single method call.

Web developers are especially keen on stateless objects,
because of the persistance and performance issues with that
interface.  I've seen projects that are entirely comprised of
stateless objects, but it's a bit of the shell game.  The
browser acts as a presentation object that persists its own
state (however erratically,) simulating a stateful object.

Are these stateless objects and representational 'objects' the
same breed?

___________________

Specifically, I should understand this statement:

> Much of the data contained in business systems is
representational;
> that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior.

Is this equivalent to saying that if you encapsulated this data
within an object, there would be no (reasonable) methods to be
associated with it?

___________________

> Example: A sale event creates a sale 'object' that represents
the
> sale.

Great, an example -- abstraction can be the end of any good
discussion.  Just to fix the image a bit more, are you
envisioning a point of sale system?

> If the item sold is returned, a sale-return event occurs that
> creates a sale-return 'object' that represents that event.

This sounds like a Return method would be handy to have on this
Sale object.

That object would/should have much of the relavant information
that could be used to flesh out any SaleReturn that you would
want to make.  With more context, I would be able to speculate
if there's even a need for a SaleReturn object, or if this
information can be adequately interfaced by a Sale object.
(For the sake of the next comment, though, I'll assume that the
model warrants a seperate SaleReturn class.)

> The original sale 'object' has no change in its state.

This does not appear to be good design to me.  One of the
earlier questions I would ask about a Sale is "what happened to
it."  If it has been returned, a Sale object should be able to
tell me.  Even if the status is not stored proximate to the
original data, or if it's derivative, it still affects the
state of that object.

__________________

> The behavioral abstractions that are discussed as part of
> object-orientation do not occur as often in business
applications
> as in other applications. What occurs frequently in business
> applications is process abstractions, such as: validate
(edit),
> sort, update, report, extract (filter), transform,
accumulate, etc.
> The combination of these abstractions are the design patterns
> of business data processing.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> creating a class with at least one method to define the
behavior
> of the process. The object data may consist of, as a
property,
> a collection of objects to hold the representation of events.
> Then if a 'summarize' method is invoked with a reference to a
> collection of detail objects, the method 'summarize' the
detail
> and fill its collection with summary objects. Other processes
> would use this collection as required by the application.
>
> It would be tempting to call 'summarize' the behavior of an
> empty collection, or of an empty file, or a data-item with
the
> value zero. I think doing so would be a mistake.

__________________

> Data processing (DP) differs from 'good' OO applications in
> at least two ways.
>
> First, DP has a lot of representational data, as discussed
> above; OO applications have data in which the state changes.

There's a bit of chicken-or-egg in this, though?

A common element to projects (I've worked on at least) that
attempt to extend legacy systems, has been to consolidate
information and process from the far reaches of the existing
application suite.  The organization's date would come into a
chute and be transformed and reported into metric tons of
data -- much of it being duplicate or made obsolete by other
transformations.  It appears that the state of the global data
set was changing, and those changes were being appended to the
data, rather than integrated with it.

This is no criticism of the method, though.  It's a reliable
and maintainable way of preserving data integrity.  Building
highly integrated systems is difficult, and sets you up for
perverse and subtle errors that can drive you insane, but those
benefits _can_ be worth it.

__________________

> Second, the nature of 'good' OO applications is that they
have
> all objects in memory at one time.

This is simply not true.  Memory is a dynamic affair, and is
probably abused by some OO practitioners, but it's not a good
design principal.

A common mistake for OO sophomores, though, is to equate every
record in their persistant storage to a class, and think only
in terms of instances of those classes.

A quick example:  Say you're looking for Sales made on January
23, 2002 over $100.

1)  You could instance each unique Sale in storage and check
its values, or...

2)  ...you could write a class method that takes your arguments
and returns matching instances, or even a list of identifiers
that can later be used to create the objects.

Clearly method #1 sucks.  Method #2 works, and keeps the
business logic centralized into your Sale class.
Implementation of a SaleQuery class that inherits from a
BizQuery class could further sharpen the distinction between
business, storage and presentation logic.

However, at this point I'm sneaking in some architectural
suggestions, which is not really the strength of OO.  OO is
more about being able to "zone" your code into different
districts.  How you gerrymander your part of the world is
really of no _consequence_ to OO -- only your program.

> I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL because the
characteristics
> that make for 'good' OO applications are different than the
> characteristics of most DP.

I would totally agree that converting a code base to an OO form
is not a persuasive argument for a project -- it doesn't even
get a "contributing factor" nomination from me.

> This does not mean that I feel there are no opportunities to
> use OO COBOL. It only means that I feel we must not jump into
> OO without first ensuring that an application is well-suited
> to OO.

Once a target has been named as a project, and I'm in the
technology selection phase, though, my regard of OO spikes.
Unless there is an availibility/quality issue with tools, I'll
pick OO every time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T12:24:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```

Grinder <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> same breed?

No. Representational objects have state (properties); but the
state, once established, does not change. A more familiar
example is playing cards. Card objects have values for
suit and rank; but have no (significant) behavior of their own.
Behavior depends upon the game being played. (I added
significant for this example because a card object may have
behavior that allows it to display an image on a screen; but
in playing the game the program depends upon the values,
not the image.)
> ___________________
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> associated with it?

Associated is kinda fuzzy.

There may be a several methods associated with it. It is
just that the sale object itself has no methods. An application,
using the sale object, might need the sale object to have
behaviors, such as, 'postable' with a state 'posted' and
'returnable' with a state 'returned'. An application may also
need a payment type, such as cash, check, credit card, etc.

The behavior associated with the sale object and required
by the application is achieved through inheritance or
aggregation.

Consider also an application to analyze sales information.
The application uses sale objects directly without regard
to behavior required by other applications.

> ___________________
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> envisioning a point of sale system?

No. More along the lines of a bookeeping entry; not the
means to achieve it.

> > If the item sold is returned, a sale-return event occurs that
> > creates a sale-return 'object' that represents that event.
>
> This sounds like a Return method would be handy to have on this
> Sale object.

Or a 'returnable' class with 'returned' state and a 'return' method.
Use multiple inheritance to create a class 'returnable-sale'.

> That object would/should have much of the relavant information
> that could be used to flesh out any SaleReturn that you would
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> model warrants a seperate SaleReturn class.)

In the 'returnable' class add an object reference to the
'sale-return' object.

> > The original sale 'object' has no change in its state.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> state of that object.

All design depends upon the application -- "form follows
function." For my part, I have made no assumptions about
a particular application; hence I have made no design
decisions.

A returnable-sale object could tell you whether it has been
returned, in which case, a sale object need not have the
behavior. If a sale occurs where 'the sale is final', it might
be necessary to distinguish between returnable and
non-returnable sales.

Much of the discussion of sale and sale-return objects
could apply to inventory applications in the form of
receipts and returns.

The central issue I was attempting to raise is that in
business processing there are objects that record events.
Once recorded, the record remains unchanged. That
business data processing has more such objects than
other types of applications. That the existence of such
large numbers of these objects may mean that we need
to think differently about OO.

> __________________
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> There's a bit of chicken-or-egg in this, though?

I think not. I have provided, what I think is, more support in my
statements, above.

> A common element to projects (I've worked on at least) that
> attempt to extend legacy systems, has been to consolidate
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> design principal.

I was unclear in presenting this point. What I was discussing
was OO theory or pure OO design. Such design proceeds
as if memory were not an issue.

Simulation and modeling objects, word processing documents,
spreadsheets, CAD drawings, etc. are, in theory, loaded into
memory before the application is run. These ahve been shown
practical applications for OO.

If we apply this same criteria to DP, then all data, from all
parts of the system must be loaded before the application
starts. For most DP applications this is impractical.

If OO theory cannot withstand the needs of practice, then
maybe we need to think diferently about OO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T13:05:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<27g%8.6105$DN4.840619@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com...

<huge snippage>

> I was unclear in presenting this point. What I was discussing
> was OO theory or pure OO design. Such design proceeds
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Perhaps it is impractical from a theoretical standpoint.  However, at the
moment you are in the position of the aeronautical engineer attempting to
prove to a beekeeper that bees cannot fly.  All the theory in the world is
not going to convince me to throw away the honey I am collecting.


> If OO theory cannot withstand the needs of practice, then
> maybe we need to think diferently about OO.

Practice?  Exactly what practice did not work?  Give me the code, and
perhaps I can help you debug it.

It seems to me that you were talking about theory, not practice. I am the
one that practiced, and oop DID withstand the need.  That you need to think
differently about OOP is very obvious.

OOP changes the level and methods of coding of certain types of symbolic
abstraction. It is extremely usefull for doing such.  Various claims about
the level of abstraction dealt with at the OOP level, and claims that OOP
cannot work at all because at some other level it is not "pure" OOP are as
silly as arguements about angels and pin heads. Nothing is "pure" once you
leave the level of theology.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujrd6gsqu8mlff@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <27g%8.6105$DN4.840619@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:27g%8.6105$DN4.840619@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
> news:ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-24T14:21:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D3EA9DC.6B90CE8D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <27g%8.6105$DN4.840619@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Perhaps it is impractical from a theoretical standpoint.  However, at the
> moment you are in the position of the aeronautical engineer attempting to
> prove to a beekeeper that bees cannot fly.  

The case of the aeronautical engineer (Neville Shute Norway is
attributed) 'proving' that Bumble Bees cannot fly was showing that the
aeronautical theory was inadequate in dealing with scaled cases rather
than trying to show that bees were wrong.  The actual issue was
discrepancies between model results and full scale results (the
technical issues revolved around the Reynolds number).  The argument was
completely distorted by the popular press, as usual, and it still seems
to be quite misunderstood.

Perhaps it can be seen as entirely appropriate to OOP by a number of
measures.

> All the theory in the world is
> not going to convince me to throw away the honey I am collecting.

From Bumble Bees ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
> All design depends upon the application -- "form follows
> function." For my part, I have made no assumptions about
> a particular application; hence I have made no design
> decisions.

But you have.  You declared that the Sale object was
"representational" -- meaning that it members will not change.
Also, that the Sale object would not have any methods.  These
are quite restrictive decisions made about the design of this
hypothetical system.

> A returnable-sale object could tell you whether it has been
> returned, in which case, a sale object need not have the
> behavior. If a sale occurs where 'the sale is final', it
might
> be necessary to distinguish between returnable and
> non-returnable sales.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> to think differently about OO.

This is tautological.  You state the form of the object, then
proceed to identify that the object provides no benefit.

> > __________________
> >
> > > Data processing (DP) differs from 'good' OO applications
in
> > > at least two ways.
> > >
> > > First, DP has a lot of representational data, as
discussed
> > > above; OO applications have data in which the state
changes.
> >
> > There's a bit of chicken-or-egg in this, though?
>
> I think not. I have provided, what I think is, more support
in my
> statements, above.
>
> > A common element to projects (I've worked on at least) that
> > attempt to extend legacy systems, has been to consolidate
> > information and process from the far reaches of the
existing
> > application suite.  The organization's date would come into
a
> > chute and be transformed and reported into metric tons of
> > data -- much of it being duplicate or made obsolete by
other
> > transformations.  It appears that the state of the global
data
> > set was changing, and those changes were being appended to
the
> > data, rather than integrated with it.
> >
> > This is no criticism of the method, though.  It's a
reliable
> > and maintainable way of preserving data integrity.
Building
> > highly integrated systems is difficult, and sets you up for
> > perverse and subtle errors that can drive you insane, but
those
> > benefits _can_ be worth it.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > This is simply not true.  Memory is a dynamic affair, and
is
> > probably abused by some OO practitioners, but it's not a
good
> > design principal.
>
> I was unclear in presenting this point. What I was discussing
> was OO theory or pure OO design. Such design proceeds
> as if memory were not an issue.

This is not the OO theory that I'm familiar with.

> Simulation and modeling objects, word processing documents,
> spreadsheets, CAD drawings, etc. are, in theory, loaded into
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> starts. For most DP applications this is impractical.

> If OO theory cannot withstand the needs of practice, then
> maybe we need to think diferently about OO.

Your descriptions sound as if you've taken an existing system,
modelled with OO techniques and tried to apply it to your
application.  I can see how that would go awry.  Instead, those
techniques should be applied directly to your application,
allowing it to shape the interface and ultimately, the
implementation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-24T20:34:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```

Grinder <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > news:ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com...
[snip]
> > [snip]
> > > > The central issue I was attempting to raise is that in
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sure you have, by setting the domain of the object.

You claimed that I somehow identified "that the object provides
no benefit. I made no such claim.

You claim that the statement is "tautological." It is not.

It is not worthwhile to respond to your false claims.
Stop making them.

----------------

In OO-by-the-book, the representation of objects on external
storage is different than objects in memory. On external strorage,
objects may have foriegn keys. The process of bringing objects
into memory requires resolution of the external references
to internal references. Internally, foriegn keys are resolved to
object references.

A sale object may contain a key to the item sold. When the
sale object is brought iton memory, the key to the item is
resolved to an object reference to the item. To create the
object reference to the item, the item itself must be brought
into memory whether its content is needed or not. This
creates a performance penalty for using OO.

One way in which we may think differently is to not use
OO-by the-book. Instead of resolving the item object
reference when loading the sale object, we may define
the object reference separate from the foriegn key and
initialize the object reference to NULL. This eliminates
the performance penalty when the item object is not
needed.

By defining a GET PROPERTY method in the sale object,
the loading of the item object is accomplished when
accessing the object reference. If the object reference is
NULL, the GET PROPERTY method creates a new item
object filling the object reference in the sale object and
returns the item object reference to the invoking program.
If the object reference is not NULL, the object reference
is returned directly.

-----------
While this answers one of my concerns about having to
load the complete system in "pure" OO or OO-by-the-book,
there are others such as the replacement of group items
and redefinitions by object references. This also has
performance issues. This may be resolved by deferring
their creation; but this would mean keeping a copy of
the external record with the object.

There is also the problem of creating the correct class
when loading the object.

Based upon prior responses, it is not likely I will get any
help from the so-called "experts".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T12:33:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca...
>
> Rick Smith wrote:
[snip]
> > Based upon prior responses, it is not likely I will get any
> > help from the so-called "experts".
>
> 'SCUSE ME ! And there I was thinking you were the bloody expert !

I few days ago, I didn't think I was. Now I'm not so sure. <g>

Last night I resolved the remaining issues. I now know that
I can build a 100% COBOL-2002 "real OO" system.

The loading of objects from ISAM would, in all likelihood
result in poor performance. The use of a RDBMS and SQL
to off-load processing from OO and to fetch objects would
provide better performance.

I remain unenthusiastic about OO COBOL because of the
performance implications of OO; but the use of some OO
features to provide better modular programming is a
good thing.

Just my opinion.
```

```
> I remain unenthusiastic about OO COBOL because of the
> performance implications of OO; but the use of some OO
> features to provide better modular programming is a

I don't care much about the performance issues.  I love some of the design
issues.  What scares me are the maintenance issues.  Maintaining reused
modules in an environment where all maintenance needs to be thoroughly
tested (money is involved), is a nightmare.
```

```
> modules in an environment where all maintenance needs to be
thoroughly
> tested (money is involved), is a nightmare.

I can agree with this, maintaining a strongly centralized code
base can be difficult to keep in place.

There is a payoff, though.  If a systematic defect is found,
say 2-digit years for example, you'll have a lot fewer
revisions to your code if you have a central data access unit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T19:39:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D405521.84144E83@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahpddm02gjt@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
> news:ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> revisions to your code if you have a central data access unit.

Bugger it ! You've played right into his hands <G>. Howard has been
dying for somebody to say that, "OO contains maintenance problems". Now
he can rest in peace and stick with Procedural COBOL <G>.

The Maintenance factor is not an OO problem but a coding problem
associated with your Editor and IDE. I think you are using Pascal - does
it come with a decent IDE and utilities ?

In my own case I consider I'm still at the experimental stage and with
trial and error I need to change things which for the most part are
working just fine. Assume I have a method "This-is-what-I-want-to-do".
Firstly of course I might consider I want a more 'succinct' title for
the function, or perhaps I've coded it slightly differently with an
additional 'sending' or 'returning' parameter.

The Net Express IDE let's me search on that lousy method name above, and
retrieves all instances where it occurs in all source files - the
results appearing in less than a minute. Now I have a record of source
that I can individually load to change it to a new method name
"This-is-It". Yes, I've got ensure each source gets changed. Having
'thought' that I changed them all I can then put in a second search
against
"This-is-what-I-want-to-do", to ensure I haven't missed any.

The other thing to note is that having loaded each source I can do a
global search/replace.

Not currently available in Net Express but one of the features of the
proposed standard is REPOSITORY. I have currently in Net Express :-

       CLASS-ID.                  MaterialsEdit
                                            inherits from Base.

       CLASS-CONTROL.   CtHelp                       is class "cthelp"
                                            ErrorMessages           is
class "errmessg"
                                            MaterialsDialog1        is
class "matdlg1"
                                            MaterialsEdit
is class "matedit"
                                            MyDialog
is class "mydialog"
                        .
Using REPOSITORY this becomes :-

       CLASS-ID.                MaterialsEdit
                                         inherits from Base.

       REPOSITORY         Class  CtHelp                     as "cthelp"
                                        Class ErrorMessages         as
"errmessg"
                                        Class MaterialsDialog1      as
"matdlg1"
                                        Class MaterialsEdit
as "matedit"

MyDialog                           as  "mydialog"
                        .
The syntax is more comprehensive than I've shown and Repository can be
used for other functions - I can't currently use them so I haven't got
the format fixed in my 'noddle'. But one feature of REPOSITORY is
"forward checking". The compile for MaerialsEdit will ensure that all
other classes invoked DO contain methods referenced from MaterialsEdit -
any missing and they will show as errors. ('Missing' could mean it
hasn't been coded or you may have made a typo in the method name).

Let's take variable names. I might have originally coded something :-

01 access-result pic 9(02).

Dumb - I need more digits - so I can do that same application search on
the above variable and do global search/replace to make it read pic
9(04).

Parameters - There is no easy way around this one. If you add/subtract
sending/receiving parameters you have to bite the bullet. Again I can do
that application search but now of course I've got to ensure that each
program/class that triggers the invoking of a method, also has the
changed parameters - and YES Howard ! On this one you would need to test
each individual class/program calling the changed method.

But reflect, how often would you actually do this. Using Procedural you
call a program. Mainly at the development stage you dicker around with
the called program until you've got it right - same applies with OO The
greater likelihood is you code additional calls/or an entirely new
method.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T21:01:43+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T22:03:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahqeeo012k5@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahpddm02gjt@enews3.newsguy.com> <3D405521.84144E83@shaw.ca>`

```
> Bugger it ! You've played right into his hands <G>. Howard
has been
> dying for somebody to say that, "OO contains maintenance
problems". Now
> he can rest in peace and stick with Procedural COBOL <G>.


I'll read on to Howard's replies, but I took is remarks to be
about component dependencies.  If I have a common set of code
that's shared by many projects, changes to it can righteously
shake the web.  As I see it, this is a benefit and a liability.

> The Maintenance factor is not an OO problem but a coding
problem
> associated with your Editor and IDE. I think you are using
Pascal - does
> it come with a decent IDE and utilities ?

Award-winning -- though those awards were probably funded by
the manufacturer.

I use UltraEdit as a text editor, which would probably qualify
as one of the better "general language" editors, but the IDEs
(Delphi, vs.net, VB, VC++, Java Cafe) all whip it with ease.
Some times "intellisense" can be a little flakey, but it's a
profound improvement in building code.  (I still sneak out to
UltraEdit once in a while, though, for mass find-and-replaces.)

> In my own case I consider I'm still at the experimental stage
and with
> trial and error I need to change things which for the most
part are
> working just fine. Assume I have a method
"This-is-what-I-want-to-do".
> Firstly of course I might consider I want a more 'succinct'
title for
> the function, or perhaps I've coded it slightly differently
with an
> additional 'sending' or 'returning' parameter.
>
> The Net Express IDE let's me search on that lousy method name
above, and
> retrieves all instances where it occurs in all source files -
the
> results appearing in less than a minute. Now I have a record
of source
> that I can individually load to change it to a new method
name
> "This-is-It". Yes, I've got ensure each source gets changed.
Having
> 'thought' that I changed them all I can then put in a second
search
> against
> "This-is-what-I-want-to-do", to ensure I haven't missed any.
>
> The other thing to note is that having loaded each source I
can do a
> global search/replace.
>
> Not currently available in Net Express but one of the
features of the
> proposed standard is REPOSITORY. I have currently in Net
Express :-
>
>        CLASS-ID.                  MaterialsEdit
>                                             inherits from
Base.
>
>        CLASS-CONTROL.   CtHelp                       is class
"cthelp"
>                                             ErrorMessages
is
> class "errmessg"
>                                             MaterialsDialog1
is
> class "matdlg1"
>                                             MaterialsEdit
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>        REPOSITORY         Class  CtHelp
as "cthelp"
>                                         Class ErrorMessages
as
> "errmessg"
>                                         Class
MaterialsDialog1      as
> "matdlg1"
>                                         Class MaterialsEdit
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The syntax is more comprehensive than I've shown and
Repository can be
> used for other functions - I can't currently use them so I
haven't got
> the format fixed in my 'noddle'. But one feature of
REPOSITORY is
> "forward checking". The compile for MaerialsEdit will ensure
that all
> other classes invoked DO contain methods referenced from
MaterialsEdit -
> any missing and they will show as errors. ('Missing' could
mean it
> hasn't been coded or you may have made a typo in the method
name).
>
> Let's take variable names. I might have originally coded
something :-
>
> 01 access-result pic 9(02).
>
> Dumb - I need more digits - so I can do that same application
search on
> the above variable and do global search/replace to make it
read pic
> 9(04).
>
> Parameters - There is no easy way around this one. If you
add/subtract
> sending/receiving parameters you have to bite the bullet.
Again I can do
> that application search but now of course I've got to ensure
that each
> program/class that triggers the invoking of a method, also
has the
> changed parameters - and YES Howard ! On this one you would
need to test
> each individual class/program calling the changed method.
>
> But reflect, how often would you actually do this. Using
Procedural you
> call a program. Mainly at the development stage you dicker
around with
> the called program until you've got it right - same applies
with OO The
> greater likelihood is you code additional calls/or an
entirely new
> method.

I agree that the interface (as opposed to the implementation)
is the first part of a project to stabilize, so these types of
changes become less and less frequent.  In a strongly-typed
environment, which an OO one typically is, the compiler can
help you find them -- it's an extensive form of the "forward
checking" you've mentioned.  The same mechanism applies to
changing method names as well.
```

```
Grinder wrote:

>
> > The Maintenance factor is not an OO problem but a coding
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just a point, like you I don't use the in-built editor but use a text
based editor (Programmers' Workbench) that came with the now defunct
Microsoft COBOL.
It's a question of 'comfort zone' - what you are used to. But I do miss
out if I don't use the in-built editor - I could go technicolor crazy to
highlight misspelled words, RESERVED words and other syntax etc...(And
of course for compiles I have to return to the IDE - fortunately it does
date/time stamp checks on what I've done to the source outside of the
IDE - so it triggers 'Program X has been changed - Recompile ?').

It was Thane who wised me up to this one, (possibly Java ?), and I know
from somebody in Edmonton the feature is available in some Smalltalk
IDEs. You are coding for a particular class type and want to use
specific methods - don't know details - but presumably it gives you a
dropdown list of methods available, description, parameters etc. - that
has to be a real winner. Anything similar with Pascal ?

With Net Express I can access a separate list (on-line book) of classes
and method names with parameters - but unfortunately this is not
automatically available from within the IDE. To get to it I've had to
include an icon on the desktop.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T21:29:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d405e00_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 25-Jul-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> tested (money is involved), is a nightmare.

No it isn't Howard. You use Base cases and standard regression testing just
as you always have. The difference is that if your Classes are designed and
written properly, they won't NEED maintaining. The secret is in the
extension of the Classes by inheritance. This, in effect, breaks the
functionality into much smaller "chunks" that are less likely to need
maintaining. When you need to alter the behaviour you simply extend the
class or provide an interface to override certain inherited methods. Then do
the same unit, regression, and UAT that you always have.

I know you have posted this same objection here before, but it stems from
trying to fit OO into the framework that you know. As I have said elsewhere,
it ISN'T modular programming re-invented; it is a new concept.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T21:28:01+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<260720022128018973%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> No it isn't Howard. You use Base cases and standard regression testing just
> as you always have. The difference is that if your Classes are designed and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Let me see if I understand this right. I'll start off with an analogy.
Suppose I have a module (written in Cobol), call it DISKIO that
performs Disk IO. It has a number of parameters (it doesn't matter what
for this exercise). Now all my projects call this module (dynamically
is best) when they want to do Disk IO. I test the module to destruction
and as it has set inputs and outputs I can rely on the tested module
whenever I call it. I no longer have to test that functionality in any
project I create.

Now I need to introduce another parameter. The traditional way would be
to update the DISKIO module with the new parameter and test the new
module to destruction. Now that the module is tested I can rely on it
again. However, there are additional tests to be conducted. that is all
those old projects that were written with the original module. they all
need to be tested to prove that the changes to the DISKIO module did
not cause any strange unforseen effects. BAD lots of testing to do.

Now suppose I created a new module DISKIO_New that handled the new
parameter and called the old module when necessary. The new project and
all subsequent projects would use this new module. I would not have to
re-test all the old projects because I did not change the old module.

Now If you replace module with CLASS in the above, you might begin to
see the benefits of OO. You only need to test what you change. You only
change what you need to change. You don't need to change the original
source, you keep extending it.

As I understand it, Interfaces are a formal way of presenting CLASSES
encapsulated into a stand alone module. In this way, I believe you can
extend a Binary Module containing Classes into another class that
inherits the package. (Peter - please correct me if I have the wrong
idea here.) You do not need the source to the extended Class, just the
Interface definitions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T14:25:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahrm2o$1qo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com> <260720022128018973%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```

On 26-Jul-2002, Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

> Let me see if I understand this right. I'll start off with an analogy.
> Suppose I have a module (written in Cobol), call it DISKIO that
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> project I create.

The trouble is, it isn't obvious that we have very many general module
functions.  And the ones we have implemented with called subprograms are the
biggest pains in our system.   Typical maintenance involves updating a
table, changing a calculation, or adjusting a form.   What kinds of objects
would we use here that don't have to be changed when we make our maintenance
to the way they function?   If we hard code the changes, we have to do
extensive testing.  If we simply inherit differently, we need to redesign
our whole system from scratch - minimally, after showing that this redesign
works.

We have called programs that edit parm files - that are very hard to
maintain and very hard to test.
We have called programs that write reports - that are very hard to maintain,
are very hard to test, and are very inflexible.
We have called programs that do aborts - making it hard to determine the
real problem!
We have called programs that call called programs to read the VSAM return
code and pass back a different return code.   I would like to modify this to
make "97" a valid return code, but can't afford to test & get sign offs from
the users of the hundreds of programs that use this.

Certainly these examples don't contain most of the benefits of OO.  But they
are what we see and they make us dislike common modules.

Our Web applications do use OO, but their reliability is nowhere near what
we need for our $$ applications.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T17:56:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahs2f3$9mc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com> <260720022128018973%josephk@iinet.net.au> <ahrm2o$1qo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
The biggest attraction of OO CoBOL in the enterprise (besides the fact that
it would be fun), is that it will give us better opportunities to
communicate between different systems.   If management gets sold on using
it, it will be for this purpose - to greatly improve a task that isn't done
that well currently.

Heck, everybody tells management that CoBOL is going away anyway - they will
want to minimize money & risk of making major changes unless the payoff is
that clear.

Someone working on his own without a shop full of standards and maintenance
programmers doesn't have the same cost justification needs - he picks the
tool he works best with and has little external justification to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T02:16:57+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d434429_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com> <260720022128018973%josephk@iinet.net.au> <ahrm2o$1qo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahs2f3$9mc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:ahs2f3$9mc$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> Someone working on his own without a shop full of standards and
maintenance
> programmers doesn't have the same cost justification needs - he picks the
> tool he works best with and has little external justification to do.
>
Very valid point, Howard.

For myself, I can do whatI like.

When working in large corporations (as I am at the moment) the environment
is much more restrictive, as you correctly point out.

There are two approaches to internal red tape:

1. Work with it.

2. Get it changed if it stops work proceeding.

(The other option - not recommended if you value your job - is simply bypass
it...)

The reality is that we do a combination of both. Nevertheless, I take your
point.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T17:53:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D418DD1.47C05606@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <ahpb0i$p7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com> <260720022128018973%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```


Joseph Katnic wrote:

> In article <3d405e00_7@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not quite true. Even though your called routine is as solid as concrete, you'd be
daft if you weren't testing each program that uses it - initially. You could
possibly have too many/too few parameters listed in the calling program.

>
> Now I need to introduce another parameter. The traditional way would be
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Joe,

Your message was addressed to Pete, so I'll let him respond. I would say you are
right on the button.

You picked up on the 'gritty' one - the extra parameters. A very broad solution,
assuming a large installation :-

*>---------------

Class-id.            DiskIO inherits from Base

*>------------------------
method-id. "Method1".
*>------------------------
Linkage section.
01 In-Params.
    05 in-1            pic 9(04).
    05 in-2            pic 9(04).
01 Out-Params.
    05 out-1            pic 9(04).
    05 out-2            pic 9(04).

Procedure Division using in-Params returning Out-Params.
.
 *> This is the initial method your wrote and it might invoke 'PRIVATE' methods in
this class for support and you have coded some 1,000 programs that access this
method.

End Method "Method1".
*>---------------------------

Now we come to your additional parameters - and you don't want to mess with the
above because of 1,000 tests. Now perhaps some of those *do* need the revised
parameters - let's say 50. You've got to go back to their original source and tell
them to invoke 'Method2' below.  No question - the 50 would have to be recompiled
(1) To recognize the revised method name, (2) And unless you want to become a
shooting target - you'd better test them - just to ensure you have correctly used
the revised set of parameters.

*>------------------------
method-id. "Method2".
*>------------------------
Linkage section.
01 In-Params.
    05 in-1            pic 9(04).
    05 in-2            pic 9(04).
    0r in-3            pic 9(04).
01 Out-Params.
    05 out-1            pic 9(04).
    05 out-2            pic 9(04).
    05 out-3            pic 9(04).
Procedure Division using in-Params returning Out-Params.

*> in BIG BLACK LETTERS :-

*> THIS METHOD MUST ONLY BE INVOKED FOR NEW APPLICATIONS
*> WRITTEN SINCE 2002 FEB 01, AND THOSE PREVIOUS APPLICATIONS
*> MODIFIED TO USE THE REVISED SET OF PARAMETERS.

 *> So it is getting used for those '50' plus any new applications since 2002 Feb
01.

End Method "Method2".
*>---------------------------

I'd like to see it - but I don't think there is an easy way when you change the
parameter count.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T21:21:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d405dfb_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com...
>
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 'SCUSE ME ! And there I was thinking you were the bloody expert !

It was obvious from earlier posts that Rick's expertise in this area is
limited to some suspect academic theory on OO.

I have deliberately refrained from refuting his original post point by point
because I don't believe it will change his mind, and quite frankly, I don't
care whether he gets the benefits of OO or not. (It is "computer
programming" not religion, and I am no Evangelist...)

The points that have been raised vis-a-vis performance of OO and loading
objects in memory are just specious. Even if Rick was correct (and he isn't,
because the behaviour he describes is  just one of many possible behaviours)
it still wouldn't matter. The future of programming is in OO. End of story.

COBOL is missing the bus on it (at least Fortress COBOL is...) and, while
that is sad, it is inevitable. I am working in a major UK corporation at the
moment where ALL systems  (apart from ONE very old mainframe application
written in PL/1, which will be finally decommissioned within the next 6
months) are Client/Server and when they talk about "Legacy" they are talking
about systems developed in VB, SQL server, and Perl! For these people, the
"New Age" is Siebel, and the migration to it is facilitated by the fact that
most of the Legacy is based around OO. Objects and Components (as I have
been saying here for some time now...) are the future.

In the programming area, I asked for a quick summary of each individual's
skill set, and their level of expertise in each package/language (so I can
see how best to pool the available knowledge, organise training where
required, and allocate work to people). The lists were impressive...in depth
expertise in VB, SQL Server, Perl, IIS, ActiveX, COM, XML, VBScript,
JavaScript, various Web Development tools, some C and C++ and a smattering
of Java...there was ONE person who listed COBOL as a programming skill (40+
people surveyed).

Having sat down with these people and looked at what they can do and the
speed with which they can do it...I am impressed. They are unconstrained by
the concepts of traditional programming as COBOL programmers understand it;
they access databases with ADO (Data Objects), they use XML as a transport
to bring the data to the presentation layer of their 3 tiered systems. The
data is presented on Web Based screens and it looks incredible... Stuff that
you would think took months to produce, produced in minutes from standard
style sheets and templates (Web Objects). No "Programming" (at least as we
would understand the term) required. All done by objects, components, and
scripts. (Yes, you can argue that scripts are programs, but they are
typically small and dynamic, and they tend NOT to be maintained, used purely
as glue to hold objects together). It is a whole different ball game. And it
is the future.

Seeing this, I really can't be bothered whether Rick wins or loses his
argument. You might as well sit on the beach like Canute and tell the water
to go back. OO is here to stay. Eventually the Fortress will crumble, and in
the meantime, is being bypassed ever more rapidly.

>
> I few days ago, I didn't think I was. Now I'm not so sure. <g>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't believe you can write such unsubstantiated nonsense, Rick. Still, it
is your prerogative...


> I remain unenthusiastic about OO COBOL because of the
> performance implications of OO; but the use of some OO
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While I vehemently disagree with your opinion, I will defend to the death
your right to have it and express it <G>.

I am very tired of the Fortress COBOL argument that OO is simply "Modular
Programming re-invented". It isn't. People who use this argument invariably
have not understood or tried OO, or else they just feel better writing it
off as another form of something they feel comfortable with ("to a man with
a hammer, everything is a nail...").

Sadly, I am much too busy at the moment to be able to spend the time here
that I would like to (after my extended sabbatical, I had forgotten how
demanding real work actually is...<G>) and I have such a lot to learn that
my time will be pretty fully occupied over the next few months, but I find
it exciting and interesting. I really hope that some of you will also expand
your horizons. IT is going into some very interesting places...don't miss
the bus.

Pete.
```

```
In article <3d405dfb_7@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> 
> Seeing this, I really can't be bothered whether Rick wins or loses his
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
However, Peter, there are just some places where the Fortress will
never crumble. I suspect that as the newer generation of programmers
reach into this area, then the demand for Fortress Cobol to implement
the OO standard will grow.

Already there are C++ and Java classes for CICS and DB2. But these are
mainly for API interface to the OS and TP.

What is needed is an LE enhancement that allows programmers to take  OO
Cobol and C++ Classes and create Component Modules with their
associated Interfaces. These can then be used to build up Component
libraries and it is this that will run into the ease of connecting
various components together in the way you describe.

Of course this pre-supposes that IBM comes out with OO Cobol. However,
I suspect that eventually, after some more standard work, they will.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T19:48:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D41A899.F113ED6C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com> <3d405dfb_7@Usenet.com> <260720022139571956%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```


Joseph Katnic wrote:

> In article <3d405dfb_7@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well Pete's already made his comments on the survey - but I'd already done this
elsewhere, before I posted the reference in CLC :-

************************
Firstly some quotes from a Micro Focus white paper, which tends to substantiate
some of my own earlier thoughts on how OO COBOL is shaking down :-

http://www.cobolportal.com/developer/future.asp

Accepting that the survey was mailed out to some 5,000, and received a poor
response, 142 (2.8%), nevertheless the statistics are probably still relevant.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

COBOL USAGE

- Developing and maintaining existing legacy code - 87%
- Effort expended in MAINTENANCE - 50%
- Mixture of new and maintenance -30%
- Embedded SQL - 70%
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The last figure kind of puts the boot to COBOL file systems !

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

IS Managers View of the Future?

- IS Mangers having reviewed literature or attended limited
    seminars relating to OO and the
Web                                                 50%
- Of the above an intent to integrate OO and Web features                     25%
- Stress that OO and Web should be included in education                     70%
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Note : Some 50% have only an inkling of what OO is about - you can imagine how
much information they actually picked up from a one day seminar ! Nevertheless,
without a comprehension of the topic, 70% stress it should be taught, (because it
is 'the IN thing' ?). It's also a guess, but those that attended seminars -
probably the majority
were mainframers - the smaller shops geared to a PC environment, would probably
prefer to get a copy of a compiler and 'suck it and see'.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

COBOL will continue to be used over next 10 years                            45%
COBOL will decline in
importance                                                       30%
COBOL will be eliminated or
replaced                                                 15%
COBOL usage will increase over next 10 years
5%
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hey, COBOL is doing great ! But note 50% of above respond in the negative.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

IS Managers Perception about Use of Object Oriented             Percent

- O-O will be popular as a structured form but will not replace
            Structured methodology - 62.9%
- O-O will become the standard for future program development - 25.7%
- Other - 6.4%
- O-O development is too risky to implement at present - 2.9%
- O-O is a fad and will not be seriously implemented  - 2.1%
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CONCLUSIONS

......However, it is important  for educators to note that most of these managers
(90 percent) expressed the need for students to have a working  knowledge and the
skill-set associated with both object-oriented and web-based COBOL capabilities
upon graduation.

.... It is of interest to note that the educator's view on this same subject was
not
significantly different from their IS manager counterparts in business.[11] In a
similar
vein, educators and IS managers find it extremely  difficult to gather sufficient
funding or the time to obtain the necessary education and training to obtain new
knowledge
with  respect to the rapidly increasing technological change associated with the
COBOL language.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It seems to me that the above two quotes are a bit in conflict. We want students
to learn OO and the Web - can only be theoretical at school - then they go out
into the big wide world where their future managers don't have a handle on OO !
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

- Let's backtrack to the early days of OO in J4. There were the 'Visionary Three'
who
first promoted OO. Courageous and I wonder if the idea was a hard sell ? Bill
indicated that in the early days ('96?), there wasn't too much savvy about the
topic in the J4 team. No criticism there - be assured had I been one of them, I
would have
played dumb as well ! (It's the old 'corporate syndrome' - ask the directors to
spend $2
million getting into computing; they'll go along with what appear sound arguments
rather than make bloody fools of themselves. One of them proposes a new bicycle
rack for staff bikes - they all chip in with their two cents worth !).  This is no
reflection
on the expertise of J4 members - you can't be an 'all knowing' expert on every
aspect of the language.

GUIs - this is where I'm at loggerheads with yourself, Bill and, yes Pete ! The
objective is COBOL should remain vanilla flavoured. My contention - we have been
shortchanged in the PC world because of this thinking. Both F/J and M/F have
PROVEN that GUIs can be written in COBOL. Now pick up on some of those negative
statistics above - the 'decline' will largely be attributable to the fact that
COBOL is not perceived as the right tool for GUIs or the Web, because these
features are not a part of the Standard, whereas, to the best of my knowledge they
are automatically embraced in most other OO languages..

I use Intuit's Quickbooks - nifty and sophisticated - but I KNOW I could
completely emulate their application using M/F GUIs. And I damned sure the same
would be possible with F/J POWER FORM.

It's worth reflecting that when I first used RM/COBOL in 80'/81' it did EVERYTHING
- three tier system - Business Logic, COBOL files and their own version of screen
handling syntax. Nothing much around then to touch it. Along come Jobs and Gates,
and the COBOL standard starts to fall behind on the market's requirement.

*************

Current comments :-

Consider, COBOL is partially 'weakened' by :-

- SQL instead of COBOL files, (the 70% above)
- GUIs are non-standard - so go for it in another language which is more
        accommodating
- Standard class structure - still to be developed (although F/J, M/F and Hitachi
        have their customized versions)
- Report Writer
- Screen Section

The last two - who needs them ? Crystal Reports and Windowing.

Take into account the 'replacements/deletions' above - what are you left with in
COBOL - a neat set of RESERVED WORDS that do IF, EVALUATE, PERFORM, REFERENCE MOD
etc....

Now there are currently three ways to write a COBOL program :-

1. Procedural - no invoking of OO
2. Procedural Programs that invoke OO/tools as necessary
3. Complete OO using the Class structure with methods

As it stands we know that the major area is #1 with a gradual shift into #2 where
necessary, ('Gotta get into this e-business !').

Back to the statistics and the IT Managers visualizing using OO - they aren't
thinking about #3 in the slightest - they are aiming themselves at #2  (They may
not know that, but I do <G>). It's worth noting Pete speculated on a further 10
years for COBOL. It may well have been how the survey question was posed, but note
the IT Managers in looking forward to the short-term future, also come up with the
magic '10'.

As for #3 - I think there's me and two others who have taken this approach <G>.
Pity - ignoring GUIs, as a standalone OO COBOL offers some pretty nifty features -
like this one :-

    ClassA, ClassB, ClassC, ClassD, ClassE, ClassF

I can pass the handles (object references) of each of the above individual classes
to all the others - they now have the ability to invoke one another. OK, not too
often you would want to do this - but something like Simulation/Forecasting ? (But
I do have situations where A invokes either B or C and C can return values by
invoking either A or B, and when finished, you are still back in C).

> Already there are C++ and Java classes for CICS and DB2. But these are
> mainly for API interface to the OS and TP.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don't hold your breath about IBM - Apart from any minor 'tweaks', COBOL 200X is
already well set, including OO with the exception of standard classes
(collections/dictionaries), still to be determined. Based on what has been fairly
static in the 'Holy Book' the latest offerings from IBM could already have taken
account of the needed changes you suggest. Their current position appears to be -
let's hang a few  extras onto COBOL that let's our guys call Java.

Doesn't help you much because you specifically mention IBM, but Micro Focus have
come out with a new mainframe tool that turns your 'legacy code'/green screens
into Components.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T02:13:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gf209.185291$eF5.4798754@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <3D3F772E.59A696AA@shaw.ca> <uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LOL! I have been avoiding saying anything in this discussion but really have enjoyed the give and take in it.

The reason I avoided joining in was that I was pretty sure you would come to something like the conclusion you came to below. Good
programmers and Software Engineers always do. *Always.*

There is no such critter as 'pure OO' out there - not even in SmallTalk, which is about as cloudy and OO as you can get and still
have a language that does anything useful. That whole bunch of shenanigans was started by <nameless but easily recognized names> who
were caught up, in the 1980's, in the excitement of the stuff.  So wars started, and people sneered at COBOL and predicted
programmers would all be put out of their jobs because computer programs would write themselves. As well as gobs of other such junk.

Not a bit of it was true then, and not a bit of it is true now.

What OO technology is - plain and simple - is the formal codification of those practices that programmers have found makes the best
software product. Encapsulation and isolation of functionality into small discrete segments of code, and identification and
explanation of data used in those programs as separate elements.

Really - there is nothing new about that. Assembler programmers have done it for decades.
Good COBOL programmers do it (in sections or paragraphs, but they do it.)
C programmers do it ("functions should be small enough to fit on a screen page!")
Pascal programmers do it in a more readable way than C programmers
Ada programmers do it in the ultimate functional way, and make the code look like English besides.

Yes, there is now, and always has been a small penalty paid in execution efficiency for that kind of
structure and design in code, but except in some very specific cases, it is definitely worth the cost.

Objects are not physical entities, even when instantiated in computer memory. They are merely ways for people (programmers) to group
related things together, and any kind of slavish adherence to somebody's
academic point of view otherwise is pretty much counter productive. Objects all have data in them, and most have code in them as
well. If well written, the code works on the data.

Some *languages* provide a lot of other functionality as well; for example, private and public variables and
methods/functions/procedures/modules/routines/paragraphs/whatever.

My view on OO COBOL is that it is a great way to interface to other languages and services; but it doesn't add much to the straight
forward Data processing tasks that COBOL excels at. Object Oriented Design, however, does. And it has every time that it has been
applied to a task in every language and environment I know of.

But then again, every good programmer on the planet uses some form of Object Oriented Design, pretty much whether they are aware of
it or not.

Thanks for bringing up such an interesting conversation.


"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:uk09ji8pd43n8c@corp.supernews.com...
>
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T12:34:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahpd3e02fvh@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> You claimed that I somehow identified "that the object
provides
> no benefit. I made no such claim.

That's definitely an extrapolation on my part, but from what
you've said:

> Much of the data contained in business systems is
representational;
> that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior. One of
the
> descriptions of object-orientation is "objects interacting".
Because
> representational 'objects' have no behavior, they cannot
interact
> with other objects.

> The behavioral abstractions that are discussed as part of
> object-orientation do not occur as often in business
applications
> as in other applications.

> I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL because the
characteristics
> that make for 'good' OO applications are different than the
> characteristics of most DP.

This sounds like you don't think that object-oriented design,
in this scenario, is of benefit.

> You claim that the statement is "tautological." It is not.

> It is not worthwhile to respond to your false claims.
> Stop making them.

These are purely academic discourse -- isn't that what your
were seeking?

> ----------------
>
> In OO-by-the-book, the representation of objects on external
> storage is different than objects in memory. On external
strorage,
> objects may have foriegn keys. The process of bringing
objects
> into memory requires resolution of the external references
> to internal references. Internally, foriegn keys are resolved
to
> object references.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> is returned directly.

> -----------
> While this answers one of my concerns about having to
> load the complete system in "pure" OO or OO-by-the-book,

So you don't need to have "all objects in memory at one time,"
then?

> there are others such as the replacement of group items
> and redefinitions by object references. This also has
> performance issues.

Object references are a tremendous boon to efficient memory
management -- they have the benefits of pointers, with the
added security of type enforcement.  As I count it, that's two
reasons for OO, not an issue.

> This may be resolved by deferring
> their creation; but this would mean keeping a copy of
> the external record with the object.

There are suggestions of this in other parts of your writing,
so I wonder if you're simply mapping a class definition to your
file record definitions.  Although this may be applicable in
some cases, it's not a good monolithic approach -- you have to
take a more holistic view of the data and processes.

> There is also the problem of creating the correct class
> when loading the object.
>
> Based upon prior responses, it is not likely I will get any
> help from the so-called "experts".

Although I don't qualify myself as an export, I have an idea
why this thread has not become much of a dialog.  You haven't
asked for theory -- you've asserted why OO was not of general
use in DP applications.

The liabilities you've mentioned appear to stem from the nature
of your approach.  I truly am interested in discussing
different theories, but you seem committed to your current
ones.

Sincerely, I have a dispassionate interest in this field, if
you're interested in a (perhaps wrong-headed) view of OO that
works, I'd like to trade ideas with you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T17:06:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uk0pksfient932@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <ahpd3e02fvh@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```

Grinder <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:ahpd3e02fvh@enews3.newsguy.com...
> > You claimed that I somehow identified "that the object
> provides
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> in this scenario, is of benefit.

But there is a difference between "the object has no benefit"
and "object-oriented design has no benefit." I never implied
the first and, for the second, I was questioning 'how much'
benefit OO provides for business.

The use of "object", by its position in the thread, could have
referred to the discussion of "sale" object. It should be obvious
that sale objects do have benefit to a business!

Consider "... behavioral abstractions ...do not occur as often
in business applications ... ." Some behavioral abstractions
do occur, therefore OO may be of benefit there. With
representational objects, behavioral abstractions do not occur;
therefore "where is the benefit of OO?"

> > You claim that the statement is "tautological." It is not.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> were seeking?

Yes, but on certain aspects of software engineering -- not
philosophy. "Tautological" and "domain" set me off. <g>

> > ----------------
[snip]
> > -----------
> > While this answers one of my concerns about having to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> then?

Yes, once I realized that I could remain true to a design without
violating OO-by-the-book.

> > there are others such as the replacement of group items
> > and redefinitions by object references. This also has
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> reasons for OO, not an issue.

For some processing, memory management may overwhelm
the time for processing a record and there are many
applications which use only one record of one type at a time.
It is not difficult to imagine simple programs using 100 times
more processor time. This inefficiency may be eliminated
by using SQL to get the same result in less time -- an indication
that there are times when the usage level of OO should be zero.

> > This may be resolved by deferring
> > their creation; but this would mean keeping a copy of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> take a more holistic view of the data and processes.

It may not be the best for a new design or a transformed
design; but anyone converting to OO would probably begin
with the existing file layouts and modify them over time;
unless they are using or convert to a database, first.

> > There is also the problem of creating the correct class
> > when loading the object.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> use in DP applications.

I didn't ask for theory because I was I was promoting one;
that there is some processing where OO might not be a
good idea. This is a negative view of OO. Advocates
don't like negative views of what they advocate.

> The liabilities you've mentioned appear to stem from the nature
> of your approach.  I truly am interested in discussing
> different theories, but you seem committed to your current
> ones.

Of course, I believe the liabilities stem from the nature of OO. <g>

> Sincerely, I have a dispassionate interest in this field, if
> you're interested in a (perhaps wrong-headed) view of OO that
> works, I'd like to trade ideas with you.

Go ahead; but keep it in the group and you might want to
change the subject line.
```
