```
> that sale objects do have benefit to a business!

I've re-read the original post with this in mind.  It still
does sound like a cohesive argument for why object-oriented
design, in data processing applications, does not provide
_enough_ benefit to warrant the overhead.  Am I getting warmer?

> Consider "... behavioral abstractions ...do not occur as
often
> in business applications ... ." Some behavioral abstractions
> do occur, therefore OO may be of benefit there. With
> representational objects, behavioral abstractions do not
occur;
> therefore "where is the benefit of OO?"

not.
> >
> > > It is not worthwhile to respond to your false claims.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> philosophy. "Tautological" and "domain" set me off. <g>

I don't understand -- what's the implication that's offensive?

This was the loop as I comprehended it:  (Please do adjust any
miscomprehensions I have.)

1)  Object in data processing applications are
"representational."

2)  Representatial objects do not realize the benefits of
object-oriented design -- or at least to the extent that they
justify the overhead.

3)  Therefore, data processing applications do not realize the
benefits of object-oriented design -- or at least ...

In retrospect, this isn't a tautology, but rather a straw man.
The original supposition, in my opinion only, is so confining,
that the conclusion would have to be true.

I would like to begin again.  There's definitely a lot of room
for differing abstractions, but ultimately they have to fit
together -- that's 1/2 the fun of discussions like these.

> > > there are others such as the replacement of group items
> > > and redefinitions by object references. This also has
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > added security of type enforcement.  As I count it, that's
two
> > reasons for OO, not an issue.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is not difficult to imagine simple programs using 100
times
> more processor time. This inefficiency may be eliminated
> by using SQL to get the same result in less time -- an
indication
> that there are times when the usage level of OO should be
zero.

Great, this recognizable ground.  It sounds totally correct to
me that instantiating one object at a time, over and over,
could more optimally performed.  This lies at the heart of my
argument though -- not all objects need represent a single
persistent storage record.

If you application warrants it, a job-type object can be
constructed to handle batch updates or reporting functions.
The may sound like I'm conveniently bending object-orientation
to fit the hypothetical, but that's largely what OO provides.

> > > This may be resolved by deferring
> > > their creation; but this would mean keeping a copy of
> > > the external record with the object.
> >
> > There are suggestions of this in other parts of your
writing,
> > so I wonder if you're simply mapping a class definition to
your
> > file record definitions.  Although this may be applicable
in
> > some cases, it's not a good monolithic approach -- you have
to
> > take a more holistic view of the data and processes.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> unless they are using or convert to a database, first.

I've found a benefical approach is to map the system.  The data
model is important, but it's the processes, and how they relate
to the data, that tends to segregate the system into
discernable objects.

> > > There is also the problem of creating the correct class
> > > when loading the object.
> > >
> > > Based upon prior responses, it is not likely I will get
any
> > > help from the so-called "experts".
> >
> > Although I don't qualify myself as an export, I have an
idea
> > why this thread has not become much of a dialog.  You
haven't
> > asked for theory -- you've asserted why OO was not of
general
> > use in DP applications.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't like negative views of what they advocate.

> > The liabilities you've mentioned appear to stem from the
nature
> > of your approach.  I truly am interested in discussing
> > different theories, but you seem committed to your current
> > ones.
>
> Of course, I believe the liabilities stem from the nature of
OO. <g>

There's bound to be some truth in that, but in this case I
genuine feel like your theory was founded upon shakey ground.

> > Sincerely, I have a dispassionate interest in this field,
if
> > you're interested in a (perhaps wrong-headed) view of OO
that
> > works, I'd like to trade ideas with you.

(excerpt from above)
> Of course, I believe the liabilities stem from the nature of
OO. <g>

How convinced are you?
```

```
Grinder <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:ahqftl015f4@enews2.newsguy.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> _enough_ benefit to warrant the overhead.  Am I getting warmer?

Actually, it says nothing about whether OO is beneficial,
or not, it merely states the we (may) need to think differently
about OO. In other words, if we do think differently about OO,
we might find it beneficial, or we may not find it beneficial,
or we might find it beneficial in some cases but not in others.

> > Consider "... behavioral abstractions ...do not occur as
> often
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't understand -- what's the implication that's offensive?

Not implication -- interruption.

You claimed the argument tautological, that is the conclusion
is true whether the premise is true or false; but you failed to
support your claim. That is, what premise results in the
conclusion being true, always?

Later you stated the domain had some effect; but failed
to explain how it affected the argument.

I wanted to concentrate on the software engineering aspects.
It takes time for programmers to process interrupts, too! <g>

> This was the loop as I comprehended it:  (Please do adjust any
> miscomprehensions I have.)
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> together -- that's 1/2 the fun of discussions like these.

Actually, I would like to end this part!

OO is technology used by programmers to write applications.
Programmers need to think about the applications they write.
Business applications are different than other applications.
Programmers need think differently when using OO in
    business applications.

> > > > there are others such as the replacement of group items
> > > > and redefinitions by object references. This also has
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> to fit the hypothetical, but that's largely what OO provides.

One of the best examples I have seen was an
Input-Process-Output abstract class. The Process method
had a loop getting objects from the Input method, passing
the objects to the Output method. The programmer need
only override the Input and Output methods to provide
the functionality. Neatest thing I ever saw and got me
really excited about OO.

> > > > This may be resolved by deferring
> > > > their creation; but this would mean keeping a copy of
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> discernable objects.

But there is nothing in what you are saying that is
particular to OO.

AP101 Edit
AP102 Sort
AP103 ...

The discernable objects are records, the processes are
whatever they need to be. Been doing that for decades. <G>

> > > > There is also the problem of creating the correct class
> > > > when loading the object.
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> How convinced are you?

I am absolutely convinced that OO is a good thing much
of the time; but the nature of OO is such that its use
under some circumstances is detrimental and in others
questionable.

I have seen some examples where authors have
suggested adding classes to decouple 'this' from
'that' without clearly stating why, as if, decoupling is
of sufficient benefit to justify the added complexity.

Earlier today, I devised a means for decoupling the
entire file system from an application. This removes
all files and even the notion of files from every existing
program. There is no reason to do it unless further
enhancement is anticipated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

```
> > > Yes, but on certain aspects of software engineering --
not
> > > philosophy. "Tautological" and "domain" set me off. <g>
> >
> > I don't understand -- what's the implication that's
offensive?
>
> Not implication -- interruption.

How can you possibly interrupt someone on a message board?

> You claimed the argument tautological, that is the conclusion
> is true whether the premise is true or false; but you failed
to
> support your claim. That is, what premise results in the
> conclusion being true, always?

I thought a tautology is a circular argument, not a
disconnected one.

> Later you stated the domain had some effect; but failed
> to explain how it affected the argument.

Here's a snippet of the exchanged where I used "domain":

Grinder> This is tautological.  You state the form of the
object,
Grinder> then proceed to identify that the object provides no
Grinder> benefit.

Rick> I made no such claim!

Grinder> Sure you have, by setting the domain of the object.

By setting the domain, I was referring to your statement that
the hypothetical object was "representational."  I though I'm
unfamiliar with this term, I worked with the definition you
gave for for it: "representational 'objects' have no behavior"

By identifying these object as such, you've restricted the
domain in which they operate.

I get the feeling, though that "domain" has a more specific
(and offensive) meaning to you?

> I wanted to concentrate on the software engineering aspects.
> It takes time for programmers to process interrupts, too! <g>
>
> > This was the loop as I comprehended it:  (Please do adjust
any
> > miscomprehensions I have.)
> >
> > I would like to begin again.  There's definitely a lot of
room
> > for differing abstractions, but ultimately they have to fit
> > together -- that's 1/2 the fun of discussions like these.
>
> Actually, I would like to end this part!

Why keep responding then?  I would like to discuss the ideas at
hand, but we can't get anywhere if you're only response to my
criticisms are "you're wrong."

> OO is technology used by programmers to write applications.
> Programmers need to think about the applications they write.
> Business applications are different than other applications.
> Programmers need think differently when using OO in
>     business applications.

Now that is a tautology, by-the-book:

tau.tol.o.gy \t�-"t�-l?-j�\ noun pl tau.tol.o.gies
   1 a : needless repetition of an idea, statement, or word

> One of the best examples I have seen was an
> Input-Process-Output abstract class. The Process method
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> really excited about OO.

Wouldn't this apply to data processing applications fairly
well?

> > I've found a benefical approach is to map the system.  The
data
> > model is important, but it's the processes, and how they
relate
> > to the data, that tends to segregate the system into
> > discernable objects.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> whatever they need to be. Been doing that for decades. <G>

There's one more step, though.  Some, if not all, of those
processes will become methods on an object.

I'm not claiming that OO invented this, it's just a means for
associating process with data -- which should be at the heart
of an object's design.

> I am absolutely convinced that OO is a good thing much
> of the time; but the nature of OO is such that its use
> under some circumstances is detrimental and in others
> questionable.

I thought your original posting was outlining one/some of those
latter circumstances, but your argument appears to have
broadened a bit.

> I have seen some examples where authors have
> suggested adding classes to decouple 'this' from
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> enhancement is anticipated.

I don't think I've conviced you of anything, but you are
more-and-more saying things that I agree with.

Taking an existing non-OO system and re-rolling it to be OO
needs to have some justification.  Just putting it into an OO
for is not enough.  For new development, or significiant
re-factoring of code, though, I've yet to see a persuasive
argument against it.
```

```
On 26-Jul-2002, "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:

> Taking an existing non-OO system and re-rolling it to be OO
> needs to have some justification.  Just putting it into an OO
> for is not enough.  For new development, or significiant
> re-factoring of code, though, I've yet to see a persuasive

Unfortunately the argument is similar to the one where you don't need fix
the leaky roof when it's sunny, and can't when it's raining:

If you don't have programmers and analysts who are skilled in OO for your
business, do you want them to start off when you do a major redesign &
rewrite?
```

```
don't need fix
> the leaky roof when it's sunny, and can't when it's raining:
>
> If you don't have programmers and analysts who are skilled in
OO for your
> business, do you want them to start off when you do a major
redesign &
> rewrite?

No, I don't think it's a good idea to not know what you're
doing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bkb09.25053$dk1.1777164@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <ahpd3e02fvh@enews3.newsguy.com> <uk0pksfient932@corp.supernews.com>`

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

You keep repeating this over and over like a mantra, and have not given a

probably the most common, and first behavioral abstraction ever device by
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T12:42:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uk2uli3imtfb7b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <ahj1ef012rk@enews1.newsguy.com> <ujr0atn071kl5e@corp.supernews.com> <ahke13029qm@enews4.newsguy.com> <ujro43f5m97abc@corp.supernews.com> <ahl2va020bi@enews2.newsguy.com> <ujuhdhqp3bto05@corp.supernews.com> <ahpd3e02fvh@enews3.newsguy.com> <uk0pksfient932@corp.supernews.com> <Bkb09.25053$dk1.1777164@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:Bkb09.25053$dk1.1777164@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
> > >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> abstraction first.

We have a difference in definitions. "Symbolic" and
"behavioral" are not the same kind of abstractions.

Symbolic abstraction refer to things: customer - meaning
a customer record (object), purchase order - meaning order
information with a collection of line items, etc.

Behavioral abstractions are the things we do with symbolic
abstractions: Deposit money - means increase the amount
in a checking account, edit thisRecord - means, that whatever
values thisRecord has, make sure they are valid, etc.


But what I was really trying to point out is that behavioral
abstraction is a term used in CS and SE to explain their
concepts of object-oriented programming.

In the rest of the paragraph (not shown), I was suggesting
that maybe business application developers need to use
the terms with which we are familiar. These terms do not
include behavior or state.

I am suggesting that, instead of saying 'edit ap-trans'
is the 'edit' behavior of 'ap-trans', we should be saying
that 'edit' is a process of 'ap-trans' and that the content
of 'balance' is not its state value, it is simply an amount.

It's another way of thinking differently about OO in business
applications.
```

#### ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T09:42:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D3D16FD.64C3B270@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Example: A sale event creates a sale 'object' that represents the
> sale. If the item sold is returned, a sale-return event occurs that
> creates a sale-return 'object' that represents that event. The
> original sale 'object' has no change in its state.

Would it be 'picky' tp point out that I mark original sales items with
sales-return data (credit note number, date, quantity returned) when I
pull out the price, discount and cost data for the returned item.  This
ensures that items are not returned more than once.  It also indicates
the return if the original invoice is re-printed.

In general I entirely agree with you.  OO techniques can be seen as a
solution to problems that Cobol and its applications do not have.  Or
specically have been solved over many years in entirely different ways.

C++ for example arose from C as a way of overcoming C's limitations in
representing data.  C only supports int, float and char in various ways
and arrays.  If, eg, it was required to handle a complex type (or other
struct) then it cannot be done with the normal operators and a library
of routines must be constructed with each operation handled by a routine
or family of routines.

In Cobol a MOVE can work on a record, group, elementary item, redefines,
or renames.  In C an assignment can only operate on the built-in types
and, eg, strcpy() is required for strings.  This leads to 'name space'
pollution as dozens of different libraries must be used for various
types of data.

C++ solves this by using the same names (and names of operators) and
having the compiler detect the types involved and name-mangle to
generate unique routine names - or use dynamic tables at run-time.

The Cobol MOVE does all so this aspect of OO is not required (though it
may be useful).

> Second, the nature of 'good' OO applications is that they have
> all objects in memory at one time. This may be seen by looking
> at OO's roots in simulation and modeling. 

Of course very many OO applications are inherently single-user and would
have no concept of sharing and locking.  Examples in many books that try
to deal with 'real-world' applications seem to fail miserably to explain
the issues that _I_ and my users need to care about.

I have even seen exaples proposed where the code incorporates the actual
data.  eg a payroll where there was an employee class and then they
instanced 'Jim' and 'Fred' (ie Employee Jim = new Employee(); ). 
_Excuse_me_ but _one_ of us is completely clueless about applications.

It might work when it is drawing circles and squares, but this cannot be
translated into actual data processing.

Certainly one can derive classes for data accessing, but it is very
unlikely that there would ever be more than one instance of each class. 
This can all be done with called routines incorporating COPY code anyway
and so doesn't provide any _significant_ advantage that overrides the
additional complexity.

In many cases the flexability of Cobol's dynamic calling can avoid the
need that C needed C++ for.  My Report Script interpreter calls the
file(s) being reported on by constructing the appropriate routine names
and calling them.  Statically linked languages can't do that.

> This does not mean that I feel there are no opportunities to
> use OO COBOL. It only means that I feel we must not jump into
> OO without first ensuring that an application is well-suited
> to OO.

Exactly, and v.v.  Much of the OO enthusiasm is over GUI which does
appear to be well suited for OO, in fact almost requires it - only
'almost' though.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T20:18:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujp7p3jm5grq2e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <3D3D16FD.64C3B270@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D3D16FD.64C3B270@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the return if the original invoice is re-printed.

No, it is not 'picky'. The OO solution to separate representational
from behavior could be to implement classes to hold the sale
and sale-return data and a class to aggregate the two. This last
class holds object references to the sale and sale-return objects.
If the object reference to the sale-return is NULL, the item has
not been returned. This is a pure OO solution that adds complexity.
Why do it?

> In general I entirely agree with you.  OO techniques can be seen as a
> solution to problems that Cobol and its applications do not have.  Or
> specically have been solved over many years in entirely different ways.

Yes, absolutely! One of the uses of OO in other languages has
been to raise the power of the language up to the level available
in COBOL. I have a 700 page book that discusses the OO
implementation of indexed files in C++ -- a problem COBOL
does not have.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T13:45:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahjmjv$6ap$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <3D3D16FD.64C3B270@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On 23-Jul-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Exactly, and v.v.  Much of the OO enthusiasm is over GUI which does
> appear to be well suited for OO, in fact almost requires it - only
> 'almost' though.

The main advantage of using OO for GUI is that you can get "good enough"
testing without getting a sign-off from every single user testing every
single instance of an object.

But when our object is a business rule, with real money - we can't get away
without much more rigorous testing.   This leads us to prefer isolation of
code over integration as we can test and implement just our change without
having to get sign-offs from half of our world.   With such isolation, the
most widely touted advantages of OO are gone and the cost benefit of
switching to OO is different.   Enough different that nobody can point to an
enterprise CoBOL shop that has moved its business programs to OO CoBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T16:06:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahjuse$9nu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <3D3D16FD.64C3B270@Azonic.co.nz> <ahjmjv$6ap$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RXd%8.3121$dk1.722097@news20.bellglobal.com> <ahjr8g$89i$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gBe%8.3134$dk1.736093@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On 23-Jul-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Five full scale Cobol OOP systems running full time, and handling 30-40
> million dollars worth of transactions a day each.  The largest is North
> American Wide, and integrated into SAP.
>
> Or does "enterprise" mean "the same mainframe I am using"?

No - but that would be most useful to me.  I am asking these questions
primarily to gather ammunition that may be useful to get consideration for
using OO CoBOL in MY job.  But the hardware isn't nearly as important as
other aspects of the environment.

What hardware are you using?   What systems does the CoBOL support?  What
databases?  How many programmers are maintaining the system?

The last may sound like a funny question - but much of the reason for using
CoBOL is because it is an easy to maintain language - and the fear is that
OO CoBOL will be expensive to maintain - so I want a handle on what types of
maintenance needs are anticipated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T13:14:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37g%8.6106$DN4.840884@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <3D3D16FD.64C3B270@Azonic.co.nz> <ahjmjv$6ap$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RXd%8.3121$dk1.722097@news20.bellglobal.com> <ahjr8g$89i$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gBe%8.3134$dk1.736093@news20.bellglobal.com> <ahjuse$9nu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:ahjuse$9nu$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 23-Jul-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

PC networks.  The os's involved are windows, NT, and Linux.  The largest
system is about 75 machines with 15-20 programmers.  The smallest is 5
machines and one programmer.  The Cobol code has evolved over close to
twenty years, so is extensive.

The Cobol is handled by one programer in each case.  The data bases are
mixed, some are buried in OOP because they are parts of other systems, so I
cannot see them.  I just ask for data and get it. What language gave it to
me, I do not have a clue.

What data I have and they need, I give them methods for, suitable to their
languages. They can get at my data, and even ask me to do updates, but they
do not have a clue how I do my stuff internally.  In fact, I use Isam for
most local data bases.

> The last may sound like a funny question - but much of the reason for
using
> CoBOL is because it is an easy to maintain language - and the fear is that
> OO CoBOL will be expensive to maintain - so I want a handle on what types
of
> maintenance needs are anticipated.
```

### OCX FILE

- **From:** "Dionisis Vrionis" <info@dksoft.gr>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T12:15:18+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahgif7$2gsg$1@ulysses.noc.ntua.gr>`

```
I'm inserting in custom controls of powercobol a special datagrid (Janus
Grid).It's working very good.I decide to delete from the form and i take a
warning like-- kernel bad or some like that .Then powercobol send a report
to Microsoft.When i get in again in powercobol and cuting the ocx from the
menu then it's work good and don't crash.
```

#### ↳ Re: OCX FILE

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T08:05:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d3d9a7f_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ahgif7$2gsg$1@ulysses.noc.ntua.gr>`

```
Some versions of PowerCOBOL (4 and 5) gave kernel errors if certain .DLLs in
the Operating system had were not compatible with the RTS, under specific
circumstances. I seem to recall having some kernel errors when using OCX
controls under version 5.

There are fixes for this that are downloadable from www.adtools.com and you
should report the problem to Fujitsu support.

Version 6 has no such problems. (AT least, iof it has, I haven't found
them...) and i use third party OCXs as well as my own COM controls with it.

Pete.
```

## Thread: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe — 26 this month / 32 total

### Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T16:53:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Someone had some code for dynamic file allocation from within IBM Cobol, but
I have had some trouble getting it to work.

Here's some code:
FD  TEST-FILE
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
    RECORDING MODE IS F
    BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS.
01  TEST-RECORD PIC X(80).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  TEST-STATUS        PIC XX.
01  RC                 PIC 9(9) COMP.
01  RC-DISPLAY         PIC 9(9).
01  ADDR-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
01  DYNALLOC.
    05 FILLER          PIC X(12)  VALUE
    'DYNAMDD=DSN('.
    05 DYN-DSN         PIC X(17).
    05 FILLER          PIC X(90)  VALUE
    'NEW TRACKS SPACE(1,1) CATALOG STORCLAS(STANDARD) MGMTCLAS(WO
    'RK)'.
  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  6 Line(s) not Displayed
    MOVE 'D5.P.NOR.GDGTEST(+1))' TO DYN-DSN.
  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  3 Line(s) not Displayed
    CALL 'PUTENV'
      USING BY VALUE ADDR-POINTER
      RETURNING RC.
    IF RC NOT = ZERO
        MOVE RC TO RC-DISPLAY
        DISPLAY 'PUTENV FAILED, RC = ' RC-DISPLAY
        GOBACK.
    OPEN OUTPUT TEST-FILE.


One thing, we don't have GDGs available here which may be part of my problem
- but I changed the file name.  I would like to dynamically send the output
to either a file or a printer with forms and destination.  I got a file
status of 98 and "IGZ0252W An invalid delimiter was found at position 22 in
environment variable DYNAMDD while processing file TEST-FILE".

What should I play with to get this working?
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

```
Howard,
  A couple of things:

1) To use "putenv" and be supported you *must* use NODYNAM and
PGMNAME(LONGMIXED). See:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.3


2) The correct name of the program to call  is "putenv" *not* "PUTENV". See:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.2.4


Finally, I am NOT positive that GDG's are supported.  Have you tried the
same program with a "simple" DSName?  I am going to check further on this.
```

```
I added PGM(LONGMIXED) and changed to a lower case call, but it didn't
compile cleanly:
A user-defined word was found as a "PROGRAM-ID" name under the
"PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)" compiler option.  When
"PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)" is in effect, a literal is expected for the
"PROGRAM-ID" name.  The name was accepted in
its uppercased format.

I changed my PROGRAM-ID to lower case and put quotes around it as per the
example and it compiled OK, but didn't link:
IEW2606S 4B39 MODULE INCORPORATES PROGRAM MANAGEMENT 3 FEATURES AND CANNOT
BE SAVED IN LOAD MODULE FORMAT.

What does it mean having a PROGRAM-ID lower case in quotes?   Is that field
used?  Is it's name meaningful?  Why won't it compile?

Thanks for your help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T17:20:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahi0fm$6qt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahhge3$e1i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <ahhm52$49r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
The world of LONGMIXED and/or POSIX is "confusing".

If you compile with LONGMIXED, you want your PROGRAM-ID to be in quotes -
but it may (probably SHOULD BE) all CAPS.  HOWEVER, you want your CALL
"literal" (putenv) to be lower-case.

Hope this helps.

P.S.  You can put PGMNAME(LONGMIXED) in a CBL card *in* your source code and
not require Endevor to process it for you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T18:08:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahi39f$dpe$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahhge3$e1i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am the originator of this program. When I tested this it DID
work.

1) NODYNAM *WAS* required. Despite what the manual says, PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)
   *WAS NOT* required. PGMNAME(COMPAT) worked just fine. To use LONGMIXED
   you MUST use the pre-linker before the link, with COMPAT you do not need
   the pre-link.

2) Despite what the manual says both 'PUTENV' and 'putenv' worked the same.

3) GDG's *ARE* supported and worked fine.

I suspect the posters problem is PIC X(17) for DYN-DSN. Either he
has truncated the closing ')' for his dataset name or has not included
the required SPACE after it.


----------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<ahi6rq$qn7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahhge3$e1i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <ahi39f$dpe$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`

```
Ron,
  Sorry - I thought that I posted this already - but may not have.  The
following is what IBM says about NOT using LONGMIXED and/or using PUTENV,

"Bill,

PUTENV and GETENV might work, but it is not documented in any C or LE books.

C function names are mangled to get them down to 8 characters, but in the
case of short names the name is sometimes just folded to uppercase in
SCEELKED.

This cannot be counted on, in general, and other C functions may not behave
this way.
Using LONGMIXED, BINDER, and SCEELKEX will work for ALL C functions, while
trying to call lowercase function names with uppercase is guaranteed to fail
in most cases. For example, how would you call _getenv (different from
getenv)?"

As far as LONGMIXED and the pre-linker, can you say WHY you think this is
required?  According to:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/2.4.39.3

"When using the extended character set supported by PGMNAME(LONGMIXED), be
sure to use names that conform to the linkage-editor, binder, prelinker, or
system conventions that apply, depending on the mechanism used to resolve
the names. "

but I haven't tried it without this - so you MIGHT be correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T11:41:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahk0ut$1vs$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahhge3$e1i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <ahi39f$dpe$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <ahi6rq$qn7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> As far as LONGMIXED and the pre-linker, can you say WHY you think this is
> required?  According to:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> but I haven't tried it without this - so you MIGHT be correct.

When using LONGMIXED I believe certain LE elements are included in the
load module that otherwise are not included. The pre-link resolves these
LE modules in preparation for the final link. Anyway, if you do not
pre-link when using LONGMIXED you get this error from the link-edit.

IEW2606S 4B39 MODULE INCORPORATES PROGRAM MANAGEMENT 3 FEATURES AND CANNOT
BE SAVED IN LOAD MODULE FORMAT.

When you do pre-link, the final link works successfully.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

```
Ron,
  What was your setting for the DLL compiler option?  As indicated in
IBM-MAIN, the messages that you are getting have to do with PDSE vs PDS -
not PGMNAME (or at least not that I  know of).

If you look at:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/4.3.4

It does say that *IF* you are creating a DLL *and* you do not use PDSE, THEN
you must use the pre-linker.  However, I don't see anything (but could be
missing it) that says that you must use DLL's if you are using
PGMNAME(LONGMIXED).
```

```
When I played around with getting this to work at my shop, I
discovered that contrary to the manuals, I needed commas between the
various parms (space, disp, etc). I got nasty soc4's without it
whining about invalid delimiters.  The manual claims spaces work. For
me they didn't. I needed commas.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T00:46:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c231e2$55d9c420$d7ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Here is another way:

      IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. COBDYNAL.
      *===============================================================*
      * This program dynamically allocates and deallocates a file
      * using the BPXWDYN IBM subroutine. No DD statements for the
      * file sould appear in the JCL:
      *===============================================================*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT FILEIN ASSIGN TO FILEIN.
      /
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  FILEIN
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS FILEIN-REC.
       01  FILEIN-REC.
           05 FILLER             PIC X(80).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  INPUT-REC             PIC X(80).
       01  IN-COUNT              PIC 9(9)   BINARY VALUE 0.
       01  SUB-PGM               PIC X(8)   VALUE 'BPXWDYN '.
       01  ALLOC-CMD.
           05  FILLER            PIC X(50)  VALUE
               ' ALLOC DD(FILEIN) DSN(EGHF3PY.TEMP80 MSG(WTP) OLD '.
           05  FILLER            PIC X(50)  VALUE
               '                                                  '.

       01 COMMAND-STRING.
           05 C-S-LEN            PIC S9(4)   BINARY VALUE +100.
           05 C-S-DATA.
               07 FILLER         PIC X(100).
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       INITIAL-STUFF.

           MOVE ALLOC-CMD TO C-S-DATA.
           CALL SUB-PGM USING COMMAND-STRING.

           OPEN INPUT FILEIN.

       READER.
           READ FILEIN INTO INPUT-REC,
               AT END GO TO E-O-J.
           ADD +1 TO IN-COUNT.
           DISPLAY FILEIN-REC.
           GO TO READER.

       E-O-J.
           CLOSE FILEIN.
           MOVE 'FREE DD(FILEIN)' TO C-S-DATA.
           CALL SUB-PGM USING COMMAND-STRING.
           GOBACK.

```

- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c231e2$55d9c420$d7ccf943@chottel> <ahjm4u$63j$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahjn4b$6in$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahjo9a$72i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
See: ftp://ftp.software.ibm.com/s390/zos/tools/bpxwdyn/bpxwdyn.html

The above explains all the various parameters accepted by BPXWDYN better
than I can.

I am not sure what an "SDSF form" is.  I believe SDSF is a TSO option for
looking at job output?  If it is a dataset you should be able to allocate
it.  Depending upon the parameters you use, the most common problem you
might experience is contention for a dataset.  If you specify OLD and some
other job is already using the dataset, your job may have to wait to get
exculsive control of the dataset.

<ahjo9a$72i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...

program
> works fine.
>

or

>
```

```
I got this code from an earlier posting and am no expert.  I will
investigate the unbalanced parenthesis.  You can find documentation for
BPXWDYN at:

ftp://ftp.software.ibm.com/s390/zos/tools/bpxwdyn/bpxwdyn.html

I believe what you coded below is equivalent to:

//FILEIN DD DSN=UMS.D44201.DYN,DISP=OLD

MSG(WTP) causes messages to be written to the job log.

Since the dataset already exists (OLD) the DCB in your program is filled in
so that it points to the DSN=
just as if you coded the JCL above. You can open it and process it and
close it.  Then if you FREE it you can the do another ALLOC and make the
DCB point to another file that you want to process.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T01:31:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2337a$e01cb680$3dc2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c231e2$55d9c420$d7ccf943@chottel> <ahjm4u$63j$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahjn4b$6in$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c232b0$daa56320$39c2f943@chottel> <ahmg6q$gel$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
This reply is based solely upon reading the documentation from the URL that
I posted.
For ALLOC the following specifiy dataset status: MOD, NEW, OLD, SHR and the
following specify data set disposition: CATALOG, DELETE, KEEP, UNCATALOG.

The equivalent to DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE) would be:
    ALLOC NEW CATALOG DD(----) DSN(----)            followed later by:
    ALLOC OLD DELETE     DD(----) DSN(----)

The equivalent to DISP=(MOD,DELETE) would be :
    ALLOC MOD DELETE    DD(-----) DSN(----)

The documentation advices that it is easier to understand messages via
MSG(WTP) than trying to deal with the return codes.

For FREE the following dispositions are available: CATALOG, DELETE, KEEP,
UNCATALOG
If you don't specify one of these with your FREE it probably does not know
what to do with your request.

I re-tested my original posting with and without the missing parenthesis
and it worked fine on my system both ways.  I am pretty busy right now or I
would experiment and get back to you with my results.  Again I have NOT yet
tested my recommendation from above.  Please let us know the solution that
you finally arrive at.  Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T21:55:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4743AA.9090002@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c231e2$55d9c420$d7ccf943@chottel> <ahjm4u$63j$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ahjn4b$6in$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c232b0$daa56320$39c2f943@chottel> <ahmg6q$gel$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> I have the code working - my CoBOL can read a file, and it can write a file
> - but I haven't yet found the CLIST commands to catalog it.  It writes the
> output file and then deletes it - which has limited utility.

CLIST? Ick! IMHO you'be much better off looking into REXX for this 
(CLIST is a dead product).

I wrote a REXX program to do this for our ISPF environment. What you can 
do is to try first to ALLOC the file as OLD. If that works, you're done, 
else ALLOC it NEW, which would look something like (this is just off the 
top):

Alloc_Routine:
/**********************************************************/
/*  DSN is passed as a parm when Alloc_Routine is called  */
/**********************************************************/
ARG dsn_name .

DSN_status  =  SYSDSN(dsn_name)

If  DSN_status  =  "OK"
   Then Do
	"ALLOC DSN('"dsn_name"') OLD REUSE"
	AllocRC  =  RC
   Else Do
	"ALLOC DSN('"dsn_name"') NEW CATLG SPACE(CYL,1,1) UNIT(SYSDA)" , 
                  	"LRECL(80) BLKSIZE(0)..."
	AllocRC  =  RC
      End

If AllocRC  =  0
   Then Nop
   Else Say "Unable to Allocate DSN("dsn_name"), RC="AllocRC

Return AllocRC


If it's writing it & deleting it in the same step, sounds as though it's 
a temporary dataset, otherwise you'd be able to find it on the volume 
shown when it's allocated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T01:27:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c231e8$105254c0$d7ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Or if you prefer using the TSo ALLOC command:

      IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
         PROGRAM-ID. CB2TSOEV.
      ******************************************************************
      *                                                                *
      *   MODULE NAME = COB2TSO                                        *
      *                                                                *
      *   DESCRIPTIVE NAME = ISSUE TSO COMMANDS FROM A COBOL PROGRAM.  *
      *                                                                *
      *   FUNCTION = THIS SAMPLE PROGRAM DEMONSTRATES HOW TO INVOKE    *
      *              TSO COMMANDS FROM A COBOL PROGRAM USING           *
      *              STANDARD TSO SERVICES AS DOCUMENTED IN THE        *
      *              TSO/E PROGRAMMING SERVICES MANUAL.                *
      *                                                                *
      *              MOST TSO COMMANDS, INCLUDING CLISTS AND REXX      *
      *              EXECS CAN BE EXECUTED USING THIS TECHNIQUE.       *
      *              TSO COMMANDS WHICH REQUIRE AUTHORIZATION          *
      *              (SUCH AS OUTPUT, SEND, TRANSMIT AND RECEIVE)      *
      *              WILL NOT WORK.                                    *
      *                                                                *
      *   AUTHOR   =  GILBERT SAINT-FLOUR <GSF@POBOX.COM>              *
      *                                                                *
      ******************************************************************
      /
       DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
         01 FILLER.
           05 WS-DUMMY        PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-RETURN-CODE  PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-REASON-CODE  PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-INFO-CODE    PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-CPPL-ADDRESS PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-FLAGS        PIC X(4) VALUE X'00010001'.
           05 WS-BUFFER       PIC X(256).
           05 WS-LENGTH       PIC S9(8) COMP VALUE 256.
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *          CALL IKJTSOEV TO CREATE THE TSO/E ENVIRONMENT         *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
           CALL 'IKJTSOEV' USING WS-DUMMY
                                 WS-RETURN-CODE
                                 WS-REASON-CODE
                                 WS-INFO-CODE
                                 WS-CPPL-ADDRESS.
           IF WS-RETURN-CODE > ZERO
             DISPLAY 'IKJTSOEV FAILED, RETURN-CODE=' WS-RETURN-CODE
                                     ' REASON-CODE=' WS-REASON-CODE
                                     'INFO-CODE='    WS-INFO-CODE
             MOVE WS-RETURN-CODE TO RETURN-CODE
             STOP RUN.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *          BUILD THE TSO/E COMMAND IN WS-BUFFER                  *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*

           MOVE 'ALLOCATE DD(SYSPUNCH) SYSOUT HOLD' TO WS-BUFFER.

      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *   CALL THE TSO/E SERVICE ROUTINE TO EXECUTE THE TSO/E COMMAND  *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
           CALL 'IKJEFTSR' USING WS-FLAGS
                                 WS-BUFFER
                                 WS-LENGTH
                                 WS-RETURN-CODE
                                 WS-REASON-CODE
                                 WS-DUMMY.
           IF WS-RETURN-CODE > ZERO
             DISPLAY 'IKJEFTSR FAILED, RETURN-CODE=' WS-RETURN-CODE
                                     ' REASON-CODE=' WS-REASON-CODE
             MOVE WS-RETURN-CODE TO RETURN-CODE
             STOP RUN.

      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *          CHECK THAT THE ALLOCATE COMMAND WORKED                *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
           DISPLAY 'ALLOCATE WORKED ! ' UPON SYSPUNCH.

           STOP RUN.


Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in article
<ahhd8i$kl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...

but

…[31 more quoted lines elided]…

problem

in
```

## Thread: Acucobol + Acu4GL for SQL Server - Comments? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Acucobol + Acu4GL for SQL Server - Comments?

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp020305@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-22T11:04:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sNX_8.6826$H67.36063@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`

```
Anyone actually successfully using Acu4GL for SQL Server? I'm just starting
to evaluate it. It would help to know if there are any show stoppers or
major concerns.

For instance, the Acu4GL docs imply in the A_MSSQL_MAX_CHARACTERS doc page,
that SQL Server has a maximum number of characters per row of somewhere
around 1962 chars. I find this a little hard to believe, and the SQL Server
admin book that I'm using implies that the chars per row just has to be less
than the block size (of course, I don't know if the block size is
configurable in SQL Server).

I'm using Acucobol 5.2 and SQL Server 2000, and Acu4GL eval is the latest
release.

Any advice or comments appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Acucobol + Acu4GL for SQL Server - Comments?

- **From:** ray@rays-web.com (Ray Smith)
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T18:05:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5654fff9.0207231705.748f2bfa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<sNX_8.6826$H67.36063@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`

```
Hi Dennis,

This rings a bell somewhere in my memory.  There should be a configuration
option somewhere to increase the row size to something like 8192 bytes.

We use Acu 5.1 and SQL Server 2000 with no problems.
(well we do have a problem when using it via AcuConnect but that's another
matter).

What are your expectations of Acu 4GL?  We found that it isn't a simple matter
of installing and everything now runs on SQL Server.  There where some 
performance issues that required us to change programs.
This I beleive was all documented in the help files.

Regards,

Ray Smith


"Dennis Edward" <temp020305@ipipeline.net> wrote in message news:<sNX_8.6826$H67.36063@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>...
> Anyone actually successfully using Acu4GL for SQL Server? I'm just starting
> to evaluate it. It would help to know if there are any show stoppers or
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Acucobol + Acu4GL for SQL Server - Comments?

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp020305@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-24T08:18:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Exz%8.6961$H67.36866@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`
- **References:** `<sNX_8.6826$H67.36063@tor-nn1.netcom.ca> <5654fff9.0207231705.748f2bfa@posting.google.com>`

```
We're actually familiar with Acu4GL in general, because we've been using
Acu4GL for Oracle (A4O) for years. But with A4O we didn't have to install a
bunch of stored procedures and lock administration tables. This could be
because the Acu4GL product has evolved over time, or it could be that A4S
required some kludges to be workable.

Unfortunately, sometimes the real "gotcha"s don't become apparent until
you've purchased the product and are attempting to use it in a production
environment. I'm just wondering if there's any such item that made you
scream with rage and regret buying the product.

As to the row limit, that appears to be for 6.5 or 7.0 . v8.0 has a limit of
8K, and that doesn't necessarily include text fields, so I don't really
anticipate a problem there.

As to just install-and-run, I remember with A4O that there were a couple of
segmented secondary key setups that A4O simply couldn't handle, so we had to
kludge the file structures, but that was just an irritation. I do anticipate
some headaches. Just no migraines.
```

```
We currently only have 1 client using this combination (they have been using
it for one year), they have a medium amount of data and seem to have no 
problems.
(except for lockfiles when trying to use Acu4GL through AcuConnect)

We expect many more users to use this combination in the future and have no 
ill fillings toward Acu4GL for MS SQL.

Regards,

Ray Smith
```

## Thread: Xpeditor — 4 this month / 5 total

### Xpeditor

- **From:** "Tim Cordsen" <extern.tim.cordsen@volkswagen.de>
- **Date:** 2002-07-23T11:07:58+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahj6d9$6bh2@doiweb4.volkswagen.de>`

```
Hello everybody,
is there anyone experienced in using Xpeditor?
I just wrote a dialo-program and tried to run it, but it doesn't, so I would
like to use Xpeditor to find the logical errors.
But it is not installed in my Host-Account. Can anybody tell me what I have
to do to use it, are there any files I should have in my working area? How
will I have to change compiling?
The system I work on is IBM OS/390 DB/2 and a tool named proba, where you
can combine functions to a compile-job, you just have to type prodpr.

Thanks in advance for your advices.

Tim Cordsen
```

#### ↳ Re: Xpeditor

- **From:** "Kjeld Hansen" <paabol@12mail.dk>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T00:25:01+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d41cc3e$0$195$edfadb0f@dspool01.news.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<ahj6d9$6bh2@doiweb4.volkswagen.de>`

```
First of all, you should contact someone responsible for the software
licenses on your installation, to find out if your installation have an
active Xpediter software license. If so, you could try invoking Xpediter by
typing XPEDITER or TSO XPEDITER on a TSO command line.

You dont't have to code special statements to use Xpediter, but you will
have modify your compiling rutines to include a processor that will generate
a symbolics member from the program module (actually from the compile list).
A symbolics member contains the source statements and maps the data and
program  storage to the source statements, and it is stored in a separate
PDS . This change should be implemented by the persons that are responsible
for installing software.

If you can start Xpediter, the only things needed is to allocate the data
files your program needs, with the ALLOC command. You can select the
DD-names from your test JCL and save the allocation specification for later
user. Then you have to specify load module name and a DB2 plan name, and you
are up and running!

If you are not familiar with Xpediter commands, you could try pressing PF1,
which will bring up Xpediter's help screens. Basically you can single step
your way through your module's statements, insert breakpoints to make
execution stop on certain points, inspect and modify data storage, and
insert commands in the execution sequence, with or without conditional
tests.

Kjeld P. Hansen

"Tim Cordsen" <extern.tim.cordsen@volkswagen.de> skrev i en meddelelse
news:ahj6d9$6bh2@doiweb4.volkswagen.de...
> Hello everybody,
> is there anyone experienced in using Xpeditor?
> I just wrote a dialo-program and tried to run it, but it doesn't, so I
would
> like to use Xpeditor to find the logical errors.
> But it is not installed in my Host-Account. Can anybody tell me what I
have
> to do to use it, are there any files I should have in my working area? How
> will I have to change compiling?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

```
eibcalen <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message
news:3d47d199$1_4@news5.nntpserver.com...
> Richard Plinston  wrote:
>   > eibcalen wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   > Cobol. Every different type of machine had several unique ones. In
fact
>   > Cobol was based on parts of several earlier languages.
>
>
> I'm very interesting to know them!! Can you tell me about?

Start with the Language List. The following is an excerpt
from that list.

---------------
The Language List - Version 2.4, January 23, 1995

Collected information on about 2350 computer languages, past and present.

Available online as:
 http://cui_www.unige.ch/langlist
 ftp://wuarchive.wustl.edu/doc/misc/lang-list.txt
...
A0 or A-0 - Grace Hopper's team at Remington Rand, 1952, for the UNIVAC I
or II.  Possibly the first compiler ever.  Later internal versions: A-1, A-
2 ("The A-2 Compiler System", Rem Rand, 1955), A-3, AT-3.  AT-3 was
released as MATH-MATIC.  Sammet 1969, p.12.
...
B-0 - Original name of FLOW-MATIC, Remington Rand.  UNIVAC I or II ca.
1958.
...
COBOL - COmmon Business Oriented Language.  1960.  CODASYL Committee, Apr
1960.  Simple computations on large amounts of data.  The most widely used
programming language today.  The natural language style is intended to be
largely self-documenting.  Introduced the record structure.  "Initial
Specifications for a Common Business Oriented Language" DoD, US GPO, Apr
1960.  Major revisions in 1968 (ANS X3.23-1968), 1974 (ANS X3.23-1974), and
1985.  A new ISO standard is expected in 1997.

COBOL-1961 Extended - Short-lived separation of COBOL specifications.
Sammet 1969, p.339.
...
FLOW-MATIC or FLOWMATIC - (originally B-0).  Remington Rand, 1958.
Possibly the first English-like DP language.  For UNIVAC I.  Sammet 1969,
pp.316-324.
...
-----------
```

<a id="t-91ea51dbe6"></a>
## Thread: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?

- **From:** Andreas Lerch <andreaslerch@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2002-07-24T18:08:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020724.18084264@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online>`

```
Hallo,

this is the second time, IBM ist doing something unpleasent changes.

The first, some years ago, verifies the recordlengt at open, 
validating the dcb. If i Code some korrekt rule:

FD Input recording mode v.
01 min-rec pic (200).
01 mac-rec pic x(220).

IBM is checking the dcb for 224 bytes recordlength. This is stupid 
because in MVS you only need a buffer to hold the longest record in 
the dataset. Is the RDW greater 220 bytes, an abend s0c1 occurs. So i 
must change all my JCL to produce the recordlength of 220 bytes?

If i code this in JCL, only for QSAM-Files:

// (IEBGENER-Step)
//SYSUT2 DD DSN=NEWDS,DISP=NEW,DCB=(LRECL=224,RECFM=VB)

// (My -Step)
//INPUT DD 	DSN=NEWDS,DISP=SHR
// 		DSN=ANYDSN,DCB=(LRECL=any-record-length-i want,RECFM=VB)

I can do my work, IBM is only checking the first file, but this 
solution is stupid.

a. VB-Datasets belongs a lot of changetime to increase decres the 
recordlength
b. mild migration, first change the jcl, then change programms are 
impractiable.

The same checking is done to VSAM-Files: Maximum and Average - this is 
the badest check i ever found.

This ist the worst case one.


The second, the modules are all going into the cobpack IGZCPAC. I use 
some programs as subroutines in CICS environment. They are designed:

	If CICS
	then 
	 <perform CICS-part>
	 exec cics read...
	 exec cics write...
	else
	 <perform batch-part>
	 open file
	 read file
	 close file
	end-if

Dokumentation: never use FD OPEN READ CLOSE in a CICS environment. *I 
DO SO*

i have coded this operations but i dont use them in CICS.

Cobol now, is stupid again. The programm-header tells, there will be 
some file-code inside the programm. Flags. LE is loading the modules 
IGZCV** for VSAM-processing, these modules are now inside the cobpack 
- *and will never be used* - where is the batch restriction, that i 
cant use EXEC-CICS in a batch program. Now i must change a 
single-program-logik in a two-program-logik:

	If CICS
	then 
	 <perform CICS-part>
	 exec cics read...
	 exec cics write...
	else
	 <perform batch-part>
	 call suproutine containing batch OPEN,CLOSE,READ and so on
	end-if

thats stupid too.

This ist the worst case two.

These two things are very expensive, because the migration of datasets 
(case 1) must be a dot-landing (german: Punktlandung) all changes must 
be done to the same time. No safe way - step by step and the changing 
of program-logic (case 2), everytime i must change two programms to 
keep the logik korrekt.

Some words in german: das ist frustrierend, so bevormundet zu werden. 
Ich bin ein erwachsener Mensch, der weiss was er macht!

Thank you all to hear my words, I must say this, i thing, now, after 
this, i can sleep well.

The next time, IBM changes something, which surprise comes?????

have a nice day
Andreas
```

#### ↳ Re: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T14:41:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahpk8j$89a$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020724.18084264@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online>`

```
First, never try and do a COBOL or an LE "migration" without reading
CAREFULLY the IBM "Migration Guides".  These clearly document what changes
occur.  (and in many cases how to avoid them - such as "odd" file attribute
problems)

Second, the requirement for "fixed file attribute" checking at OPEN time
came from ANSI and ISO - not from IBM.  Complain to the German Standards
body - not IBM.  See:

    Germany (DIN)
    Address        DIN Deutsches Institut f�r Normung
                        Burggrafenstrasse 6
                        DE-10787 Berlin

Postal Address
DE-10772 Berlin

Tel+49 30 26 01-0
Fax+49 30 26 01 12 31
E-mail directorate.international@din.de
Webhttp://www.din.de

   ***

Finally, as to what LE modules are and are not loaded at run-time, Assuming
you are using a "current" compiler (with RES behavior as the default) -
there is no "real" loading of modules at run-time.  If you are experiencing
an unexpected performance problem, contact your IBM support team - as this
should NOT happen.
```

```
oh no - never change a running system - thats not the intention i
have.

I want to write good programms to do the work they must do. first i am
online, second batch, third i have ims-databases and then udm.

I can use ims-databases in any system (cics, ims/dc, ims/db) with the
same interface. Dynamic call!

Db2 i mus have different loadmodules - source can be identical, becaus
i have to link different hll-interfaces.

Now i must have different sources to handle the same request in batch
and cics. That is what i want to say.


> Second, the requirement for "fixed file attribute" checking at OPEN
time
> came from ANSI and ISO - not from IBM.  Complain to the German
Standards
> body - not IBM.  See:

for fixed files - i can live whith this test
for variable files - which can this test preserve?

15 years ago i write a programm to handle qsam-files like vsamio (i
you know this product) now i use this programm to convert vb-file from
download to fix files. So i can work with thist handicap.

> Finally, as to what LE modules are and are not loaded at run-time,
Assuming
> you are using a "current" compiler (with RES behavior as the default)
-
> there is no "real" loading of modules at run-time.  If you are
experiencing
> an unexpected performance problem, contact your IBM support team - as
this
> should NOT happen.

I am not shure whats happend. We use the same subprogramms in batch
and in cics dynamic calls and language-environment are good. I can use
callable services like ceeloct in all runtime environments.

Your final point is not easy, our host is served by a supporter who
primary works with assembler and c - he is the connector to ibm, and
it is not his intention to support a language, the dont use often. So
they do with cics. And they dont understand, whats my problem is. They
say: read the manual, if they write you can use, you can use - if they
write not - you cant use it!

My host must support the following:
batch, cics, ims-db, db2, cobol and some assembler also a little rexx

if i say i have a problem with cobol and cics, they say cics, thats
not my world. Cobol, whats that?

Thank you to talk with me - i dont want to change anything, i only
want to know someone else which reaction some actions can start

have a nice day

Andreas
```

<a id="t-9ba5fc5097"></a>
## Thread: How to get the Job Id for a submitted job? — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to get the Job Id for a submitted job?

- **From:** ksvenbak@yahoo.com (Krishna Venbakkam)
- **Date:** 2002-07-24T15:32:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<179c47c8.0207241432.52569604@posting.google.com>`

```
All:

I am working in z/os, using COBOL. Here is my scenario:

I have a batch program in which I am creating a JCL for submission to
JES, via the internal reader. Is there a way to get the job number
(JES assigned number) of the submitted job in my batch program?

Thanks for any help,
Krishna.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to get the Job Id for a submitted job?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T14:33:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahpjq5$f5m$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<179c47c8.0207241432.52569604@posting.google.com>`

```
See the topic
  "Appendix A.3 - MVS (or OS/390) Control Blocks"

in the COBOL FAQ at:
  http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: How to get the Job Id for a submitted job?

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T00:25:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TF009.33837$qB5.10498@news1.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<179c47c8.0207241432.52569604@posting.google.com>`

```
Are you referring to the number of the JOB executing your Cobol program that
creates the JOB to be submitted, or are you referring to returning the
number of the JOB you are going to submit from your Cobol program via the
Internal Reader?  You can capture the JOB number for both scenarios above,
but you have to utilize different methods ...

TL
```

- **From:** ksvenbak@yahoo.com (Krishna Venbakkam)
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T10:49:06-07:00

```
Hi,
I am not referring to the job executing my COBOL program. The job
number I need is the for the job(s) that I will be submitting from the
batch program, via the internal reader. Is there a way to do this in
COBOL/

Thanks,
Krishna. 

"Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net> wrote in message news:<TF009.33837$qB5.10498@news1.central.cox.net>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to get the Job Id for a submitted job?

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T21:55:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Czj09.36480$qB5.6522@news1.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<179c47c8.0207241432.52569604@posting.google.com> <TF009.33837$qB5.10498@news1.central.cox.net> <179c47c8.0207260949.1a3c0d7c@posting.google.com>`

```
I wrote an assembler routine to do this - I can't give you the code (due to
copyrights), but I can give you hints on how to do it.

Check out the VSAM "ENDREQ" macro in the "MVS Auth Assembler Services Guide"
bookserver manual.  The output ACB is allocated to the Internal Reader and
the JCL records are written to it.  Upon close, the ENDREQ macro will return
the JES JOB number that was submitted.  I believe there is also example code
in one of the Assembler manuals on this.

HTH - TL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to get the Job Id for a submitted job?

- **From:** James Campbell <jacampbell@acslink.remove_nospam.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T23:39:42+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d43f41e_1@news.iprimus.com.au>`
- **References:** `<179c47c8.0207241432.52569604@posting.google.com> <TF009.33837$qB5.10498@news1.central.cox.net> <179c47c8.0207260949.1a3c0d7c@posting.google.com>`

```
If you really want a method, not the one I'ld use of course, but 
something simple (to write), run the COBOL program as a batch TSO/ISPF 
session, call the TSO environment from the COBOL program to run a 
CLIST/REXX.  CLIST/REXX uses OUTTRAP, SUBMIT and then analyses the 
trapped output to extract the jobid.  Inserts JOBID into ISPF variable, 
and returns.  COBOL program extracts JOBID from ISPF variable.

Otherwise it's into assembler.

James Campbell  

ksvenbak@yahoo.com (Krishna Venbakkam) wrote in
news:179c47c8.0207260949.1a3c0d7c@posting.google.com: 

> Hi,
> I am not referring to the job executing my COBOL program. The job
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0207252136.61a8e62e@posting.google.com...
> Does anyone know what the heck this stuff is?  I saw it many months
> ago - around the time of US terrorist attacks and I just wonder if
> perhaps this isn't some sort of code....

http://groups.google.com/groups?q=dipslime&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&hl=en

Dipslime (our pet name for him) spams several newsgroups, usually on
Fridays.

He routes his stuff through open relays (usually Korea) and, up to now, has
not been identified.

One of his favorite targets is news.admin.net-abuse.email and this is where
I first encountered him.

On a related matter, I can't tell you how much fun I have reading the
news.admin.net-abuse.email newsgroup. One source of merriment involves
administrators who post whining that their mail is being blocked just
because they have Verio (or UUNet or Sprint) as an upstream provider or are
located in spam-friendly environs (Portugal, Costa Rica, Brazil). These
pitiful dregs usually get flamed with comments like:

"You live in a crack-house and you wonder why Domino's doesn't deliver?"
"You're telling us you went to the whore-house just to hear the piano
player?"
"FOAD" (Fsk Off And Die)
"Rot in SPEWS" (Spam Prevention Early Warning System - a block list)

Watch out if you see a post beginning with "[C&C]" "C&C" stands for "Cat and
Coke (or Coffee)" and, should you have either in your physical possession as
you read the post, you will suffer either scratches or coke-in-the-nose due
to the uncontrolled hilarity generated by the message.

Another subject descriptor is "Cartooney" which means the post contains a
copy of a threatening letter from somebody claiming to be a lawyer (or
someone who knows a lawyer). The threats usually claim block-lists violate
the 1st Amendment, interfere with interstate commerce, slanderous,
yak-yak-yak. Sometimes the lawyer's IP is placed on block-lists in the
category of 'spammer supporter.'

Word of warning: Don't ever use the word "SPAM." "SPAM" (all caps - it's an
acronym for SPiced hAM) is a registered trademark of Hormel and yummy. Use
"spam" instead.
```

- **References:** `<3D42BC2E.8010805@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
>From: Liam Devlin LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net 
>Date: 2002-07-27 11:28 Eastern Daylight Time

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> Does anyone know what the heck this stuff is?  I saw it many months
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>& timing.

I think the generated text in this and similar spam messages is very
good.  I enjoy reading the spam hiders a lot more than the spam itself.  
I had thought about this kind of programming about twenty years ago,
but gave it up because it was beyond my ability.  Whoever wrote the
code that generates this text has earned my respect.  Whoever posted
the spam should slide down a twelve-foot razor and land in a
barrel of alcohol.  

(Incidentally I love the potted meat "Spam."  The taste is so excellent 
that--unlike baloney, or ham, etc.--it does not require any mayonnaise
or lettuce to help out.  Just Spam on white bread.  Totally rad.) 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

```
cookbooks, salt+pepper shakers, stadium cups, potholders, coolers, beer
cozies, mugs, carving knives, magnets, cheese slicers, oven mitts,
keychains, pet leashes, reading lamps, playing cards, earrings, clocks,
pens, letter openers, rulers, postcards, snow-domes, notebooks, a book on

clocks, radios, carabineer hooks, tape measures, sour-balls in SPAM jar,
flashlights, lapel pins, luggage tags.

So many ways to enjoy SPAM.

or, you can call: 1-800-LUV-SPAM
```

```
I agree that it seems to be a spam trap.  But the "verse" has obviously been
created by Racter (Not certain about spelling), a computer program that
creates original prose.

    Look for "The policeman's beard is half constructed".  That is one of
Racter's books (only one?).

   The style is unmistakeable.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: he should converse tamely, unless Waleed recollects raindrops behind Ghassan's twig

- **From:** "FDW" <fdewit_remove_@planet.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T22:18:21+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.clu,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai6sae$kme$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`
- **References:** `<d2fjpqhinqfrqtqjyfkuhsewigcje@4ax.com> <bfdfc3e8.0207252136.61a8e62e@posting.google.com>`

```

My complaint about Mr. Thane Hubbell
I am writing this letter in simple English in order that everyone can read
and understand my words. As a preliminary, I want to turn Mr. Thane
Hubbell's unconscionable subliminal psywar campaigns to our advantage. At
the very least, he wonders why everyone hates him. Apparently, he never
stopped to think that maybe it's because his ideas may have been conceived
in idealism, but they quickly degenerated into unforgiving obstructionism.

Mr. Hubbell should just face the facts. Added to this is something else: If
Mr. Hubbell is going to talk about higher standards, then he needs to live
by those higher standards. I understand that it is better to remain silent
and be thought a fool than to speak and remove all doubt, but statistical
details released by a third-party agency indicate that only by taking risks
and pushing boundaries with this letter can I improve the living conditions
of the most vulnerable in our society -- the sick, the old, the disabled,
the unemployed, and our youth -- all of whose lives are made miserable by
Thane Hubbell. More than that, we need to look beyond the most immediate and
visible problems with him. We need to look at what is behind these problems
and understand that in my speaking engagements, I have found in audience
questions an alarming increase in concern about the most callow jerks you'll
ever see (also known as Mr. Hubbell's minions). That's pretty transparent.
What's not so transparent is the answer to the following question: What in
perdition does Mr. Hubbell think he's doing? A clue might be that Mr.
Hubbell's insults are based on a technique I'm sure you've heard of. It's
called "lying". Looking at it on the bright side, Mr. Hubbell thinks that
post-structuralism is a noble goal. Of course, thinking so doesn't make it
so.

Hey, it's not my fault that the surest way for his emissaries to succeed is
for them to advocate measures that others criticize for being excessively
inarticulate. Do I blame society for this? No, I blame Mr. Hubbell. If
nothing else, he doesn't care about freedom, as he can neither eat it nor
put it in the bank. It's just a word to him. For those who need very
specific examples in order to grasp the significance of Mr. Hubbell's
recommendations, I'll give a very specific example: Think for a moment about
the way that by using bombastic language and selective quotation, Mr.
Hubbell is able to relabel millions of people as "squalid". Excuse me;
that's not entirely correct. What I meant to say is that we should agree on
definitions before saying anything further about Mr. Hubbell's jealous
tracts. For starters, let's say that "cameralism" is "that which makes Mr.
Hubbell yearn to kill the goose bearing the golden egg." In a nutshell, I
take seriously the view that Mr. Thane Hubbell has overstated his case
against me by alluding to an illusory past.

Frans



"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0207252136.61a8e62e@posting.google.com...

> Does anyone know what the heck this stuff is?  I saw it many months
> ago - around the time of US terrorist attacks and I just wonder if
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Pvt. Y. O. von Ekenstein" <LARKO@likraro.org.pe> wrote in message
news:<d2fjpqhinqfrqtqjyfkuhsewigcje@4ax.com>...
> > tgos@spamcop.net
> >
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Nowadays, I'll comb the dryer.  It can receive once, reject
unbelievably, then
> > lift behind the envelope for the forest.  Little by little, go
> > explain a ulcer!  Better attack printers now or Sayed will freely
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> > These days Henry will judge the lentil, and if Corey wanly laughs it
too, the
> > onion will improve in the distant navel.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > gardner.  I am stupidly sick, so I fill you.
```

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T19:48:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.clu,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<d2fjpqhinqfrqtqjyfkuhsewigcje@4ax.com> <bfdfc3e8.0207252136.61a8e62e@posting.google.com> <ai6sae$kme$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`

```
FDW,

My apologies if my query offended you.  You have inferred much from my
words that just isn't there or intented.  I asked one question,
posited a possible scenario and specified a time frame.

I what this seemingly random text was.
I posited that it could be a cypher.
I noted the time frame when I had last observed messages like this
one, around the time of September 11th.

You assume too much.  All I posted is above.  Text I quoted - asking
what it was - may be what you are responding to.  If so - you are not
responding to the proper individual.  I didn't make the original
posting.
```

```
In article <uNS19.12548$pg2.1013931@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>    Didn't anyone get my message?  This odd text clearly resembles
>the output of the program Racter.  As before, look for the published book
>by this "Author" entitled "The policeman's beard is half constructed".

You see how clever Mr Hubble is?  To draw attention away from his 
'thinking that post-structuralism is a noble goal' (and all that this 
entails... or means, I'm not quite sure) and his making-miserable of the 
lives of 'the sick, the old, the disabled, the unemployed, and our youth' 
he does... errrrr... a whole bunch of Other Stuff, including impersonating 
computer-programs!

DD
```
