###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: he should converse tamely, unless Waleed recollects raindrops behind Ghassan's twig

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "FDW" <fdewit_remove_@planet.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T20:49:18+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.clu,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai9bfg$gh0$1@reader12.wxs.nl>`
- **References:** `<d2fjpqhinqfrqtqjyfkuhsewigcje@4ax.com> <bfdfc3e8.0207252136.61a8e62e@posting.google.com> <ai6sae$kme$1@reader12.wxs.nl> <bfdfc3e8.0207301848.3217306a@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell,

There was no intent to offend any one in my message. When reading your
question ( and the copy of the original message ) I thought that a reply
might be appreciated containing the same ( but other ;-) strange contents as
in the original message.

To generate the reply message I did visit the
'http://hugin.sigusr1.org/~pakin/complaint' web site and pressed the
generate button. Weird letters do appear ....

Frans

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0207301848.3217306a@posting.google.com...
> FDW,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> "FDW" <fdewit_remove_@planet.nl> wrote in message
news:<ai6sae$kme$1@reader12.wxs.nl>...
> > My complaint about Mr. Thane Hubbell
> > I am writing this letter in simple English in order that everyone can
read
> > and understand my words. As a preliminary, I want to turn Mr. Thane
> > Hubbell's unconscionable subliminal psywar campaigns to our advantage.
At
> > the very least, he wonders why everyone hates him. Apparently, he never
> > stopped to think that maybe it's because his ideas may have been
conceived
> > in idealism, but they quickly degenerated into unforgiving
obstructionism.
> >
> > Mr. Hubbell should just face the facts. Added to this is something else:
If
> > Mr. Hubbell is going to talk about higher standards, then he needs to
live
> > by those higher standards. I understand that it is better to remain sile
nt
> > and be thought a fool than to speak and remove all doubt, but
statistical
> > details released by a third-party agency indicate that only by taking
risks
> > and pushing boundaries with this letter can I improve the living
conditions
> > of the most vulnerable in our society -- the sick, the old, the
disabled,
> > the unemployed, and our youth -- all of whose lives are made miserable
by
> > Thane Hubbell. More than that, we need to look beyond the most immediate
and
> > visible problems with him. We need to look at what is behind these
problems
> > and understand that in my speaking engagements, I have found in audience
> > questions an alarming increase in concern about the most callow jerks
you'll
> > ever see (also known as Mr. Hubbell's minions). That's pretty
transparent.
> > What's not so transparent is the answer to the following question: What
in
> > perdition does Mr. Hubbell think he's doing? A clue might be that Mr.
> > Hubbell's insults are based on a technique I'm sure you've heard of.
It's
> > called "lying". Looking at it on the bright side, Mr. Hubbell thinks
that
> > post-structuralism is a noble goal. Of course, thinking so doesn't make
it
> > so.
> >
> > Hey, it's not my fault that the surest way for his emissaries to succeed
is
> > for them to advocate measures that others criticize for being
excessively
> > inarticulate. Do I blame society for this? No, I blame Mr. Hubbell. If
> > nothing else, he doesn't care about freedom, as he can neither eat it
nor
> > put it in the bank. It's just a word to him. For those who need very
> > specific examples in order to grasp the significance of Mr. Hubbell's
> > recommendations, I'll give a very specific example: Think for a moment
about
> > the way that by using bombastic language and selective quotation, Mr.
> > Hubbell is able to relabel millions of people as "squalid". Excuse me;
> > that's not entirely correct. What I meant to say is that we should agree
on
> > definitions before saying anything further about Mr. Hubbell's jealous
> > tracts. For starters, let's say that "cameralism" is "that which makes
Mr.
> > Hubbell yearn to kill the goose bearing the golden egg." In a nutshell,
I
> > take seriously the view that Mr. Thane Hubbell has overstated his case
> > against me by alluding to an illusory past.
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > annually taste the smart hill.  I was shouting to burn you some of
my
> > > > sad forks.  Just behaving alongside a candle at the obelisk is too
> > > > weak for Moustapha to call it.  For Sayed the sticker's polite,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > She can solve happily, unless Terrance dreams farmers among
Brahimi's
> > > > jar.
> > > >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > gardner.  I am stupidly sick, so I fill you.
```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T14:49:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.clu,comp.lang.cobol

```
Mr. FDW:

Mr. Hubbell was asking a simple question and your conclusions
regarding Mr. Hubbell are not only highly speculative in nature, they
are simply very false.  I know Thane very well and your character
assassination of him is completelly unwarranted.  You are obviously a
very angry person and I feel badly for you.

I hope that you receive the professional counseling to help reduce
your anger and allow you to have a more accurate perception of
newsgroup communications.

Relax.

"FDW" <fdewit_remove_@planet.nl> wrote:

>
>My complaint about Mr. Thane Hubbell
…[160 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T10:57:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1027594557@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Cobol and hard disk serial number — 11 msgs

### Cobol and hard disk serial number

- **From:** "Pennywise" <angelsymphonyNO@SPAMlibero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T13:29:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it>`

```
Hello everybody!
I have this (easy?) question... How can I get hard-disk serial number in
cobol, without using dos command "vol" ?
Is there a way to make this?

Tnx all!
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and hard disk serial number

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T15:03:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WqU%8.280685$iB1.14378650@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it>`

```

"Pennywise" <angelsymphonyNO@SPAMlibero.it> wrote in message
news:E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it...
> Hello everybody!
> I have this (easy?) question... How can I get hard-disk serial number in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tnx all!

The following should work for most compilers:

DISPLAY 'Enter serial number of hard disk:'
ACCEPT SERIAL-NUMBER

For a more specific answer, we'd need the compiler and operating system in
use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and hard disk serial number

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T12:43:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahpdk102h5a@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it> <WqU%8.280685$iB1.14378650@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ahp4jq$c7q$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`

```
> seek advice in the Microsoft SDK, an api call should be
there.

A win api that can get this is "GetVolumeInformation."  An
added provisio -- in case you haven't seen it -- this id can
easily be changed, and is unlikely to be organization-unique if
drive cloning was used to install the systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and hard disk serial number

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@kub.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T15:34:52+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahrj1b$791$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`
- **References:** `<E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it> <WqU%8.280685$iB1.14378650@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ahp4jq$c7q$1@mailnews.kub.nl> <qi909.4113$n04.100489@twister2.libero.it>`

```

"Pennywise" <angelsymphonyNO@SPAMlibero.it> wrote in message
news:qi909.4113$n04.100489@twister2.libero.it...
> > seek advice in the Microsoft SDK, an api call should be there.
>
> What's SDK?

Microsoft's Software Development Kit.

> Where can I seek?

You can download the MS SDK at
http://www.microsoft.com/msdownload/platformsdk/sdkupdate/. The description
of GetSystemInformation can be found on
http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/default.asp?url=/library/en-us/fileio/volr
ef_6wfi.asp. You can also download the windows api written for Visual Basic
at http://www.allapi.net/agnet/apiguide.shtml.

What you must do when you gathered the information is to rewrite the used
Visual Basic/C++ structures into COBOL. You must make a call like the
following:

call "GetSystemInformation" with STDCALL using [args].

The [args] are described on the ms website and in the SDK. You need to make
typedefs within cobol or make sure that you pass the arguments in the right
form. For instance, the COBOL equivalent for DWORD is PIC S9(9) comp-5.
Though this is based on my experience with NetCOBOL 6.1a. Your compiler
might need an other defenition, see manual.

On thing to remember, is that the output of an API call must be directed to
pointers in your based-storage area and are passed through to Windows using
'by reference' instead of 'by value' in the initial call.

On the dowload part of the www.netcobol.com website you can find an API call
for messageboxes and starting an exe, check it for an example.

Hope you can use this information. Remember that using the Windows API isn't
that easy, but when you get the hang of it, it can be tammed.

Rik Hendriks.

> I need to get hd serial number and move it in a cobol variable.
>
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and hard disk serial number

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T14:10:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JLc09.374268$vq.20858579@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it> <WqU%8.280685$iB1.14378650@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ahp4jq$c7q$1@mailnews.kub.nl> <qi909.4113$n04.100489@twister2.libero.it>`

```

"Pennywise" <angelsymphonyNO@SPAMlibero.it> wrote in message
news:qi909.4113$n04.100489@twister2.libero.it...
> > seek advice in the Microsoft SDK, an api call should be there.
>
> What's SDK?
> Where can I seek?
> I need to get hd serial number and move it in a cobol variable.

SDK = Software Development Kit / Microsoft.

Something like this:

CALL 'GetVolumeInformationA' WITH STDCALL USING
   BY REFERENCE lpRootPathName  (String)
   BY REFERENCE lpVolumeNameBuffer (String)
   BY VALUE   nVolumeNameSize (4byte integer)
   BY VALUE  lpVolumeSerialNumber  (4byte integer)
   BY VALUE  lpMaximumComponentLength (4byte integer)
   BY VALUE  lpFileSystemFlags   (4byte integer)
   BY REFERENCE  lpFileSystemNameBuffer  (string)
   BY VALUE  nFileSystemNameSize  (4byte integer)

and something like weasels.

Check:

http://www.allapi.net/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and hard disk serial number

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T22:09:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PMj09.302376$Bt1.15483055@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<E2T%8.860$n04.13133@twister2.libero.it> <WqU%8.280685$iB1.14378650@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ahp4jq$c7q$1@mailnews.kub.nl> <qi909.4113$n04.100489@twister2.libero.it>`

```

"Pennywise" <angelsymphonyNO@SPAMlibero.it> wrote in message
news:qi909.4113$n04.100489@twister2.libero.it...
> > seek advice in the Microsoft SDK, an api call should be there.
>
> What's SDK?
> Where can I seek?
> I need to get hd serial number and move it in a cobol variable.

One more warning:

Calls to APIs are case sensitive. Many compilers fold lower-case module
names to upper before they pass them to the linker. Make sure the
appropriate compiler option is set disabling this automatic upper-casing.
```

<a id="t-f8ba2e5e7b"></a>
## Thread: Enterprise COBOL's XML PARSE — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Enterprise COBOL's XML PARSE

- **From:** "Steve Dearth" <steve@dearth.info>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T17:01:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M9W%8.2807$XG5.2140@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
I'm examining IBM Enterprise COBOL and trying to come to an understanding of
its new XML PARSE facilities.  My basic problem/question is this - it
appears from the fairly limited examples in the documentation that the
command expects an entire XML document as its argument.  This seems weird to
me in a record oriented environment.

Does anyone have any experience with this?  Am I correct in my assumptions?
If so, any suggestions on the best way to get the entire file into memory
and process what could be incredibly variable data?
```

#### ↳ Re: Enterprise COBOL's XML PARSE

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@heliotropicsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T17:26:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NwW%8.11124$ub.8475@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<M9W%8.2807$XG5.2140@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Steve,

Yes, the way the COBOL parser works is the way you read and understood it;
the entire file is read into working storage and is then parsed.

The rules for parsing the file are based on your application's business
logic.  You (the application programmer) have to determine which tags
contain the data required by the application program.  You can then perform
any additional processing once you have the data.

For some additional information about this topic, please take a look at my
recent article in Technical Support magazine: Generation X Meets
Tyrannosaurus Rex: Working with XML on the Mainframe
(http://www.naspa.com/02articlesbymonth.htm#may).

I hope that helps!

Larry Kahm
Heliotropic Systems, Inc.
```

"rig" <rig01@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:23u0kuk2nmmqot662tees7ksplhe5bpekj@4ax.com...
> Hi Folks :
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any input is welcome

Check the intrinsic functions section of your manual. For example, to find
today minus 1234 days:

Compute TempDate = Function Integer-of-date(TodaysDate) - 1234
Compute NewDate = Function Date-Of-Integer(TempDate)

Or,
Compute NewDate = Function Date-Of-Integer(Function
Integer-of-date(TodaysDate) - 1234)
```

### From the Chair of the J4 (COBOL) Committee

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-25T22:08:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahqeer$qon$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,

"The Working Draft Technical Report on Object finalization in COBOL is
available on the INCITS/J4 web site:  http://www.cobolportal.com/j4 - top
link in body of page

This document is available for informal review and comment.  There will not
be a formal public review until later stages in the development of the
technical report.  However, it is easier to change things at this point in
response to comments, so I request you to review the document and send me
your comments.

Since this is an informal review, you will receive an acknowledgement of
your comments, but no formal response.  I will notify you when the updated
version is available.

Please inform other colleagues who may be interested in this subject.
Don Schricker
Micro Focus
Product Manager, Language Standards
Chairman J4, the COBOL technical committee of INCITS
Don.Schricker@microfocus.com
http://www.cobolstandards.com
http://www.microfocus.com "
```

## Thread: FINAL (hopefully?) draft of ISO COBOL Standard available for purchase & U.S. comment — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### FINAL (hopefully?) draft of ISO COBOL Standard available for purchase & U.S. comment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-26T13:16:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahs3l5$ghd$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
According to "ANSI Standard Action" dated July 26, 2002 and available at:
  http://www.ansi.org/rooms/room_14/Public/pdfs/SAV3324.pdf

"Comment Deadline: September 9, 2002
   ...

BSR/ISO/IEC FDIS 1989-200x, Information Technology - Programming Languages -
COBOL (new standard)

This International Standard specifies the syntax and semantics of COBOL. Its
purpose is to promote a high degree of machine independence to permit the
use of COBOL on a variety of data processing systems.

Single copy price: $18.00

Obtain an electronic copy from: http://webstore.ansi.org/ansidocstore

Order from: Customer Service, Global Engineering Documents;
 ansionline@ansi.org or 212-642-4980

Send comments (with copy to BSR) to: Deborah J. Donovan, ITI
 (NCITS): ddonovan@itic.org "

    ***

(personal notes follow - there is NOTHING (except quoted text) official in
these)

NOTE WELL:
   It is my understanding that the U.S. (via ANSI) will be required to
submit its ballot to ISO *before* the Sept 9, 2002 deadline for comments
from this U.S. (ANSI) Public Review.  Therefore, if you want your comments
to be received before the U.S. votes, then I suggest you send any comments
as SOON AS POSSIBLE.  In fact (and I could be totally mistaken on this), I
believe that at least an "initial" U.S. position has already been
considered.  Therefore, I don't know if "public review" comments received
immediately will or will not have any impact on the ultimate disposition of
this document as either an ANSI or ISO Standard.

NOTE 2:
 Although I *think* that this document is available for purchase (18USD)
from anywhere, the information on Public Review comments *only* applies to
ANSI (the U.S.  national standards body).  If you want to participate in the
"review process" of some other country, I suggest you contact your own
national body.  How to do this can be found at:
  http://std.dkuug.dk/JTC1/SC22/

by selecting the appropriate link from the line that reads
  "To obtain the international standards from SC22, please contact your
national member body."
In the past, ANSI has accepted "public review comments" from those outside
the US, so you MAY want to send any comments to BOTH your own national
standards body *and* to ANSI (per the instructions above).

NOTE 3:
  In the copy that I have seen of this ISO FDIS (Final Draft International
Standard), the following information occurs:

Page ii:

 "Copyright notice

This ISO document is a Draft International Standard and is
copyright-protected by ISO. Except as permitted
under the applicable laws of the user's country, neither this ISO draft nor
any extract from it may be reproduced,
stored in a retrieval system or transmitted in any form or by any means,
electronic, photocopying, recording or
otherwise, without prior written permission being secured.

Requests for permission to reproduce should be addressed to either ISO at
the address below or ISO's member
body in the country of the requester.

    ISO copyright office
    Case postale 56 . CH-1211 Geneva 20
    Tel. + 41 22 749 01 11
    Fax + 41 22 749 09 47
    E-mail copyright@iso.ch
    Web www.iso.ch

Reproduction may be subject to royalty payments or a licensing agreement.

Violators may be prosecuted."

and on EVERY page,

 "� ISO/IEC 2002 - All rights reserved"
```

### Re: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?

- **From:** Andreas Lerch <andreas@andreas-lerch.de>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T15:55:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020727.15550279@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online>`
- **References:** `<20020724.18084264@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online> <5cu3kuklom8dul4dnj9lh4v7tov3u8n9ns@4ax.com>`

```


>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Ursprüngliche Nachricht <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

Am 27.07.02, 01:42:06, schrieb Michael Todd <mikltodd@comcast.net> zum 
Thema Re: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?:


> Because you are specifying "recording mode v" IBM COBOL automatically 
adds a length attribute to the front of each
> record.  That's why the record length is longer than 220.  That all in 
the IBM manuals, by the way.  If you read the
> records with a COBOL program, the lengths will not be part of the 
record area (they're stripped off as the records are
> read).  Utility programs and other languages (especially assembler) 
may "see" the record lengths as part of the
> reocord-- it depends upon how they define the file.

I dont know, how cobol is working on a pc or in dos/vse but since my 
early days of computing cobol is only using a pointer inside the 
file-section:

if you use this in an MVS-environment:

FD File recording mode v....
01 short-rec pic x(20).
01 log-rec pic x(1000).

An you are moving the long-rec at the ent of the qsam-buffer, you will 
get a systemabend 0C4.

So if you use this:
01 tab pic s9(04) comp occurs 10 indexed by ind-tab.

And this smal code:

set ind-tab to 1
set ind-tab down by 3

move tab (ind-tab) to...

you will have acces to the rdw-bytes of the current record. This migt 
be work only if you use the compiler directive NOSSRANGE :-)

the records are stored:
aabbxxyyssssssxxyysssssss....

aa --> is the block-length-field
bb --> spare bytes
xx --> is the rdw
yy --> spare bytes
ss --> content of the record

the aa+bb fields can only accessed at a new block.

And here some information from DCB: Construct a Data Control Block 
(BPAM) IBM-Book:

    LRECL=absexp (maximum value is 32760)
       specifies the length, in bytes, for fixed-length records, or it
       specifies the maximum length, in bytes, for variable-length and
       undefined-length records.  The value specified in LRECL cannot 
exceed
       the value specified in BLKSIZE.

>snip>

       Variable-length records:  the value specified in LRECL must 
include
       the maximum data length (up to 32752 bytes) plus 4 bytes for 
the
       record-descriptor word (RDW).

<snip>

there is nothing that say: must specify the maximum data....
it can be more....

have a nice day

Andreas
```

#### ↳ Variable length records in IBM COBOL 85 was Re: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T20:03:59-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D44785F.D8112EC7@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20020724.18084264@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online> <5cu3kuklom8dul4dnj9lh4v7tov3u8n9ns@4ax.com> <20020727.15550279@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online>`

```
The maximum length of the COBOL record must agree with the maximum
length in the Data Set Label (COBOL length + 4) for non-VSAM or the
maximum length as defined in the Catalog for VSAM files (COBOL record
length).  While I believe that this protection should be that the COBOL
maximum length is not less than the length specified for the file in the
data set label or Catalog that is not what the ANSI idiots specified. 
For non-VSAM files you can get around this by coding LRECL in the JCL
for the file so that it agrees with the COBOL record length.  Similar
overrides can be used for TSO (see the manual or help for TSO commands).
In the COBOL program you can code the FD to have RECORD VARYING
DEPENDING ON YOUR-UNSIGNED-NUMERIC-FIELD and then MOVE LARGEST-RECORD (1
: YOUR-UNSIGNED-NUMERIC-FIELD) TO YOUR-WORKING-STORAGE-RECORD.  For best
results YOUR-UNSIGNED-NUMERIC-FIELD should USAGE BINARY.  

In CICS the point is moot because your COBOL program should not contain
any FD's and should use CICS commands to access all files.

Andreas Lerch wrote:
> 
> >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Ursprï¿½ngliche Nachricht <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
> Andreas
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records in IBM COBOL 85 was Re: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T01:04:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai2ltm$anv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020724.18084264@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online> <5cu3kuklom8dul4dnj9lh4v7tov3u8n9ns@4ax.com> <20020727.15550279@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online> <3D44785F.D8112EC7@istar.ca>`

```
Clark,
  Although I mentioned (in a previous post) that "fixed file attribute"
checking is a requirement of the ANSI Standard and *not* something that IBM
came up with - there is another side to this story.

In one of the very earliest "interpretation" requests of the '85 Standard,
ANSI (via the J4 technical committee) made it ABUNDANTLY clear that it is up
to EACH implementor exactly what "attributes" they do and do not check.  In
fact, this was made explicit in an Amendment to the ANSI Standard.

Therefore,  your statement (below),
 "While I believe that this protection should be that the COBOL maximum
length is not less than the length specified for the file in the data set
label or Catalog that is not what the ANSI idiots specified."

is "slightly" misleading.  It was IN DEED, IBM and not ANSI (or ISO) that
decided to do this "checking".

Personally, I don't think it is a "bad" idea to make certain that anything
that you specify in your COBOL source code actually MATCHES the way the file
"exists" on outside media.  I think that everyone would have been "happier"
if IBM had done an extension similar to "BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS" that says
to "check the file attributes" - to say "check the file attributes for the
record length or lengths".  But as they didn't, I don't (again personally)
think that it is such a bad idea to have the code match the file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records in IBM COBOL 85 was Re: Language-Environment Cobol for MVS&VM zOS 1.2?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T10:49:20-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D469960.322D49C7@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20020724.18084264@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online> <5cu3kuklom8dul4dnj9lh4v7tov3u8n9ns@4ax.com> <20020727.15550279@04307839700-0001.dialin.t-online> <3D44785F.D8112EC7@istar.ca> <ai2ltm$anv$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My rant came about from dealing with SMF (Systems Management Facility)
accounting and performance records.  The maximum record length had been
specified differently for different files.  The mainframe system
function can deal with that (SMF is an operating system component) in
the production of the files so in this instance I was getting protection
I neither wanted nor needed.  In general, I would be content with
allowing RECORD 0 VARYING DEPENDING ON and this probably would be a good
cross platform extension.  There also may be value in not having the
check in instances where there are subset and superset versions of files
with different fixed lengths for the records depending on the file but a
common base.   
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Clark,
…[126 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Andreas
```

<a id="t-2cd4643950"></a>
## Thread: Cobol and Performance — 14 this month / 25 total

### Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "News" <Martina.Moritz@chello.at>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T20:54:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at>`

```
I just started to do some research abt. performance and try to use it for my
work.
Years ago we used VS-Cobol II and still have some load modules out of this
time.
meanwhile we have LE/370 on OS/390 2.10 an Cobol for MVS Version 2.2.1.

Our Systemgroup defined compiler directives years ago and until today there
are no changes.

So first I tried to change some things (according to
http://www-3.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/library/booksabc/cobpf120.pdf):

 optimize(full), numproc(pfd, was mig), nossrange, norent, trunc(opt, was
bin)

the programs were avg. 6 percent faster (CPU-time)

when possible I try to avoid "search all" (only with our national zip-codes)
I use a table with 10.000 Elements and load the zip-codes.
The tables is filled abt. 25% but now I am able to
move field (zip-code) to io-field

all opt. together my programs are now faster for abt. 26 %.

"Fastsrt" with dfsort brought 50%

This week I changed inspect converting lowercase to uppercase with an
external subprogram
changing lower to upper. I can not use a function because of special
national character.
my external prg is 10 times faster (one million calls).

Are there some more things to optimize ?

thanks !
Gerald Moritz
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-27T21:46:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahvltp$ig3$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at>`

```
Just one thing you might want to look into.  *IF* you are on the latest IBM
COBOL compiler, see:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGY3LR00/7.1.48

which says (in part) concerning the UPPER-CASE intrinsic function,

"If argument-1 is national, each lowercase letter is replaced by its
corresponding uppercase letter based on the specification given in the
Unicode database UnicodeData.txt, available from the Unicode Consortium at
http:/www.unicode.org/."

This might "solve" your upper-/lower-case conversion problems (even with
"special characters")

Also when/if you upgrade to the latest IBM mainframe compiler, you should
check out the NEWER "performance" paper at:
   http://www-1.ibm.com/support/manager.wss?rt=4&org=SW&doc=7001475&aid=1

If you are really using the "older" compiler that is referenced in your
original note, you should be aware that IBM has *ALREADY* announced a "drop
of support" date for it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T09:26:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at>`

```
News wrote:
> 
> when possible I try to avoid "search all" (only with our national zip-codes)

Why ?

If the table is in sequence then a SEARCH ALL will use a binary chop
search while a SEARCH will use a serial search.  Even with a small table
there will be significantly fewer comparisons done (as long as data is
ordered with unique keys).
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4cH09.317341$iX5.15887779@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz...
> News wrote:
> >

zip-codes)

Because the way he's doing it is much faster (26% - I'm surprised the saving

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T17:22:29+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D441A45.F3D25A5C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <4cH09.317341$iX5.15887779@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> > If the table is in sequence then a SEARCH ALL will use a binary chop
> > search while a SEARCH will use a serial search.  Even with a small table
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is not more).

That is not a valid conclusion from what he said.  He did say that with
_all_ the various optimisations that he had tried there was a 26%
improvement.  He only said that he avoided SEARCH ALL not that he was
able to produce any code that was faster or that he had even tried it
except for the zip-codes.

Even with a small table of, say, 200 items if the table items are
ordered and unique then a SEARCH ALL can be 10 or mores _times_ faster
than a serial SEARCH.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T17:42:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R1W09.329483$iX5.16402204@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <4cH09.317341$iX5.15887779@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3D441A45.F3D25A5C@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D441A45.F3D25A5C@Azonic.co.nz...
> JerryMouse wrote:
>
> > > If the table is in sequence then a SEARCH ALL will use a binary chop
> > > search while a SEARCH will use a serial search.  Even with a small
table
> > > there will be significantly fewer comparisons done (as long as data is
> > > ordered with unique keys).
> >
> > Because the way he's doing it is much faster (26% - I'm surprised the
saving
> > is not more).
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> than a serial SEARCH.

It IS a valid conclusion from what he said. The way I took it, he's not
using SEARCH instead of SEARCH ALL; he's not using EITHER. He said:

"when possible I try to avoid "search all" (only with our national
zip-codes)
I use a table with 10.000 Elements and load the zip-codes.
The tables is filled abt. 25% but now I am able to
move field (zip-code) to io-field"

It's obvious to the most casual observer that he defined an array:

   MYCITY OCCURS 10000 PIC X(30).

filled the table (only 25% of the 10000 elements exist) and, when he wanted
a city, used:

   MOVE MYCITY(ZIP) TO OUTPUT-CITY

His problem definition implies he has about 2500 postal codes and that his
postal codes are numeric. Further, he used the delimiter "10-period-000"
instead of "10-comma-000." Based on the facts, he's from Austria.

The technique of using the data itself as a pointer is, of course, way, way
faster than any kind of search technique.

A second-generation technique would work in the U.S:

   MYPOINTER OCCURS 100000 PIC 9(4) COMP.

   MYCITY OCCURS 5000 PIC X(30).

Load MYCITY sequentially and put the index of each MYCITY element in the
entry appropriate to the city's ZIP code in the appropriate MYPOINTER field.
So, if MYCITY(3456) contains

"HOUSTON"

the MYPOINTER array might end up containing:
        ...
   (76998) xxxx
   (76999) xxxx
   (77000) 1234
   (77001) 1234
   (77002) 1234
       ...

Then, when confronted with ZIP code "77002" you access the 77,002 entry in
MYPOINTER which gives you the table element of the desired city. Or:

   MOVE MYCITY(MYPOINTER(ZIP)) TO REPORT-CITY

It's a fast technique.
```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D44EC7E.F3541100@Azonic.co.nz...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> >
> > > > Because the way he's doing it is much faster (26% - I'm surprised
the
> > saving
> > > > is not more).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> over a SEARCH ALL was quite invalid.

The best I'll concede is the 26% was ambigious. It looked to me he was
claiming:

6% by various compile options
26% by ditching SEARCH ALL in favor of a direct-lookup table
50% by using fastsort
90% by using an external lower-to-upper routine

One can also look at this list and get he went from 6 to 26 percent by
eliminating the SEARCH ALL - accounting for 20% of the improvement, or a 26%
improvement AFTER the compiler options fix and before the sort fix.

>
> > > He only said that he avoided SEARCH ALL not that he was
> > > able to produce any code that was faster or that he had even tried it
> > > except for the zip-codes.

Well he certainly wouldn't have to claim a direct lookup was faster than a
SEARCH ALL. That's self-evident. In fact, I wouldn't be surprised if, on a
modern operating system, an ISAM lookup wouldn't be in the same ballpark as
a SEARCH ALL.

>
> > The way I took it, he's not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > not that he was able to produce any code that was faster .. except for
the zip-codes.
>
> which you seemed to miss.

I didn't miss it. He did produce a faster executable, partly through
recoding (lookup and case-folding) and partly through external manipulations
(compiler options and sorting).

>
> My question was 'why does he avoid SEARCH ALL when possible' ?.

I'll type slowly: Because he found faster ways to accomplish the same things
that were being done by the SEARCH ALL.

>
> Perhaps he only avoids it on the zip-table and uses it elsewhere.

Perhaps. Perhaps not. It could be his watch was wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T15:05:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D454B90.E59EC417@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <4cH09.317341$iX5.15887779@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3D441A45.F3D25A5C@Azonic.co.nz> <R1W09.329483$iX5.16402204@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3D44EC7E.F3541100@Azonic.co.nz> <iC019.370017$Im2.19226764@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> > > > except for the zip-codes.
> 
> Well he certainly wouldn't have to claim a direct lookup was faster than a
> SEARCH ALL. That's self-evident. 

Which is why it was excepted.  

However direct lookups are only useful for numeric codes with a smallish
range.  Obviously his country's zip codes are 4 digit numeric and there
are enough to warrant such a large table size.  This is not suitable as
a general replacement mechanism for SEARCH ALL.

Certainly it is also possible to access a table using a hashed key
mechanism too, but this usually requires that clashes be dealt with too
making the code far more complex and slower.

> In fact, I wouldn't be surprised if, on a
> modern operating system, an ISAM lookup wouldn't be in the same ballpark as
> a SEARCH ALL.

I would be entirely surprised if a single access using ISAM was within
an order of magnitude of a single SEARCH ALL.  Have you ever done such a
comparison ?  

In fact I am beginning to doubt that you actually know how a SEARCH ALL
works.

The ISAM file lookup through the index of keys would do roughly the same
number of comparisons as a SEARCH ALL binary chop and this part would be
roughly equivalent, but the ISAM file would add disk accesses or, at
least, buffer accesses on top of those comparisons.

Now it may be that in some cases an ISAM lookup may be a better option
than storing the data in the table, this would be the result of the
number of table accesses required to be done in a program run, and the
cost of setting up the table.  In the case of the zip table the set up
is the cost of reading approx 25,000 records into the table.  Obviously
if only a few lookups are required in a run then this is a very high
cost compared to just doing the lookup into the ISAM file.  If, however,
there are several thousand lookups required then the initial cost may
outweigh the large savings on the random ISAM reads.

> I'll type slowly: Because he found faster ways to accomplish the same things
> that were being done by the SEARCH ALL.

Only on the zip-table, which is why it was excepted.

That mechanism is not suitable as a general replacement for table
handling and  searching, which is why there are several different
mechanisms, and why I asked why he avoids SEARCH ALL 'when possible'
(except on the zip-codes).
```

>From: "News" Martina.Moritz@chello.at 
>Date: 2002-07-27 16:54 Eastern Daylight Time

>when possible I try to avoid "search all" (only with our national zip-codes)
>I use a table with 10.000 Elements and load the zip-codes.
>The tables is filled abt. 25% but now I am able to
>move field (zip-code) to io-field

I believe you are saying that since the zipcode is 5 digits, you
just load the 10,000-element table from an external file at run
time.  Those numbers which do not have any place names are
just left blank.
Then, rather than doing any kind of search, you just go 
directly to the element using the zipcode as the index.  So, for 
example, if you know the zipcode "29376," you just say 
MOVE PLACENAME (ZIPCODE) TO ADDRESSNAME 
and immediately get "Roebuck, South Carolina."  
This sounds very fast.  How would the performance times be
affected if the zipcodes and place names were kept on an
ISAM or database external file? 



Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T20:17:11-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D447B77.485067BD@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at>`

```
Beware of NUMPROC(PFD).  If you have any data in signed fields that
might contain an F zone because your shop uses CSP or other product that
has an F zone for plus and D zone for minus, you are in trouble.  Since
INSPECT CONVERTING now generates just a translate instruction if your
translate table uses constants in the from and to (a constant being
either a literal or a field that is never a target field), the
replacement of INSPECT CONVERTING by a CALL is counter productive.

You also are limited to the United States in the zip code table.  Most
countries have alpha-numeric postal codes, some relatively straight
forward like that of Canada and some wierd like England (IIRC it is
variable length).

News wrote:
> 
> I just started to do some research abt. performance and try to use it for my
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gerald Moritz
```

       01 COMPANY-NAME PIC X(24).

PERFORM 500-READ-RECORDS.

500-READ-RECORDS.
           READ EMP-FILE RECORD
               AT END MOVE "YES" TO END-FILE.


I would very greatful if someone could figure out what problem is
because I'm stumped.....     Thanks.

#### ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T13:06:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello

I think you should use the file status with the file that you are
reading and test it for success after every I/O action, i.e. OPEN,
READ and CLOSE.  This would at least tell you whether or not such
actions are accepted as valid.  I take it that prior to the code you
showed us, that you read the header record separately.  You could also
consider using a debugger or putting some displays into your program
to show the contents of the records as they are read, and perhaps also
the eventual contents of the table.

To use the file status, you must include a two byte data item for it,
associate it with the file via the FILE STATUS clause of the SELECT
statement and test its contents as appropriate for the specific I/O
action, displaying the value when it is considered to be a valid
value.  Look in your manual for the appropriate codes and their
meanings.

Good luck,

Robert

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T03:26:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020728232628.01761.00001453@mb-cf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Gug,

You should try to solve the problem in a top down fashion. For example:

perform read header rec
if input-file-eof 
   display error msg1
   stop run
if not a header rec
   display error msg2
   stop run
end-if
perform process header
if input-file-eof 
   display error msg3
   stop run
end-if

perform read 1st detail rec
perform process detail-recs
     until input-file-eof
perform end it
stop run
.

Now in your process detail recs routine
you move the data from the previous read
to your table; bump the index; read the next detail rec.

Sometimes the performed routines are so trivial, you may want to code them in
line. But it helps you think at the "macro" (as opposed to "micro") level and
not get too bogged down with the details. It's like peeling an onion, one level
at a time. Sometimes it'll even make you cry :-).

HTH, Jack.
```

#### ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T03:37:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020728233723.25242.00001612@mb-cl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm a mainframe body and things are a tad different in that environment than in
the PC world but what I see is this....

1)  The FD doesn't have a bunch of clauses after it which in my environment
implies a VSAM (keyed file - ISAM in the PC world?).

2)  Two 01 levels under an FD is a redefinition in both environments - so your
company record will occupy the the EMP-RECORD definition regardless.

3)  Since the 01 for the EMP-RECORD is smaller than the 01 for the COMPANY-NAME
the file is variable in length?

4)  Your read is READ EMP-FILE RECORD which implies a keyed file read
sequentially in my environment (only we say NEXT RECORD) 

5)  The first perform of the 500 paragraph is not followed by a move of the
COMPANY-NAME definition to WS so when the perform is done the first time thru
the COMPANY-NAME is moved into the first occurance of the table.

I don't know what it is that makes you assume you aren't reading the file since
you say you get the company record which is the first record in the file so you
are reading the file at least once.  If it is because the table isn't correct
see #5, if not that are you saying you aren't getting a 2nd read of the file?

Eileen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T08:09:13+01:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3D463B99.5E9AA1D3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <20020728233723.25242.00001612@mb-cl.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama wrote:
> 
> 1)  The FD doesn't have a bunch of clauses after it which in my environment
> implies a VSAM (keyed file - ISAM in the PC world?).

I haven't any clauses under an FD for many years.  Lack of them only
implies they aren't needed.

> 4)  Your read is READ EMP-FILE RECORD which implies a keyed file read
> sequentially in my environment (only we say NEXT RECORD)

NEXT RECORD is only required when ACCESS IS DYNAMIC which may be appled
to RELATIVE or INDEXED files.  NEXT RECORD is optional on ACCESS
SEQUENTIAL or ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL files. There is no implication
that it is a keyed file at all, with ot without a NEXT RECORD.

> 5)  The first perform of the 500 paragraph is not followed by a move of the
> COMPANY-NAME definition to WS so when the perform is done the first time thru
> the COMPANY-NAME is moved into the first occurance of the table.

I think that the code is only a sample of the program - the bits he
feels doesn't work - and the read of the Company header was elsewhere.
```

#### ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T07:59:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D44E7EF.F398D76F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com>`

```
Lost wrote:
> 
> ____________________________Start data file___
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> records.  Heres what my records look like.

You need to ensure that the data file actually is like the way that you
have told the program that it is.

If you have defined the file as ORGANISATION SEQUENTIAL then it may be
expecting that each record is a 24 byte chunk of data and thus will read
the 2nd record staring at byte 25.  If the data file is actually just a
text file created with, say, notepad, then the data will not line up
with how you have told the compiler to expect it.

File (if done by notepad '\n' = carriage return line feed line
terminator (CR/LF)):
koch.IND\n7115554444505000444\n7115564444405000001\n 

Record layout for SEQUENTIAL specifies:
<-record number 1------><-record number 2------><-re .. etc

If you have defined the file as LINE SEQUENTIAL then each record is 'up
to the next CR/LF'.

As you have not indicated the SELECT statement or given us enough detail
on what the actual data file bytes are I can only guess that you need to
make the ORGANISATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
```

#### ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T21:29:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D45B49C.58644075@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com>`

```


Lost wrote:

> I was wondering if someone can help me figure out what I'm doing
> wrong.  The data file that my program needs to read looks like this...
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> because I'm stumped.....     Thanks.

The guessing game ! You 'newbies' are inclined to feed us only what you
think is relevant. Given you wrote subsequently, "I musn't alter the text
file....", this has to be a homework assignment. As Richard has already
pointed out, indications are you are using a text-file = LINE SEQUENTIAL.
(For a further explanation see my reply to thread "Re: Var Length
Records")

Although you have 'displayed' the input records, it doesn't indicate their
length. I've just typed the following into an editor :-

this is (23) characters
this is (25) characters
this is (30) characters

They all look like 23 characters to you, but I have padded the last two
with spaces before you get to the automatic CR/LF (x'0d0a').

My best guess -  and please confirm - your test paper reads something like
the following :-

"You have an input file created in Notepad ( = LINE SEQUENTIAL) and each
record is xx characters long. The first record you read is a header record
giving the company name, followed by a series of records containing the
following data........"

Give us a quote from your test paper. I'd be pretty certain your tutor
wouldn't throw a zinger like VARIABLE LENGTH records at you. Confirm the
actual test text, (the part which refers to the input file), and you will
get a more definitive answer.

To clarify something in your last message - you made the name 05 pic x(24)
under one Level 01 for ONE record format - that wont do it for you -
you've merely increased the record size, but you aren't reading in that
number of characters per record.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-455ec37140"></a>
## Thread: waterfall development — 13 this month / 14 total

### waterfall development

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T12:07:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
I have been hearing the term "waterfall" used a lot recently, and in 
general it has a bad meaning.  But I don't really understand how
there can be any methodology other than waterfall.  Probably, I
do not understand the meaning of the term.  
Looking at Ron Jeffries's site on "Xtreme Programming," he 
describes Waterfall as the Development Life Cycle:  1. Analysis
2. Design   3. Code   4. Test 
It is a "waterfall," because you can never go backwards.  I.e., once
the third step, Code, is begun, you cannot go back to Design, let
alone to Analysis.  As an alternative, he suggests "Test First" 
development.  I could not find any example of Test First on the website.  

My problems: 
1.  What other way is possible besides Analyze, Design, Code, and Test? 
Although there are frequent jokes about "in this shop, we code first and
design later"--meaning that the designer did not think of everything--whether
good or bad, the design is always done before the coding. 
Quite aside from corporate-sized COBOL projects, I follow exactly the 
same steps if I am writing a hobby-level Java program, or making
a new Excel spreadsheet to figure my gas mileage, or even cooking 
breakfast in the morning.  

2.  I don't think the idea that there are clearly marked Points of No
Return is a valid concept.  Suppose a test reveals improper coding?  
Do you say "Sorry, the test failed.  Trash the project"?  No, you go
back and fix the code.  If the test reveals that some basic principle
was overlooked you go back to Analysis and make the corrections.  
If a coder finds himself in a cul de sac, he might go back to the
designer and say, "You can't get there from here--let's find a different
approach."  Regardless of what's written on a document, there is
a constant interaction among the various phases. 

3.  Just because a program goes into production doesn't mean that
it is complete.  Otherwise, why would software vendors release a new 
version every year?  I don't think a program or a system is like a 
chair, in that you build it once and use it forever.  It is more like a
garden, in that you build it and then spend the rest of your life
maintaining and enhancing it.  

4.  In 1980, where I started programming, we did not follow any 
explicit methodology.  You just talked to an accountant, tried to
figure out what he was asking for, wrote a program, installed it, and
went to the accountant and asked, "Is this it?" and then made the
changes.  This process was ongoing for varying lengths of time.  
If it was a simple process, maybe the first try was acceptable. 
In a multifunctional accounting system, the process went on
forever, as new functions were added, old bugs were identified, and
revised definitions appeared.  We were told that this was very bad--
such fix-it-on-the-fly programming was very costly.  We should use
written test plans, specifically identified milestones requiring 
someone's signature, and so forth.  It seems to me that every
recommendation to avoid the Waterfall consists of an attempt
to return the days of happy chaos. 


Will someone who opposes "Waterfall" development explain his
reasons for opposing it? 




Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: waterfall development

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T14:19:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R3T09.219125$q53.5567658@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
You have not quite got the whole picture -
Waterfall development, along with the others, is a PROJECT MANAGEMENT methodology,
not a programming technique. ;)

In a project oriented environment, you often are required to get from the start of a project through delivery in a very specific
amount of time, and so you try very hard to never go 'backwards.'  For one things, changes in the requirements for a software
delivery are MUCH more expensive if they occur in say, the testing phase, than in the design phase. It is also a way to control
cost.

There are other methodologies out there, usually applied in corporate settings where the management views IT as an evolving process,
and not a hated cost center. One is cyclic development, which is pretty much what you describe yourself as doing back in the 1980's.
You get the requirements, design,code,and test the product, then go back to the users for feedback and do the whole thing over
again. Sounds inefficient, but it is actually very efficient in some settings.

It really *is* all in your point of view- at the lowest level, your point of view is always going to be to figure out how to do
something, do it, test it, then revise it. At a much higher level, a software project's cost can be much better controlled by
specifying and controlling those steps on a project wide basis.

-Paul

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message news:20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com...
> I have been hearing the term "waterfall" used a lot recently, and in
> general it has a bad meaning.  But I don't really understand how
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: waterfall development

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T11:26:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uk82r556gd1463@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
I recommend the article,
< http://www.eng.uwaterloo.ca/~msci738/clippings/00000059.pdf >.
It discusses different methodologies, including Waterfall,
and presents the Spiral Model.

Waterfall applies, generally, to all development; but has
a specialized meaning, as well. In this specialized sense,
the opposite of waterfall is RAD, XP fits within RAD with
an emphasis on OO.

The ultimate in RAD is that users do their own analysis,
design, code, and test. For example; spreadsheets,
macros, etc. A user or small group of users may do
their own analysis ("This is what we need."), but may not
have the technical expertise to make it work. This is
when a programmer gets involved.

As the focus shifts away from a known set of users, it
becomes necessary to assign a surrogate user, an analyst.
As the project increases in size, a team of analysts is
assigned, along with designers, coders, testers, etc., and
the distinct (and formal) characteristics of waterfall emerge.

It is not a question of whether waterfall, in the general sense,
will be used; it is a question of how formal, in the specialized
sense, it will become.

While it is true that water falls, the analogy to a waterfall
only applies to the primary flow of the process. Each
step has a secondary flow "upstream" to the previous step.
This allows for clarification and correction.

I am not certain what "Test First" means; but having looked
at XP I would not be surprised to find that it is the first of
two steps.

1. Test what the user likes or doesn't like about
    (some or) your program.
2. Code or modify the program to present to the user.

Repeat these steps until the user is satisfied. This works
because the user is the ultimate tester. It works well with
OO because it is relatively easy to refactor classes to
shift 'behavior', to inherit from existing classes to modify
'behavior', and add classes to create 'behavior'.


Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com...
> I have been hearing the term "waterfall" used a lot recently, and in
> general it has a bad meaning.  But I don't really understand how
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> design later"--meaning that the designer did not think of
everything--whether
> good or bad, the design is always done before the coding.
> Quite aside from corporate-sized COBOL projects, I follow exactly the
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: waterfall development

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T17:40:14+00:00

```
>From: "Rick Smith" ricksmith@mfi.net 
>Date: 2002-07-28 11:26 Eastern Daylight Time
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>and presents the Spiral Model.

If I read it correctly, the Waterfall method is Document-driven,
while the Spiral method is Risk-driven. 

Regarding the advantages of Evolutionary Development over
The Waterfall, he comments: 
"It is generally difficult to distinguish it [the 
Evolutionary Development model] from the old code-and-fix 
model, whose spaghetti code and lack of planning were the 
initial motivation for the waterfall model." 

My sentiments exactly.  


Regarding the advantages of Spiral over Waterfall, he comments: 
"Internal software developments have a great deal of 
flexibility and freedom to accommodate stage-by-stage 
commitments....The world of contract software has a harder 
time achieving these degrees of flexibility and freedom 
without losing accountability and control."  

and 

"For large-scale use in situations where people bring widely 
differing experience bases to the project, added levels of 
elaboration-such as have been accumulated over the years 
for document-driven approaches-are important in ensuring 
consistent interpretation..." 

Since 1988, when the article was written, the move has been 
away from in-house and towards outsourcing.  So it would 
seem that Spiral development is already a dead horse, from
that standpoint.  If you bring in contractors, you have to
use Waterfall.  
And any methodology which depends on a uniform level of
expertise would seem to be highly suspect, wouldn't it?  
A typical corporation will have people ranging from Ph.D.'s 
with 30 years' experience to part-timers from the local drug 
rehab facility.  If all such organizations are forced to use
the Waterfall, that doesn't leave much room for Spiral. 


Furthermore, it seems that everything from "Transform 
model" onwards requires a code-generator, which sort of 
rules out COBOL, doesn't it? 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: waterfall development

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020728134014.01739.00001683@mb-cf.aol.com...
> >From: "Rick Smith" ricksmith@mfi.net
> >Date: 2002-07-28 11:26 Eastern Daylight Time
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> consistent interpretation..."

Beginning on page 69, first column bottom, he began an
evaluation of the Spiral Model. The parts you are quoting are
from a section titled "Difficulties," which begins on page 69, third
column, bottom. Rather than being advantages over Waterfall,
these are risks (as of 1988) of using Spiral. The risks of using
Waterfall is why Spiral came about.

> Since 1988, when the article was written, the move has been
> away from in-house and towards outsourcing.  So it would
> seem that Spiral development is already a dead horse, from
> that standpoint.  If you bring in contractors, you have to
> use Waterfall.

Actually, Spiral seems to be alive and kicking with large
government projects. If you'll take another look at the text, in the
diagram, you will find that the end of the Spiral is the Waterfall.
The early parts of the Spiral have to do with eliminating the risks
that Waterfall could not accommodate. The two actually work
together, at the end. The Spiral Model also allows early exit
to other methodologies, depending upon the risks.

> And any methodology which depends on a uniform level of
> expertise would seem to be highly suspect, wouldn't it?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Waterfall, that doesn't leave much room for Spiral.

There is a paper at Microsoft that discusses what appears to
be their use of Spiral. (I can never be certain whether MS is
saying "This is what we do." or "This is what you should do.")
< http://www.microsoft.com/TechNet/ittasks/plan/sysplan/PROCESS.asp >
In this document, Microsoft implies that Spiral and RAD
are the same, "Two of the more popular life cycle models are
the traditional waterfall model and the spiral or rapid application
development (RAD) model."

> Furthermore, it seems that everything from "Transform
> model" onwards requires a code-generator, which sort of
> rules out COBOL, doesn't it?

I am not sure what you mean by 'onwards'. The Transform
Model was the last methodology discussed and is still the
"dream" of Formal Methods. The Transform Model, the
creation of code from formal specifications, could be used
to generate COBOL programs. If sucessful, it would, when
used, eliminate COBOL programmers.
```

#### ↳ Re: waterfall development

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T12:37:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai1act023pk@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
> I have been hearing the term "waterfall" used a lot recently,
and in
> general it has a bad meaning.  But I don't really understand
how
> there can be any methodology other than waterfall.  Probably,
I
> do not understand the meaning of the term.

> Looking at Ron Jeffries's site on "Xtreme Programming," he
> describes Waterfall as the Development Life Cycle:  1.
Analysis
> 2. Design   3. Code   4. Test

> It is a "waterfall," because you can never go backwards.
I.e., once
> the third step, Code, is begun, you cannot go back to Design,
let
> alone to Analysis.  As an alternative, he suggests "Test
First"
> development.  I could not find any example of Test First on
the website.

Other's have said it already, but the classic waterfall
approach does not precluded you from going upstream.  It does
however, in contrast to XP, presume that you "mostly" complete
one phase before moving to the next.  There's nothing
intrinsically wrong with the classic approach, but it has a
hard time reacting to environments where the requirements are
_not_ relatively static.

Another classic approach was to scale your project proportional
to the set of requirements, much like you would double or
triple a recipe.  Although this is not really part of the
waterfall method, it's often lumped into the "standard methods"
that are contrasted with XP.

The relieve the project under these conditions, XP suggests a
couple of techniques:

1)  Drop the presumption that the project's progress is
essentially linear, and consider it more of a to do list.  When
your list is complete, the project is.  There's a bewildering
flowchart at that tries to give an overview of a typical
project:
http://www.extremeprogramming.org/map/project.html

2)  Use smaller teams with small, but well-defined, sets of
requirements.  Larger projects are harder to maneuver, so XP's
working theory is that a large project should be decomposed
into a group of smaller project with achievable goals.

3)  Design your tests and your application at the same time.
What's more, design your application such that it can easily be
tested.  As you are implementing the design, repeatedly
validate your work with those tests.  A well-written suite of
tests should validate most, if not all, of your project's
requirements.  I think this is what's being referred to by
"Test First."

> My problems:
> 1.  What other way is possible besides Analyze, Design, Code,
and Test?
> Although there are frequent jokes about "in this shop, we
code first and
> design later"--meaning that the designer did not think of
everything--whether
> good or bad, the design is always done before the coding.
> Quite aside from corporate-sized COBOL projects, I follow
exactly the
> same steps if I am writing a hobby-level Java program, or
making
> a new Excel spreadsheet to figure my gas mileage, or even
cooking
> breakfast in the morning.

It's no so much the types of tasks you'll be doing, but rather
the order and periodicity of those tasks.

> 2.  I don't think the idea that there are clearly marked
Points of No
> Return is a valid concept.  Suppose a test reveals improper
coding?
> Do you say "Sorry, the test failed.  Trash the project"?  No,
you go
> back and fix the code.  If the test reveals that some basic
principle
> was overlooked you go back to Analysis and make the
corrections.
> If a coder finds himself in a cul de sac, he might go back to
the
> designer and say, "You can't get there from here--let's find
a different
> approach."  Regardless of what's written on a document, there
is
> a constant interaction among the various phases.

> 3.  Just because a program goes into production doesn't mean
that
> it is complete.  Otherwise, why would software vendors
release a new
> version every year?  I don't think a program or a system is
like a
> chair, in that you build it once and use it forever.  It is
more like a
> garden, in that you build it and then spend the rest of your
life
> maintaining and enhancing it.


These are some of XP's argument for why the waterfall method
does not work very well.

> 4.  In 1980, where I started programming, we did not follow
any
> explicit methodology.  You just talked to an accountant,
tried to
> figure out what he was asking for, wrote a program, installed
it, and
> went to the accountant and asked, "Is this it?" and then made
the
> changes.  This process was ongoing for varying lengths of
time.
> If it was a simple process, maybe the first try was
acceptable.
> In a multifunctional accounting system, the process went on
> forever, as new functions were added, old bugs were
identified, and
> revised definitions appeared.  We were told that this was
very bad--
> such fix-it-on-the-fly programming was very costly.  We
should use
> written test plans, specifically identified milestones
requiring
> someone's signature, and so forth.  It seems to me that every
> recommendation to avoid the Waterfall consists of an attempt
> to return the days of happy chaos.

It is a bit of a joke that XP is a legitimization of
developmental sloth, and it can certainly be exploited in that
manner.  It appears to me, though, that it's an attempt to open
up the structure of project management, not obliterate it.

Do a macroscopic project plan that segments your application
into relatively small, but testable units.  Within that unit,
do lots of iterations through the analyze-design-implement
loop, with testing being considered/executed at each step in
the process.  Ultimately, the loops become thinner and faster
as the requirements, design and implementation "evolve" to the
most adapted solution.

In spite the the chaos that was present when you started
programming, you probably miss the responsiveness of that
"system."  XP attempts to preserve that ability to react,
without sacrificing the project's long view.

> Will someone who opposes "Waterfall" development explain his
> reasons for opposing it?

I sound liking I'm selling XP, but I've yet to render a
verdict.  I've used it (or a near variant of it) on half a
dozen 40-100K projects with great success.  That success,
however, may owe a larger part to cohesivness and experience of
the team than the project management style.
```

#### ↳ Re: waterfall development

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T18:49:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d442e4a_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20020728080758.05962.00001043@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
Ross,

I vehemently oppose waterfall development (except under some very limited
extremely specific circumstances).

The reason why I oppose it is that I used it for nearly thirty years (there
was no other option) and found that it consistently underdelivered and
overcost.

It did more to damage the perception of  IT and IT professionals than ANY
other single mistake we made...

In addition to the following article:
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/?AURL=%2FArchives%2FV3%2FV30201%2Easp

I have debated and written at length in this newsgroup about the Waterfall.
Please do a Google Groups search for stuff with my name on it. I simply
don't have time at the moment to regurgitate these arguments, but I'll keep
an eye here and try to contribute when I can.

Pete.

>From: "Peter E. C. Dashwood" dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz

That's all right.  I liked the Components article, too.  A lot more baloney
in that one, though.  
I liked your picture, too.  You look like a newspaper opera critic. 

On a grammatical note, I think 
'Business are "afraid" to approach IT' ought to be
'Businesses are "afraid" to approach IT' or 
'Business people are "afraid" to approach IT' or
'Business is "afraid" to approach IT.' 


You pointed out: 
'The Waterfall is based around a Civil Service mentality of "cover 
your arse". Hence, EVERYTHING is signed off and documented. 
(This is often incorporated into ISO 9000 or BS 5750 Certification 
and specific forms MUST be complete before anything further can hapen...'

To me, a "waterfall" is something natural and beautiful, so I resent
the image having been applied to the bureaucratic obstacle course
described in your article.  But I guess it's too late to complain 
about that.  It turns out that I am an antiwaterfallian after all, so
long as we are talking about over-segmented task-focused
development where the goal becomes, not to see your program
running in the field, but rather to get Document 24-B signed by
the Committee for Monitoring Document 24's.  
But as you point out, the ISO 9000 comes down very heavily
on the side of rigid phases and extensive documentation.  Is that
because ISO is comprised of waterfall true believers, like "Dave," or
because as bureaucrats they believe the solution to any problem
is more bureacracy, or what? 
Are you saying, perhaps, that achieving ISO 9000 certification 
might be an indication that you are doing something wrong? 

 
Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte

indicates that your Organisation has an audit trail of what it does (whether
it is "right" or "wrong" is irrelevant to the Certification.). It also often
indicates a certain type of culture within the organisation and again, this
is only "right" or "wrong" depending on how you view it. It is interesting
that most organisations seek Certification in order to win business from
other corporations or Government, NOT because they believe their own
organisation actually NEEDS ISO 9000...<G>

## Thread: regression testing — 17 msgs

### regression testing

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T12:22:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020728082249.05962.00001045@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
Part 2 of my project to upgrade my vocabulary is "regression testing." 

A definition I found says: 
"Regression testing is re-testing of a previously tested program 
following modification to ensure that faults have not been 
introduced or uncovered as a result of the changes made."

I think it is saying, "If you change a program, test it."  
Like, duhh.   
What am I missing? 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T14:28:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%bT09.219135$q53.5569026@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20020728082249.05962.00001045@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
If your program has three modules and you change one, to do a regression test you must test the functions provided by all three
modules. Module testing would test only the functions in the module you changed.
-Paul

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message news:20020728082249.05962.00001045@mb-cn.aol.com...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T21:26:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020728172637.29335.00001538@mb-ci.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<%bT09.219135$q53.5569026@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
>From: "Paul Raulerson" praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com 
>Date: 2002-07-28 10:28 Eastern Daylight Time

>If your program has three modules and you change one, to do a regression test
>you must test the functions provided by all three
>modules. Module testing would test only the functions in the module you
>changed.

Part of my problem is that I have moved from a totally unstructured 
if-it-feels-good-do-it environment to a highly structured environment, with
walkthroughs, turnover approval committees, and so forth.  
Assuming a pure COBOL environment, if I change a program, I should
then test the WFL, or JCL, that is to going to execute the changed program,
along with some other programs that I have not changed.  This is 
called, if I understand the terminology, "system testing."  
The "unit testing" is testing the stand-alone version of the COBOL program. 
When you say "module," I guess you mean some reasonably well-isolated
portion of the code.  
For example, I recently fixed a bug in a program that reads input in
16 possible formats and routes the output to one of 18 possible output
files.  In testing, I checked only the input format and the output file that
were involved.  Then I ran the jobstream, just to see that the jobstream
results were the same as the stand-alone results.  Some things I did
not do:  
1.  I did not test the other 15 formats (I don't know what they do). 
2.  I did not verify that the other programs in the job behaved normally 
    (I don't know how they're supposed to behave).
So your judgement would be:  
A.  I performed a Unit Test only 
B.  I performed a Unit Test and a System Test, but not a Regression Test
C.  Something else



Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T00:42:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bb019.138175$88.2561461@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%bT09.219135$q53.5569026@twister.austin.rr.com> <20020728172637.29335.00001538@mb-ci.aol.com>`

```
By 'module', I was referring to a unit of code that satisfies a distinct requirement, and can be tied directly to a requirement.
However, that does not fit into the typical COBOL development model.

You also point out a very important point; you *should* have been able to test an entire application, because the tests and the
expected results should have been pre-written for you, and should be set in a relatively easy to repeat model.

It is also the reason to have a QA department, so you can do true regression testing (after you do your unit/module testing of
course! :) by handing it over to the Configuration Management department, who puts it in a build for the QA department to test.

<grin>

-Paul

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message news:20020728172637.29335.00001538@mb-ci.aol.com...

Which is unfortunate, I have never been able to understand why a large
number of COBOL programmers are loath to write multiple subprograms 
rather than use "perform" as a subprogram equivalent. 

I have done a couple of large commercial software development projects
using COBOL and the code was always divided up into subprograms that 
were independant. This made unit testing fairly simple and allowed 
later "regression testing" to identify problems related to a single 
subprogram change.

I think what means is, "If you change only one part of a program, you still
have to test the WHOLE program, not just the part of the program you
changed."

As in the old programmers' ditty..

Ninety-nine little bugs in the code,
Ninety-nine little bugs,
Fix one bug,
Test it again,
One hundred and one little bugs in the code.........
```
