#### ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-28T20:26:31-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D447DA7.4CE68021@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<20020728082249.05962.00001045@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
Regression testing means that you rerun the tests from the previous
testing cycle to make sure existing function still works in addition to
the tests to make sure the new function works. 

Ross Klatte wrote:
> 
> Part 2 of my project to upgrade my vocabulary is "regression testing."
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T03:00:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020728230050.01761.00001444@mb-cf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3D447DA7.4CE68021@istar.ca>`

```
Regression testing is saying : So you made a change and you want to test it.
But remember, it’s not sufficient to prove that your change does what you
intended it to do. You also have to prove that you didn’t undo or muck up
what was working before you made your change.

A regression test-case set will contain all the test cases  that proved the
functionality of the original code, plus all the test cases that prove the
functionality of the mods and  fixes applied to date.  

Jack
```

```
In the "Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy" (Volume 5, I think) there
appears the story of Ross McKenna, the Rain God.  It's pointed out there
that scientists would have to call him/it something much more complicated
and obscure, just to make sure that non-scientists wouldn't think they
understood it without the scientists' help.  Thus 'prestidigitators'
instead of 'magicians'.  The same priciple may be operating here.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T04:33:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020730003332.23231.00000274@mb-mp.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3D45D4C8.512AB9A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
>From: Peter Lacey lacey@mb.sympatico.ca 
>Date: 2002-07-29 19:50 Eastern Daylight Time

>scientists would have to call him/it something much more complicated
>and obscure, just to make sure that non-scientists wouldn't think they
>understood it without the scientists' help.  Thus 'prestidigitators'
>instead of 'magicians'.  The same priciple may be operating here.

That was my first impression, but I am backing away from that
view at present.  
My current impression is that in that the world of class-and-method
programming, the test of a class constitutes a Unit test.  One might
not feel a strong need to retest the parent class or the sibling classes
of said parent.  Regression testing would mean, I guess, that you
go ahead and test the parent class or classes and the relevant 
inheriting classes anyway, even though you don't see how they
could be affected.  
A system would comprise several "main" type classes, and the
the test of all the classes making up the system would comprise
a System test. 

In the typical COBOL program, a sort of undisciplined regression 
testing comes along with the unit testing, whether you want it 
or not.  Not too many people that I know of would attempt to test 
a paragraph without running the whole program.  
If the COBOL program were constructed unusually as a collection
of called subprograms, then the concept of Regresssion testing
would make sense.  Testing the subprogram would be the Unit
test, while testing the calling program and its other subprograms
would be the Regression test. 

As another poster pointed out, the idea of regression testing
is meaningless unless you have a predetermined set of tests 
with a predetermined expected outcome that can be run and 
verified even if you don't understand what all the other little 
doohickeys are doing.  


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T05:52:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D462703.4B05DFC4@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3D45D4C8.512AB9A@mb.sympatico.ca> <20020730003332.23231.00000274@mb-mp.aol.com>`

```


Ross Klatte wrote:

> >From: Peter Lacey lacey@mb.sympatico.ca
> >Date: 2002-07-29 19:50 Eastern Daylight Time
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well disagree vis a vis OO. Consider Donald's emphasis that OO design is top
down. You start at the top. Not always easy because like it or not you have
to think lower levels to get what you want to achieve. But having a broad
spectrum of your needs you can then return to the top class for design,
because you have worked out the methods it requires to be invoked. Lets
assume the following model :-

Class A inherits from Base ( Base = Super
Class B inherits from Class A  (Class A = Super for Class B)
Class C etc....

You could go on and on, but it gets tricky if there are too many 'levels',
but that's not suggest it can't be done - just that we 'humans' tend to have
a hard time with a deep hierarchy. (But without checking it,  I guess Net
Express for Collections or GUIs probably drops down to Class F).

We've now got a Business Class 'Z' that needs access to the above structure.

Class A is written and compiled.

You know what you have included in Class A so Class B is written to invoke
the super's (Class A) methods as appropriate. Alternatively Class B has its
own methods which don't require messages to be passed up to Class A. Again
compile.

Class Z is written with the invokes against the above hierarchy - there are
three possibilities (1) invoke MethodB1 in Class B (2) Invoke MethodB2 in
Class B which in turn invokes super MethodA2 for additional 'support', or
(3) Invoke MethodA3 - ClassB is 'bypassed' because it doesn't contain the
invoked method.

It's not there in OO COBOL yet, (I think Thane said there is a variation in
current Fujitsu ?) - but the REPOSITORY SECTION allows each class being
compiled to "forward check" any immediate classes it will invoke - so Class
B will forward check against Class A and Class Z will forward check against
Class B. That's a major boon, because you ascertain whether or not named
methods are missing, including typos on the method names.

You are still stuck with passing/returning  incorrect/incomplete parameters
via Linkage, plus the bugaboo of specifying the wrong object, (back to the
'Pure OO' - you ONLY need to pass object references when relevant -
otherwise pass strings of pic 9's/x's).

Does the above work ? Having compiled Classes A, B and Z - you most
definitely want to test Z. But Z may only send messages to a portion of the
methods available. Testing Z may prove say 25% of the methods in A and B,
although ideally you would hope for a better percentage. But given the 25%,
you know that by using Z, 25% of A and B definitely works. One solution, if
practical,  might be to write a dummy Business Class X which invokes ALL the
methods in A and B

The Class X approach is a bit tedious and perhaps you can ensure complete
tests against A and B by actually doing the coding for Business Classes Z1,
Z2, Z3 etc., (at least the part of those classes that will invoke A or B),
covering all the remaining 75% of methods. That's not unreasonable, because
taking you top down approach you planned to include methods in A and B which
you already know will be required by Z, Z1, Z2, Z3 - even before you write a
line of code for the 'Zebras'.

From my own limited experience the above approach works. Major errors I find
occur in the Zebra classes - wrong parameters/wrong objects - and NOT in the
invoked A and B. Inevitably, (and here I'm thinking in the realm of GUIs),
what I want to invoke doesn't quite do it. (Don has found the same kind of
problems, which caused him to backtrack in his design).

No major overhaul is required, but there are two possibilities :-

(1) Perhaps you have to 'tweak' an invoked method with more parameters - in
effect you have created a new method.- and as a minimum - requires that you
recheck the Zebra programs for correct parameter usage. And this really
isn't to do with regression testing - it's more at the design stage when you
first start out - you've discovered a design flaw, and the likelihood is you
will discover it before you have written too many Zebras.

(2) Assume you have changed a value in MethodA1 - you have changed a
percentage discount calculation from 1.5% to 2.75% - the percentage being a
CONSTANT in the method. Only one test of any of the Zebra classes that
invokes that method need be checked. Once proven, it works for all Zebras.

<snipped> the rest - non-OO is a different animal !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 Just in general this is not true - if you have an object that depends upon lower level services, you
simply stub method/procedure/class that provides those services. You progress downward,
deferring the implementation of each lower level until you are ready to handle it. This works even when you are ultimately going to
have to write device drivers.
```

```
On 29-Jul-2002, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Thus 'prestidigitators'
> instead of 'magicians'.  The same priciple may be operating here.

"Prestidigitators" has an advantage in that it says "it isn't really magic,
it is just tricks".

I saw a cartoon the other day which was unintentionally funnier than it
showed - marketing gurus were renaming cemeteries to weight-loss
environment.   Why is that unintentionally funnier, you may ask?   Because a
century ago grave-yard marketing gurus invented the word cemetery, based
upon Greek, to get rid of the connotations "grave yard" had for people.    I
try not to use the word because it was created by marketing.
```

#### ↳ Re: regression testing

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T15:31:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d484508$1_14@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<20020728082249.05962.00001045@mb-cn.aol.com>`

```
Ross,
My understanding of Regression Testing comes from The Progeni Corporation's
3R's product, a pure regression testing tool.
.
It assumes that a working system exists before maintenance changes begin.
You run a test scenario, with a test database through and 3Rs captures all
the screen output, the report outputs and snapshots the database.  You save
this output.
Then after making changes, you run the same test scenario with the same test
database.
Again 3R's captures all the screens, reports and resulting database.
The package then compares the two copies of output and reports on
differneces.  The important feature of this package is it's ability to
discern and filter out "noise".  For example the report date on the top of a
report should not be reported as an error.  You can also "tell" the package
that what you changed was the length of an account number field.  It will
then filter out these inteneded changes and only report on any unintended
changes that occur.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
Lost wrote:
>            SELECT EMP-FILE ASSIGN TO "A:\DATA4.TXT".

> and it is written in notepad. 

Then it needs to be ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL, as in:

 
             SELECT EMP-FILE 
                 ASSIGN TO "A:\DATA4.TXT"
                 ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL
                 .
```

```
Hey all,

I'm trying to read in some variable-length records from a file using
MicroFocus on a PC, and whenever I try to step through the file (or
run the compiled version), and get to my open statement in the
procedure division, I keep getting an error that says "Error 139: 
Record length or key data inconsistency".  I'm a tad bit inexperienced
with COBOL, so there may be a pretty simple solution I'm overlooking..
I looked in the help manual for MicroFocus, but it was of no help to
me at all.  Here's some snippets from my program that may be of some
help to decipher my problem.
...
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
         FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT TOWNSHIP-FILE ASSIGN TO 'TWNSHP.TXT'
               ORGANIZATION IS RECORD SEQUENTIAL.
...
       DATA DIVISION.
           FILE SECTION.
               FD TOWNSHIP-FILE
               RECORD VARYING 3 TO 30 CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON
                   TOWNSHIP-REC-LENGTH.
               01 TOWNSHIP-RECORD      PIC X(30).
...
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
..
           OPEN INPUT TOWNSHIP-FILE

I get the error when I step to this last line..  I commented out the
'record varying' part of my fd statement, and I did not get the
```

#### ↳ Re: var length records

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T18:57:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D459147.9D19FBF9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<1dad5c39.0207291014.6bff88c7@posting.google.com>`

```


cschepers wrote:

> Hey all,
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris Schepers

Chris,

The clue is in your "ASSIGN TO 'TWNSHP.TXT'". You have used an editor to
create a text file which *automatically" contains CR/LF, (x'0d0a')  after
each record. The concept harkens back to ye oldie typewriters - get to the
right of the page, Carriage Return and then lever for Line Feed <G>.

Your definition should be :-

FILE-CONTROL.

select             TOWNSHIP-FILE
assign             'TWNSHP.TXT'
organization    LINE SEQUENTIAL
access            SEQUENTIAL
file status        ws-fileStatus.

and I'm assuming you have in Working-Storage :-

01 TOWNSHIP-REC-LENGTH. pic 9(04).  *> use a 'big' number and you are

playing it safe.

If you have the Animator you can check each READ and you will see the
'actual length' stored in Township-Record-Length.

Note that LINE SEQUENTIAL is the format from text editors. SEQUENTIAL with
NO CR/LF is typically where you are initially WRITING transactions from
WITHIN  a program to a file and then you can subsequently READ that file
sequentially. You can't inter-mix SEQUENTIAL and LINE-SEQUENTIAL

Sometimes the 'obvious' can be difficult to anticipate and describe even
in Help files - part of the learning curve, I'm afraid <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB..
```

#### ↳ Re: var length records

- **From:** ctimmer@gci.net (Curt Timmerman)

```
>Hey all,
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>Chris Schepers

On the AIX I would have to include a second record like this:
01 TOWNSHIP-RECORD-SHORT      PIC X(3).

This makes the compiler happy with the 'record varying' clause.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: var length records

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T01:52:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020729215214.10770.00000182@mb-ca.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns925A748C498F1ctimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32>`

```
Hi Chris,

You can try redefining:
01 TOWNSHIP-RECORD      PIC X(30).

as:
01 TOWNSHIP-RECORD.    
     05  TS-REC-BYTE  OCCURS 30
           DEPENDING ON
           TOWNSHIP-REC-LENGTH            
                                     PIC X(001).

This should clear up the problem. After the READ, TOWNSHIP-REC-LENGTH will
contain the true length of the record just read.

When you want to write a Variable rec move the desired length of the rec to be
written to TOWNSHIP-REC-LENGTH before the WRITE stmt is executed.    

Regards, Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: var length records

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T03:21:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D460502.C728E330@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<Xns925A748C498F1ctimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32> <20020729215214.10770.00000182@mb-ca.aol.com>`

```


Jnjsle wrote:

> Hi Chris,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards, Jack.

Well he has done it according to the on-line help, which we know he referenced :-
:-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

file section.
fd fd-name.
 record is varying in size
 from 5 to 80 characters depending on ws-record-count.
01 fd-name-record.
 05 fd-name-key    pic x(5).
 05 fd-name-data   pic x(75).
...
working-storage section.
01 ws-record-count   pic 99 comp-x.

In this example an indexed file with variable-length records, varying in length
from 5 to 80 bytes, is defined. Note that any key(s) must lie within the fixed
part of the record.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

And I've checked my own OO Class for Line Sequential and I know this works :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 1500 characters
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record           pic x(1500).
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

No reason for him to change but agree it could be done as you suggest - the online
help/books cover both formats. Wont know until he's tried but I'm sure the error
is in referring to the file as being SEQUENTIAL when it is a LINE SEQUENTIAL. (The
139 compatibility error is because he has defined max record length as 30, for a
SEQUENTIAL and it's finding the CR/LF tagged after his true record length).

*** Ignore this bit Chris, it's for Jack ***.

Using the same format as Chris, has allowed me to write FOUR programs in OO that
will access ALL files, ISAM, Relatrive, Sequential and Line Sequential. Each of
the classes looks like my FD Data-File above, with one exception - the assumption
is that the PrimeKey for an ISAM is in the first 20 positions; you could make it
50. (The idea came from Donald Tees and Pete Dashwood has a variation). Your
Primekey might be 6 or 12 characters - just fill the rest with spaces. So for ISAM
it reads as follows :-

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 20 to 1500 characters *> compiler tells you it *must* be a
minimum of 20
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record.
        05 Data-PrimeKey          pic x(20).      *> This is what causes the
*must* above
        05                                   pic x(1480).

That's all the File classes need. The *real* record definition is held in the
Business Logic class as a copyfile and is passed to the File Class with current
record length, as you suggest, or it returns the 'generic' Data-record above from
a READ.

Yes I know, above doesn't cover Alternate Keys - haven't got that clever yet <G>.
Besides I'm switching to SQL, so it's hardly a priority for me  !!!

I haven't tried it, but looking at M/F file handling examples I believe the same
technique is available outside of OO - providing you pass the full path/filename
and for fixed-length records you pass the max record length - but you have to
create the files INITIALLY as VARIABLE, (even if they are truly fixed length),
otherwise it wont work. The only snag in non-OO is that given you want to access 3
ISAM files you can't call the same ISAM program - depending upon how many you have
in a Business program, you would have to rename the ISAM, and compile for each,
so that you can call ISAM1, ISAM2, ISAM3 etc.

Compare that to OO where I could have 20 INSTANCES of ISAM Files in one program
but only the ONE Class ISAM File in memory.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T23:51:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4727D3.7FE0AC95@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<Xns925A748C498F1ctimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32> <20020729215214.10770.00000182@mb-ca.aol.com> <3D460502.C728E330@shaw.ca> <3D4619E3.2030008@notmyrealaddress.com>`

```
Chris Schepers wrote:

<snip>

> Either way, I'm sure you'll be hearing more from me..  I recently
> graduated from college, and am starting some independent programming
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Chris,

A tip about learning. Each to his own style of course. You didn't say which Micro Focus
compiler - but if you have Net Express or the University Edition then the following apples
:-

I find the standard Windows help feature irritating, you find yourself hoppity-skippty
jumping all over the place, nothing flows in a logical topic sequence. Both Fujitsu and Net
Express offer some excellent On-line books as well as the standard On-line Help. Net Express
has two specific books - Language Reference Manuals 1 and 2. Bit of a pain but worth
printing out.

I taught myself COBOL from scratch using the printed RM/COBOL manual produced for Radio
Shack. Hadn't a clue, (1) Quickly skimped through it, (2) Then a second slightly slower
read, and (3) a more in depth read of topics which seemed immediately pertinent. Did the
same as I switched to Microsoft and Micro Focus COBOLs.

That's how the legal sharks operate. Shelves and shelves of legal tomes - they haven't
absorbed the contents but they build up a memory index so they can zero in on the right
shelf and the correct book, knowing that the topic is there 'in the second part of the book,
somewhere....'. For their current client they are now armed with precedents from the court
case of A versus B etc.

A Language Referenced Manual is a good starting point, followed up by extras that the vendor
supplies as additional features. Your 'memory index' triggers a reference to the topic,
which you can refresh by reviewing from the book, or immediately jump to the on-line Help
for quick examples.

Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ available at cobolreport.com, makes reference to some sites where you
can get examples. Check out following, some are vendor specific, but still worth a look for
ideas  :-

University of Limerick - http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/
Fujitsu - http://www.adtools.com/student/samples/index.htm
Liant (RM/COBOL) - http://www.liant.com/support/code/
Micro Focus - http://www.syntax.gr/departments/CobolSolutions/files/downloads/demos.htm

Best of luck in your programming.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: COBOL 2000 — 5 this month / 6 total

### COBOL 2000

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T22:43:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ryj19.44866$A35.6534710@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I haven't followed all the proposed COBOL2000 changes, (except that I like
EXIT PARAGRAPH), but I wondered if there were any changes to the COMPUTE
verb to improve clarity.

Consider filling a number of variables, VAR1 - VAR5:
MOVE                   VAR-A    TO                                  VAR1
SUBTRACT          VAR-B     FROM                            VAR2
ADD                      VAR-C     TO  VAR-D  GIVING    VAR3
ADD                       1             TO
VAR4
COMPUTE            VAR5   =    (VAR-D +  VAR-E)  /  2

Does anyone else think it would be clearer if you could code:

COMPUTE           (VAR-D +  VAR-E)  /  2   GIVING   VAR5
or
COMPUTE           (VAR-D +  VAR-E)  /  2   INTO        VAR5
or some other construct with the destination at the end, to match the other
constructs?
```

## Thread: Converting Index Flat File to CSV — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** QCBLSRC@WORLDNET.ATT.NET (John)
- **Date:** 2002-07-29T17:50:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,

I have read all the posts regarding converting to CSV but I haven't
come across an answer to my particular situation. First let me say
that I am a "junior" cobol programmer so the answer may be blatently
obvious to someone out there :o

My goal of converting to CSV is to reduce the file size by removing
trailing spaces in my fields and also for it to be easily viewed in
MS-Excel.

I am successfully able to convert to CSV format but I'm having trouble
trying to remove the trailing spaces in the fields. Some of the fields
(like address fields) have two or three parts separated by spaces.  My
fields aren't converting correctly when I delimit by space. Sometimes
I get a comma in the output and sometimes I don't.  Most of my records
(93000) don't have any commas in them so that's really not a problem. 
I see that the INSPECT statement has the LEADING clause, which would
be great if my records had leading spaces. But I don't see that it has
a TRAILING clause :(.   That would be nice.

Any ideas anyone?

Regards,

QCBLSRC
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** "Denis Bacon" <DenisBacon@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T02:05:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gvm19.71352$Ag2.3370446@news2.calgary.shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com>`

```
Don't be lazy.  Just make your own code to go through each field byte by
byte.

you must make your output file sequential, not line sequential and use X'0A'
X'0D' to force the Line Feed, Carriage Return.

Give this a try.


"John" <QCBLSRC@WORLDNET.ATT.NET> wrote in message
news:495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com...
> Hello,
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> QCBLSRC
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T02:25:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020729222518.10820.00000247@mb-ca.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<gvm19.71352$Ag2.3370446@news2.calgary.shaw.ca>`

```
Hi John,

You can try this if your compilersupports the REVERSE function:

INSPECT REVERSE your-fld TALLYING
trailing-blanks FOR LEADING SPACES

COMPUTE true-len = LENGTH OF your-fld - trailing-blanks

STRING your-fld(1:true-len) DELIMITED BY
SIZE INTO csv-rec etc....

Note: All lower case words describe WS elements.

Can't comment on the line seq stuff. I'm a Mainframe type.

HTH, Jack.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T14:16:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D46919F.234FC61A@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Denis Bacon wrote:
> 
> you must make your output file sequential, not line sequential and use X'0A'
> X'0D' to force the Line Feed, Carriage Return.

Why ?

That would seem to me to quite poor advice as it will probably give
fixed length records and waste space while LINE SEQUENTIAL on any
reasonable compiler will remove trailing spaces from the record and give
minimum file size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T07:47:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D47881F.3C6B44D2@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com> <gvm19.71352$Ag2.3370446@news2.calgary.shaw.ca> <3D46919F.234FC61A@Azonic.co.nz> <4cu19.1076$sI2.631868@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> >
> > That would seem to me to quite poor advice as it will probably give
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not on Fujitsu.

We must define 'reasonable compiler' to mean one that will give variable
space truncated records when using LINE SEQUENTIAL  ;-)

Or you can use @CBR_TRAILING_BLANK_RECORD=REMOVE (on version 6.x).

But how does using SEQUENTIAL and adding CR/LF yourself help ?  Either
the file format will be fixed length blocks, or it will be some file
mechanism that includes a header block and variable records with length
indications that may be quite unusable by programs expecting text.

I have on occasion used one byte sequential file and WRITEn every byte
(or READ one byte at a time) for weird formats.  I have even used a
short fixed record (128 byte) and used a sliding buffer when CR/LF was
not the terminator (apostrophe was the segment terminator).  But these
are more complicated than just 'use SEQUENTIAL ..'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T16:56:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N5D19.1632$672.846469@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com> <gvm19.71352$Ag2.3370446@news2.calgary.shaw.ca> <3D46919F.234FC61A@Azonic.co.nz> <4cu19.1076$sI2.631868@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D47881F.3C6B44D2@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D47881F.3C6B44D2@Azonic.co.nz...
> Donald Tees wrote:
> > >
> > > That would seem to me to quite poor advice as it will probably give
> > > fixed length records and waste space while LINE SEQUENTIAL on any
> > > reasonable compiler will remove trailing spaces from the record and
give
> > > minimum file size.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> space truncated records when using LINE SEQUENTIAL  ;-)

Completely agree.  It annoyed me immensely,

>
> Or you can use @CBR_TRAILING_BLANK_RECORD=REMOVE (on version 6.x).
>

And pay runtime royalties for the privilege.

> But how does using SEQUENTIAL and adding CR/LF yourself help ?  Either
> the file format will be fixed length blocks, or it will be some file
> mechanism that includes a header block and variable records with length
> indications that may be quite unusable by programs expecting text.

Their "line sequential" does everything right except truncate trailing
blanks. That can be overcome by making the record length vary from 0 to
maximum depending on variable in the FD, and then setting the variable
appropriately in the code.  I bury that in a method, but it is a pain in the
ass nevertheless.

>
> I have on occasion used one byte sequential file and WRITEn every byte
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> are more complicated than just 'use SEQUENTIAL ..'.

I have done the same.  As you say "sequential" and "line sequential" are two
different things.  Now, I put that sort of thing in an object called an
"editor-compatable" file, rather than line-sequential. On unix systems, it
uses the single terminator.

Donald
```

```
for "add-on" features such as their enhanced SORT).
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T02:25:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xOm19.3237$cm.217529@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com>`

```

"John" <QCBLSRC@WORLDNET.ATT.NET> wrote in message
news:495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com...
> Hello,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are several techniques:

1. INSPECT field-name TALLYING byte-counter
   FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE 'bb' <== that's a double blank

Actually, you needn't do this; you can imbed it in your STRING command, viz:
STRING
...
  '"'               <== that's a double-quote inside a single-quote
   field-name DELIMITED 'bb'
   '"'
   ...
2. COMPUTE byte-counter = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(field-name)
   (apparently Fujitsu compiler only)

3. INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSED(field-name) TALLYING trailing-blank-count FOR
LEADING ' '

Enjoy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T14:40:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D469749.E3A3E6B8@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com> <xOm19.3237$cm.217529@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> There are several techniques:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    '"'

Only useful if there is some mechanism that guarantees that there are no
embedded double spaces.

'John  Smith' is sent as 'John'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T14:26:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4693FE.5D4BDE0E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com>`

```
John wrote:
> 
> I am successfully able to convert to CSV format but I'm having trouble
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> a TRAILING clause :(.   That would be nice.

For each character field move it to a working-storage area and replace
all trailing spaces by nulls (x"00").  You could do this using FUNCTION
REVERSE and INSPECT or by writing a routine that does:

              PERFORM
                  VARYING I FROM Max-Length BY -1
                    UNTIL I < 1 
                       OR Work-Char(I) NOT = SPACE
      
                  MOVE x"00"       TO Work-Char(I)
              END-PERFORM

Each field could be moved to Work-Text (made up of Work-Char OCCURS 100)
and its length to Max-Length then perform this and move it back.

Then output the CSV with:

              STRING '"'     DELIMITED BY SIZE
                     Field-1 DELIMITED BY x"00"
                     '","'   DELIMITED BY SIZE
                     Field-2 DELIMITED BY x"00"
                     .... etc

Putting quotes around the test fields ensures that commas within the
field aren't a problem.

Numeric fields shouldn't be enclosed in quotes and can be moved to an
edited numeric --------9.99, left justified then null terminated for
stringing.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** qcblsrc@worldnet.att.net (John)
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T19:03:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2cb1565.0207301803.5b93c4d6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com>`

```
Thanks everyone for all your help.
But I burned the midnight oil and figured it out.
It's archaic and unconventional so I've been told, but it works.
I guess it's called "sliding ODOs" ?????

Here's the code:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.  CSV.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.


       SELECT WARRANTY
       ASSIGN TO "C:\WARRANTY\ALTWARRANTY"	
	     ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
       RECORD KEY IS WARRANTY-ID
       ALTERNATE KEY IS CUST-NAME WITH DUPLICATES
       ALTERNATE KEY IS CUST-CITY WITH DUPLICATES
       ALTERNATE KEY IS CUST-STATE WITH DUPLICATES
       ALTERNATE KEY IS CUST-ZIP WITH DUPLICATES
       ALTERNATE KEY IS DEALER-CODE WITH DUPLICATES
       ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC
       LOCK MODE IS AUTOMATIC.


       SELECT WARRANTYCSV
	 ASSIGN TO "C:\WARRANTY\EXPORTCSV.TXT"
	 ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
	 ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.


 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.

 FD    WARRANTY.
 01    WARRANTY-RECORD.
       05  WARRANTY-ID             PIC 9(11).
       05  TRANS-TYPE              PIC X(50).
       05  CUST-NAME               PIC X(50).
       05  CUST-ADDRESS            PIC X(50).
       05  CUST-CITY               PIC X(50).
       05  CUST-STATE              PIC X(2).
       05  CUST-ZIP                PIC X(10).
    

 FD	WARRANTYCSV
		RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE
		FROM 1 TO 1075 CHARACTERS
		DEPENDING ON RECORD-LENGTH.

       01  CSV-RECORD.
       05  CSV-WARRANTY-ID             PIC 9(11).
       05  COMMA-1 					 PIC X.
       05  CSV-TRANS-TYPE.
			 07 PIC X
				OCCURS 1 TO 50 TIMES
				DEPENDING ON FIELD-LENGTH-2.		
       05  COMMA-2					 PIC X.
       05  CSV-CUST-NAME.
			 07 PIC X
				OCCURS 1 TO 50 TIMES
				DEPENDING ON FIELD-LENGTH-3.
       05  COMMA-3 					 PIC X.
       05  CSV-CUST-ADDRESS.
			 07 PIC X
				OCCURS 1 TO 50 TIMES 
				DEPENDING ON FIELD-LENGTH-4.
		 05  COMMA-4					 PIC X.
       05  CSV-CUST-CITY.
			 07 PIC X
				OCCURS 1 TO 50 TIMES
				DEPENDING ON FIELD-LENGTH-5.
       05  COMMA-5					 PIC X.
       05  CSV-CUST-STATE.
			 07 PIC X
				OCCURS 1 TO 2 TIMES
				DEPENDING ON FIELD-LENGTH-6.
		 05  COMMA-6					 PIC X.
       05  CSV-CUST-ZIP.
			 07 PIC X
				OCCURS 1 TO 10 TIMES
				DEPENDING ON FIELD-LENGTH-7.
   


 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01  RECORD-LENGTH PIC 9(4).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH PIC 9(2).
 01  END-OF-FILE PIC X VALUE "N".
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-1 PIC 9(3).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-2 PIC 9(3).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-3 PIC 9(3).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-4 PIC 9(3).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-5 PIC 9(3).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-6 PIC 9(3).
 01  FIELD-LENGTH-7 PIC 9(3).
		
 
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.



            OPEN INPUT WARRANTY.
	    OPEN OUTPUT WARRANTYCSV.


            PERFORM EXPORT-CSV UNTIL END-OF-FILE = "Y".
	    GO TO PROGRAM-END.


 EXPORT-CSV.

		MOVE ZERO TO RECORD-LENGTH.		
		READ WARRANTY NEXT RECORD
			AT END 
			MOVE "Y" TO END-OF-FILE.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-1 = FUNCTION LENGTH(WARRANTY-ID).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.	
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-1.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
                MOVE WARRANTY-ID TO CSV-WARRANTY-ID.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-2 = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(TRANS-TYPE).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-2 TO RECORD-LENGTH.	
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-2.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		INSPECT TRANS-TYPE CONVERTING "," TO SPACE.
		MOVE TRANS-TYPE TO CSV-TRANS-TYPE.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-3 = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(CUST-NAME).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-3 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-3.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		INSPECT CUST-NAME CONVERTING "," TO SPACE.
		MOVE CUST-NAME TO CSV-CUST-NAME.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-4 = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(CUST-ADDRESS).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-4 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-4.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		INSPECT CUST-ADDRESS CONVERTING "," TO SPACE.
		MOVE CUST-ADDRESS TO CSV-CUST-ADDRESS.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-5 = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(CUST-CITY).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-5 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-5.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		INSPECT CUST-CITY CONVERTING "," TO SPACE.
		MOVE CUST-CITY TO CSV-CUST-CITY.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-6 = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(CUST-STATE).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-6 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-6.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		INSPECT CUST-STATE CONVERTING "," TO SPACE.
		MOVE CUST-STATE TO CSV-CUST-STATE.

		COMPUTE FIELD-LENGTH-7 = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(CUST-ZIP).
		ADD FIELD-LENGTH-7 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		MOVE "," TO COMMA-7.
		ADD 1 TO RECORD-LENGTH.
		INSPECT CUST-ZIP CONVERTING "," TO SPACE.
		MOVE CUST-ZIP TO CSV-CUST-ZIP.		
      
		WRITE CSV-RECORD.



 PROGRAM-END.
		STOP RUN.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Index Flat File to CSV

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T22:32:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai7lor$t1g$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<495ccd45.0207291650.5d99df37@posting.google.com> <a2cb1565.0207301803.5b93c4d6@posting.google.com>`

```
Just so you know in advance, "sliding ODO's" (where there are either nested
OCCURS DEPENDING ON - or fixed data FOLLOWING an ODO):

 - is not in the ANSI COBOL Standard, therefore
 - not every compiler supports it, and furthermore,
 - those that allow it at compile-time do NOT all treat it the same at
run-time

This doesn't (necessarily) mean you shouldn't use them - but you should be
aware of the "portability" problems in doing so.
```

---

<a id="t-4efb1722cc"></a>
## Thread: test — 1 msgs

<a id="t-8ba8f91f63"></a>
## Thread: What does INV-LRECL mean? — 5 msgs

### What does INV-LRECL mean?

- **From:** J M Pittman <jjmpittmaniiii@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T18:44:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4740E5.30108@mindspring.com>`

```
I'm getting an odd file open error with fujitsu of  'INV-LRECL'.    I 
thought it might be record length at variance with the file description, 
but it appears that is not the answer.  Does anyone know what it means?
```

#### ↳ Re: What does INV-LRECL mean?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T21:04:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai7gjk$bil$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3D4740E5.30108@mindspring.com>`

```
I don't know the answer - but you MIGHT get a better reply if you show us
the entire message *and* the COBOL exact statement that is being executed
when you get the message
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What does INV-LRECL mean?

- **From:** J M Pittman <jjmpittmaniiii@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T19:10:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4746FA.8070307@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<3D4740E5.30108@mindspring.com> <ai7gjk$bil$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The statement is OPEN INPUT PE-FILE.
The message is "OPEN ERROR File=PE_FILE.TXT. 'INV-LRECL'  PRGM=PETAPE.EXE".

William M. Klein wrote:

>I don't know the answer - but you MIGHT get a better reply if you show us
>the entire message *and* the COBOL exact statement that is being executed
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: What does INV-LRECL mean?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T02:38:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l4I19.149590$88.2897455@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3D4740E5.30108@mindspring.com>`

```
Probably it means INVALID LOGICAL RECORD LENGTH
Check your JCL, there is probably a mismatch. -Paul


"J M Pittman" <jjmpittmaniiii@mindspring.com> wrote in message news:3D4740E5.30108@mindspring.com...
> I'm getting an odd file open error with fujitsu of  'INV-LRECL'.    I
> thought it might be record length at variance with the file description,
> but it appears that is not the answer.  Does anyone know what it means?
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What does INV-LRECL mean?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-30T22:36:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai7lvk$q3c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3D4740E5.30108@mindspring.com> <l4I19.149590$88.2897455@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
JCL probably has NOTHING to do with it, given the original post was
mentioned "fujitsu" - unless this really is from one of those places with
IBM-look-alike Fujitsu products.

However, I agree that it probably DOES mean that the file "on disk" does NOT
match the FD (or Select/Assign) attributes.  On the other hand, the original
poster "claimed" that he had already checked this.
```

<a id="t-a16dc15a88"></a>
## Thread: MF Licensing — 1 this month / 39 total

### MF Licensing

- **From:** "James J.Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T18:40:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca>`

```
Please see following quotes,  bearing in mind John Bradley, CEO of
Liant, was quite forthcoming in highlighting the problems, (making
money),  for those compiler vendors who are solely software developers.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With the last service pack for Net Express 3.1 MF again has changed the
license.

Now one must do the following (someone sent me this):

>
> we were told, yes, MF changed their licence-politics and from now on
> the customer has to purchase a licence for every System (Workstation
or
> Server) on wich the software is installed. Under UNIX is es even worse
-
> a licence for each process (yikes).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I must admit I never read this before since I only do programming, but

> this also puzzles me:
>
> AUDITS:  Licensee agrees to (i) keep records of and provide the
> location, model and serial number of any and all machines on which the

> Software is installed or from which the Software is accessed, and how
> many users are accessing the Software, (ii) implement a reasonable
> mechanism or process to ensure that Licensee?s use of the Software
does
> not exceed the number of licenses granted,
>
> Copyright (C) 2002 Micro Focus International Ltd. All rights reserved.

Bottom line- in further query - we found that one must buy a serial
number for
EACH runtime AND tell MF the customers information --- meaning you must
tell MF who your customers are.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*** In confirmation of above quote, somebody sent me the following :-

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Yes, Jimmy, this is (unfortunately) entirely true.  You can find more
info
here:

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/slpubb.htm

http://downloads.microfocus.com/websync/servicepacks/n31/documentation/cust3

1sp1.doc

It involves a different approach that includes a Licensing Database! on
your
users' machines.  The application will fail to load the run-time in case
the
license info is not present or is invalid.

As far as unique serial numbers for each run-time, I don't think that's
true.  I think you will get one serial/license which you distribute to
all
your users; the rest is probably an honour system.  This is what I
understand......,
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

<a id="t-4061ae3018"></a>
## Thread: Help with memory addresses in IBM COBOL for OS/390 &amp; VM 2.2.1 — 2 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with memory addresses in IBM COBOL for OS/390 &amp; VM 2.2.1

- **From:** harouvis <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T15:16:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d484588$1_5@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
Is there any differences in release IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM 2.2.1 and
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM 2.1.2 regarding the need to reference all
addresses in the linkage section when setting pointers to these
addresses?

In other words has there been an enhancement that will allow COBOL
addressibility to areas in the WORKING STORAGE section instead of
requiring these areas to be in the linkage section?

Any help or feedback is appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with memory addresses in IBM COBOL for OS/390 &amp; VM 2.2.1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-07-31T15:41:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ai9i8c$1a8$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d484588$1_5@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
From,
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGYLR205/FRONT_2.1?

"Support for format 5 of the SET statement (SET pointer TO ADDRESS OF
identifier-7) extended to the Working-Storage Section and the Local-Storage
Section ("Format 5: SET for USAGE IS POINTER data items"  in topic 6.2.33.5)
"
```
